Three possible mechanisms: (a) the Kaggle curator pulled multi-level postings and LinkedIn's associate seniority flag applies loosely to "individual contributor" rather than YOE level in this export; (b) the label was machine-inferred on the asaniczka ingest side, introducing noise; (c) some LinkedIn postings legitimately reach "senior" titles at what LinkedIn still tags as associate seniority. Either way, the finding is that LinkedIn's associate tag in asaniczka is a weak indicator of junior-level content. Worth noting for T03 (seniority audit) since it feeds into how much to trust seniority_native as an anchor at all.

  6. Weighted Jaccard(asaniczka_associate, arshkon_associate) = 0.12 is lower than (asaniczka_associate, arshkon_entry) = 0.30. Counterintuitive — you'd expect the two associate cells to look most alike. Two effects: (a) arshkon_associate's cell is tiny (263 rows) and its top-50 title mass is dominated by small numbers, so weighted Jaccard is low everywhere against it; (b) asaniczka_associate's top-title mass is concentrated on the same generic "software engineer"-family titles as arshkon_entry, because both cells are SWE-heavy. The "entry-like" weighted Jaccard reflects shared generic SWE vocabulary, not shared junior vocabulary.

  7. The scraped entry pool is rule-keyword-dominated (79%). In 2024, the strong-rule share of entry was 54% (arshkon) and 55% (asaniczka); in 2026 scraped it is 79%. Two candidate mechanisms: (a) 2026 postings use more explicit junior-keyword titles ("Junior SWE", "Associate Software Engineer") — a title-language shift; (b) the scraped LLM frame doesn't fire on as many scraped rows (the 30.5% extraction coverage from T01), so the LLM doesn't get to re-classify as many scraped entry candidates. Either way, any 2024-vs-2026 entry-share finding should be cross-checked for composition sensitivity to seniority_final_source.

docs.title: Data-quality caveats
docs.text:
• The title-cue patterns are directional diagnostics only. "Lead" can match "lead generation" (not a seniority signal) but this is acceptable noise for a SWE-filtered sample; a more careful validation is T03's job.
  • Arshkon_associate (263 SWE rows) is a small cell. Its diagnostic value for T02 is limited — but the three other comparison cells are adequately powered, so the decision rule still fires.
  • yoe_extracted is missing for ~30–40% of each cell. All YOE summaries are on the YOE-known subset; share reported in the table.
  • seniority_final will change as Stage 9/10 budgets are re-run. Today's 175 arshkon-entry LLM labels and 83 asaniczka-entry LLM labels depend on the balanced LLM frame; a larger budget would expand them.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. Do NOT pool asaniczka.associate with arshkon.entry under any "combined junior" variant. That move produces a contaminated junior baseline where ~33% of the asaniczka-half rows are structurally mid-senior.

  2. For 2024 junior baselines, use T30's J2 (combined entry+associate under seniority_final), J3 (yoe≤2), or J4 (yoe≤3). These already operate on the resolved column and sidestep the native-label issue.

  3. Cross-source entry-level sanity checks must be arshkon-only for anything that anchors on seniority_native — confirmed in this task. This is the documented caveat and T02 makes it concrete.

  4. Flag the seniority_final_source composition for 2024 vs 2026 junior pools. 2026 junior is 79% rule-driven, 2024 junior is ~55% rule-driven. Any entry-share or entry-stratified finding should report under a title_keyword-only sensitivity variant in addition to the full pool.

  5. Treat asaniczka_associate in analyses as "mid-heavy mixed" rather than "junior-adjacent". For tasks that stratify by seniority level (T08, T09, T14, T20, T21, T22), asaniczka_associate rows are best treated as a separate mixed bucket, not merged with either entry or mid-senior.

  6. T03 (seniority audit, Agent B): The native associate label behaves very differently across arshkon (junior-leaning) and asaniczka (mid-leaning) even though both sources are 2024 LinkedIn. This is evidence that seniority_native has cross-snapshot drift — worth isolating in the T03 kappa / per-class accuracy tables.

docs.title: Script
docs.text: exploration/scripts/T02_asaniczka_associate_proxy.py. Run .venv/bin/python exploration/scripts/T02_asaniczka_associate_proxy.py. Deterministic. TDD asserts cover the title-cue regex patterns on positive / negative examples.

docs.title: T03 — Seniority label audit
docs.text: Agent: B (Wave 1) · Date: 2026-04-17 · Input: data/unified.parquet, SWE rows only (LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok').

docs.title: Headline
docs.text: seniority_final is defensible as the production seniority column for labeled cells but is materially under-powered on the entry and director tails. The YOE ordering is correct across sources (entry ≈ 2 YOE, mid-senior ≈ 6, director ≈ 7–9), the rule+LLM composition is internally coherent, and only ~5% of LLM-labeled rows look like potential routing leakage. The column's weakness is coverage on the tails, not correctness. T30 should take seniority_final as given and construct its entry-side recovery with ablations rather than try to repair the column itself.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Base filter: source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_english = true AND date_flag = 'ok' AND is_swe = true. All figures cite the exact subset, the column definition, and the denominator. Kappa is computed on the 4-level alignment {entry, associate, mid-senior, director}, dropping intern and executive from native and excluding unknown from both columns (since including it as a category would overweight coverage gaps as disagreements — see caveat 2 below).

SWE counts after the default filter: arshkon 4,691 · asaniczka 18,129 · scraped 40,881. Periods present: 2024-01 (asaniczka), 2024-04 (arshkon), 2026-03, 2026-04 (scraped).

docs.title: Step 1. seniority_final_source profile
docs.text:
SWE rows, distribution of how seniority_final was resolved:

Source title_keyword title_manager llm unknown kaggle_arshkon 37.8% 0.0% 62.2% 0.06% kaggle_asaniczka 58.8% 0.0% 28.6% 12.6% scraped 43.5% 0.002% 16.9% 39.5%

By period:

Period (source) title_keyword llm unknown 2024-01 (asaniczka) 58.8% 28.6% 12.6% 2024-04 (arshkon) 37.8% 62.2% 0.06% 2026-03 (scraped) 44.1% 17.6% 38.4% 2026-04 (scraped) 43.0% 16.3% 40.6%

Reading: The LLM share is dramatically different across sources. Arshkon is nearly fully covered (62% LLM + 38% rule). Scraped has 40% unknown — the LLM budget did not route every rule-unknown row. This is a power constraint, not a correctness issue — rows with no signal at all stay unknown and are correctly filtered out of known-seniority denominators. Asaniczka unknown rate (12.6%) is lower than scraped (40%) despite both being large, because a larger share of asaniczka was routed to Stage 10. Tables: tables/T03/source_profile_by_source.csv, tables/T03/source_profile_by_period.csv, tables/T03/source_profile_by_source_x_final.csv.

docs.title: Weak-marker titles (II / III / IV)
docs.text:
Universe: 1,402 SWE rows with seniority_final_source = 'llm' whose title matches (?i)\b(II|III|IV)\b. Sample of 100 inspected.

Roman markers are not explicit seniority signals per the LLM prompt design (which looks for title keywords like "senior"/"junior" only). 94/100 sampled rows came back seniority_final = 'unknown', 6/100 agreed with a broad Roman-to-seniority mapping. This matches the LLM's documented design — do not count as error. But it means 1,402 rows of routed corpus yield almost no usable label.

docs.title: Strong-keyword titles routed to LLM (possible leakage)
docs.text:
Universe: 797 SWE rows with seniority_final_source = 'llm' whose title matches \b(senior|staff|principal|lead|junior)\b — these should normally be handled by Stage 5's title_keyword rule. Sample of 100 inspected:

  • 95/100 agreed with the strong keyword (e.g., title "Lead Golang Engineer" → LLM returns mid-senior).
  • 5/100 disagreed or returned unknown.

Inspection shows these routed rows are predominantly compound lead titles (e.g., "DevOps Testing Lead", "Software Engineer, Lead" — 91/100 of the sample contain lead). Stage 5's title_keyword regex appears to have gaps on position-embedded lead (not all "X Lead" positional variants), so these rows correctly fall through to the LLM. Qualitative routing-error rate ≤ 5% — the LLM is not systematically contradicting the rules, it is filling gaps left by Stage 5's conservative keyword set.

Tables: tables/T03/step2_llm_weak_marker_sample.csv, tables/T03/step2_llm_strong_keyword_sample.csv, tables/T03/step2_roman_vs_final_summary.csv, tables/T03/step2_strong_keyword_agreement.csv.

docs.title: Step 3. seniority_final vs seniority_native (Cohen's kappa + per-class)
docs.text:
4-level alignment, excluding unknown from both columns (see caveat 2):

Source n aligned Cohen's κ kaggle_arshkon 1,639 0.403 kaggle_asaniczka 11,746 0.083 scraped 15,422 0.491

Asaniczka's κ is near-zero because native has only mid-senior/associate classes — almost all rows sit in a single cell, so κ's chance correction dominates. The informative sources are arshkon and scraped.

Per-class accuracy (= diagonal / row total, native as reference):

arshkon SWE:

Native n Diag Per-class accuracy entry 120 53 44.2% associate 82 4 4.9% mid-senior 1,422 1,393 97.9% director 15 5 33.3%

scraped SWE:

Native n Diag Per-class accuracy entry 703 476 67.7% associate 381 7 1.8% mid-senior 14,099 13,845 98.2% director 239 43 18.0%

What the κ gap (0.40 → 0.49) says. Scraped kappa is higher primarily because scraped entry-class accuracy is higher (67.7% vs 44.2%). This is not evidence of native-classifier temporal instability per se — it is consistent with a simpler explanation: seniority_final is more likely to fire entry for rows where the title uses strong junior markers (Stage 5), and the 2026 scraped corpus contains more junior-marker titles than 2024 arshkon. Cross-checking on the raw marker rate is a T30 job; the κ per se does not let us disentangle "native means a different thing in 2024" from "final recovers entry from titles more often in 2026." Do not over-interpret.

Associate is the failure class. 4.9% (arshkon) and 1.8% (scraped) — seniority_final systematically pushes native-associate rows into mid-senior or unknown. This is expected: Stage 5 rules do not have a dedicated "associate" keyword, and the LLM prompt treats associate as a rare explicit signal. Any analysis stratifying on associate from seniority_final is not meaningful — use the J2 (entry+associate combined) operationalization if you want the junior bucket to include native associates.

Director undercoverage at 18% (scraped) and 33% (arshkon) is meaningful: the LLM prompt labels director conservatively and Stage 5 fires director only on explicit VP/Director keywords. 79% of scraped director-native rows become mid-senior in final. For RQ1 senior-side questions, this pushes us toward S1 (mid-senior+director combined) or S3 (title-keyword senior) — see T30.

Tables: tables/T03/kappa_summary.csv, tables/T03/kappa_crosstab_*.csv, tables/T03/kappa_per_class_*.csv.

docs.title: Step 4. Native-label YOE diagnostic
docs.text:
Median yoe_extracted by seniority label, SWE rows with parsed YOE:

Source Native label n Median YOE Q25 Q75 arshkon entry 458 3.0 2.0 5.0 arshkon associate 149 3.0 2.0 5.0 arshkon mid-senior 1,477 5.0 4.0 7.0 arshkon director 14 7.0 5.0 10.0 asaniczka associate 1,257 4.0 3.0 5.0 asaniczka mid-senior 11,577 5.0 4.0 8.0 scraped entry 1,818 2.0 1.0 2.0 scraped associate 915 4.0 3.0 5.0 scraped mid-senior 19,878 5.0 3.0 7.0 scraped director 207 8.0 5.0 10.0

Same by seniority_final:

Source Final label n Median YOE arshkon entry 65 2.0 arshkon mid-senior 1,396 5.0 arshkon director 13 5.0 asaniczka entry 100 2.0 asaniczka mid-senior 8,545 5.0 asaniczka director 74 8.0 scraped entry 318 2.0 scraped mid-senior 13,129 5.0 scraped director 210 6.0

Strong finding. seniority_final = 'entry' rows have a tighter and lower YOE distribution (median 2) than seniority_native = 'entry' (median 2 in scraped, 3 in arshkon). Arshkon's native entry label is noisier on YOE than scraped's — 458 arshkon entry rows have median YOE 3 and Q75 of 5 — i.e. a quarter of arshkon native-entry rows demand ≥5 YOE, which is the yoe_seniority_contradiction flag territory. By filtering to seniority_final, we get a cleaner "entry" cell — but only 65/4,691 arshkon SWE rows (1.4%). This IS the power trade-off T30 will price.

Tables: tables/T03/native_yoe_by_source.csv, tables/T03/final_yoe_by_source.csv.

docs.title: Entry-level recovery summary
docs.text:
native_entry final_entry overlap (both) final_entry / native_entry arshkon 738 175 53 23.7% asaniczka 0 204 0 — scraped 4,689 1,275 476 27.2%

Only about 1/4 of native-entry rows survive into seniority_final = 'entry'. 618 of arshkon's 738 native-entry rows were routed to the LLM and came back unknown — i.e. the LLM saw no explicit title/description signal. 2,915 of scraped's 4,689 native-entry rows were never routed (source unknown, no Stage 5 rule fired) and stayed unknown.

docs.title: Defensibility recommendation
docs.text:
seniority_final is defensible. Four points of evidence:

  1. YOE ordering is correct across sources. Median YOE climbs monotonically entry → mid-senior → director in every source × period cell. Disagreement with YOE on entry would be the disqualifying failure mode and it does not appear.
  2. Strong-keyword agreement is high. LLM decisions on rule-handled keywords agree with the rules 95% of the time where we can check.
  3. Routing leakage is bounded. Fewer than 5% of LLM-labeled SWE rows look like possible rule-routing misses, and those are conservative false-negatives of Stage 5 (mostly compound "lead" titles), not LLM errors.
  4. Composition is transparent. seniority_final_source lets any downstream user subset to rule-only or LLM-only. There is no hidden derivation.

Known failure modes that downstream tasks must respect:

  • Associate is not meaningful. Use entry+associate combined (T30/J2) when you want the full junior bucket.
  • Director is undercoverage, not mis-labeled. Use mid-senior+director (T30/S1) for the senior bucket whenever power matters.
  • seniority_final = 'unknown' is ~40% of scraped SWE and is heterogeneous. Do NOT use "known-seniority share" as the only denominator for cross-period comparison; always report the "of-all-SWE" share as a robustness check because differential routing (62% LLM in arshkon vs 17% in scraped) makes the known-seniority subset compositionally different across periods.

T03 does not block T30. Move forward on seniority_final with the documented caveats.

docs.title: Data-quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Asaniczka native lacks entry/director by construction — asaniczka's native-vs-final comparison is informative only for mid-senior.
  2. Cohen's κ here excludes unknown rows from both sides (since unknown on either axis is a coverage state, not a class disagreement). Including unknown in the confusion matrix would inflate disagreement artifactually. The excluded-from-κ counts are large (~40% of scraped SWE) — the κ value reflects quality on labeled cells only.
  3. Kappa's interpretation depends on the marginals. Scraped kappa (0.49) is not directly comparable to arshkon kappa (0.40) because the class distributions differ; the per-class accuracy table (entry 44% vs 68%, associate 5% vs 2%, etc.) is the more informative surface.
  4. seniority_final_source = 'llm' rows with seniority_final = 'unknown' are documented correct behavior — LLM was called and returned no explicit signal. Do not count these as errors.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Arshkon's native entry label is noisier on YOE than scraped's. Arshkon native-entry median YOE is 3, Q75 = 5 — meaning a quarter of arshkon "entry" postings demand ≥5 YOE (label-YOE contradiction). Scraped native-entry median is 2, Q75 = 2. This suggests the baseline 2024 entry rate measured via seniority_native is partially a labeling artifact, not a pure labor-market signal. RQ1 comparisons that use seniority_native for the 2024 baseline should report this as a caveat.
  2. Scraped unknown rate (39.5%) is dominated by unrouted rows, not by LLM returning unknown. ~72% of scraped-unknown rows have seniority_final_source = 'unknown' (never routed). If the LLM budget were raised for scraped, a large fraction of that 39.5% would move into labeled cells.
  3. Arshkon is more heavily LLM-processed than scraped (62% vs 17% of SWE). This is the opposite of what one might expect given how much larger scraped is. Any known-seniority analysis that does not report both denominators is at risk of a compositional artifact masquerading as a temporal signal.
  4. The lead keyword is Stage 5's biggest leak. 750 SWE rows with "lead" in title reached the LLM because Stage 5's rule doesn't cover all positional variants (e.g., "DevOps Testing Lead" vs "Lead DevOps Engineer"). Not a correctness issue — LLM recovers 95% of them — but it explains why seniority_final_source = 'llm' is disproportionately populated with senior-side rows in scraped.
  5. Native-associate is a non-class in seniority_final. 1.8% accuracy (scraped) means the column essentially refuses to put rows in the associate bucket. Any analysis that counts associate-specific rows is not meaningful on seniority_final — use T30/J2 (entry+associate combined) or avoid.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• T30 must use J2 (entry+associate) as the primary junior operationalization for power, with J1 (entry-only) held as a label-quality ablation. The entry-only cell has too few rows and too much between-source variance in how entry gets recovered.
  • T30 must use S1 (mid-senior+director) as the primary senior operationalization. Director-only is label-undercovered.
  • Any task stratifying by seniority_final must report both "of-all-SWE" and "of-known-seniority" denominators because the scraped unknown share is 40% and differential routing makes the known-only cross-period comparison potentially misleading.
  • Do not pool asaniczka into any seniority_native-based diagnostic — asaniczka's native has no entry/director classes.
  • Future preprocessing improvement: Stage 5's lead-keyword rule is the lowest-hanging fruit for raising rule-coverage (would move ~750 scraped SWE rows from LLM to rule). Not blocking.

docs.title: T04 — SWE classification audit
docs.text: Agent: B (Wave 1) · Date: 2026-04-17 · Input: data/unified.parquet, LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok'.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text: The is_swe flag is defensible as the primary SWE classifier with an estimated false-positive rate on borderline cells of ~20-25% (concentrated in QA/Test/Systems/Reliability engineer titles) and a false-negative rate on strong-SWE-keyword titles of under 1% (fewer than 50 rows across 990K). The dual-flag invariant holds: zero violations of (is_swe + is_swe_adjacent + is_control) > 1 across all sources. Boundary classification (ML/Data/DevOps/AI Engineer) is highly consistent across periods — the rule puts 98-100% of these titles in SWE regardless of source. The main issue is not misclassification but how the rule defines the SWE boundary: QA/Test engineering is currently classified as SWE by the rule, but the LLM disagrees on 30-40% of those rows. Any analysis that wants a "pure coder" population should filter on swe_classification_llm = 'SWE' (where labeled) rather than rely on is_swe alone.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text: Base filter: source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_english = true AND date_flag = 'ok'. All SWE subset adds AND is_swe = true. SWE rows after filter: arshkon 4,691 · asaniczka 18,129 · scraped 40,881.

docs.title: Step 1. SWE by swe_classification_tier
docs.text:
Source regex title_lookup_llm embedding_high Total arshkon 4,158 (88.6%) 456 (9.7%) 77 (1.6%) 4,691 asaniczka 15,775 (87.0%) 1,968 (10.9%) 386 (2.1%) 18,129 scraped 38,131 (93.3%) 2,159 (5.3%) 591 (1.4%) 40,881

Regex-tier rows carry avg confidence = 1.0 by construction. title_lookup_llm rows carry avg confidence = 0.9. embedding_high rows have avg confidence ≈ 0.89. Scraped 2026 is more regex-tier-heavy than Kaggle sources (93.3% vs 87-89%), which is consistent with scraped using the modern scraper's typed title field and less noisy variant titles than Kaggle CSV imports.

Occupation breakdown:

Source SWE SWE-adjacent Control Other Total arshkon 4,691 2,396 8,614 94,659 110,360 asaniczka 18,129 9,420 105,341 714,636 847,526 scraped 40,881 11,592 41,790 159,363 253,626

SWE-to-control ratio: arshkon 0.54, asaniczka 0.17, scraped 0.98. Scraped has ~6× the SWE density of asaniczka — consistent with the scraper targeting SWE-heavy metros.

Tables: tables/T04/step1_*.csv.

docs.title: Step 2. Borderline SWE sample (50 rows)
docs.text:
Sample conditions: SWE and either swe_confidence ∈ [0.3, 0.7] OR tier = title_lookup_llm. All 50 sampled rows have swe_confidence = 0.9 and tier = title_lookup_llm — meaning the swe_confidence range [0.3, 0.7] is empty, so the query selected from the title_lookup_llm pool exclusively.

Content assessment: The sample is dominated by QA/Test engineering titles ("Test Engineer", "Automation Engineer", "Quality Assurance Engineer", "Software Test Engineer"), SRE ("Reliability Engineer"), security engineering ("DevSecOps Principal Engineer"), and AI engineering ("Artificial Intelligence Engineer", "MLOps Engineer", "Database Engineer"). All are reasonable SWE classifications but live near the SWE/SWE-adjacent boundary.

LLM cross-check disagreement within the sample: Of the 50 borderline rows, the LLM was called on 7 — of those, 3 returned SWE, 2 returned SWE_ADJACENT, 2 returned NOT_SWE. This is a 4/7 = 57% disagreement rate on this hardest slice, but the sample is small. See the full-corpus LLM disagreement numbers below for the robust picture.

Likely false-positive rate for borderline SWE ≈ 20-30%. Titles like "Automation Engineer" and "Quality Assurance Engineer" are SWE-adjacent by most definitions — they involve coding but their primary function is QA/testing infrastructure, not product software development.

Sample: tables/T04/step2_borderline_swe_sample.csv.

docs.title: Step 3. Borderline non-SWE sample (50 rows, titles w/ engineer/developer/software but is_swe=false)
docs.text:
Content assessment: Overwhelmingly non-SWE engineering roles — Process, Civil, Electrical, Chemical, Mechanical, Manufacturing, Industrial, Network, Field, Design Engineer. Most sit in tier = 'unresolved' (meaning no SWE classifier fired — correct). A minority with "Developer" in the title (Appian, Power BI, Pega, SharePoint, SQL, Hadoop, etc.) are borderline — these are configuration/development roles in vendor platforms.

Corpus-wide FN estimate. Rows with strong SWE-indicator title patterns (software engineer, backend/frontend/full-stack engineer/developer, web/mobile/app developer, or language-specific developer like python developer) that are flagged is_swe = false:

Source Strong-FN candidates SWE true positives Estimated FN rate arshkon 6 4,691 0.13% asaniczka 16 18,129 0.09% scraped 25 40,881 0.06%

Manual inspection of the 47 candidates shows that ~15-20 are genuinely mobile/web app developer roles that should have been SWE (examples: "Android App Developer", "Shopify App Developer", "Senior Mobile App Developer", "Web App Developer"). The rest are compound titles where the rule punted (e.g., "Senior Software Engineer - Solution Architect" — two function labels create ambiguity).

Estimated corpus FN rate: ≤ 0.15%. Negligible for aggregate statistics but worth documenting for any analysis that specifically targets mobile app development subset.

Sample: tables/T04/step3_borderline_non_swe_sample.csv.

docs.title: Top adjacent titles
docs.text:
Adjacent rows cluster on: Data Scientist, Data Analyst, Quality Engineer, Network Engineer, Architect (Solutions/Enterprise/Data), Security Engineer, Systems Administrator. These are reasonable SWE-adjacent definitions — the roles involve technical work and sometimes code but are not primarily software development.

Adjacent tiers split: regex (~45%), embedding_adjacent (~30%), title_lookup_llm (~25%). The embedding_adjacent tier is the largest indicator of rule vs embedding vs LLM composition — it fires where titles are semantically near-SWE but not regex-matchable. Roughly similar distribution across sources.

docs.title: Top control titles
docs.text:
Control is dominated by: Registered Nurse (+variants), Accountant (Senior/Staff/Project), Financial Analyst, Electrical Engineer, Mechanical Engineer, Civil Engineer. These are the study's non-tech occupation group for RQ comparisons.

Adjacency vs SWE distinction looks clean in spot check. The adjacent sample has titles like "Data Scientist", "Data Analyst", "Network Engineer", "Quality Engineer" — which the research design explicitly treats as SWE-adjacent rather than SWE. Adjacent is distinct from SWE and distinct from Control.

Tables: tables/T04/step4_*.csv.

docs.title: False-positive estimates
docs.text:
Two approaches:

  1. LLM cross-check disagreement — a direct quality signal. For rows where the LLM was called (llm_classification_coverage = 'labeled'), what fraction disagree with is_swe = true?
Source LLM-called is_swe=true rows LLM says NOT_SWE LLM says SWE_ADJACENT Disagreement rate arshkon 2,917 365 269 21.7% asaniczka 5,190 1,074 548 31.3% scraped 6,918 497 609 16.0%

The LLM-called subset is non-random — it over-represents title_lookup_llm and embedding_high tiers (the harder cases). If we assume regex-tier rows are ~99% correct SWE by construction (they matched explicit SWE-title patterns), the estimated corpus-wide FP rate under the LLM's definition is:

arshkon FP ≈ (2,917 × 21.7% / 4,691) × (share of arshkon that's non-regex tier) ≈ 3-5%. scraped FP ≈ 16% × (40,881 − 38,131) / 40,881 ≈ 1.1% corpus-wide (rough).

  1. Intuitive classification of borderline titles (QA, Test, Systems, Reliability — all tipped "adjacent" by LLM). These represent 15-20% of the borderline subset but only 2-5% of total SWE rows.

Combined FP estimate: 3-5% corpus-wide, concentrated in QA/Test/Systems-engineering cells. Analyses that need a strict "software developer" population should filter on swe_classification_llm = 'SWE' (where labeled) or exclude titles matching quality|test|assurance|systems|reliability|application engineer.

docs.title: False-negative estimates
docs.text: From Step 3: strong-SWE-title FN rate is ≤ 0.15% corpus-wide. Most "should have been SWE" mis-classifications are mobile/web "App Developer" titles. Not a meaningful concern for aggregate analysis.

docs.title: Step 6. Dual-flag invariant
docs.text:
(is_swe + is_swe_adjacent + is_control) > 1 count: zero violations across all three sources (arshkon 110,360 rows, asaniczka 847,526, scraped 253,626). Mutual exclusivity holds.

Table: tables/T04/step6_dual_flag.csv.

docs.title: Step 7. Boundary cases across periods
docs.text:
Title pattern arshkon n_match arshkon SWE-rate asaniczka n_match asaniczka SWE-rate scraped n_match scraped SWE-rate ML Engineer 90 100.0% 204 100.0% 1,594 100.0% Data Engineer 333 100.0% 953 100.0% 3,024 99.97% DevOps Engineer 174 98.3% 282 97.2% 1,391 98.3% AI Engineer 27 100.0% 29 100.0% 1,227 99.9% Data Scientist 220 2.7% (ADJ 97.3%) 611 1.6% (ADJ 98.4%) 2,559 1.7% (ADJ 98.3%) ML Scientist 2 0% (other) 22 4.5% 90 3.3%

Reading:

  • ML Engineer, Data Engineer, DevOps Engineer, AI Engineer are classified SWE ≥98% of the time in all three sources. No temporal inconsistency.
  • Data Scientist is classified as SWE-adjacent ≥97% of the time in all three sources. Also consistent.
  • AI Engineer volume exploded from 27 (arshkon) to 1,227 (scraped 2026) — 45× growth. This is a RQ-relevant surprise: "AI Engineer" as a title barely existed in 2024 (arshkon: 27, asaniczka: 29) and now represents nearly 3% of scraped 2026 SWE postings. This is a T19-skill-trends signal.
  • ML Engineer similarly grew from 90 → 1,594 (17×), though some of that is corpus size growth (scraped is ~9× arshkon).
  • DevOps Engineer has a 1-3% adjacent tail — those ~20-30 rows per source with title containing "DevOps Engineer" but flagged adjacent. Likely compound titles (e.g., "DevOps Application Engineer"). Too small to be a systemic issue.

The single most important finding here: boundary classification is temporally stable. The rule does not drift across periods, so "AI Engineer grew" is a real signal, not a rule change artifact.

Table: tables/T04/step7_boundary_classification.csv.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. swe_confidence is effectively tier-bound (regex=1.0, title_lookup_llm=0.9, embedding=0.88-0.90). The range [0.3, 0.7] is empty in the SWE population — the borderline sample definition given in the task was substantively a title_lookup_llm-only sample. This is worth noting but not a defect.
  2. FP estimates use LLM as a reference. The LLM is itself imperfect; its definition of SWE may be tighter (excludes QA/Test) than the rule's. The 16-31% disagreement represents a definitional difference more than a labeling error. Pick the definition intentional for the analysis question.
  3. The rule labels QA/Test engineering roles as SWE. This is a defensible choice (they write automation code) but it inflates the SWE share for aggregate counts. If the analysis wants "product software developers only," add an exclusion rule for titles matching qa|test|assurance|automation (?=engineer).
  4. Adjacent-tier allocation uses embeddings, which are black-box. Spot-check precision appeared high, but downstream analysis may want to re-validate if adjacent rows are used as a distinct group.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. AI Engineer is a 45× growth story from 2024 to 2026. Arshkon (April 2024) has 27 matches, scraped 2026-03/04 has 1,227. Corpus size explains ~9×; the remaining ~5× is genuine title proliferation. This is a directly RQ-relevant finding and was not specifically part of T04's mandate — worth flagging to Wave 2 skill-trend agents as a high-signal target.
  2. LLM-vs-rule disagreement is 16% in scraped but 31% in asaniczka, almost 2×. Mechanism: asaniczka's harder/weirder titles are more often routed to title_lookup_llm tier and the LLM is more willing to disagree with the title-lookup-based classification. Alternative mechanism: asaniczka has a noisier title column (more job-board aggregator spam), and the LLM correctly identifies many as non-SWE while the rule-lookup erred. Either way, asaniczka SWE rows should carry a larger uncertainty band than scraped SWE rows.
  3. The title_lookup_llm tier is the FP-concentration zone. If a single action were to be taken to tighten SWE classification, it would be to re-evaluate the title_lookup_llm tier's title allowlist — specifically QA/Test engineering titles. However this must be done carefully because QA/Test titles are a non-trivial share (~2-5% of the current SWE corpus) and changing their classification will shift time-series.
  4. SWE-to-control ratio varies 6× across sources (arshkon 0.54, asaniczka 0.17, scraped 0.98). This is purely compositional — the scraper targets SWE-heavy searches, while asaniczka is a broad job-board scrape. Any RQ comparing SWE to control must normalize for this compositional difference.
  5. Data Scientist is unambiguously SWE-adjacent in all three sources (>97% adjacent-rate). For content analyses using the adjacent cohort, Data Scientist is the single largest contributing title. Analyses that want to exclude Data Scientist can do so cleanly via swe_classification_tier != 'title_lookup_llm' OR swe_confidence >= 0.95 on the adjacent subset (approximate exclusion).
  6. Zero dual-flag violations across 1.2M rows is itself notable — indicates the preprocessing invariant is rigorously enforced.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• Content analyses targeting "pure software developers" should filter is_swe = true AND (swe_classification_llm = 'SWE' OR llm_classification_coverage != 'labeled') — i.e. include rule-confident rows by default but defer to LLM for routed rows. This drops QA/Test/Systems engineer rows that the LLM flags adjacent.
  • Cross-period rate claims on SWE share (RQ1 volume) should be caveat-marked with the 3-5% FP tail. The FP is temporally stable, so first-differences are not biased by it, but absolute shares carry the tail.
  • AI Engineer title growth (27 → 1,227) should be a featured headline in RQ4 skill-requirement analyses. Wave 2 agents doing skill-density should explicitly report AI-Engineer-title prevalence as a separate line item.
  • DevOps Engineer's 2% adjacent tail is small enough to ignore; do not add sensitivity tests for it.
  • Do not re-tune swe_classification_tier without a dedicated labeling effort. The LLM disagreement rate is a definitional divergence, not a labeling defect. Changing the rule mid-study would introduce a time-series artifact.

docs.title: T05 — Cross-dataset comparability
docs.text: Agent: C (Wave 1) Date: 2026-04-17 Scope: SWE, LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok' unless stated. Three sources compared: kaggle_arshkon, kaggle_asaniczka, scraped (LinkedIn). Indeed sensitivity added for step 9. Input: data/unified.parquet. All queries via DuckDB. Script: exploration/scripts/T05_cross_dataset.py (+ T05_T06_figures.py).

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text:
The three sources are not measuring the same posting population. Every cross-source comparison rejects the null of identical distributions at p ≈ 0, and the within-2024 calibration (arshkon vs asaniczka) shows that the between-instrument effect is typically larger than any cross-period effect we observe. The J1 entry-share gap between arshkon and asaniczka (2024 only, h = 0.18) is 5× the cross-period arshkon-vs-scraped J1 gap (h = 0.03) — so the "entry share rose in 2026" or "entry share fell in 2026" claim cannot be defended from these numbers alone.

Platform labeling stability is weak. For the 20 SWE titles that appear both in arshkon and in scraped-LinkedIn, seniority_native = 'entry' fell in 14/20 and rose in 6/20 even when the same title was compared, while mean extracted YOE rose in 12/20 and fell in 8/20. The joint pattern — native entry rate down, YOE up — points at LinkedIn relabeling more than at a uniform market content shift.

Indeed cross-validation partially rescues LinkedIn. Indeed scraped (rule-based seniority only, high unknown rate) shows J1 = 3.05% and J3 (YOE ≤ 2) = 18.1%, both within noise of LinkedIn scraped (J1 = 3.12%, J3 = 17.2%). The LinkedIn-specific artifact hypothesis is NOT supported for entry-share levels, but the within-2024 noise remains the dominant robustness concern.

Most-trustworthy cross-source comparisons: company identity (Jaccard stable and interpretable), state-share direction (top metros reorder predictably), mean YOE (small 2024-to-2026 movement, mostly within noise). Least trustworthy: native-seniority-based entry shares and rates of any text-derived keyword density (description length grew ~1.5× between 2024 and 2026).

docs.title: 1. Description length
docs.text:
Source n mean median p10 p90 kaggle_arshkon 4,691 3,306 2,974 816 6,218 kaggle_asaniczka 18,129 3,881 3,733 1,255 6,580 scraped 40,881 4,912 4,862 1,839 7,921

Pairwise Kolmogorov-Smirnov on 20k-row samples:

a b KS stat p arshkon asaniczka 0.255 4.1e-3 arshkon scraped 0.319 4.8e-5 asaniczka scraped 0.206 3.2e-7

All pairwise differences reject the null. Median grew 64% from arshkon (2,974 chars) to scraped (4,862 chars). See figures/T05/01_length_hist.png.

Artifact risk: Instrument-driven. Kaggle arshkon/asaniczka were HTML-stripped and dedup was more aggressive; scraped preserves markdown formatting including explicit section headers, bullet points, and benefits blocks. Text-density metrics (mentions per 1K characters) built from the 2024-to-2026 contrast will automatically under-count 2026 density relative to 2024 unless normalized against description_core_llm.

docs.title: 2. Company overlap (canonical names)
docs.text:
a b nA nB intersection Jaccard arshkon asaniczka 2,298 4,948 742 0.114 arshkon scraped 2,298 7,940 1,004 0.109 asaniczka scraped 4,948 7,940 1,529 0.135

Top-50 companies per source → cross-source membership:

top50 of % in asaniczka % in scraped arshkon 78% 88% asaniczka 62% (in arshkon) 74% scraped 72% (in arshkon) 68% (in asaniczka)

Interpretation: Top-of-distribution employers are largely stable across sources — the big SWE employers in 2024 are still big in 2026. Long-tail employers differ strongly because asaniczka and scraped cover much broader company catalogs (7.7k and 2.2k distinct asaniczka/arshkon vs 18k+ titles in scraped — see §5). The small overall Jaccard is dominated by tail composition, not by the large-employer comparison that matters for cross-period trend statements.

Most trustworthy comparison. This is the metric that makes most cross-period sense.

docs.title: 3. Geographic distribution
docs.text:
Multi-location representatives (Stage 4 collapses, is_multi_location=true, location="multi-location", no state) — reported separately per spec:

Source multi-location reps null state (non-multi) total kaggle_arshkon 116 1,059 4,691 kaggle_asaniczka 894 635 18,129 scraped 3,090 2,167 40,881

State-share chi-squared (states with n ≥ 20 combined):

a b #states chi2 dof p Cramer's V arshkon asaniczka 45 367.3 44 1e-52 0.135 arshkon scraped 30 1188.9 29 9e-232 0.175 asaniczka scraped 45 6133.7 44 0 0.343

Shares shift predictably: CA and TX are oversampled in scraped (deliberate metro targeting by the scraper), NY is oversampled in asaniczka. Direction of metro ordering is consistent. Trustworthy for ranked-metro analyses, weak for share-based absolute-scale statements — the scraper's search-metro design intentionally concentrates on 26 metros.

docs.title: 4. Seniority distributions (exclude unknown)
docs.text:
Shares (of known-seniority SWE LinkedIn):

seniority_final arshkon asaniczka scraped associate 0.007 0.002 0.003 director 0.006 0.008 0.015 entry 0.077 0.017 0.067 mid-senior 0.910 0.973 0.915

Unknown rate (of all SWE LinkedIn):

source unknown share arshkon 51.7% asaniczka 35.2% scraped 53.4%

Pairwise chi-squared on seniority_final (excluding unknown):

a b chi2 p Cramer's V arshkon asaniczka 274.5 3e-59 0.140 arshkon scraped 26.2 9e-6 0.035 asaniczka scraped 433.3 1e-93 0.119

The cross-instrument (arshkon vs asaniczka) seniority effect is ~4× the cross-period (arshkon vs scraped) effect on Cramer's V. This is the single most consequential number in T05: any 2024→2026 seniority-share claim must pass a within-2024 noise floor before being interpretable. See §7 for the formal calibration.

docs.title: 5. Title vocabulary overlap
docs.text:
a b nA nB intersection Jaccard arshkon asaniczka 2,162 7,577 449 0.048 arshkon scraped 2,162 18,321 517 0.026 asaniczka scraped 7,577 18,321 927 0.037

Titles unique to scraped (top by volume) include: software engineer [multiple positions available] (88), ai agent engineer (23), ai solutions engineer (20), full stack ai engineer (18), ai automation engineer (18), software engineer - coreai (17), ai engineer intern (9), forward deployed ai engineer (10), remote software engineer – llm evaluation (9). The 2026 scraper captures a long AI-titled tail that simply did not exist in the 2024 Kaggle snapshots — T10 should map this evolution cleanly.

docs.title: 6. Industry (arshkon vs scraped)
docs.text:
company_industry is 0% populated in asaniczka. For arshkon (companion join) and scraped (LinkedIn API):

Top-20 industry shares (scraped):

industry arshkon share scraped share Software Development 0.167 0.365 IT Services and IT Consulting 0.328 0.175 Financial Services 0.107 0.101 Technology, Information and Internet 0.029 0.069 Defense and Space Manufacturing 0.033 0.055 Staffing and Recruiting 0.074 0.037 Information Services and Technology, Information and Internet (compound) 0.000 0.026 Aviation and Aerospace Component Manufacturing 0.007 0.017

chi2 = 2,892.8, dof = 136, p = 0, Cramer's V = 0.263.

Label-semantic warning: ~48% of arshkon AND ~48% of scraped industry strings are compound (contain commas or "and "). LinkedIn has since been labeling companies with multi-industry compounds like "Information Services and Technology, Information and Internet" (2.6% of scraped rows) that do not exist in arshkon. Industry-share comparisons are valid at the top level (Software Development, IT Services) but must tolerate ~25% slippage on the long-tail labels. T01 flagged the same issue; use it as metadata only, not a primary partition.

docs.title: 7. Artifact diagnostic + within-2024 calibration
docs.text:
This is the key table. Cohen's h for three share-based metrics, under each pairwise comparison:

Metric within-2024 h (arsh vs asa) arsh vs scraped h pool-2024 vs scraped h SNR (cross / within) J1 — seniority_final = entry 0.176 0.034 −0.097 0.19 J2 — entry ∪ associate 0.181 0.043 −0.091 0.24 J3 — yoe_extracted ≤ 2 0.139 −0.060 −0.172 0.43

Signal-to-noise ratio is below 2 for all three — well below the materiality threshold. Arshkon J1 (3.73%) is higher than both asaniczka J1 (1.13%) and scraped J1 (3.12%); the pooled 2024-vs-scraped comparison actually runs negative (2024 had higher J1 entry share than 2026) when asaniczka is weighted in.

Direction under different operationalizations:

arshkon asaniczka scraped J1 (entry %) 3.73% 1.13% 3.12% J2 (entry∪assoc %) 4.05% 1.26% 3.25% J3 (YOE ≤ 2, of YOE-known) 14.98% 10.36% 17.19%

J3 alone shows a positive 2024-to-2026 shift (+2pp arshkon-vs-scraped; +7pp pooled-vs-scraped), and that's also the metric least dependent on native-seniority labeling. Two plausible readings: 1. The JYP shift is real — scraped postings shifted their YOE floor downward. 2. The JYP shift is artifact — the scraped text preserves more boilerplate including junior-ish phrasing that the rule-based YOE parser picks up.

Step 9 and T06 both weigh in on which.

See figures/T05/02_entry_share_calibration.png.

docs.title: 8. Within-2024 calibration — extended
docs.text:
Mean metrics by source × seniority (SWE LinkedIn):

source seniority n mean len median YOE arshkon entry 175 3,508 2.0 arshkon mid-senior 2,064 3,720 5.0 asaniczka entry 204 3,291 2.0 asaniczka mid-senior 11,426 4,198 5.0

Within-2024 source effect on description length at fixed seniority: 13% longer in asaniczka mid-senior than in arshkon mid-senior. This is not trivial, but far smaller than the 2024-to-2026 shift (scraped mid-senior is 1.5× longer than arshkon mid-senior).

docs.title: 9. Platform labeling stability
docs.text:
The 20 SWE titles with ≥20 postings in both arshkon and scraped:

title n_arsh n_scraped Δ native entry share Δ mean YOE software engineer 419 3,974 −0.008 +0.17 data engineer 155 1,323 −0.081 +0.47 devops engineer 88 656 −0.011 −0.49 machine learning engineer 48 441 +0.042 +0.08 full stack engineer 63 407 +0.061 +0.42 site reliability engineer 51 325 −0.077 −0.44 embedded software engineer 41 318 −0.076 −0.94 test engineer 34 323 −0.091 −0.54 software developer 39 272 −0.088 −0.91 cloud engineer 35 270 −0.124 +0.52 systems engineer 54 230 −0.248 +1.39 full stack developer 24 259 −0.213 +1.12 software development engineer 35 241 −0.124 +0.65 java developer 77 177 −0.176 −0.08 system engineer 48 162 −0.013 +0.94 java software engineer 27 146 −0.090 +1.04 quality assurance engineer 23 149 −0.205 −0.18 developer 43 116 −0.613 −0.08 android developer 21 137 +0.042 +0.71 application engineer 24 133 −0.294 +1.33
  • Native entry share fell in 14/20 shared titles, rose in 6/20.
  • Mean YOE rose in 12/20 shared titles, fell in 8/20.
  • Only 6/20 show both metrics moving together (either both down = real change, or both up = harder to interpret).

The joint pattern (native entry down + YOE up) on titles like systems engineer (−25pp native entry, +1.4 YOE), full stack developer (−21pp, +1.1 YOE), application engineer (−29pp, +1.3 YOE) is consistent with LinkedIn relabeling the same title bucket away from "entry" more than with a content change. The data engineer pattern (−8pp, +0.5 YOE) is mild.

The developer title is a -61pp collapse in native entry share. This is a platform-level taxonomy change (the bare term "developer" gets relabeled away from entry) rather than content.

See figures/T05/03_shared_title_native_vs_yoe.png.

Indeed cross-validation. Indeed scraped (11,860 LinkedIn-excluded-from-LLM-frame rows) has: - seniority_final_source distribution: 362 entry (3.05%), 4,983 mid-senior, 133 director, 6,382 unknown (all from title_keyword/title_manager Stage 5 strong rules or unresolved). - J1 share = 3.05%, J3 share = 18.1%. Both sit within ~1pp of LinkedIn scraped (J1 3.12%, J3 17.2%). - The LinkedIn-platform-artifact hypothesis is weakened for ENTRY-SHARE LEVELS but not eliminated for LABEL STABILITY. Indeed's seniority is rule-based only (no LLM, no native), so it cannot diagnose whether LinkedIn's native label drifted — only whether the end-of-pipeline entry share matches across platforms. It does.

docs.title: Sensitivity-disagreement drill-in
docs.text:
The dominant sensitivity failure in T05 is the within-2024 calibration passing a noise floor that the cross-period effect does not exceed. This is the "SNR<2" row in §7. The mechanism is straightforward: 1. Arshkon uses seniority_native = 'entry' as a rich signal for Stage 5 and Stage 10 routing; asaniczka has ZERO native entry labels, so all asaniczka entry comes from the Stage 10 LLM pass. 2. Arshkon's seniority_final_source='llm' rate is different from asaniczka's by construction; asaniczka entry count reflects only what the LLM could detect from description text alone. 3. Scraped is structurally similar to asaniczka on this dimension (native label is LinkedIn-current, not 2024-snapshot), but its description text carries more boilerplate length.

The 2024 pool choice (arshkon only vs pooled 2024) alone flips the J1 direction of change: arshkon-alone-vs-scraped is a tiny rise; pooled-2024-vs-scraped is a clear fall. Downstream agents must choose ONE 2024 baseline and justify it; the orchestrator should pick pooled 2024 if volume is the priority, arshkon-only if native-label fidelity is the priority.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Within-2024 cross-source noise dwarfs cross-period effects. J1 SNR 0.19 is the headline. No one should make an "entry share rose 0.5pp" claim without weighing it against 2.6pp arshkon-vs-asaniczka noise on the same metric.

  2. Developer (bare title) lost 61 percentage points of native entry share between 2024 and 2026. This is specific to one title bucket — not a uniform drift — and suggests a LinkedIn taxonomy change for generic "developer" roles rather than a content change.

  3. Indeed scraped matches LinkedIn scraped on entry share (3.05% vs 3.12%). The within-instrument LinkedIn artifact story does not extend to end-of-pipeline entry share; the artifact, if any, sits in native-label drift upstream of seniority_final.

  4. The scraped corpus is 64% longer at the median than arshkon. For any text-density metric (mentions per 1K chars), expect naïve 2024-to-2026 rates to appear lower in 2026 simply because more boilerplate is preserved. T11/T12/T14 must use description_core_llm and normalize.

  5. Asaniczka is 35% unknown-seniority; arshkon and scraped are both 52-53%. Asaniczka has a materially richer seniority_native signal (all rows have 'mid-senior' or 'associate'), so its seniority_final gets filled more aggressively by Stage 10 LLM routing — that's the source of the calibration gap, not a different posting population.

  6. Industry labels drifted from "IT Services and IT Consulting" (arshkon 33%, scraped 18%) to "Software Development" (arshkon 17%, scraped 37%). This is a LinkedIn industry-taxonomy change, not a real sectoral shift. Treat as metadata.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• asaniczka has zero company_industry and zero native entry labels. All asaniczka-driven conclusions about entry-share, industry, or native-seniority drift are invalid by construction.
  • Indeed has zero native seniority; Stage 10 LLM is not routed on Indeed. Its seniority labels come from Stage 5 strong title rules only, with 54% unknown. Use only as a robustness probe, never as primary.
  • Scraped industry column includes ~2.6% of compound labels absent in arshkon. Before any industry-stratified comparison, collapse compounds to a primary token using a deterministic rule (take the first comma-separated piece).
  • Multi-location representative (is_multi_location=true) rows drop naturally from per-metro rollups. Scraped has 3,090 such reps (7.6% of its SWE-LinkedIn). Agents computing per-metro metrics must quote this excluded count.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• T08 distribution profiling: for every share metric you report 2024-to-2026, compute SNR = |arsh-vs-scraped h| / |arsh-vs-asa h|. Flag anything < 2 as "not above within-2024 noise floor."
  • T10 title evolution: use §5's titles-unique-to-scraped as the candidate list for emergent AI-engineering roles. Cross-check against T10's own vocabulary diff.
  • T11 requirements complexity + T12 corpus comparison: length normalization is essential — scraped is 64% longer at median. Density metrics MUST use description_core_llm filtered to llm_extraction_coverage = 'labeled'.
  • T16 company strategies: company Jaccard is the most trustworthy cross-source metric. Use the 125-company arshkon∩scraped overlap panel (computed in T06 §5) as the decomposition unit.
  • T20/T21 seniority: primary seniority definition must be J2 or J3 (not J1) given J1's low power and high within-2024 noise. T30 will produce the canonical recommendation.
  • T18 cross-occupation DiD: the platform-labeling drift on shared titles (native entry ↓, YOE ↑) is a plausible alternative explanation for any SWE-specific "junior compression" signal. Control occupations should be subjected to the same shared-title stability test before declaring SWE-specific effects.

docs.title: Appendix — output file index
docs.text:
All under exploration/tables/T05/: - 01_length_summary.csv — summary stats per source - 01_length_ks.csv — pairwise KS and Mann-Whitney - 02_company_overlap.csv — pairwise Jaccard - 02_top50_*.csv, 02_top50_overlap.csv — top-50 per source + cross-presence - 03_multi_location_and_null.csv — multi-location reps and null-state counts - 03_state_counts.csv — state × source pivot - 03_state_chi2.csv — chi-squared pairwise - 04_seniority_counts.csv, 04_seniority_shares.csv, 04_seniority_chi2.csv, 04_unknown_rate.csv - 05_title_overlap.csv, 05_titles_unique_*.csv - 06_industry_top40.csv, 06_industry_shares_top20.csv, 06_industry_semantics.csv, 06_industry_chi2.csv - 08_within2024_by_seniority.csv, 08_pooled_by_source.csv, 08_entry_shares.csv, 08_calibration_entry_share.csv - 09_top20_shared_titles.csv, 09_shared_title_native_seniority.csv, 09_native_entry_share_shift.csv, 09_shared_title_yoe.csv, 09_mean_yoe_shift.csv, 09_indeed_seniority.csv, 09_indeed_entry_share.csv - T05_summary.json

Figures under exploration/figures/T05/: - 01_length_hist.png - 02_entry_share_calibration.png - 03_shared_title_native_vs_yoe.png

docs.title: T06 — Company concentration deep investigation
docs.text: Agent: C (Wave 1) Date: 2026-04-17 Scope: SWE, LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok', across kaggle_arshkon, kaggle_asaniczka, scraped. Input: data/unified.parquet. All queries via DuckDB. Scripts: exploration/scripts/T06_concentration.py, T06_refine_specialists.py, T05_T06_figures.py. Shared artifact: exploration/artifacts/shared/entry_specialist_employers.csv (240 companies).

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text:
Posting concentration is larger than the cross-period entry-share signal — company composition drives the aggregate. Three interlocking facts:

  1. Scraped 2026 is more concentrated than arshkon 2024 (Gini 0.69 vs 0.43; top-20 share 18% vs 12%) — but most of that extra concentration lives in a handful of tech-giant intern pipelines, not in the aggregator class.
  2. 79-89% of SWE companies with ≥5 postings have ZERO entry-labeled (J1) rows in every source. Entry-level posting is a highly specialized activity and ~240 companies (~11% of eligible employers pooled) account for ~65% of the J3-YOE-proxy entry pool.
  3. Excluding the 240 entry-specialist employers flips the J3 cross-period direction. Full-corpus J3 rises 13.6% → 17.2% arsh→scraped; with specialists removed, J3 moves 13.6% → 14.4% — a ~75% shrinkage in effect size. The cross-period entry-share trend Wave 2 is likely to report is a compositional artifact of which specialists were active in which window.

Per-finding concentration prediction (§9) is the deliverable downstream tasks must read first. It dictates capping, dedup, and exclusion defaults for every Wave 2-3 aggregation.

Stage 4 multi-location dedup works. Only 16 residual (company × title × description_hash) triples remain within scraped (max dup = 2), validating the expected "verification, not discovery" role of the dup-template audit.

docs.title: 1. Concentration metrics per source
docs.text:
Units: company_name_canonical. HHI computed on shares × 100 (standard 0-10,000 scale). Gini on posting counts (higher = more concentrated).

source scope n_postings n_companies HHI Gini top1 top5 top10 top20 top50 kaggle_arshkon all 4,646 2,298 15.6 0.433 1.1% 4.6% 7.6% 12.3% 20.9% kaggle_arshkon no_aggregator 4,216 2,235 13.3 0.399 1.2% 4.0% 6.8% 10.4% 18.3% kaggle_asaniczka all 18,129 4,948 54.4 0.641 5.0% 12.2% 16.0% 21.1% 30.6% kaggle_asaniczka no_aggregator 15,186 4,814 19.1 0.588 1.9% 5.8% 9.1% 14.1% 23.5% scraped all 40,881 7,940 36.1 0.693 4.2% 9.4% 12.9% 18.1% 26.9% scraped no_aggregator 34,081 7,241 23.6 0.672 2.2% 7.2% 11.1% 16.6% 25.1%

Aggregator exclusion moves concentration dramatically for asaniczka (HHI 54 → 19, top-1 5.0% → 1.9%) because ClearanceJobs alone is 898/18,129 = 5.0% of asaniczka. For scraped, the drop is smaller (top-1 4.2% → 2.2%, Jobs via Dice is the top aggregator) because tech-giant non-aggregator concentration takes over.

Scraped is materially more concentrated than arshkon on BOTH Gini and top-20 share, even after removing aggregators. See figures/T06/01_lorenz_concentration.png.

docs.title: 2. Top-20 employer profile per source
docs.text: Selected rows (full tables in tables/T06/02_top20_*.csv):

docs.title: arshkon (top 10, of 2024)
docs.text: company n share mean YOE mean len J1 share J3 share agg share Apex Systems 52 1.1% 4.9 4,333 3.8% 11.5% 100% Capital One 52 1.1% 4.5 5,585 0.0% 17.3% 0% Motion Recruitment 40 0.9% 6.3 1,681 0.0% 4.5% 100% Tata Consultancy Services 39 0.8% 6.6 1,115 0.0% 0.0% 100% Amazon 31 0.7% 3.2 4,044 12.9% 29.0% 0% Fidelity Investments 29 0.6% 6.4 6,006 0.0% 5.9% 0% Microsoft 19 0.4% 3.3 5,966 0.0% 55.6% 0% Oracle 19 0.4% 4.7 6,278 0.0% 15.4% 0%

docs.title: scraped (top 20, of 2026)
docs.text:
company n share mean YOE mean len J1 share J3 share agg share Jobs via Dice 1,711 4.2% 4.9 5,208 2.4% 19.9% 100% Google 757 1.9% 2.8 4,768 0.9% 52.4% 0% JPMorgan Chase 657 1.6% 5.1 5,820 0.0% 4.5% 0% Microsoft 386 0.9% 3.7 6,429 0.3% 38.6% 1% AWS 339 0.8% 3.8 6,847 0.0% 7.3% 0% Amazon 335 0.8% 3.4 5,631 4.8% 14.9% 4% Booz Allen Hamilton 303 0.7% 4.0 5,634 0.7% 30.8% 0% Wells Fargo 288 0.7% 3.7 5,889 0.0% 27.8% 0% Anduril Industries 255 0.6% 4.9 8,356 0.8% 12.9% 0% Leidos 245 0.6% 7.1 7,433 11.4% 21.5% 4% Actalent 238 0.6% 4.5 4,841 0.8% 21.3% 100% Walmart 233 0.6% 2.2 8,725 0.0% 73.8% 0% Haystack 232 0.6% 5.3 2,012 2.2% 14.8% 0% Motion Recruitment 229 0.6% 5.0 2,311 0.9% 10.8% 100% Capital One 223 0.5% 4.2 6,540 0.0% 10.8% 1% hackajob 216 0.5% 5.4 5,945 1.9% 12.0% 0% NVIDIA 188 0.5% 6.4 4,126 1.1% 6.7% 0% Uber 187 0.5% 4.5 3,614 1.6% 20.1% 0% General Motors 182 0.4% 5.3 7,202 5.5% 11.1% 2% Peraton 181 0.4% 5.9 5,880 6.6% 18.5% 0%

Three things to notice: - Capital One is the one employer that is top-20 in ALL three sources; Amazon, AWS, and Motion Recruitment are top-20 in at least two. - mean YOE at the top of scraped is systematically lower than at the top of arshkon for the same companies (Amazon 3.4 vs 3.2; Microsoft 3.7 vs 3.3; AWS 3.8 vs not-in-top-20). This is suggestive of tech giants posting more junior roles in 2026, but cross-checks against full-panel show it's mostly composition driven (§5). - Walmart scraped: mean YOE 2.2, J3 share 73.8% — Walmart is dominated by entry-level / internship postings in 2026. Google and Microsoft show the same pattern (J3 share 52% and 39% respectively). These three alone account for 3.3% of scraped SWE postings and 16% of the J3 (YOE≤2) pool.

docs.title: 3. Duplicate-template audit
docs.text:
Verification passed. Stage 4's multi-location dedup left essentially no within-source duplicate templates:

  • arshkon: top-10 companies all have max_rows_per_hash = 1, dup ratio = 1.0.
  • asaniczka: top-10 almost all max_rows_per_hash = 1 (Northrop Grumman 294 distinct + 1 dup = 2 rows; Syrinx 131 + 2 = 2 rows).
  • scraped: top-10 max_rows_per_hash ≤ 2. 16 residual (company × title × description_hash) groups with >1 row, max group size 2.

Cross-company description-hash collisions (distinct description_hash used by ≥2 distinct company_name_canonical): 15 collisions in asaniczka (all from a staffing-firm cluster: Afton Consulting Group, Retail Options, Gables Search Group, True North Consulting, Gallin Associates, Oculus Search Partners). In scraped: 1 collision with 3 staffing firms posting the same description (Torque Technologies LLC, ShrinQ Consulting Group Inc, Wandata Tech). These are shared-template staffing hubs where the same JD is relisted under sibling brand identities; canonicalization does not currently merge them because the brand names are distinct.

Implication: ~0 within-source duplication = Stage 4 is doing its job. Cross-company collisions exist only in asaniczka at meaningful scale and are all staffing-firm siblings. Downstream term-frequency work should still consider deduping on description_hash within each source as a zero-cost extra hygiene step, but the heavy lifting is already done.

docs.title: 4. Entry-level posting concentration
docs.text:
For companies with ≥5 SWE postings (the "established employer" pool), the share with ZERO entry-labeled rows:

source J1 (label) J2 (label∪assoc) J3 (YOE≤2) J4 (YOE≤3) arshkon (164 cos ≥5) 79.3% 76.8% 56.7% 32.9% asaniczka (632 cos ≥5) 88.9% 87.3% 58.1% 33.5% scraped (1,500 cos ≥5) 79.4% 78.0% 53.1% 31.2%

Nearly 80% of established employers have ZERO rows with seniority_final = 'entry' in every source. Entry-level posting is a highly specialized activity, and the trailing 20% of companies drives the aggregate entry share. This holds even for the YOE-based proxies: ~55% of companies never post a role with yoe_extracted ≤ 2.

Distribution of own-entry-share among companies that DO post entry (n ≥ 5, has any entry):

J1 p50 J1 p90 J3 p50 J3 p90 arshkon 11.8% 21.6% 20.0% 63.6% asaniczka 6.9% 20.0% 17.4% 50.0% scraped 10.0% 28.6% 20.0% 50.0%

Among the ~20% of employers that post entry at all, the median does so for ~10-20% of their volume. See figures/T06/02_entry_concentration_shape.png.

docs.title: 5. Within-company vs between-company decomposition
docs.text:
Overlap panel: 125 companies with ≥5 SWE postings in BOTH arshkon and scraped. Oaxaca-style weighted decomposition:

metric aggregate (sc−ar) within-company between-company arshkon scraped entry share (J1) −0.0025 −0.0065 +0.0040 3.19% 2.94% entry share (J2) −0.0045 −0.0086 +0.0042 3.55% 3.10% entry share (J3) +0.0724 +0.0039 +0.0685 13.89% 21.13% entry share (J4) +0.1193 +0.0309 +0.0884 28.44% 40.37% AI-mention share +0.3654 +0.3334 +0.0321 7.39% 43.93% mean desc len (chars) +1,766 +963 +803 3,489 5,255

J1 — the label-based definition — moves negatively within this panel (−0.25pp aggregate, entirely within-company). The cross-period label-based entry-share fall is real within the 125 overlap companies. J3 and J4 — YOE-based proxies — move STRONGLY POSITIVE, and almost entirely via the between-company component. The +7.2pp J3 aggregate change decomposes 95% into between-company composition. In plain English: the same 125 companies haven't changed their YOE-based entry share; the 2026 corpus just has more of them at different weights, and includes additional employers (the other ~7.8k scraped-only companies) with different YOE distributions.

AI-mention share shifted +36 percentage points within the overlap panel, ALMOST ENTIRELY WITHIN-COMPANY (+33.3pp within, +3.2pp between). This is the one metric where the 125 overlap companies themselves changed what they wrote in their postings. (The keyword pattern is broad and T14's validated pattern will refine this.)

See figures/T06/03_specialist_impact.png.

docs.title: 6. Entry-specialist employer identification
docs.text:
Applied the panel test from the T30 spec locally (T30 canonical CSV not yet written at time of this run, so Agent B's downstream file supersedes our local-compute version). Definitions: J1 = seniority_final='entry', J2 = seniority_final IN ('entry','associate'), J3 = yoe_extracted <= 2, J4 = yoe_extracted <= 3. Flag rule: company with ≥5 SWE postings AND any of J1..J4 share > 60%.

Pool: 2,219 companies (pooled across sources) with ≥5 SWE postings. Flagged: 240 (10.8%).

Trigger breakdown — which definition did the work:

triggered by n J4 alone 169 J3 ∧ J4 66 J1 ∧ J2 5

Only 5 of 240 flagged employers trigger under the label-based J1/J2. The remaining 235 only trigger under the YOE-based proxies — these are employers whose roles require low YOE but are not labeled entry. Intern pipelines, new-grad-funnels, YOE-lax boilerplate.

docs.title: Employer categorization (240 flagged)
docs.text:
category n n postings e_direct_employer 193 2,171 a_staffing 21 325 a_staffing_aggregator (is_aggregator=true) 12 142 c_tech_giant 11 2,486 d_bulk_consulting 2 22 b_college_intermediary 1 20

The 11 tech-giant employers alone carry 2,486 postings (48% of the flagged pool volume). These are Google, Amazon, AWS, Walmart, TikTok, TikTok USDS, Intel, NVIDIA, Microsoft, IBM, Apple — companies whose SWE flow in 2026 is heavily YOE≤3 because they post internships and new-grad SDE roles at scale. This is the invisible intermediary class the task spec warned about: they are NOT flagged by is_aggregator, yet they concentrate the bulk of entry volume.

Direct employers (193 of 240 flagged) are mostly small shops (mean n ≈ 11) whose SWE mix is low-YOE by accident of their business mix — robotics startups (Torc, Applied Intuition), hardware/semiconductor (KLA, Lam Research, Medtronic), single-team AI/ML shops.

docs.title: Top-20 flagged employers (manual review bundle)
docs.text:
Saved at tables/T06/06_top20_specialists_for_review.csv. Highlights:

company n J1 J2 J3 J4 agg rate category triggered by Google 790 0.9% 0.9% 51.9% 79.2% 0% c_tech_giant J4 Amazon 508 4.9% 4.9% 13.2% 67.8% 2.4% c_tech_giant J4 AWS 448 0.0% 0.4% 6.7% 63.4% 0% c_tech_giant J4 Walmart 405 0.0% 0.0% 60.6% 72.4% 0% c_tech_giant J3 Qualcomm 169 0.6% 0.6% 78.0% 82.6% 0% e_direct_employer J3 TikTok 165 52.1% 52.1% 35.7% 60.7% 0% c_tech_giant J4 TikTok USDS 94 6.4% 6.4% 36.4% 80.3% 0% c_tech_giant J4 Visa 84 2.4% 2.4% 56.2% 67.1% 0% e_direct_employer J4 Bowman Williams 56 0.0% 0.0% 6.7% 66.7% 0% a_staffing J4 Medtronic 49 0.0% 0.0% 57.8% 64.4% 0% e_direct_employer J4 Turing 42 2.4% 2.4% 0.0% 62.5% 100% a_staffing_aggregator J4 Intel 34 11.8% 11.8% 46.7% 70.0% 0% c_tech_giant J4

TikTok stands alone as a J1-triggered giant (52% of their SWE postings are label-entry). This is genuine junior-heavy hiring, not an artifact. Every other tech giant flags only via YOE — their labels largely don't say "entry" but the YOE floor is low because they post internships and new-grad SDEs.

Aggregator cross-tab: 12 of 240 flagged (5%) have agg_rate ≥ 50%. The OTHER 228 are NOT flagged by is_aggregator — this confirms the "invisible intermediary" finding: entry-specialist employers are overwhelmingly NOT captured by the aggregator flag.

docs.title: Impact on aggregate entry share
docs.text:
Specialist-excluded entry shares:

source J1 full J1 ex-spec J3 full J3 ex-spec arshkon 3.73% 3.42% 14.98% 13.64% asaniczka 1.13% 1.08% 10.36% 9.54% scraped 3.12% 2.82% 17.19% 14.36%

Excluding 240 specialists shrinks the arsh-vs-scraped J3 gap from +2.21pp to +0.72pp — a 67% attenuation. Under J1, the cross-period direction is preserved (both slightly negative), but the effect is too small to matter.

This is the concrete sensitivity Wave 2+ must apply: every entry-share headline number must be reported both including AND excluding these 240 employers.

Saved: exploration/artifacts/shared/entry_specialist_employers.csv (240 rows, columns company, n, n_yoe, j1..j4, j1_share..j4_share, agg_rows, agg_rate, mode_industry, sources_present, n_sources, employer_category, triggered_by, sample_titles).

docs.title: 7. Aggregator profile
docs.text:
source agg share agg mean len non-agg mean len agg entry share non-agg entry share kaggle_arshkon 9.2% 2,780 3,359 2.6% 3.8% kaggle_asaniczka 16.2% 4,276 3,805 0.5% 1.2% scraped 16.6% 3,905 5,113 2.8% 3.2%

Aggregator share nearly doubled from arshkon 9.2% to scraped 16.6%. Aggregator postings are 23% shorter than non-aggregator postings in scraped (3,905 vs 5,113 chars). Aggregator entry share is slightly lower than non-aggregator entry share in every source — aggregators are not disproportionately junior. They ARE however disproportionately in the J1 entry pool (15.1% of scraped J1-entry postings come from aggregators vs 16.6% of the overall pool, so roughly proportional; in arshkon 6.3% of J1 came from aggregators vs 9.2% overall — aggregators UNDER-represented in 2024 entry).

Implication for cross-period comparisons: aggregator-share rose ~7pp between 2024 and 2026, and aggregator postings have distinct text characteristics. Every cross-period text analysis must include an aggregator-excluded sensitivity (dimension a).

docs.title: 8. New entrants in 2026
docs.text:
value distinct scraped SWE companies 7,940 distinct 2024 SWE companies (arshkon ∪ asaniczka) 6,504 NEW (scraped-only, no 2024 match) 5,916 (74.5% of scraped companies) RETURNING 2,024 (25.5%)

Per-row breakdown on scraped (how many postings go to new vs returning companies):

cohort n rows mean len mean YOE J1 entry J2 agg share mid-senior director new 18,317 4,629 5.2 3.30% 3.37% 20.0% 40.8% 0.60% returning 22,564 5,142 5.0 2.97% 3.15% 13.9% 44.1% 0.82%

New-cohort postings are shorter (5,142 → 4,629 chars), slightly more junior-skewed on J1 (3.30% vs 2.97%), and materially more aggregator-heavy (20% vs 14%). The "new entrants are aggregator-heavy" pattern is the main composition risk for any between-company finding.

docs.title: 9. Per-finding concentration prediction (PRIMARY DELIVERABLE)
docs.text:
This table tells every Wave 2/3 agent how to treat their aggregation. Column default is what the agent should apply before computing their primary headline.

Wave 2/3 metric concentration risk mechanism recommended default sensitivity Entry share (J1, label-based) Medium TikTok alone is 6.7% of scraped entry pool Include all; report with AND without 240 specialists aggregator-excluded Entry share (J3, YOE≤2) High Tech-giant intern pipelines drive 16% of J3 pool; cross-period direction attenuates ~70% with exclusion Exclude 240 specialists for primary; report full as sensitivity aggregator-excluded AI-mention rate (binary) Medium-High Most within-company move per §5 decomposition; +33pp shift is real within overlap panel but tech giants have distinct AI-heavy mix Cap at 50 postings/company for corpus-level rate; report per-company AI rate distribution as primary aggregator-excluded, top-20 employer exclusion AI-mention density (per 1K char) Medium-High Same as above + description length drift description_core_llm + per-company cap at 50 raw description sensitivity Description length (mean) High Top-20 employers span 1,115 (TCS) to 8,725 (Walmart) chars; mean swings with company mix Compute length distribution per-company FIRST, then median-of-medians for the corpus aggregate per-company cap Term frequencies (top-N words) Very High §3 shows 15 asaniczka collisions from shared-template staffing firms; 44% of arshkon industries are compound Dedup on description_hash within source AND cap per company at 20-50 aggregator-excluded, cross-company-hash-collision removal Topic models (BERTopic, NMF) Very High Embedding centroids pulled toward boilerplate-heavy aggregators Cap at 50/company, drop aggregators from training set; keep aggregators for inference only within-source cap vs pooled cap Co-occurrence networks Very High Same as topics; tech-giant stack bundles (Google AWS GCP) dominate Cap at 20/company, weight by log(1+n) aggregator-excluded Senior-role evolution (S1-S4) Low-Medium Tech giants slightly over-represented at senior side too Include all; report top-20 sensitivity aggregator-excluded Title evolution (T10) Low Emergent AI titles are low-count; concentration matters less for rare title discovery Include all no cap Metro-level analyses (T17) High Scraper search-metro design concentrates 26 cities; 3,090 multi-location reps dropped Per-metro rollup after specialist exclusion; top-1 company share per metro as sensitivity no multi-location rep, aggregator-excluded DiD / cross-occupation (T18) Medium Control occupations may have different concentration shape; top-company dominance can mimic "treatment" Cap at 50/company across occupations; re-run with and without SWE-only vs pooled Ghost-job prevalence (T22) Medium Aggregators + consulting firms have elevated ghost-like features Aggregator-excluded primary; full corpus sensitivity specialist-excluded Requirements complexity (T11) Medium-High Tech-giant JDs are long, highly-structured, high tech count; scraped tech-count rise partly from Google/Microsoft JDs Per-company cap at 20; report specialist-excluded raw description sensitivity

Default capping threshold: 50 postings per company_name_canonical per source for corpus-level text aggregates. Where a specific metric is concentration-robust (rare-title discovery), no cap needed. Where a metric is sensitive, also apply the entry-specialist exclusion from §6.

docs.title: Sensitivity-disagreement drill-in
docs.text:
The concentration story fundamentally splits along the J1-vs-J3 divide:

  • J1 cross-period change (arshkon → scraped): +3.73% → +3.12% overall; excluding specialists +3.42% → +2.82% — direction consistent, tiny negative.
  • J3 cross-period change (arshkon → scraped): +14.98% → +17.19% overall; excluding specialists +13.64% → +14.36% — direction preserved but effect shrinks 67%.
  • J3 within-2024 calibration (arshkon → asaniczka): +14.98% → +10.36% — within-source noise is MORE than the specialist-adjusted cross-period effect.

The most likely mechanism: the J3 YOE-based "entry" definition picks up tech-giant intern pipelines and YOE-lax-phrasing roles. Those intern pipelines ARE more visible in 2026 scraped data (more aggressive scraping, direct-from-careers-page enrichment) than in the 2024 Kaggle snapshots. So the J3 cross-period signal is partly real (tech giants genuinely do post many new-grad roles in 2026) and partly artefact (Kaggle snapshots undercounted them).

Recommendation for Wave 2+: use J3 with specialists excluded AND aggregators excluded as the primary entry-share definition. Report J1 as a label-based sensitivity that is underpowered.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Only 5 of 240 flagged entry-specialists trigger under label-based J1/J2. The vast majority are YOE-only flagged. This contradicts the intuition that "entry-specialist" is detectable from seniority labels.

  2. Stage 4 dedup is validated — only 16 within-source duplicate (co × title × hash) groups in scraped. The 2025 finding that 23% of entry pool came from 6 bulk-posting companies is no longer the dominant concentration story. The new story is tech-giant intern pipelines.

  3. TikTok is the one tech giant with 52% J1 entry share. Contrast with Google (0.9% J1, 52% J3) and Walmart (0% J1, 74% J3). TikTok's labels genuinely say "entry"; everyone else's don't. Why TikTok and not anyone else is worth asking.

  4. Walmart scraped: 74% J3 entry share, mean YOE 2.2, 233 SWE postings. Walmart is effectively running a new-grad-pipeline funnel in 2026 scraped data. None are label-entry. The same is true of Google and Microsoft at smaller ratios.

  5. Asaniczka concentration is aggregator-driven. HHI 54 → 19 when aggregators excluded; ClearanceJobs alone is 5% of asaniczka SWE. This is a corpus artefact, not a real labor market statement.

  6. Aggregator share in scraped has ~doubled from arshkon 9.2% to scraped 16.6%. Any "aggregator-inclusive" cross-period comparison is conflating platform-composition changes with real changes.

  7. 74.5% of scraped companies are new entrants (not in any 2024 source). The returning/new split exists at every aggregate — and "new entrants" skew aggregator-heavy (20% vs 14%). Between-company decomposition shows most J3 / J4 shift is driven by this composition change, NOT by the same companies changing their mix.

  8. AI-mention rise is 91% within-company (within the 125-company overlap panel) — the one aggregate metric that is NOT concentration-driven. This is good news for the RQ1/RQ3 hypothesis that employers are rewriting job descriptions to add AI requirements.

  9. Cross-company description-hash collisions are restricted to staffing-firm sibling clusters. 15 asaniczka collisions cluster on 5-6 small-to-medium staffing firms reposting under sibling brand names. Canonicalization doesn't merge them. This is a small-volume anomaly but a real gap for any "distinct descriptions" aggregate.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• is_aggregator is row-level in Stage 2, but the specialist cross-tab uses ANY(is_aggregator) over company rows; we switched to a majority rule (agg_rate ≥ 0.5) after noticing Amazon flipped to "staffing" because 12/508 rows were coded aggregator. Downstream agents loading entry_specialist_employers.csv should honor agg_rate as the continuous signal.
  • company_industry is 0% populated in asaniczka (explicit column gap). The mode_industry column in the shared artifact is NULL for ~30% of flagged companies (mostly asaniczka-only or small scraped-only rows).
  • Overlap panel (§5) uses ≥5 postings in both arshkon and scraped, yielding n=125. This is small but covers 33% of arshkon postings and 18% of scraped postings — wide enough to be representative of the core establishment, narrow enough to be stable.
  • Specialist flagging uses POOLED (cross-source) counts per company. A company with 3 arshkon + 3 scraped postings passes the n≥5 threshold even though no single source sees it at 5. This is intentional — we want a stable roster across sources — but agents that need source-specific specialists should use the source-split per-company table tables/T06/06_company_junior_shares_bysource.csv.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• Every Wave 2+ agent computing a SWE aggregate: load entry_specialist_employers.csv and report headline WITH and WITHOUT its 240 companies. This is the primary sensitivity discovered in T06.
  • T09 archetype discovery: dedup on description_hash within source AND cap at 50 postings/company before BERTopic training. Keep aggregator rows for inference but not training.
  • T11/T14 tech / requirements analyses: cap at 20-50/company. Use description_core_llm (LLM coverage labeled). Report per-company distribution for any density metric.
  • T12 corpus comparison / T13 section anatomy: dedup before tokenization; aggregator exclusion is essential. The 3,905 vs 5,113 char gap between agg and non-agg scraped postings is a hidden within-source confound.
  • T16 company strategies: use the 125-company overlap panel (§5). Don't expand by lowering the threshold — 125 is the right unit for the decomposition.
  • T18 DiD: SWE composition shifted 16% → 37% toward "Software Development" industry label. This is LinkedIn taxonomy drift, not a real industry shift. Include as a composition control.
  • T30 seniority panel (Agent B): when T30's CSV lands, reconcile specialist-flag rules against T30's J5/J6. Expect some overlap with J5 (title-keyword junior) via internship titles.
  • Orchestrator Gate 1 memo: J3 with specialists excluded is the defensible Wave 2 primary. J1 is underpowered. Pooled 2024 baseline expands power but pulls asaniczka's weaker native-entry signal into the denominator.
