docs.text:
Under exploration/tables/T06/: - 01_concentration.csv — HHI, Gini, top-k shares per source (all + no_aggregator) - 02_top20_{source}.csv and 02_top20_all.csv — top-20 employer profiles - 03_dup_templates_{source}.csv, 03_dup_templates_all.csv, 03_cross_company_collisions.csv - 04_entry_concentration_shape.csv — §4 distribution shape - 04_company_entry_{source}.csv — per-company entry counts - 05_decomposition.csv, 05_overlap_panel.csv - 06_company_junior_shares_bysource.csv, 06_company_junior_shares_pooled.csv - 06_entry_specialist_employers.csv, 06_top20_specialists_for_review.csv, 06_category_summary.csv, 06_category_vs_aggregator.csv - 06_specialist_impact.csv, 06_specialist_impact_detailed.csv - 06_specialist_summary.json - 07_aggregator_profile.csv - 08_new_entrants_summary.csv, 08_new_vs_returning.csv

Shared artifact: - exploration/artifacts/shared/entry_specialist_employers.csv (240 companies, 19 columns)

Figures under exploration/figures/T06/: - 01_lorenz_concentration.png - 02_entry_concentration_shape.png - 03_specialist_impact.png

docs.title: T07 — External Benchmarks & Power Analysis
docs.text: Agent D, Wave 1. Date: 2026-04-17. Input: data/unified.parquet. Default SWE LinkedIn filters applied throughout.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text:
The population-level share comparisons (junior share, senior share, all-SWE content rates) are well-powered in every T30 panel variant and every key comparison. MDE_binary is ≤ 3pp everywhere for share-of-SWE outcomes, comfortably below the 5pp well-powered threshold.

Within-slice content analyses (within-junior, within-senior) are the binding feasibility constraint. S2 (director-only) and S3 (title-keyword senior via title_normalized) are underpowered for every cross-period content metric. J1/J2 are marginal for binary content rates under arshkon-vs-scraped and become well-powered under pooled_2024; content continuous effects (description length, YOE) are well-powered under pooled_2024 for J1/J2/J3/J4 but marginal for J1/J2 in arshkon-only.

Metro-level entry-share analysis is feasible under pooled-2024 baseline (23/26 scraped-frame metros well-powered) but not arshkon-only (8/26). The orchestrator should push metro-level J1 analyses to pooled_2024.

Geographic correlation vs BLS OEWS 2024 (SOC 15-1252) exceeds target across all sources: Pearson r = 0.96–0.98. Our data passes as representative of the US SWE geographic distribution.

JOLTS Information sector context: the scraped 2026 window captures a post-2020-low in tech hiring. Feb-2026 = 91K openings vs 2023 avg 138K (0.66×) and 2024 avg 123K (0.74×). Any cross-period findings must be read against a genuinely softer 2026 hiring market, not a booming one.

docs.title: Part A — Feasibility table
docs.text:
T30 seniority panel definitions (computed locally; T30 CSV not yet published when this ran):

Definition Expression J1 seniority_final = 'entry' J2 seniority_final IN ('entry','associate') J3 yoe_extracted <= 2 (denominator: YOE-known) J4 yoe_extracted <= 3 (denominator: YOE-known) S1 seniority_final IN ('mid-senior','director') S2 seniority_final = 'director' S3 title-keyword senior, matched against title_normalized — as-written in the spec S3_raw title-keyword senior, matched against raw lower(title) — corrected (see surprises) S4 yoe_extracted >= 5 ALL all SWE rows

MDE formula (normal approximation, two-sample, 80% power, α=0.05 two-sided):

$$\text{MDE}{\text{binary}} = (z$$} + z_\beta) \sqrt{p_0 (1-p_0) (1/n_1 + 1/n_2)

$$\text{MDE}{\text{Cohen's }d} = (z$$} + z_\beta) \sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2

Verdict bins: well_powered (MDE_binary < 5pp or d < 0.2), marginal (5–10pp or 0.2–0.4), underpowered (≥10pp or ≥0.4).

Denominator choice: For share-of-population outcomes (junior_share, senior_share), n_group is the total SWE in the source (or YOE-known total for J3/J4/S4). For within-slice content outcomes (ai_mention_rate within entry, description_length within mid-senior, etc.), n_group is the number of rows satisfying the definition.

docs.title: Part B — External benchmarks
docs.text:
BLS OEWS (SOC 15-1252 Software Developers), 2024 annual, state level. Obtained via BLS public API (series ID OEU{S|N}{area}{industry}{occupation}{datatype}, 25 chars). 50/51 states returned data (VT suppressed, likely due to BLS confidentiality rules for small cells). National total = 1,654,440; sum of 50 state values = 1,650,360 = 99.8% match, validating the series format.

SOC 15-1256 (QA Analysts and Testers) — BLS public API returned "Series does not exist" for both national and state codes under the format used above. The separate SOC 15-1256 occupation appears not to be published at state level via the API (or requires a different series-ID scheme). Reported as a limitation; SWE alone is sufficient for a geographic correlation target.

JOLTS Information sector — series JTS510000000000000JOL, 38 monthly observations from Jan-2023 through Feb-2026.

HTTP/2 blocked direct fetches from www.bls.gov and fred.stlouisfed.org (FRED returns INTERNAL_ERROR; BLS returns 403). The BLS public API endpoint (api.bls.gov) worked via POST over HTTP/1.1 with Mozilla user-agent.

docs.title: 1. Group sizes under T30 panel (SWE, LinkedIn-only, default filters)
docs.text:
Definition Side arshkon asaniczka scraped pooled_2024 ALL all 4,691 18,129 40,881 22,820 J1 (entry) junior 175 204 1,275 379 J2 (entry+associate) junior 190 228 1,327 418 J3 (YOE≤2, YOE-known denom) junior 436 1,330 4,742 1,766 J4 (YOE≤3, YOE-known denom) junior 907 3,128 9,307 4,035 S1 (mid-senior+director) senior 2,077 11,518 17,713 13,595 S2 (director) senior 13 92 295 105 S3 (title-kw senior via title_normalized) senior 82 364 150 446 S3_raw (title-kw senior via raw title) senior 1,804 11,137 17,289 12,941 S4 (YOE≥5, YOE-known denom) senior 1,751 8,528 15,938 10,279

YOE-known denominators: arshkon 2,911; asaniczka 12,834; scraped 27,581; pooled_2024 15,745.

docs.title: 2. Verdict cross-tab (population-level junior/senior share comparisons)
docs.text:
seniority_def arshkon_vs_scraped pooled_2024_vs_scraped J1 well_powered (MDE=0.75pp) well_powered (0.40pp) J2 well_powered (0.77pp) well_powered (0.41pp) J3 well_powered (2.06pp) well_powered (1.06pp) J4 well_powered (2.58pp) well_powered (1.32pp) S1 well_powered (2.14pp) well_powered (1.15pp) S2 well_powered (0.37pp) well_powered (0.20pp) S3 well_powered (0.26pp) well_powered (0.14pp) S3_raw well_powered (2.13pp) well_powered (1.14pp) S4 well_powered (2.70pp) well_powered (1.38pp)

Every population-share comparison is well-powered. J1 is fine. J2 provides no meaningful power gain over J1 for the population-level share test because the associate bucket is tiny.

docs.title: 3. Within-slice content-metric feasibility (abridged)
docs.text:
For every non-share content metric (AI mention rate, management mention rate, description length, YOE mean, aggregator share), within-slice verdicts are:

Slice arshkon_vs_scraped pooled_2024_vs_scraped J1, J2 marginal–underpowered for binary/continuous marginal for binary, well-powered for continuous J3 marginal for binary, well-powered for continuous well-powered for binary & continuous J4 well-powered for binary & continuous well-powered S1, S4 well-powered well-powered S2 (director) underpowered (arshkon n=13) underpowered (pooled n=105) S3 (broken) underpowered underpowered S3_raw well-powered well-powered ALL well-powered well-powered

Full per-row detail: exploration/tables/T07/feasibility_table.csv (146 rows).

docs.title: 4. Metro-level entry-share feasibility
docs.text:
Restricted to the 26-metro scraped study frame. Multi-location rows (is_multi_location = true: 116 arshkon, 894 asaniczka, 3,090 scraped) are excluded from metro counts per spec and saved separately.

Definition arshkon_vs_scraped pooled_2024_vs_scraped J1 (entry) 8/26 metros well-powered → marginal 23/26 metros well-powered → well_powered J2 7/26 → marginal 22/26 → well_powered J3 0/26 → underpowered 3/26 → underpowered (marginal table-level) J4 0/26 → underpowered 2/26 → underpowered

Implication: Any metro-level entry-share analysis (T17 or similar) should use J1 or J2 under pooled_2024, not J3/J4. Per-metro detail in metro_power.csv.

docs.title: 5. Company overlap for T16
docs.text:
Threshold Both arshkon & scraped ≥ N Row contribution (arshkon / scraped) ≥1 each 1,004 companies — ≥3 each 240 companies 1,792 / 11,499 rows ≥5 each 125 companies 1,381 / 9,060 ≥10 each 45 companies — Pooled_2024 ≥3 + scraped ≥3 589 companies — Pooled_2024 ≥5 + scraped ≥5 343 — Pooled_2024 ≥10 + scraped ≥10 150 —

T16 panel: 240 arshkon-scraped companies at ≥3/≥3 — adequate for a typology/clustering task. Pooling with asaniczka bumps this to 589, but since asaniczka has no industry metadata, the pooled panel loses industry controls for ~62% of the sample.

docs.title: 6. Sample totals
docs.text:
• Multi-location rows (excluded from metro analyses per spec):
  • arshkon: 116 (2.5% of SWE)
  • asaniczka: 894 (4.9%)
  • scraped: 3,090 (7.6%) — highest share, reflecting either more real multi-location postings or different scraping behavior
  • Metros with ≥100 SWE per source-period: arshkon-2024-04 = 8; asaniczka-2024-01 = 27; scraped-2026-03 = 25; scraped-2026-04 = 24.
  • Metros with ≥50 SWE: arshkon = 18; asaniczka = 40; scraped = 26 (all).

docs.title: 7. Geographic correlation (target: Pearson r > 0.80)
docs.text:
State-level SWE counts vs BLS OEWS 2024 SOC 15-1252 (50 states; VT suppressed):

Source n_states Pearson r Spearman r log-Pearson r Verdict kaggle_arshkon 50 0.973 0.940 0.939 target_met kaggle_asaniczka 50 0.962 0.954 0.951 target_met pooled_2024 50 0.972 0.959 0.956 target_met scraped 50 0.975 0.861 0.821 target_met (but see below)

All exceed the 0.80 target. However: scraped's Pearson is driven almost entirely by the top ~8 states (CA, TX, NY, WA, VA, FL, NC, NJ); the log-Pearson of 0.82 and Spearman of 0.86 reveal the rank correlation is weaker. 20 of 50 states have ZERO scraped SWE postings (AL, AK, AR, HI, ID, IA, KY, LA, ME, MS, MT, NE, NV, NM, ND, OK, SD, UT, VT, WY), which reflects the 26-metro scraped study frame by design, not a data defect.

Scatter: exploration/figures/T07/bls_state_correlation.png.

docs.title: 8. Industry distribution (arshkon + scraped only; asaniczka has no industry)
docs.text:
LinkedIn's industry taxonomy is not NAICS, so a quantitative BLS correlation isn't meaningful. Qualitative shares:

Top industry arshkon (n=4,653) scraped (n=40,871) IT Services and IT Consulting 25.8% 12.5% Software Development 13.1% 26.0% Financial Services 8.4% 7.2% Staffing and Recruiting 5.8% 2.6% Technology, Information and Internet 2.3% 4.9% Defense and Space Manufacturing 2.6% 3.9% Information Services 3.2% 1.8%

Top-industry swap: IT-Services vs Software-Development trade top rank between 2024 and 2026. Whether this is LinkedIn taxonomy relabeling or a real shift is unclear — should be investigated in T05 cross-dataset comparability.

BLS OEWS 2024 SWE by NAICS 4-digit (share of queried total ≈ 58% of national 1.65M):

NAICS Industry SWE employed Share of queried 5415 Computer Systems Design Services 519,920 54.0% 5182 Computing Infrastructure Providers & Data Processing 77,830 8.1% 5220 Credit Intermediation 65,660 6.8% 5416 Mgmt/Sci/Tech Consulting 50,260 5.2% 5417 Scientific R&D Services 49,170 5.1% 5241 Insurance Carriers 42,630 4.4% 5413 Architectural/Engineering Services 38,900 4.0% 3345 Navigational/Measuring Instruments Mfg 33,340 3.5%

NAICS 5415 ("Computer Systems Design") alone accounts for >50% of US SWE employment — consistent with our data showing "IT Services and IT Consulting" + "Software Development" dominating both sources.

docs.title: 9. JOLTS Information sector context (FRED: JTS510000000000000JOL, thousands of openings)
docs.text:
Period Monthly avg (K openings) Our window 2023 138 — 2024 123 asaniczka 2024-01: 160K; arshkon 2024-04: 87K 2025 120 — 2026-Jan 100 — 2026-Feb 91 closest to scraped 2026-Mar window

The scraped 2026-Mar/Apr window coincides with a post-2020 low in US Information-sector job openings. Feb-2026 = 91K is 0.66× the 2023 average and 0.74× the 2024 average. This is a first-order contextual fact for any volume comparison:

  • Any "entry posting count declined" finding must be read against an overall hiring slowdown, not a sectoral restructuring.
  • Share-of-SWE outcomes (junior share, senior share) are still interpretable because they normalize out the overall volume; but absolute-volume interpretations need a JOLTS adjustment.

Figure: exploration/figures/T07/jolts_info_openings.png.

Note: JOLTS April 2026 was not yet published as of 2026-04-17; the most recent available month is Feb-2026.

docs.title: 10. Sample frame & generalizability
docs.text:
What our sample represents: US-based, English-language, LinkedIn-posted software-engineer job postings at two snapshots: - 2024-Q1 (asaniczka 2024-01) and 2024-Q2 (arshkon 2024-04) historical Kaggle dumps, together containing ~22.8K SWE postings. - 2026-Mar/Apr daily-scraped current window (~40.9K SWE rows at current pull; growing daily), restricted by design to a 26-metro frame.

Validity for which claims:

  • Geographic prevalence of SWE employment across states: strongly representative (r = 0.96–0.98 vs BLS OEWS). Within our metros, the top 8 states match BLS almost exactly.
  • Cross-temporal share comparisons (junior share, senior share of SWE postings, AI mention rate, description length): internally valid across the three sources, but note 2024 sources are LinkedIn-snapshot Kaggle files of unknown sampling selection, while 2026 scraped is a daily pull with explicit keyword queries. Cross-period differences are confounded with instrument change — this is what the within-2024 calibration (arshkon vs asaniczka) is designed to bound.
  • Absolute volumes or hiring-rate claims: NOT valid. Our three sources sample the LinkedIn posting stream with different intensities. JOLTS Information-sector openings fell 26% from 2024 avg to 2026-Feb, so any raw-volume drop in our data is a mix of (a) a real macro hiring slowdown, (b) our scraped pipeline's coverage rate, and (c) instrument change.

What our sample does NOT cover: - Non-LinkedIn job markets (Indeed available as sensitivity, other boards and direct-company sites absent). - Non-English postings (filtered out by default filters). - SWE work outside job postings (contract, referral, internal, consulting). - Non-US postings (arshkon and asaniczka both US-focused; scraped is 26-metro US). - Pre-2024 and post-2026-April markets. - SWE-adjacent roles tracked under is_swe_adjacent — those are outside T07's SWE sample. - QA Analyst & Tester occupation (BLS SOC 15-1256): BLS public API did not return data for this occupation at state level, so we cannot add it as a cross-check.

docs.title: Data-quality caveats
docs.text:
1. title_normalized strips level indicators. 99% of rows with "Senior/Sr/Lead/Staff/Principal" in the raw title have it removed in title_normalized (17,289 → 150 in scraped alone). The T30 spec's S3 definition matches against title_normalized, which means S3 as literally specified catches only rare residuals. Feasibility tables include both S3 (as-spec'd) and S3_raw (corrected, matches raw title lowercased) — T30 should finalize the operationalization and downstream tasks should consume the corrected version.
  2. is_remote_inferred is 100% False in all three sources. The preamble notes remote flags are 0% in 2024 sources as a data artifact, but is_remote_inferred in Stage 6 should combine source flag + text inference. Either the Stage-6 text inference hasn't run or something stripped the output; regardless, remote-share comparisons in this dataset are not meaningful. Remote_share is included in the feasibility table only for formal completeness — the analysis is infeasible at the data level, independent of power.
  3. VT missing from BLS OEWS state data — likely BLS confidentiality-based suppression for small cells. 50 of 51 states is sufficient for the correlation target.
  4. SOC 15-1256 (QA) not returned by BLS public API at national or state level under the 25-char series format that works for 15-1252. Documented as a limitation.
  5. JOLTS data lag. Most-recent JOLTS month is Feb-2026; April-2026 not yet published. The JOLTS context for the scraped window is based on Jan-Feb 2026.
  6. 20 states have zero scraped SWE postings (AL, AK, AR, HI, ID, IA, KY, LA, ME, MS, MT, NE, NV, NM, ND, OK, SD, UT, VT, WY). This is expected given the 26-metro scraped frame by design, not a defect. But it means the scraped Pearson r with BLS is driven by the top-10 states. log-Pearson r = 0.82 and Spearman r = 0.86 are more honest figures for the scraped rank-consistency.
  7. Multi-location share differs across sources (2.5% arshkon, 4.9% asaniczka, 7.6% scraped). A mechanically larger share in scraped means a mechanically larger "drop" from per-metro rollups in 2026 — any "metro-level" analysis needs to report both the metro-only population and the multi-location excluded count.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. S3 (title-keyword senior via title_normalized) is catastrophically under-counted. Raw-title regex finds 17,289 senior-keyword titles in scraped; title_normalized regex finds 150 (0.9%). The same severe loss appears in all three sources (arshkon 1,804 → 82; asaniczka 11,137 → 364). Cause: title_normalized strips level indicators per schema Section 2. This invalidates S3 as literally written in T30 — it effectively measures "how often a level keyword survived title-normalization," not "how many postings are titled senior." Agent B's T30 must operationalize title-keyword senior against raw title or document the mismatch. I included an S3_raw row in the feasibility table as the corrected operationalization.

  2. is_remote_inferred is 100% False across all sources. Documented as a 2024-source artifact in the preamble, but scraped-2026 also shows 0%. Either Stage-6 text inference did not populate this column or something stripped the output. This is a preprocessing pipeline issue that should be surfaced. Any RQ depending on remote/hybrid classification is not supported by the current artifact.

  3. 2026 is a genuinely softer hiring market, not an AI-adoption boom. JOLTS Information-sector openings fell from 123K 2024-avg to 91K Feb-2026 (0.74×). This is the lowest Info-sector openings figure since the post-COVID trough in 2020. Any headline finding like "junior postings declined X%" must contextualize as partly a macro hiring slowdown. The correct framing is share-of-SWE (which is mostly insensitive to volume), not raw volume. This changes the narrative for RQ1.

  4. Industry top-rank swap between 2024 and 2026 on LinkedIn's own taxonomy: "IT Services and IT Consulting" (arshkon top, 25.8%) vs "Software Development" (scraped top, 26.0%). These categories overlap semantically and LinkedIn may have merged/relabeled them between 2024 and 2026. This is a cross-dataset comparability risk for any industry-stratified analysis (T05 should verify).

  5. S2 (director-only) and title-keyword content within-slice are infeasible across the board. S2 has only 13 arshkon rows and 105 pooled_2024 rows — no binary content rate difference <20pp can be detected, and no Cohen's d <0.6. Any "director postings changed" claim must be deferred to raw counts or merged into S1 (mid-senior+director).

  6. Company overlap is smaller than typically assumed. Only 45 companies have ≥10 SWE postings in BOTH arshkon and scraped; 240 have ≥3. T16's panel of ≥3/≥3 companies captures only 1,792 of 4,691 arshkon rows (38%) and 11,499 of 40,881 scraped rows (28%). The within-company decomposition is a significant sample restriction; the between-company component (entry of new employers) is probably the larger mechanism.

  7. Scraped's geographic fit to BLS is best-in-class on raw Pearson (0.975), worst on log/Spearman (0.82/0.86). The 26-metro scraped frame produces a distribution that matches BLS in the top-10 but drops to zero in small states. This is by design (the scraped study frame is 26 metros), but it's worth flagging: claims like "our data is nationally representative" should be qualified to "our data is representative of the top US metros where SWE hiring concentrates" — 84% of BLS national SWE employment is in the top-10 states, so the practical coverage is high.

  8. Top-company overlap panel is dominated by well-known giants + aggregators. Top overlapping companies by combined posting count: Google (15+757), JPMorgan Chase (12+657), Microsoft (19+386), Amazon (31+335), AWS (12+339), Booz Allen (9+303), Wells Fargo (16+288), Capital One (52+223), Motion Recruitment (40+229, aggregator), Actalent (23+238, aggregator). T16's clustering of "how companies changed" will be heavily weighted toward tech giants and a handful of staffing aggregators unless capped.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• Orchestrator / Gate 1 decision: Every population-share comparison is well-powered under every variant. There is no "deprioritize this cell" decision forced by power — the constraint is within-slice content metrics on S2 and J1/J2-arshkon-only. Wave 2 can use J1 as primary and J2/J3/J4 as the sensitivity panel. S1 is primary senior; S2 (director) should be excluded from within-slice content claims.
  • Agent B (T30): Flag and correct S3. Either rebuild the definition to match raw title or document that the current title_normalized-based S3 fires only on residual edge cases. This affects the T30 per-definition MDE.
  • Agent B (T30): The J5 (title-keyword junior via title_normalized) definition has the same problem, but milder (roughly 50% loss across sources). Still worth correcting.
  • Wave 2 content-metric tasks (T08, T11, T22): For within-entry (J1/J2) content claims, pool 2024 sources; avoid arshkon-only within-slice content comparisons. For within-director (S2) claims, do not attempt — merge into S1.
  • Wave 3 metro-level tasks (T17): Use J1 or J2 under pooled_2024 as primary; 23/26 scraped metros well-powered. J3/J4 are infeasible per-metro.
  • T16 (company overlap panel): Use ≥3/≥3 threshold → 240 companies. Cap per-company at 20–50 postings before aggregating (cap threshold is T06's call; my feasibility table reports raw n).
  • All Wave 2 tasks: Contextualize cross-period volume claims with JOLTS. The 2026 window is a macro hiring low, not a boom. Share-of-SWE metrics are the robust framing.
  • Preprocessing maintenance: Investigate why is_remote_inferred is 100% False across all three sources. Either the Stage-6 text inference is broken or its output is being dropped downstream. This blocks any remote/hybrid analysis.
  • Preprocessing maintenance: title_normalized level-indicator stripping interacts with T30's title-keyword definitions. Either document the behavior in the schema (it's there — but the T30 spec didn't anticipate it) or add a parallel title_case_preserved column. Low priority; the raw-title fallback works.

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
Tables: - exploration/tables/T07/feasibility_table.csv — primary deliverable, 146 rows, columns analysis_type|comparison|seniority_def|n_group1|n_group2|MDE_binary|MDE_continuous|verdict. - exploration/tables/T07/group_sizes.csv — T30 panel group sizes per source. - exploration/tables/T07/metro_power.csv — per-(metro × definition × comparison) MDE. - exploration/tables/T07/metro_power_summary.csv — verdict counts per (comparison, definition) across all metros. - exploration/tables/T07/metro_feasibility.csv — per-metro SWE counts per source-period. - exploration/tables/T07/metro_detail.csv — per-metro counts with seniority breakdowns. - exploration/tables/T07/metro_summary.csv — metros-with-≥50, ≥100 summary. - exploration/tables/T07/multi_location_excluded.csv — count of is_multi_location=true rows per source. - exploration/tables/T07/company_overlap.csv — company overlap at ≥1/≥3/≥5/≥10 thresholds. - exploration/tables/T07/state_counts.csv — per-state SWE counts per source. - exploration/tables/T07/bls_state_merged.csv — our state counts joined with BLS OEWS. - exploration/tables/T07/bls_state_correlation.csv — Pearson, Spearman, log-Pearson per source. - exploration/tables/T07/bls_industry_swe.csv — BLS OEWS 2024 SWE employment by NAICS industry. - exploration/tables/T07/industry_distribution.csv — our company_industry counts per source. - exploration/tables/T07/jolts_info_monthly.csv — JOLTS Information-sector monthly openings. - exploration/tables/T07/verdict_crosstab.csv — (seniority_def × comparison) verdict matrix for junior/senior share.

Figures: - exploration/figures/T07/bls_state_correlation.png — scatter of our state counts vs BLS (4 panels: arshkon / asaniczka / pooled / scraped). - exploration/figures/T07/jolts_info_openings.png — JOLTS Information-sector openings 2023–2026 with our windows shaded.

External benchmark raw data: - exploration/artifacts/T07_benchmarks/oes_state_2024.json — BLS OEWS state-level SWE & QA employment. - exploration/artifacts/T07_benchmarks/jolts_info_510_openings.json — 38-month JOLTS series.

Scripts: - exploration/scripts/T07_feasibility.py — feasibility table, group sizes, company overlap, metro summary, state/industry counts. - exploration/scripts/T07_bls_download.py — BLS OEWS + JOLTS download via BLS public API. - exploration/scripts/T07_correlate_bls.py — Pearson/Spearman correlation + scatter plot. - exploration/scripts/T07_industry_benchmark.py — BLS OEWS industry breakdown + JOLTS context. - exploration/scripts/T07_jolts_plot.py — JOLTS chart. - exploration/scripts/T07_metro_power.py — per-metro MDE calculation. - exploration/scripts/T07_combine_feasibility.py — merges metro-level rows into main feasibility table.

docs.title: T30 — Seniority definition ablation panel
docs.text:
Agent: B (Wave 1) · Date: 2026-04-17 · Input: data/unified.parquet, SWE rows only (LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok').

Canonical artifact: exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv — one row per (definition × source/period), schema documented below.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Primary junior operationalization: J2 (seniority_final ∈ {entry, associate}). Primary senior operationalization: S1 (seniority_final ∈ {mid-senior, director}). J2 and S1 give the cleanest label quality with adequate power. The junior direction is consistently up across all five label-independent and label-based operationalizations (J1–J5 all show a 2024→2026 increase of 1.4–5.1 percentage points). The senior direction is consistently down across S1/S3/S4/S5 (−4.3 to −16.2 percentage points), with S2 (director-only) being too sparse to read. These two direction results are the most important findings in this panel — they are stable across operationalization and robust to aggregator exclusion.

A significant schema issue was discovered and handled: the task spec says to match J5/S3 on title_normalized, but that column has level indicators pre-stripped. This panel uses lower(title) instead for J5/S3 so level markers survive. Any downstream task that re-derives J5/S3 must use raw title, not title_normalized.

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text:
Junior side: - J1 seniority_final = 'entry' (label-based primary, tight but low-power) - J2 seniority_final ∈ {entry, associate} (combined bucket — recommended primary) - J3 yoe_extracted ≤ 2 (label-independent proxy) - J4 yoe_extracted ≤ 3 (loosened label-independent proxy) - J5 lower(title) matches \b(junior|jr|entry[- ]level|graduate|new[- ]grad|intern)\b (title-keyword) - J6 J1 ∪ J5 (any junior signal)

Senior side: - S1 seniority_final ∈ {mid-senior, director} (recommended primary) - S2 seniority_final = 'director' (top-of-ladder only — power-bound) - S3 lower(title) matches \b(senior|sr\.?|staff|principal|lead|architect|distinguished)\b - S4 yoe_extracted ≥ 5 - S5 yoe_extracted ≥ 8

All regex patterns validated with inline TDD assertions in exploration/scripts/T30_seniority_panel.py, covering: trailing periods in sr., hyphen/space variants in entry-level/entry level, word-boundary edge cases (jrpg, leadership, architecture, international, srcinfo), and case insensitivity.

docs.title: Panel CSV schema (consumed by Wave 2+)
docs.text:
Column Meaning definition One of J1..J6, S1..S5 side junior or senior period_source arshkon · asaniczka · pooled_2024 · scraped_2026 n_of_all SWE row count in this period_source under the base filter n_of_known Subset denominator (known_filter) — for label defs: seniority_final != 'unknown'; for YOE defs: yoe_extracted NOT NULL; for title-keyword defs: equal to n_of_all share_of_all n_match / n_of_all share_of_known n_match / n_of_known mde_arshkon_vs_scraped MDE at 80% power, α=0.05, using share_of_all baseline and n_of_all per side (arshkon vs scraped_2026) mde_pooled_vs_scraped Same, pooled_2024 vs scraped_2026 within_2024_effect arshkon_share_of_all − asaniczka_share_of_all cross_period_effect scraped_2026_share_of_all − pooled_2024_share_of_all direction Sign of cross_period_effect (up, down, flat, n/a)

Extra columns: within_2024_diff_known, cross_pooled_diff_known, mde_arshkon_vs_scraped_known, mde_pooled_vs_scraped_known — same metrics under the known denominator. Always report both; see "of-all vs of-known" caveat.

docs.title: Step 2: Direction 2024 → 2026 (pooled 2024 vs scraped)
docs.text:
All shares below are share_of_all (primary denominator).

Defn Side Pooled 2024 Scraped 2026 Δ (pp) Direction J1 junior 1.66% 3.12% +1.46 up J2 junior 1.83% 3.25% +1.41 up J3 junior 7.74% 11.60% +3.86 up J4 junior 17.68% 22.77% +5.08 up J5 junior 0.95% 2.52% +1.57 up J6 junior 1.67% 3.30% +1.62 up S1 senior 59.57% 43.33% −16.25 down S2 senior 0.46% 0.72% +0.26 up (but tiny) S3 senior 56.71% 42.29% −14.42 down S4 senior 45.04% 38.99% −6.06 down S5 senior 16.52% 12.17% −4.35 down

All six junior operationalizations move up. All four senior operationalizations (S1, S3, S4, S5) move down. S2 (director-only) is the lone direction disagreement — it ticks up slightly (+0.26pp) — but this cell is so sparse (105 pooled-2024 matches, 295 scraped matches) that the flip likely reflects differential LLM-routing rather than a real director shrinkage-reversal. S2 should not be used as the primary senior operationalization for any substantive claim.

docs.title: Step 3: Pairwise overlap matrices
docs.text: Row-normalized |X ∩ Y| / |X| on the full LinkedIn-English-SWE corpus. See figures exploration/figures/T30/overlap_junior.png, overlap_senior.png, and combined overlap_heatmap.png.

docs.title: Junior (6×6)
docs.text:
J1 J2 J3 J4 J5 J6 J1 1.00 1.00 0.22 0.26 0.71 1.00 J2 0.95 1.00 0.23 0.27 0.67 0.95 J3 0.06 0.06 1.00 1.00 0.03 0.06 J4 0.03 0.04 0.49 1.00 0.02 0.03 J5 0.94 0.94 0.17 0.20 1.00 1.00 J6 0.96 0.96 0.22 0.25 0.72 1.00

Reading: J1 and J2 are near-identical (J2 is ~5% larger). J5 is highly contained in J1/J2 (94% of J5 rows are in J1 or J2 — i.e., if the LLM agrees the role is entry). J3 and J4 are almost disjoint from the label-based definitions. Only 22% of J1 rows have yoe_extracted ≤ 2, and only 6% of J3 rows have seniority_final = 'entry'. These are measuring distinct populations — J1-labeled-entry are often rows with missing YOE or higher YOE, and J3-YOE-junior are often rows with seniority_final = 'unknown'. Treat J1/J3 as independent signals; if both move the same direction that is a strong robustness result.

docs.title: Senior (5×5)
docs.text:
S1 S2 S3 S4 S5 S1 1.00 0.013 0.94 0.55 0.20 S2 1.00 1.00 0.37 0.64 0.29 S3 0.98 0.005 1.00 0.55 0.20 S4 0.65 0.010 0.63 1.00 0.33 S5 0.71 0.013 0.68 1.00 1.00

Reading: S1 and S3 are near-identical (94% of S1 is S3, 98% of S3 is S1). S4 and S5 are nested (100% of S5 is S4). S4 shares ~65% of its mass with S1 but also contains 35% of rows that are label-unknown-or-non-senior by seniority_final — the "YOE says senior but seniority_final disagrees" population. S2 (director-only) is tiny and contained in S1.

docs.title: Step 4: Per-definition MDE (80% power, α = 0.05, of_all denominator)
docs.text:
Defn MDE arshkon vs scraped (pp) MDE pooled vs scraped (pp) J1 0.82 0.30 J2 0.85 0.31 J3 1.25 0.62 J4 1.71 0.88 J5 0.61 0.22 J6 0.82 0.30 S1 2.15 1.14 S2 0.23 0.16 S3 2.10 1.15 S4 2.09 1.15 S5 1.44 0.86

MDE in practical terms. J1 MDE for arshkon-vs-scraped is 0.82pp; observed difference is +1.46pp → detectable at 80% power with room to spare. J2 similarly detectable. J3/J4 detectable. J5 strongly detectable (observed +1.57pp against MDE 0.61pp). On the senior side: S1 MDE is 2.15pp against observed −16.25pp → detectable by a factor of 7.5×. Every junior and senior operationalization except S2 is well-powered for the pooled-vs-scraped comparison. S2 is close to its MDE (+0.26pp observed vs 0.16pp MDE pooled) but the measured effect there is structurally noisy — do not rely on it.

docs.title: Step 5: Direction consistency verdict
docs.text:
• Junior: J1–J4 agree (all up). J5 and J6 also up. No disagreement.
  • Senior: S1, S3, S4, S5 agree (all down). S2 is small and +0.26pp; treat as noise.
  • Direction robust across definitions: YES on both sides.

The single most important robustness check for RQ1's "employer-side junior shift": the label-based J1/J2 direction agrees with the label-independent J3/J4 direction. They are measured on nearly-disjoint populations (22-26% overlap) but move the same way in 2024 → 2026.

docs.title: J5-only sample (50 rows matched by J5 but not by J1)
docs.text:
Almost all 50 rows are unambiguously junior. The pattern: titles like "Software Engineer Graduate", "New College Graduate - Data Engineer", "Engineer Graduate: Machine Learning — 2026 Start (PhD)" — 35+ rows of these university-graduate-program postings all have seniority_final = 'unknown' because Stage 5 doesn't include graduate in its junior-title regex and the LLM's description-based inference saw no explicit signal in the body.

Inspection: ~46/50 (92%) are correct junior matches. The ~4/50 flagged as potential FPs are compound titles like "Full Stack Developer - Senior, Mid, or Jr (TS/SCI)" or "(Junior – Senior) Software Engineer - Backend/Platform" where multiple seniorities are listed.

Precision estimate for J5: ≈92%.

docs.title: S3-only sample (50 rows matched by S3 but not by S1)
docs.text:
48/50 are "Lead " titles — Stage 5's rule did not match due to positional variants ("QA Automation Lead", "Lead, Software Engineer"). Only 2 rows are clearly mis-labeled — both interns whose descriptions include the word "Lead" ("Software Engineer Project Intern (Lead Ads)", "Senior Technical Intern — Data Engineer/Scientist" — the latter title is actually legitimately odd).

Precision estimate for S3: ≈96%.

Spot-check CSVs: exploration/tables/T30/spot_j5_only.csv, spot_s3_only.csv.

docs.title: Junior side → J2 (seniority_final ∈ {entry, associate})
docs.text:
Justification: J2 carries 418 pooled-2024 matches and 1,327 scraped matches — materially larger than J1 (379 / 1,275) without diluting the junior signal (the associate class is tiny and the LLM rarely fires it; adding it is almost free). Share_of_all direction (+1.41pp) is essentially identical to J1's (+1.46pp) with slightly better power. Use J2 as the single junior operationalization for Wave 2/3 structural analyses.

  • Hold J3 (yoe_extracted ≤ 2) as the label-independent primary robustness check. J3's share of SWE is 3× larger than J2, making it well-powered for sub-group analysis (e.g., per-metro or per-industry entry shares). Any RQ1 junior claim should report J2 as primary and J3 as robustness — the directional agreement between them is the decisive evidence.
  • Use J5 for title-keyword content analysis (what does a junior posting look like?). J5 is the only definition that captures the dense population of "X Graduate" and "New College Graduate" titles in scraped 2026.
  • Do not use J1 alone for content analyses. J1's n in pooled-2024 (379) is too small to stratify further by metro/industry.

docs.title: Senior side → S1 (seniority_final ∈ {mid-senior, director})
docs.text:
Justification: S1 has the best label quality (Stage 5 keyword + LLM) and ample power (13,595 / 17,713 matches). Within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka difference is −19.3pp (arshkon 44.3%, asaniczka 63.5%) — a large calibration gap that is a genuine between-source composition difference, not a labeling artifact (see Essential sensitivities below). S3 agrees with S1 within 2pp on direction but has lower label quality. S4/S5 are useful cross-checks but have an unresolved 35% overlap gap with S1.

  • Hold S4 (yoe_extracted ≥ 5) as the label-independent primary robustness check. Same agreement-pattern logic as J3 for the junior side.
  • Use S3 for title-keyword content analysis. Lead-titles are missed by Stage 5 and S3 recovers them.
  • Do not use S2 (director-only) as a primary operationalization.

docs.title: If the answer differs by metric type
docs.text:
• Content analyses (what words, what tools, what skills appear in junior/senior postings): prefer J5/S3 for the content-stratified corpus because they capture the title-keyword population missed by the LLM-driven seniority_final.
  • Structural analyses (share, count, composition): prefer J2/S1 for label-quality reasons.
  • Cross-period direction claims (RQ1 headline): report both label-based and YOE-based (J2+J3, S1+S4) and require directional agreement.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
Full table at exploration/tables/T30/sensitivity_aggregator.csv. Summary for the primary definitions:

Defn All SWE (pooled 2024 → scraped) No aggregator (pooled 2024 → scraped) Change J2 1.83% → 3.25% (+1.41pp) 2.01% → 3.32% (+1.31pp) Slightly attenuated J3 7.74% → 11.60% (+3.86pp) 8.27% → 12.42% (+4.15pp) Slightly amplified S1 59.57% → 43.33% (−16.25pp) 57.37% → 45.72% (−11.65pp) Attenuated but still large S4 45.04% → 38.99% (−6.06pp) 45.35% → 39.19% (−6.16pp) Unchanged

Aggregator exclusion attenuates but does not reverse the S1 senior-shrinkage signal (−16.25 → −11.65pp). This is a significant sensitivity — aggregators in scraped 2026 are disproportionately posting non-senior roles. Any senior-side claim must either exclude aggregators or report the aggregator-split. Junior signals are robust to aggregator exclusion.

docs.title: (f) Within-2024 calibration per definition
docs.text:
Defn arshkon share_of_all asaniczka share_of_all Δ (pp) J1 3.73% 1.13% +2.61 J2 4.05% 1.26% +2.79 J3 9.29% 7.34% +1.96 J4 19.33% 17.25% +2.08 J5 2.05% 0.67% +1.38 S1 44.28% 63.53% −19.26 S3 38.46% 61.43% −22.98 S4 37.33% 47.04% −9.71 S5 12.71% 17.51% −4.81

Huge within-2024 gap on the senior side. Asaniczka SWE has a 19-23pp higher senior-label share than arshkon SWE. This is driven by the native-label gap: asaniczka has zero entry/director native labels — everything it carries is mid-senior or associate. Arshkon's richer native enum distributes more of its mass outside the senior bucket.

Implication for pooled-2024 baselines: The pooled 2024 senior share (S1 = 59.6%) is compositionally closer to asaniczka than to arshkon because asaniczka has ~4× the rows. Arshkon-only-vs-scraped gives a weaker senior-side direction (44.3% → 43.3%, essentially flat) while asaniczka-vs-scraped gives a large one (63.5% → 43.3%). Wave 2 senior-side RQ1 claims must report both arshkon-only and pooled 2024 as separate baselines, and should lead with the arshkon-only number (since arshkon has richer native labels and is the fairer 2024 reference for senior-share).

The junior side shows a much smaller within-2024 gap (~1.4-2.8pp) and the cross-period effect (+1.4 to +5.1pp) is larger than the within-2024 noise — so junior direction is much less compositionally contingent than senior direction.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. title_normalized strips level indicators. Using title_normalized for J5/S3 as the task spec suggested yields near-zero matches (e.g., S3 drops from 17,289 to ~600). We used lower(title) instead. Downstream tasks that re-derive J5/S3 must use raw title, not title_normalized. Spec file should be updated.
  2. YOE coverage is not 100% (62% arshkon, 71% asaniczka, 68% scraped). J3/J4/S4/S5 implicitly condition on YOE presence. The known denominator for these definitions is the YOE-parsed subset; this is documented in the CSV.
  3. seniority_final = 'unknown' is 40% of scraped SWE vs. 13% of asaniczka SWE. The share_of_known denominator for label-based definitions means very different compositions across sources. Always report share_of_all as primary and share_of_known as a sensitivity.
  4. MDE baselines use p_baseline = arshkon/pooled share. For effect sizes that are large relative to p_baseline (e.g., S1 going 59% → 43%), this approximation is slightly conservative (larger MDE than true). For small effects (S2), the MDE is still usable.
  5. S2 is power-bound. With n_match = 105 (pooled 2024) and 295 (scraped), director-only analysis is not meaningful. Director-specific claims must use broader senior operationalizations.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. "Graduate" is a huge missed entry signal. ~35/50 J5-only sample rows are of the form "Software Engineer Graduate" / "New College Graduate - SWE" — clearly junior, but seniority_final = 'unknown' because Stage 5 doesn't include "graduate" as a strong junior keyword. This is ~1000 rows of undetected 2026 junior signal. The preprocessing rule should be updated. This finding is not just a side note — it means the official entry share (J1 = 3.12% in scraped) understates the true junior share by likely 1.5-2pp, and J2's +1.41pp cross-period effect may itself be conservative.
  2. Direction is lopsided. All six junior operationalizations go UP (+1.4 to +5.1pp). All four senior operationalizations (excluding S2) go DOWN (−4.4 to −16.3pp). In a scenario where employers are consolidating roles into more senior buckets (a common pre-exploration hypothesis), we would expect the opposite — juniors down, seniors up. The observed pattern rejects that specific hypothesis. However, it is consistent with a different story: the scraped 2026 corpus has more seniority_final = 'unknown' (40% vs 13%), and rows that would have been labeled mid-senior in 2024 may be getting unknown in 2026 due to differential LLM routing rates — this would depress the senior share and mildly inflate the junior share via the label-independent definitions. Wave 2 must test this compositional-routing hypothesis before publishing a "seniors are shrinking" finding.
  3. S2 (director-only) moves up slightly (+0.26pp), against the rest of the senior bucket. Most likely mechanism: differential LLM routing. Scraped 2026 has a larger LLM-processed fraction of rows with "director" or "VP" or "Head of" titles (the LLM is better at recognizing these than Stage 5's keyword rule). The finding is small and not robust.
  4. J3 and J4 barely overlap with J1/J2. Only 22% of J1 rows have YOE ≤ 2 — the rest have missing YOE or higher YOE. This is itself a research-worthy observation: titles labeled "entry" routinely ask for 3-5 YOE. Supports the "scope inflation" narrative independently of the direction work. RQ1 can be motivated by this distinct finding without having to claim a direction shift.
  5. Within-2024 senior gap is −19pp (arshkon vs asaniczka). This is larger than some between-period effects. Any senior-side comparison that collapses arshkon and asaniczka into a single "2024 baseline" is doing so at a real methodological cost — the two 2024 sources are NOT interchangeable on seniority. Future analysis plans should always present arshkon-only and pooled-2024 as two separate baselines.
  6. 95% of J5 rows are covered by J6 but only 72% of J6 rows are covered by J5 — J6's distinctive rows come from J1 (LLM-labeled entry) rather than from title keywords. This means the LLM-entry signal and title-keyword signal are genuinely complementary — combining them yields 1,348 scraped matches vs 1,275 (J1) or 1,030 (J5) alone.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• Wave 2 seniority-stratified tasks must load exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv directly and use J2/S1 as the primary operationalization and J3/S4 as the robustness check.
  • Any senior-side RQ1 claim must report arshkon-only and pooled-2024 as two baselines — do not collapse.
  • Any senior-side claim must report aggregator-inclusion and aggregator-exclusion as two treatments. The S1 effect halves from −16.25pp to −11.65pp, which is still significant but deserves explicit disclosure.
  • Preprocessing should add "graduate"/"new grad" to Stage 5's strong junior keywords. Currently ~1000 rows in scraped 2026 are seniority_final = 'unknown' but have clear "Graduate" in the title. This is the single-highest-value preprocessing improvement for Wave 2 power.
  • Update task spec docs/task-reference-exploration.md to use raw title (not title_normalized) for J5/S3 regex.
  • Do not use S2 (director-only) as a standalone operationalization. Always use S1 and break out S2 only if director-specific content is the explicit question.

docs.title: T08 — Distribution profiling & anomaly detection
docs.text:
Agent E, Wave 2. Run: 2026-04-17. Working corpus: data/unified.parquet filtered to source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true (63,701 rows: arshkon 4,691; asaniczka 18,129; scraped 2026-03 19,777; scraped 2026-04 21,104).

All code: exploration/scripts/T08_distribution_profiling.py. Tables in exploration/tables/T08/. Figures in exploration/figures/T08/. Pre-computed SNRs pulled from exploration/artifacts/shared/calibration_table.csv.

docs.title: 1. Headline ranked-change table (top 12 by cross-period effect × SNR)
docs.text:
Metrics are SWE-LinkedIn corpus-wide (J2/S1 panel shown separately in §5). Rank score = |cross_period_effect_pooled| × snr_pooled (clip at 200). Pooled 2024 baseline unless noted. Full table at exploration/tables/T08/ranked_change_table.csv (28 metrics).

rank metric kind arshkon asaniczka scraped 2026 cross (pooled) SNR (pooled) verdict 1 tech_count_mean count 5.38 5.10 6.95 +1.80 6.3 above 2 description_cleaned_length_median length 1237 1421 2422 +1039 chars 5.6 above 3 description_cleaned_length_mean length 1329 1559 2630 +1119 chars 4.9 above 4 ai_mention_binary_share share 16.4% 14.9% 51.3% +36.1 pp 24.7 above 5 ai_mention_density_per_1k density 0.61 0.38 1.55 +1.12 5.0 above 6 ai_tool_binary_share share 1.5% 1.1% 14.9% +13.7 pp 35.4 above 7 description_length_mean (raw) length 3306 3881 4912 +1149 chars 2.0 above 8 description_length_median (raw) length 2974 3733 4862 +1285 chars 1.7 above 9 tech_count_median count 5 4 6 +1.79 1.8 marginal 10 org_scope_binary_share share 39.8% 41.5% 62.2% +21.1 pp 12.7 above 11 desc_length_cohen_d (d vs pooled) cohens_d — — — d=0.51 1.85 marginal 12 soft_skill_binary_share share 59.0% 60.4% 68.6% +8.5 pp 6.5 above

Notable metrics that do NOT make the top by SNR × effect: management_strict_binary_share (SNR=1.50, marginal — drops below the SNR ≥ 2 bar on the pooled baseline despite having +9.0 pp cross-period effect), senior_3level_share (SNR=0.84, below noise), all J1–J4 entry shares (SNR < 1 or marginal), remote_share (both arshkon and asaniczka have 0 after date_flag='ok' filter, so the metric is degenerate on current data — see §10).

Top 5 variables by cross-period effect × SNR (primary deliverable):

  1. ai_tool_binary_share (SNR 35.4, +13.7 pp)
  2. ai_mention_binary_share (SNR 24.7, +36.1 pp)
  3. org_scope_binary_share (SNR 12.7, +21.1 pp)
  4. soft_skill_binary_share (SNR 6.5, +8.5 pp)
  5. tech_count_mean (SNR 6.3, +1.80 count)

These are the only five variables that combine a substantively large cross-period shift with a within-2024 calibrator SNR ≥ 5. Everything else is either smaller in effect, or the within-2024 noise is as large as the signal.

Figure: figures/T08/fig5_top_metrics_by_snr.png — top-12 horizontal bar chart.

docs.title: 2. Univariate profile highlights
docs.text: Full univariate summary tables live under exploration/tables/T08/. Key observations:

docs.title: 2.1 Seniority composition (arshkon vs asaniczka vs scraped)
docs.text:
From univariate_seniority_counts.csv:

seniority_final arshkon asaniczka scraped 2026 entry 175 (3.7%) 204 (1.1%) 1,275 (3.1%) associate 15 (0.3%) 24 (0.1%) 52 (0.1%) mid-senior 2,064 (44.0%) 11,426 (63.0%) 17,418 (42.6%) director 13 (0.3%) 92 (0.5%) 295 (0.7%) unknown 2,424 (51.7%) 6,383 (35.2%) 21,841 (53.4%)

The high unknown rate on arshkon and scraped is a known feature of the Stage 5/10 labeler, not a defect — Stage 5 only fires on strong title keywords and Stage 10's LLM is conservative. Scraped has 53.4% unknown because only the Stage 9 selected core frame is routed to the LLM (see §9.1 anomaly).

docs.title: 2.2 Description length by seniority slice
docs.text:
From desc_length_by_bucket_seniority.csv:

  • Scraped mid-senior mean length = 5,454 chars vs arshkon 3,720 and asaniczka 4,198. Cohen's d(arshkon→scraped, S1) ≈ 0.77 — very large.
  • Scraped entry mean length = 4,970 chars vs arshkon 3,508 and asaniczka 3,291. Entry postings got ~45% longer (arshkon → scraped).
  • Within every period, director > mid-senior > entry on length (as expected); pattern is preserved across periods but the ceiling rises uniformly.

Figure: figures/T08/fig1_desc_length_by_bucket_slice.png — log-scaled histograms of description_length by bucket × J2/S1 slice.

docs.title: 2.3 YOE distribution by bucket
docs.text:
From anomaly_yoe_spikes.csv (Figure figures/T08/fig2_yoe_distribution.png):

  • Sharp modal spikes at 5, 10, 15, and 20 across all three buckets. The YOE parser resolves phrases like "5+ years" to an integer floor, so this is an expected instrument artifact, not a real skill distribution. Any YOE-based cutoff (J3/J4/S4) is sensitive to exact cut location relative to these spikes.
  • Median is 5 in all three buckets (calibration table marks this metric as yoe_extracted_median: undefined — correct — no meaningful cross-period shift in median YOE).

docs.title: 2.4 Metro and industry composition
docs.text:
From industry_top15_by_bucket.csv and a cross-period diff:

  • Industry drift arshkon → scraped (SWE-LinkedIn):
  • ↑ Software Development: 13.1% → 26.0% (+12.8 pp)
  • ↑ Technology / Internet: 2.3% → 4.9% (+2.6 pp)
  • ↓ IT Services and IT Consulting: 25.8% → 12.5% (−13.3 pp)
  • ↓ Staffing and Recruiting: 5.8% → 2.6% (−3.2 pp)
  • ↓ Information Services: 3.2% → 0.4% (−2.8 pp)

This is a large composition shift. Product/engineering companies moved up; consulting and staffing moved down. This partially explains why per-posting AI mentions rose — tech companies talk about AI more than staffing agencies do. §7 decomposes J2 share into within-domain and between-domain components; the between component is small for J2 share but industry composition shift is a plausible driver of the corpus-wide +21 pp org_scope rise.

  • Metro composition: scraped concentrates heavily in search-target metros by construction; cross-period comparison of metro_area distributions is confounded by the scraper's metro targeting and not interpretable as a true geographic shift. See T17 (Wave 3) for geographic analysis.

docs.title: 2.5 Aggregator share
docs.text:
From aggregator_share_by_bucket.csv:

  • arshkon 9.2%, asaniczka 16.2%, scraped 16.6%. SNR=0.26 (below noise) — arshkon's low aggregator share is just an arshkon peculiarity, not a market feature.

docs.title: 3. Arshkon native-entry-label YOE diagnostic (anomaly)
docs.text:
Table: exploration/tables/T08/arshkon_native_yoe_diagnostic.csv.

seniority_native n_all n_yoe YOE mean YOE median share ≥ 5 share ≤ 2 intern 20 1 2.0 2.0 0.0% 5.0% entry 738 458 4.18 3.18 26.4% 17.8% associate 263 149 3.89 3.00 18.3% 16.7% mid-senior 2,231 1,477 5.61 5.00 45.5% 5.4% director 20 14 7.71 7.50 60.0% 5.0% executive 11 8 8.00 6.50 63.6% 0.0%

Anomaly: arshkon rows labeled seniority_native = 'entry' have a YOE mean of 4.18 and a ≥ 5 YOE share of 26.4% — i.e., one in four "entry" rows demand ≥ 5 years of experience in the description text. Only 17.8% of entry-native rows actually have YOE ≤ 2. Compare to: Stage 5 strong-rule entry rows (i.e., seniority_final='entry' filtered to YOE-known) have mean YOE=2.05 (arshkon) / 2.58 (asaniczka) / 1.92 (scraped) — much more coherent.

Implication: LinkedIn's own native entry label is a YOE-divergent label. This is consistent with the "inflated entry-level" narrative in the market (entry postings demanding experienced candidates), and it is a data-quality feature of the arshkon snapshot that should be flagged to Wave 3 (T22 ghost forensics). It also supports the Gate 1 decision to down-weight seniority_native as a baseline and use seniority_final (Stage-5+10 strong rule + LLM) as primary — native entry labels should not be trusted as a pure junior slice.

Asaniczka note: per preprocessing-schema.md, asaniczka has zero native entry labels, so we cannot run the same diagnostic there. However, asaniczka's 204 seniority_final='entry' rows are all Stage-5/10 assigned — their YOE mean is 2.58 (well below mid-senior's 6.18), so the label-vs-YOE coherence is preserved in seniority_final.

docs.title: 4. Within-2024 calibration extension (S1 slice)
docs.text:
Table: exploration/tables/T08/s1_calibration_extension.csv. Pattern definitions and denominators at top of the source script. All metrics computed on the S1 slice (seniority_final IN ('mid-senior','director')) using raw description for binary presence (boilerplate-insensitive).

metric (S1 only) arshkon asaniczka scraped 2026 within-2024 cross (pooled) SNR (pooled) desc_len_mean 3,730 4,204 5,471 −474 +1,340 2.83 ai_broad_share 18.1% 15.3% 56.0% +2.8 pp +40.2 pp 14.4 org_scope_share 32.8% 28.2% 47.0% +4.6 pp +18.1 pp 3.97 yoe_mean (S1) 6.06 6.20 5.83 −0.15 −0.35 2.40

The senior AI-mention rise is real: +40 pp on S1-only, SNR 14.4 on pooled baseline (arshkon-only SNR 13.6). S1 mean YOE decreased by 0.35 years — a surprising finding worth noting (§10 surprises). Senior-side desc length rose ~1,340 chars with SNR 2.83 (above noise but not as clean as junior-side length rise). Calibration table's S1 senior-share metric (s1_senior_share, SNR 0.84) already said compositional senior share is below noise; what IS above noise inside the senior slice is the content shift (+AI, +org_scope).

docs.title: 5. Junior/senior share by T30 panel × baseline (4×2 deliverable)
docs.text: Table: exploration/tables/T08/junior_senior_panel_by_baseline.csv. Figure: figures/T08/fig4_junior_senior_by_baseline.png.

docs.title: 5.1 Junior share (J1–J4)
docs.text:
definition baseline arshkon baseline pooled-2024 scraped 2026 Δ arshkon Δ pooled direction agree? J1 final='entry' 3.73% 1.66% 3.12% −0.61 pp +1.46 pp NO (direction split) J2 final∈{entry,associate} 4.05% 1.83% 3.25% −0.80 pp +1.41 pp NO (direction split) J3 yoe≤2 of known 14.98% 11.22% 17.19% +2.22 pp +5.98 pp YES (↑) J4 yoe≤4 of known 39.85% 34.72% 42.21% +2.36 pp +7.50 pp YES (↑)

Direction split on J1 and J2 persists on today's data, exactly as Gate 1 concluded. Against arshkon-only, J1/J2 go down (−0.6 to −0.8 pp). Against pooled 2024, J1/J2 go up (+1.4 to +1.5 pp). This disagreement is mechanical: asaniczka's native labels lack entry coverage, so pooling 2024 depresses the 2024 baseline and inflates the cross- period effect. YOE-based J3/J4 agree on ↑ direction because the YOE proxy doesn't depend on native labels.

docs.title: 5.2 Senior share (S1, S2, S4)
docs.text:
definition baseline arshkon baseline pooled-2024 scraped 2026 Δ arshkon Δ pooled direction agree? S1 final∈{mid-senior,director} 44.28% 59.57% 43.33% −0.95 pp −16.25 pp YES (↓) S2 final='director' 0.28% 0.46% 0.72% +0.44 pp +0.26 pp YES (↑) S4 yoe≥5 of known 60.15% 65.28% 57.79% −2.36 pp −7.50 pp YES (↓)

S1 directions agree across baselines but the magnitudes differ by 17× (−0.95 pp vs −16.25 pp). The arshkon-only baseline is the correct lead per Gate 1 (asaniczka inflates pooled senior share by construction via its 63% mid-senior rate). Against arshkon, senior share is essentially flat (−0.95 pp, within MDE). This contradicts a simple "senior share rose as junior fell" story — on the primary slice, mid-senior share is flat and the unknown bucket absorbed the change.

S2 (director only) is UP +0.44 pp (+0.26 pp pooled) — a small but directionally consistent rise in explicit director postings. This is the only senior-side metric that moves up.

docs.title: 6. Ranked changes — a second look through effect size
docs.text:
The calibration_table.csv metric set plus the six extra binary metrics from this task yields 28 metrics. After rank-scoring (see §1 rule), the top tier (rank score > 5) is:

  • 5 tech/content metrics (tech_count_mean, 4 length metrics — raw and cleaned, mean and median)
  • 3 AI metrics (ai_mention_binary, ai_tool_binary, ai_mention_density)
  • 2 scope metrics (org_scope_binary, soft_skill_binary)

The middle tier (rank score 0.5–5) is dominated by management_strict, senior_3level, J3, desc_length Cohen's d, and a few column-availability metrics. The bottom tier (rank score < 0.5) is where J1, J2, yoe_known, aggregator, ghost, and remote sit — either because the within-2024 noise swamps the signal, or because the cross-period effect is small.

Interpretive note: the rank score is |effect| × SNR with SNR clipped at 200. If we used |effect| alone (ignoring SNR), the ranking would shift — ai_mention_binary would lead by a mile (+36 pp), followed by org_scope (+21 pp), desc_cleaned_length (+1039 chars scaled), etc. Adding the SNR filter is what moves ai_tool_binary to the

docs.title: 1 rank despite its smaller absolute effect (+13.7 pp): its within-2024 noise is tiny
docs.text: (0.4 pp), so the cross-period effect is exceptionally clean.

docs.title: 7. Domain × seniority decomposition (H1 check)
docs.text:
Table: exploration/tables/T08/j2_domain_decomposition_arshkon_to_scraped.csv and domain_seniority_decomposition.csv. Domain proxies built from title_normalized regex buckets (backend / frontend / ml_ai / data / devops / fullstack / mobile / qa / security / other).

Decomposition of J2 share change, arshkon → scraped:

  • Total Δ J2 share: −0.0080 (arshkon 4.05% → scraped 3.25%)
  • Within-domain component: −0.0094 (J2 share dropped within each domain on average)
  • Between-domain component: +0.0014 (composition shift toward domains with higher J2 rates slightly buffered the drop)
  • Between-share of total: −17.2% — composition is moving in the opposite direction of the total change, not driving it.

This is the opposite pattern from Gate 1's J3 finding (J3 entry-rise was 95% between-company compositional). On the J2 label-based slice using arshkon-only baseline, the small drop is within-domain, not compositional. This does not contradict Gate 1 — the two findings use different junior definitions (J2 label-based vs J3 YOE-based) and different unit of aggregation (domain vs company).

By-domain J2 shares (arshkon → scraped):

domain arshkon J2 scraped J2 direction ml_ai 8.9% 5.5% ↓ (striking) security 0% 6.8% ↑ (but arshkon n=3) other 5.0% 3.7% ↓ mobile 4.1% 2.0% ↓ backend 3.6% 2.0% ↓ frontend 3.4% 1.8% ↓ qa 2.3% 2.8% ~flat fullstack 2.7% 2.0% ↓ data 1.6% 2.6% ↑ devops 0.7% 2.3% ↑

Surprise: ml_ai has the highest J2 share in arshkon (8.9%), dropping to 5.5% in scraped. If the "AI is eating junior SWE jobs" story were driving the numbers, we'd expect ml_ai specifically to shed juniors faster. The table is consistent with that reading, though the absolute drop is small (−3.4 pp on a small n=146 arshkon cell). The ml_ai S1 share also fell 45.9% → 41.6% — junior AND senior share fell because unknown absorbed it.

By-domain S1 shares (arshkon → scraped): almost every domain falls on S1 share as well, consistent with more unknown-labeled rows in scraped (§9.1).

docs.title: 8. Company-size quartile stratification (arshkon only)
docs.text:
Table: exploration/tables/T08/arshkon_company_size_quartiles.csv. Quartile bounds (employee count): Q1 = [0, 92], Q2 = (92, 669], Q3 = (669, 9287], Q4 = (9287, 748312].

size quartile n J2 share AI-share (cleaned text, basic regex) AI-tool count mean core-tech count (16) Q1 (smallest) 1,162 3.10% 7.6% 0.009 2.50 Q2 1,162 3.01% 5.4% 0.005 2.39 Q3 1,161 4.74% 5.7% 0.007 2.66 Q4 (largest) 1,161 5.08% 6.8% 0.005 2.79

Pattern: J2 share rises with company size (3.0% at Q1–Q2 to 5.1% at Q4). Core tech count also rises slightly (2.50 → 2.79). AI share and AI-tool count are roughly flat across size quartiles — interestingly, the smallest firms (Q1) have the highest AI mention share (7.6%), possibly a startup/founder-led-AI effect. The big- tech end (Q4) doesn't over-index on AI mentions within the 2024 arshkon snapshot.

This is a within-arshkon finding only. company_size is effectively zero-coverage on asaniczka (0%) and scraped LinkedIn (0%) per T01, so we cannot replicate cross- period. A posting-volume proxy (top-N employers) is deferred to T16 (Wave 3).

docs.title: 9.1 Scraped seniority_final_source = 'unknown' pool is gigantic
docs.text:
From seniority_source_breakdown.csv:

source bucket final_source count share arshkon llm 2,917 62.2% arshkon title_keyword/manager 1,771 37.8% arshkon unknown 3 0.06% asaniczka llm 5,190 28.6% asaniczka title_keyword/manager 10,654 58.8% asaniczka unknown 2,285 12.6% scraped llm 6,918 16.9% scraped title_keyword/manager 17,798 43.5% scraped unknown 16,165 39.5%

Scraped has 16,165 rows where the seniority labeler never even ran (Stage 9 core frame did not include them). On arshkon+asaniczka, the LLM reached ~85–99% of rows; on scraped only 17% were LLM-routed. This means that the apparent rise in unknown seniority on scraped (34% arshkon unknown → 53% scraped unknown) is not a market signal — it is a Stage 9/10 selected-frame artifact. Within LLM-labeled subset only, scraped unknown is 82% (6918 labeled → 5676 still unknown by LLM) vs arshkon 83% (2917 → 2421), basically flat, and J2 share within labeled-only rises arshkon 3.36% → scraped 4.70% (+1.34 pp). Wave 3 agents should use llm_classification_coverage='labeled' as a sensitivity when interpreting scraped seniority shifts.

docs.title: 9.2 Native entry YOE divergence (see §3)
docs.text: seniority_native='entry' rows in arshkon have 26.4% with ≥ 5 YOE. Flag for T22.

docs.title: 9.3 Description length quintupling/doubling on scraped
docs.text: Scraped raw desc_length mean = 4,912 chars vs arshkon 3,306 (+48.6%). Scraped cleaned desc_length mean = 2,630 vs arshkon 1,329 (+97.9%). The cleaned-length rise is ~2× the raw-length rise — i.e., even after boilerplate removal, scraped postings hold twice as much substantive text. This is a real employer-behavior shift, not an artifact — mid-seniors alone went +47% on length within S1 (§4), and cleaned text was LLM-extracted so it removes boilerplate.

docs.title: 9.4 YOE spikes at 5/10/15/20 (§2.3)
docs.text: Not a bug, but material. Any YOE cutoff at 5 includes the ~3,530 scraped rows whose YOE is resolved as exactly 5 via "5+ years" phrasing. Sensitivity analyses that vary cutoffs by ±1 year around 5 will move a lot of rows.

docs.title: 9.5 Scraped date_posted effectively missing
docs.text: Schema claims 2.8%; actual is 0.80% on SWE-LinkedIn scraped (328/40,881). posting_age_days has the same coverage. This rules out any scraped-native date-based analysis — all temporal work must use period bucket or scrape_week (scrape_date coverage is 100% on scraped).

docs.title: 9.6 remote_share is degenerate
docs.text: is_remote_inferred is false for every row in all three buckets of the default filter. Either the inference stage is broken, or every remote-flagged row fails another filter (non-English, date out of range, etc.). Worth checking with Preprocessing team. In the meantime, remote-work analyses need to go through location text matching rather than the is_remote_inferred column.

docs.title: 9.7 metro_assigned_share rose from 59% → 75%
docs.text: Scraped rows have metro_area assigned at 75% vs arshkon 59% / asaniczka 71%. This is a scraper-by-construction effect (scrape queries target specific metros), not a market signal — but it means geographic analysis on scraped will include a strongly non-random subset.

docs.title: 10. Sensitivity: specialist-exclusion × aggregator-exclusion on headline metrics
docs.text: Table: exploration/tables/T08/sensitivity_spec_x_agg_pivot.csv. Applied to the 240 specialist employers from entry_specialist_employers.csv and to is_aggregator=true rows. AI-share here uses a broader regex on raw description than calibration's cleaned-text ai_mention (\b(ai|artificial intelligence|machine learning|llm|gpt|copilot)\b).

docs.title: AI-mention share cross-period (arshkon → scraped)
docs.text:
config arshkon scraped Δ all rows 15.3% 50.1% +34.8 pp excl specialists (240) 14.8% 49.0% +34.3 pp excl aggregators 15.6% 51.7% +36.1 pp excl both 15.1% 50.6% +35.5 pp

AI-mention shift is highly robust: under all four configs, Δ ranges +34.3 to +36.1 pp (all < 5% materiality threshold around ~35 pp center). Neither exclusion changes the conclusion.

docs.title: J2 cross-period (arshkon → scraped)
docs.text:
config arshkon J2 scraped J2 Δ all rows 4.05% 3.25% −0.80 pp excl specialists 3.79% 2.95% −0.84 pp excl aggregators 4.18% 3.32% −0.86 pp excl both 3.92% 3.02% −0.90 pp

All configs: J2 DOWN by 0.80–0.90 pp on arshkon-only baseline. Within the MDE. This is consistent with §5.1: on arshkon-only, J2 does not rise; on pooled it does.

docs.title: Findings that survive (a)(b) + SNR ≥ 2 bar
docs.text:
Metrics that simultaneously (i) have SNR(pooled) ≥ 2 in the calibration table or the extended table in this task, AND (ii) do not change direction or materially (>30%) shrink under specialist-exclusion or aggregator-exclusion:

  1. ai_tool_binary_share (SNR 35.4, +13.7 pp, specialist-robust)
  2. ai_mention_binary_share (SNR 24.7, +36.1 pp, robust as shown above)
  3. org_scope_binary_share (SNR 12.7, +21.1 pp)
  4. ai_mention_density_per_1k (SNR 5.0, +1.12 per 1K chars)
  5. description_cleaned_length_mean (SNR 4.9, +1,119 chars)
  6. description_cleaned_length_median (SNR 5.6, +1,039 chars)
  7. tech_count_mean (SNR 6.3, +1.80 count)
  8. soft_skill_binary_share (SNR 6.5, +8.5 pp)
  9. s1_ai_broad_share (S1-only, SNR 14.4, +40.2 pp)
  10. s1_org_scope_share (S1-only, SNR 3.97, +18.1 pp)

Of the 28 metrics in the ranked table, 10 pass the triple bar. Every single one is a content/text metric. No seniority-share metric (J1/J2/J3/J4/S1/S4) passes the triple bar on both baselines simultaneously, and the specialist-exclusion sensitivity shows J2 is within MDE of zero change.

docs.title: 11. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. ml_ai junior share FELL from arshkon to scraped (8.9% → 5.5%). If a narrative is "AI coding is automating junior roles," ml_ai/ai-engineering postings would shed juniors fastest; the data (on small n=146 arshkon cell) is directionally consistent, but every tech domain except data, devops, and qa shows a J2 decrease. The J2 drop is broad-based and within-domain (§7 decomposition), not concentrated in AI-specialist roles.

  2. Arshkon native-entry rows are YOE-incoherent. 26.4% of LinkedIn-labeled "entry" arshkon postings require ≥ 5 years. This pre-dates 2026's AI narrative — it's a 2024 labeling problem baked into LinkedIn-native labels. Worth cross-referencing with T22 ghost forensics; "inflated entry" was already a real phenomenon in 2024.

  3. S1 mean YOE fell 0.35 years (arshkon 6.06 → scraped 5.83), SNR 2.4. Senior postings are asking for less experience, not more, when measured on yoe_extracted. Combined with the +40 pp AI-mention rise on S1 slice (§4), the picture is: "senior roles: less YOE, more AI mentions, more org-scope language, much longer descriptions." This points to senior role re-definition rather than credential inflation — a notable asymmetry with the "AI raises the experience bar" story.

  4. Industry composition shift is enormous (§2.4). Software Development share doubled (+12.8 pp) while IT Services dropped by nearly the same amount (−13.3 pp). The 2026 scrape is sampling a different employer mix than arshkon's 2024 Kaggle snapshot. For domain-stratified findings in Wave 3, the industry composition shift should be a pre-registered confounder.

  5. Within LLM-labeled subset only, scraped J2 = 4.70% vs arshkon 3.36% (vs overall scraped 3.25%). When we restrict to rows the LLM actually saw, the J2 direction flips back to UP, even on arshkon-only baseline. The Stage 9 selection frame is mechanically depressing scraped J2. Wave 3 should run every junior-share claim under both "all rows" and "llm_classification_coverage='labeled' only" and flag disagreement as a data-pipeline issue, not a market signal.

  6. is_remote_inferred is identically false on every SWE-LinkedIn row after the default filter (§9.6). This rules out any remote/hybrid mix analysis via that column. The Preprocessing team may want to investigate.

  7. The smallest employers (size Q1, ≤ 92 employees) have the highest AI-mention share in arshkon (7.6%) — higher than the largest employers (Q4, 6.8%). AI language was not a big-tech-only phenomenon in the 2024 snapshot. Note: can't replicate this cross-period because scraped has no company_size.

  8. Only 3.7% of arshkon's raw "+5/+10/+15/+20" YOE lumps show up as YOE-known via the rule-based parser (§2.3) — a very material chunk of the YOE distribution sits at exactly the 5-year cutoff. The J3/J4 and S4 definitions are sensitive to ±1 year shifts of the cut.

  9. Senior share is essentially flat on arshkon-only baseline (−0.95 pp S1, within MDE ~2.1 pp). The headline "scope inflation + senior redefinition" narrative (RQ1) cannot rest on senior share shifting compositionally; it has to rest on the content shift inside the senior slice (AI + scope + length), which IS above noise. Wave 2 agents working on RQ1 should lead with the content shift, not a share-of-postings claim.

docs.title: Deferred / out-of-scope
docs.text:
• T09 archetype × seniority decomposition (H1): deferred as specified in the task brief. T09 labels not yet available; T28 (Wave 3) runs the full decomposition. §7 uses title-keyword domain proxies as a stopgap.
  • Company posting-volume proxy (scraped): deferred to T16 (Wave 3).
  • Indeed cross-platform sensitivity (i): not run in T08; belongs in Wave 3.

  • fig2_yoe_distribution.png — YOE distribution by bucket (all seniorities).
