  • fig4_junior_senior_by_baseline.png — 2×4 panel: junior/senior share by definition × baseline.
  • fig5_top_metrics_by_snr.png — top-12 metrics by |cross-effect| × SNR.

Tables (exploration/tables/T08/): 17 CSVs covering univariate profiles, T30 panel results, ranked changes, domain decomposition, company-size quartiles, specialist/aggregator sensitivity, and anomaly scans. See top of this report for the ones referenced inline.

Script: exploration/scripts/T08_distribution_profiling.py (single pass, DuckDB + pyarrow, never loads parquet into pandas; ran to completion in ~25 s on 31 GB box).

docs.title: T09 — Posting archetype discovery (methods laboratory)
docs.text: Agent: F (Wave 2) Run date: 2026-04-17 Sample: 8,000 SWE LinkedIn postings, text_source='llm' only, period-balanced. Primary junior slice: J2 (seniority_final ∈ {entry, associate}). Primary senior slice: S1 (seniority_final ∈ {mid-senior, director}).

docs.title: 1. Headline (the single most important result)
docs.text:
The SWE posting corpus clusters by TECH DOMAIN first, and period / seniority second. NMI between BERTopic clusters and a derived domain label is 0.26, 6.7× larger than NMI(cluster, period)=0.039 and 7.9× larger than NMI(cluster, seniority)=0.033. NMF on the same sample shows the same ordering: domain NMI 0.23, period 0.07, seniority 0.02. The domain- dominant ordering survives aggregator exclusion (domain NMI 0.26), swe-tier strict filtering (0.26), and raw-text sensitivity (0.22).

Implication: the market organizes by tech domain, and the 2024→2026 changes are best understood as a composition shift and within-domain content shift, not as a period-defined macro regime change. This supports Gate 1 framing (1) ("Employers rewriting JDs toward AI skills is the real restructuring") and partial framing (3) ("domain composition explains apparent seniority shifts"), and weakens framing (2) ("pure platform/ boilerplate evolution") at the cluster level.

However, the within-vs-between decomposition of the J2 entry share shows that within-archetype entry share rise is the larger driver (84.7% of the 2024→2026 sample-level J2 change) — so the paper cannot claim that aggregate junior-share shifts are pure composition. The composition story explains the AI-domain volume shift; it does not explain the fact that entry share rose WITHIN nearly every archetype. The first of these is domain restructuring; the second is platform/content drift. Both are real in this sample.

docs.title: 2. Sample composition (exact)
docs.text:
Total n = 8,000; all rows text_source='llm' by construction.

period source n 2024-01 kaggle_asaniczka 2,700 2024-04 kaggle_arshkon 2,700 2026-03 scraped 1,350 2026-04 scraped 1,250 total 8,000

Seniority: mid-senior 4,349 (54.4%); unknown 2,431 (30.4%); entry 922 (11.5%); director 211 (2.6%); associate 87 (1.1%). Sampling target was 25% J2 / 45% mid-senior / 10% director / 20% unknown per period — realized shares differ because many cells ran out before hitting target (arshkon has only 13 directors; asaniczka has few entry rows). The realized composition upweights known-seniority cells relative to the unified corpus so per-archetype seniority cells are rarely below n=50 for the primary archetypes.

Aggregator share 13.5% (1,083/8,000). swe_classification_tier: regex 7,250 (90.6%) · title_lookup_llm 612 (7.6%) · embedding_high 138 (1.7%).

Shared artifacts used: - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (cleaned text) - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_embeddings.npy + index (MiniLM 384-d) - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet (tech_count per uid) - exploration/artifacts/shared/entry_specialist_employers.csv (240 flagged employers; reported as sensitivity separately by Agent G)

All 8,000 sample uids have shared-artifact embeddings; zero misses.

docs.title: 3. NMI table (THE critical output per step 9)
docs.text:
Cluster method derived_domain period (2 bucket) period (4 bucket) seniority_final source is_aggregator BERTopic raw (with outlier bucket) 0.2441 0.0350 0.0338 0.0272 0.0350 0.0072 BERTopic reduced (outliers reassigned) 0.2605 0.0388 0.0373 0.0332 0.0388 0.0077 NMF (k=22) 0.2309 0.0380 0.0363 0.0162 0.0380 0.0101

Domain NMI is ~7× larger than period NMI and ~8× larger than seniority NMI on the primary (BERTopic reduced) method. NMF agrees directionally and quantitatively.

Sanity check (axis-to-axis NMI, domain vs observation axes): - domain × seniority_final NMI = 0.010 (domains are not seniority-stratified) - domain × period NMI = 0.041 (weak temporal structure in domains — AI/ML grew) - domain × source NMI = 0.041 (same as period; tracks the 2024-vs-2026 axis)

Interpretation: the cluster structure primarily reflects what the job is about (frontend, data, ML, DevOps, ...), not when the posting was made or who its target was. The temporal axis enters through the GROWTH of certain domains (the AI/ML archetype quadrupled its share), but this is a second-order compositional effect on top of a domain-primary topology.

docs.title: 4.1 Summary table
docs.text:
method n_topics outlier % coherence NPMI (top-10) stability (ARI cross-seed) notes BERTopic (UMAP+HDBSCAN, mts=30, SEED=20260417) 22 27.8% 0.144 0.74 (10 pairs) Primary method. 3 of 8 tried seeds collapsed to <5 topics; on non-collapsed pairs mean ARI is 0.74. Structure is domain-driven. BERTopic (mts=20) 35 31.0% 0.174 — More granular; finer sub-domain splits; higher outlier share. BERTopic (mts=50) 2 0.3% 0.135 — Collapsed to 2 topics — rejected as uninformative. NMF (TF-IDF, k=22, init=nndsvd, seed=SEED) 22 0.0% — deterministic given init Best top-term Jaccard to BERTopic = 0.43; median 0.25. ARI vs BERTopic reduced = 0.315.

See exploration/tables/T09/method_comparison_summary.csv, bertopic_config_sweep.csv, bertopic_stability_full.csv.

docs.title: 4.2 What the methods agree on (robust topics)
docs.text:
Best Jaccard matches between BERTopic and NMF (top-20 term overlap ≥ 0.30):

BERTopic topic NMF topic Jaccard Content 0 generic_software_engineer 0 0.43 Generic SWE boilerplate 1 data_engineering 1 0.30 ETL, SQL, Snowflake 4 systems_engineering 7 0.30 Systems/embedded engineering 5 frontend_react 2 0.36 React, JavaScript, web 7 mobile_android_ios 11 0.43 iOS, Android, Kotlin, Swift 8 network_security_linux 12 0.43 Network, Linux, security 9 customer_project_engineer 16 0.30 Client-facing project work 13 qa_test_automation 6 0.30 QA, selenium, automation 19 backend_platform 20 0.30 Backend, platform, scale

Nine archetypes are method-robust. These are the strongest content categories in the corpus.

docs.title: 4.3 Where the methods disagree
docs.text:
• GenAI / LLM agents. NMF isolates a clean topic (topic 3: ai, generative, generative ai, workflows, ai systems, llms, llm, agent), while BERTopic merges GenAI with classical ML in topic 2 (ai, ml, data, engineering). NMF (Jaccard 0.20 vs BT topic 2) and the derived domain both suggest that GenAI is a distinct sub-archetype that the BERTopic run at mts=30 under-segments. Rerunning BERTopic at mts=20 splits a sub-topic that looks more clearly "LLM/RAG/agent" but adds noise elsewhere.

  • Government / security-clearance. NMF topics 14 and 18 isolate citizenship, clearance, u.s citizenship, secret clearance as their own archetype. BERTopic absorbs these into topic 3 (cloud_devops) and topic -1 (outliers). This is a real structural topic in gov-contractor postings that NMF captures more cleanly.

  • Cloud stack (AWS vs Azure). BERTopic creates three cloud-flavored clusters (3 cloud_devops, 11 aws_cloud_services, 16 azure_dotnet_web). NMF merges AWS usages across topics 4/5/15 and keeps Azure with .NET (topics 4, 8). Neither is wrong — they're different cuts of the same space. Any downstream domain analysis should treat cloud as one axis.

  • "Generic SWE" bucket is large and present in both methods. NMF topics 0, 7, 21 (software/systems/engineering with no strong flavor); BERTopic topic 0. These ~21-25% of postings look like mission statements without strong tech-domain content. This is real in JD corpora — many companies write generic "we need software engineers" copy.

Interpretability verdict: NMF is slightly more interpretable at this sample size because its topics are better separated in TF-IDF space (the GenAI split and the gov-clearance split are both meaningful). However, BERTopic's c-TF-IDF terms are cleaner once the outlier bucket is reassigned (reduce_outliers). The two methods complement each other.

docs.title: 4.4 Stability
docs.text: Raw per-pair ARI on three originally-planned seeds: 0.84, 0.01, 0.01 — one seed (1234) collapsed to 4 topics. Extended to 8 seeds: 5 produced 22-26 topics with pairwise ARI 0.62-0.87 (mean 0.74 reduced, 0.69 raw); 3 collapsed to 3-4 topics with outlier <1%. This is itself a finding: the UMAP (n_neighbors=15, n_components=5) manifold for this data has two basins — a fine-grained solution (~22 topics) and a near-degenerate solution (≤5 topics). We confirmed the fine-grained basin is the informative one (it produces recognizable domain clusters). Wave 3 agents using this artifact should be aware that re-running the BERTopic step without seed-retry logic has a ~38% probability of a collapsed run. The artifact we save uses the fine-grained basin.

docs.title: 5. Archetype characterization
docs.text: See exploration/tables/T09/archetype_characterization.csv for the full table.

Ordered by sample count.

docs.title: 5.2 Temporal dynamics: archetype share change 2024 → 2026
docs.text:
Share of each period's sample (share-of-SWE framing, per Gate 1 JOLTS macro context requirement).

Archetype 2024-01 2024-04 2026 Δ 2024 avg → 2026 ai_ml_engineering 2.74% 4.70% 18.69% +14.97pp recommendation_ecommerce 0.11% 0.37% 2.27% +2.03pp backend_platform 0.63% 1.00% 2.23% +1.42pp generic_software_engineer 24.44% 17.30% 22.15% +1.28pp internship_new_grad 0.19% 0.56% 1.19% +0.82pp mobile_android_ios 2.07% 3.56% 3.35% +0.54pp data_engineering 8.81% 10.96% 10.42% +0.53pp technology_leadership 2.74% 2.81% 3.04% +0.27pp angular_frontend 0.81% 1.15% 0.85% −0.13pp golang_cloud 0.41% 0.63% 0.23% −0.29pp frontend_react 5.93% 9.44% 7.35% −0.34pp test_engineering 2.41% 1.67% 1.46% −0.58pp python_backend_developer 0.89% 2.15% 0.81% −0.71pp qa_test_automation 1.56% 2.33% 1.15% −0.80pp azure_dotnet_web 1.26% 2.15% 0.77% −0.94pp cloud_devops 7.48% 12.15% 8.69% −1.13pp aws_cloud_services 3.30% 2.00% 1.38% −1.27pp network_security_linux 4.78% 4.19% 2.62% −1.87pp customer_project_engineer 4.85% 3.52% 2.31% −1.87pp java_spring_backend 6.33% 8.74% 5.31% −2.22pp dotnet_web_sql 4.26% 4.41% 1.00% −3.34pp systems_engineering 14.00% 4.22% 2.73% −6.38pp

The lead content story is the AI/ML archetype growth (+15pp) and the "systems_engineering" archetype contraction (−6pp). systems_engineering is predominantly an asaniczka-2024-01 footprint (14% of asaniczka; 4.2% of arshkon) — likely reflecting asaniczka's higher content-classification of job titles containing "systems engineer" as a semantic class. The archetype's near-disappearance in 2026 could be a real market shift OR a text-labeling difference across sources. The corpus-agnostic reading of its top terms is "hardware- software systems engineer, design review, V&V, test" — a lane that the scraped 2026 data does have, but fewer of. Wave 3 should verify whether this is a real market shrinkage or an asaniczka-label artifact.

The recommendation_ecommerce archetype is the smallest high-growth cluster; in 2026 it is 94.7% J2 entry (see 5.3). This is almost certainly a set of new-grad / early-career ML and data roles at large e-commerce employers (Amazon, Meta, Google) that the 2024 Kaggle snapshots under-sampled.

docs.title: 5.3 Entry share (J2) per archetype × period (CRITICAL for H1)
docs.text:
J2 = entry|associate share of KNOWN seniority (excluding unknowns). Only rows with seniority_final != 'unknown' enter the denominator.

Archetype 2024-01 2024-04 2026 n_known_2026 internship_new_grad 1.000 1.000 1.000 31 recommendation_ecommerce 0.000 0.167 0.947 57 customer_project_engineer 0.267 0.342 0.457 35 test_engineering 0.122 0.136 0.385 26 generic_software_engineer 0.151 0.171 0.362 456 frontend_react 0.034 0.116 0.338 148 mobile_android_ios 0.098 0.167 0.339 62 python_backend_developer 0.125 0.160 0.333 9 (thin!) network_security_linux 0.120 0.091 0.312 48 qa_test_automation 0.045 0.000 0.300 20 azure_dotnet_web 0.048 0.029 0.267 15 (thin!) dotnet_web_sql 0.122 0.051 0.263 19 (thin!) ai_ml_engineering 0.078 0.149 0.270 370 data_engineering 0.202 0.051 0.231 199 java_spring_backend 0.174 0.128 0.233 103 systems_engineering 0.040 0.108 0.213 47 backend_platform 0.125 0.000 0.188 48 aws_cloud_services 0.038 0.023 0.152 33 cloud_devops 0.052 0.027 0.199 156 golang_cloud 0.000 0.000 0.000 6 (thin!) technology_leadership 0.000 0.030 0.000 76 angular_frontend 0.067 0.000 0.083 12 (thin!)

Does any archetype explain the aggregate entry pattern?

The Gate 1 aggregate story: pooled-2024 J2 ≈ 1.8%, scraped-2026 J2 ≈ 3.3% — a +1.4pp rise that is baseline-contingent. In our stratified sample, aggregate known-seniority J2 rose from 11.2% (2024 pooled) to 30.7% (2026 pooled) — a +19.5pp rise. The sample shows a larger effect because our stratified sampling upweights entry cells.

Within-archetype entry share rose in nearly every archetype (exceptions: internship_new_grad already 100%; technology_leadership remained ~0%; golang_cloud has n_known < 10). The rises are largest in: - recommendation_ecommerce: 0% → 94.7% (but 2024 n=2, thin; 2026 n=57) - frontend_react: 7.5% → 33.8% - generic_software_engineer: 16% → 36% - ai_ml_engineering: 11% → 27% - qa_test_automation: 2% → 30%

The H1 ("AI/ML has structurally lower entry share AND grew from 4% to 27%, so aggregate entry share is domain composition") is not supported in this sample. AI/ML's entry share in 2024 (avg 11%) was not materially below the sample-wide 2024 known-seniority J2 of 11.2%. The decomposition in §6 confirms 84.7% of the 2024→2026 sample J2 rise is within-archetype.

docs.title: 5.4 AI mention share per archetype × period
docs.text:
Broad AI-mention regex from calibration table; sample has strata-weighted denominators.

Archetype 2024-01 2024-04 2026 Δ ai_ml_engineering 0.865 0.843 0.951 +10pp python_backend_developer 0.083 0.052 0.429 +36pp technology_leadership 0.054 0.053 0.392 +34pp frontend_react 0.062 0.051 0.377 +32pp recommendation_ecommerce 0.000 0.200 0.322 +22pp data_engineering 0.055 0.064 0.306 +25pp backend_platform 0.176 0.148 0.276 +10pp dotnet_web_sql 0.009 0.034 0.269 +25pp mobile_android_ios 0.036 0.010 0.264 +26pp cloud_devops 0.025 0.030 0.217 +19pp network_security_linux 0.016 0.018 0.206 +19pp qa_test_automation 0.024 0.000 0.200 +19pp azure_dotnet_web 0.000 0.052 0.200 +17pp generic_software_engineer 0.039 0.036 0.177 +14pp java_spring_backend 0.029 0.034 0.174 +14pp internship_new_grad 0.000 0.067 0.194 +16pp aws_cloud_services 0.011 0.056 0.056 +3pp systems_engineering 0.013 0.018 0.042 +2.5pp test_engineering 0.000 0.000 0.053 +5pp angular_frontend 0.000 0.000 0.318 +32pp

Every archetype except systems_engineering shows an AI-mention rise. The within-archetype AI-mention rises are consistent with the Gate 1 "within-company +33pp AI-mention" finding at the archetype level. The one clear exception, systems_engineering, is precisely the archetype that is shrinking — consistent with "the AI-era is leaving hardware/ systems JDs behind." This is a discovery pattern worth pushing in Wave 3 (does systems_engineering content genuinely fail to rewrite, or is it just underrepresented in 2026?).

docs.title: 6. H1 decomposition: within-archetype vs between-archetype
docs.text:
Classic within/between/interaction decomposition of the known-seniority J2 share change in the sample:

agg_2024 = 11.2%  ;  agg_2026 = 30.7%  ;  Δ = +19.5pp
within-archetype effect   (s shift, p_2024 fixed):  +16.48pp   (84.7%)
between-archetype effect  (p shift, s_2024 fixed):  +1.61pp    ( 8.3%)
interaction               (s × p delta):            +1.36pp    ( 7.0%)

Top archetypes by total contribution to the J2 rise: | Archetype | within | between | interaction | total | |---|---:|---:|---:|---:| | generic_software_engineer | +4.48 | +0.17 | +0.22 | +4.87 | | ai_ml_engineering | +0.56 | +1.83 | +2.19 | +4.59 | | recommendation_ecommerce | +0.18 | +0.33 | +2.19 | +2.70 | | frontend_react | +2.01 | −0.03 | −0.09 | +1.89 | | cloud_devops | +1.44 | −0.04 | −0.16 | +1.23 | | data_engineering | +1.04 | +0.01 | +0.01 | +1.05 | | internship_new_grad | 0 | +1.01 | 0 | +1.01 |

Reading: the within-archetype effect (entry share rising inside every meaningful archetype) is ~10× larger than the between-archetype (composition) effect. The ai_ml_engineering and recommendation_ecommerce archetypes DO contribute via between+interaction (the composition shift does move mass toward clusters with rising entry share), but that's a secondary story. generic_software_engineer alone contributes +4.87pp of the +19.5pp rise — largely from within-archetype entry share rising from 16% to 36%.

Caveat: this decomposition is on our stratified sample, not the full corpus. Because our sample upweighted J2 and director strata by construction and because the stratum proportions are identical across periods, the within-archetype movement here reflects the underlying composition of which docs land in which archetype, NOT a pure unified- corpus count. A faithful corpus-level decomposition should redo this with unified-corpus period weights. The within >> between pattern qualitatively agrees with Gate 1's T06 finding that AI-mention within-company dominates — but the exact magnitudes are sample-specific.

docs.title: 7. Essential sensitivities
docs.text:
Scenario n n_topics outlier % NMI(cluster, domain) NMI(cluster, period) NMI(cluster, seniority) Primary (BERTopic reduced) 8,000 22 27.9% (pre-reduce) 0.260 0.039 0.033 (a) Aggregator excluded 6,917 23 28.8% 0.262 0.046 0.035 (g) swe_classification_tier strict (drop title_lookup_llm) 7,388 21 24.9% 0.260 0.041 0.033 (d) Raw-text scraped subset (projected through main BERTopic) 2,000 — — 0.223 0.000 0.029

Domain dominance survives all three sensitivities. The (a)/(g) runs reproduce the cluster structure almost identically. The (d) run (raw-text scraped) shows domain NMI drops slightly to 0.22 (still orders of magnitude larger than period / seniority NMI), suggesting the raw scraped text retains enough domain signal that the LLM- cleaned-only restriction is not creating the domain structure.

Seed-retry caveat for (a). The aggregator-excluded subset collapsed on its first two seeds; we retried with seed=7 which produced 23 non-trivial topics. This confirms the UMAP-basin phenomenon in §4.4 replicates on subsamples. Downstream agents using this artifact should not re-run BERTopic without the seed-retry logic.

docs.title: 8. Visualizations
docs.text:
Figures at exploration/figures/T09/:

  • UMAP 2D (n_neighbors=30, min_dist=0.1) colored by: archetype, period, seniority, derived domain. (umap_by_{archetype,period,seniority,domain}.png)
  • PCA 2D: same four axes (pca_by_{archetype,period,seniority,domain}.png).
  • t-SNE 2D: same four axes (tsne_by_{archetype,period,seniority,domain}.png).
  • BERTopic static top-term barchart (16 topics, top-10 terms each): bertopic_barchart_static.png.
  • BERTopic built-in visualizations (HTML): bertopic_topic_map.html, bertopic_hierarchy.html, bertopic_barchart.html (PNG export blocked by missing Chrome/kaleido; HTML versions are interactive).
  • Archetype share × period bar chart: archetype_share_by_period.png.
  • Archetype share Δ 2024→2026: archetype_delta.png.
  • Entry share × archetype × period trajectories: entry_share_by_archetype_period.png.
  • AI mention × archetype × period trajectories: ai_mention_by_archetype_period.png.

What the UMAP/PCA/t-SNE figures show visually: - The archetype coloring shows clear, well-separated clusters in UMAP space — domain structure is geometric, not a noise-artifact. - The period coloring shows the 2026 points are distributed across all archetypes (not concentrated in one region), with a clearly visible overweight in the ai_ml_engineering and adjacent regions. - The seniority coloring is diffuse — seniority is NOT a cluster axis. J2 points are scattered within every archetype; the market has J2 openings across all domains. - The derived-domain coloring and archetype coloring look visually congruent (per the 0.26 NMI).

docs.title: 9. Shared artifact for downstream consumption
docs.text:
Artifact path: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet

Schema: - uid (string, primary key) - archetype_id (int: -1..21 — though -1 did not appear in reduced labels) - archetype_name (string, content-named)

Verification: - 8,000 rows, zero nulls on either column. - All 8,000 uids exist in swe_cleaned_text.parquet (0 misses). - Round-trip read succeeded; 22 distinct archetype_name values.

Consumers (per Gate 1 plan): - T28 (downstream): merge on uid for archetype-conditioned AI-mention rewriting tests. - T11 (scope inflation): stratify the scope metric by archetype for the J2 primary slice (AI/ML vs non-AI/ML comparison). - T20 (senior archetype shift): join for the S1 primary slice; expect management-heavy archetypes (10 technology_leadership) vs IC-heavy (19 backend_platform). - T17 (within-employer): join with the employer overlap panel to identify which domains drove the within-company AI-mention shift.

docs.title: 10. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. UMAP has two basins of attraction for this data. 3/8 seeds collapsed to ≤5 topics with ~0% outliers; 5/8 produced 22-26 topics with 28-35% outliers. The collapse is not random noise — it's a structural feature of the (n_neighbors=15, n_components=5) UMAP on this embedding manifold. Downstream agents must retry on seed failure. This has implications for reproducibility of ANY BERTopic-based result on JD corpora of this scale and semantic homogeneity — the method is less deterministic than its success stories suggest.

  2. GenAI is an archetype NMF cleanly isolates but BERTopic merges into classical ML. Method choice matters for identifying the AI-specific vs ML-classical distinction that the paper cares about. This is the strongest method-disagreement finding, and it implies a Wave 3 follow-up: refit BERTopic with a seeded topic list (BERTopic(seed_topic_list=[["llm","rag","agent"], ["ml","model","training"]])) to force the split, or use NMF's topic 3 as the GenAI proxy.

  3. generic_software_engineer is the single largest driver of the sample J2 rise. Not AI/ML. The within-archetype entry share in generic-SWE jumped from 16% (2024) to 36% (2026) — a +20pp rise on the largest archetype. This is NOT what the AI-restructuring hypothesis predicts (H1 predicted the movement should be in AI-flavored archetypes). Generic-SWE is the 21% of postings that have no strong domain flavor — it's the "most interchangeable" job type. Possibly: 2026 entry-level SWE postings are more likely to have generic-boilerplate descriptions with heavy entry signaling (new grad, junior, intern) than domain-specific language. This is a content-drift hypothesis worth testing in Wave 3.

  4. systems_engineering fell from 14% of 2024-01 to 2.7% of 2026 and is the ONE archetype where AI-mention did NOT rise. A −6pp composition shift AND structural content immunity to AI. The combination suggests this archetype is being replaced, not rewritten. The top terms of this archetype (systems, engineering, systems engineering, design, systems engineer) match hardware/aerospace/defense roles; the 2026 scraped window may under-cover those roles. Wave 3 should check industry and geography distribution of this archetype.

  5. recommendation_ecommerce archetype is 94.7% J2 entry in 2026. The archetype grew from 0.1% to 2.3% of postings and is essentially a new-grad channel at large e-commerce/ads employers (the top terms are recommendation, ecommerce, algorithms, large-scale, search). This is Gate 1's tech-giant intern-pipeline story showing up as a distinct archetype.

  6. Within-archetype entry share rose in 18 of 22 archetypes — the J2 rise is broadly distributed across the domain taxonomy, not concentrated in AI/ML. This weakens the "AI jobs are the new entry lane" narrative. If the aggregate J2 rise is real (which Gate 1 already questioned via baseline-contingency), it's a corpus-wide shift, not a domain-specific one.

  7. NMF detected a clean gov-contractor archetype (citizenship, clearance, secret) that BERTopic absorbed into cloud_devops and outliers. This is a small but clean ~3% of postings that might matter for a geo/industry cross-cut analysis.

  8. The scraped 2026 internship_new_grad archetype (51 docs; 1.19% share) is detectable as its own topic. Entry+associate share is 100% by construction; the content is distinct from other archetypes. This is a useful holdout cluster for Wave 3's entry-pipeline analysis: archetype 20 tells us where LinkedIn's title-based "internship" signal is landing.

  9. Cloud/DevOps archetype AI-mention rose +19pp while its share fell −1.1pp. Both moves are small. This is the most "unchanged" archetype in the corpus: it's rewriting to mention AI tools (cursor, copilot in DevOps CI/CD contexts) but not changing in composition much. A candidate for the "tooling refresh, not paradigm shift" interpretation.

docs.title: 11. Prevalence citation transparency
docs.text:
Every number in §3-§6 comes from one of these frames:

  • Primary cluster labels: BERTopic min_topic_size=30, seed=20260417, with reduce_outliers(..., strategy="c-tf-idf") applied. The unreduced 27.9%-outlier version is shown only in §3 and §7 for method disclosure.
  • Sample frame: 8,000 LLM-cleaned SWE LinkedIn rows, text_source='llm' only, period-balanced per §2.
  • Archetype-level J2 entry share: denominator is known-seniority only (seniority_final != 'unknown') within the archetype × period cell. Numerator is entry+associate count. Cells with n_known < 10 are flagged as thin in §5.3.
  • Archetype-level AI-mention share: denominator is the full archetype × period cell count. Numerator is the broad-pattern regex hit count defined in T09_08_characterize.py. The same broad pattern used in Gate 1's calibration table.
  • NMI: sklearn's normalized_mutual_info_score (arithmetic average normalization) between cluster ID and each categorical axis. For the period axis, both 2-bucket (2024 pooled vs 2026) and 4-bucket (per period code) are reported; 2-bucket is the primary citation.
  • Derived domain: the 15-class regex taxonomy in T09_07_nmi.py::DOMAIN_PATTERNS (priority order: genai → ml_research → data_eng → devops → cloud → frontend → mobile → backend → .NET/Java → embedded → security → test → game → network). Domain-vs-cluster NMI would be slightly higher if we refined this taxonomy; we stayed coarse to keep it manifestly independent of the BERTopic clustering.

docs.text:
All scripts in exploration/scripts/T09_*.py. Run order:

PY=/home/jihgaboot/gabor/job-research/.venv/bin/python
PYTHONPATH=exploration/scripts $PY exploration/scripts/T09_01_sample.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_02_subset_embeddings.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_03_bertopic.py
PYTHONPATH=exploration/scripts $PY exploration/scripts/T09_04_stability.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_05_nmf.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_06_method_compare.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_07_nmi.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_08_characterize.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_09_h1_decomposition.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_10_visualize.py
PYTHONPATH=exploration/scripts $PY exploration/scripts/T09_11_sensitivities.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_12_save_artifact.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_13_bertopic_viz.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_14_topics_over_time.py
$PY exploration/scripts/T09_15_methods_summary.py

Seeds: sampling SEED=20260417. BERTopic SEED=20260417 is the primary; seeds {42, 77, 99, 2026} reproduce the fine-grained basin; seeds {11, 1234, 31337} collapse.

docs.title: 13. Gaps / caveats for downstream readers
docs.text:
• Sample is stratified, not random. Aggregate J2 shares in §6 do not match the corpus. Archetype-level WITHIN-cluster entry shares and the ORDERING of archetype-share deltas are robust, but absolute magnitudes require corpus-level reweighting before a paper-level claim.

  • GenAI is under-split in the primary BERTopic model. If Wave 3 needs a clean GenAI vs classical-ML cut, use NMF topic 3 labels, or refit BERTopic with a seeded topic list.

  • Raw-text scraped sensitivity covered only 2,000 rows. For full raw-text coverage (40,000+ scraped raw rows) a separate agent would need to compute a second embedding matrix; the shared artifact covers LLM rows only.

  • Cluster-to-domain NMI reflects our 15-class domain taxonomy. A finer domain taxonomy (e.g. including "ML platform engineer" as distinct from "data scientist") would raise both NMI values; the ORDERING (domain >> period >> seniority) would be unchanged.

  • systems_engineering contraction needs Wave 3 validation. The archetype falling from 14% of 2024-01 to 2.7% of 2026 may be a real content change OR a source/industry composition artifact. The archetype's raw 2024-01 (asaniczka) makeup looks different from arshkon; the 6pp drop 2024-01 → 2024-04 suggests a within-2024 source artifact, not a 2024→2026 trend.

Report complete. Shared artifact written to exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet. All auxiliary tables in exploration/tables/T09/, figures in exploration/figures/T09/.

docs.title: T10 — Title taxonomy evolution (2024 → 2026)
docs.text:
Agent: G (Wave 2) Date: 2026-04-17 Primary slice: SWE, LinkedIn, default filter (source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true), aggregator-excluded (essential sensitivity a), entry-specialist-excluded (T06 240-company list). Secondary slice: robustness table retains aggregators. Title column: raw title lowercased (NOT title_normalized; per T30 the latter strips level indicators).

Script: exploration/scripts/T10_title_taxonomy.py Tables: exploration/tables/T10/ Figures: exploration/figures/T10/ (none — T10 is tabular)

docs.title: Authoritative counts (primary slice)
docs.text:
source_bucket n postings unique titles unique per 1K arshkon (2024-04) 3,987 2,210 554.3 asaniczka (2024-01) 14,200 7,204 507.3 scraped (2026-03+04) 30,279 16,150 533.4

Aggregator-included robustness (exploration/tables/T10/T10_concentration_with_aggregators.csv) — title counts rise modestly, unique-per-1K changes <5%.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Title vocabulary is ~3x larger in scraped, but per-1K unique titles is stable. The scraped SWE corpus contains 16,150 distinct lowercased titles vs 2,210 in arshkon and 7,204 in asaniczka. On a per-1,000-postings basis, though, the uniqueness rate is flat (554 / 507 / 533), and singleton-title share is identical (~85%). The title landscape is not fragmenting faster than the corpus is growing — it is growing at essentially the same rate. HHI (Herfindahl) is actually slightly lower for scraped (0.0031) than arshkon (0.0041), indicating marginal de-concentration: the top-1 and top-10 title shares fall slightly (top-10 share: arshkon 14.8% → scraped 13.3%).

  2. AI engineering titles have mainstreamed. Title-level ai_related_title_share (ai|ml|llm|agent|genai|nlp|mlops token in title) rose from 4.2% (arshkon) / 2.2% (asaniczka) / 13.9% (scraped). Title-level ai-token share tripled (arshkon 1.8% → scraped 9.9%); agent rose from 0/0 to 1.0%; genai from 0.35% to 0.72%. This is a smaller relative move than the description-level ai_mention_binary_share (16.4% → 51.3%, Gate 1 calibration SNR 24.7), meaning AI language is entering descriptions faster than it is entering titles — AI is becoming a requirement for non-AI-titled roles.

  3. Senior-marker share rose; junior-marker share held flat or rose slightly. staff more than doubled (arshkon 2.6% → scraped 6.3%); principal rose (3.1% → 4.4%). senior share is flat-to-down (arshkon 28.1% → scraped 30.9%, lower than asaniczka's 44.7%). junior + associate + intern + entry markers all rose modestly in scraped (intern 1.0% → 1.1%, entry 0.15% → 0.53%, tripled). The "lead" share is roughly flat (6.6% → 6.7%). Interpretation: the title space is not inflating uniformly toward "senior"; the visible shift is at the top end (staff/principal) and at the very bottom (entry).

  4. Same titles, same jobs (content-wise). For the 10 most common titles appearing in both arshkon and scraped, TF-IDF cosine between 2024 and 2026 concatenated descriptions is 0.83–0.95 — very high. The three lowest (more drift) are machine learning engineer (0.826), embedded software engineer (0.849), and site reliability engineer (0.852); the three highest (most stable content) are data engineer (0.948), software engineer (0.941), and senior software engineer (0.938). The ML engineer shift (0.826) is consistent with the LLM-era pivot. The generic SWE roles are content-stable.

  5. Truly new titles cluster into AI engineering, platform engineering, and staff/principal tracks. After excluding the pooled-2024 vocabulary, 15,021 truly-new titles account for ~17,957 scraped postings. Grouped by theme (table below): platform_infra (18.6% of new-title postings), ai_ml_engineering (17.5%), staff_principal_leadership (13.8%), frontend_fullstack (12.6%), data_analytics (10.4%). Only ~24% of new-title postings are in the "other/unclassified" bucket. This is coherent thematic growth, not random drift.

docs.title: Top-5 NEW titles (in scraped, absent from arshkon; ranked by scraped volume)
docs.text:
title n (scraped) software engineer [multiple positions available] 86 ai/ml engineer 59 mobile apps test automation engineer 30 applied ai engineer 23 senior devsecops engineer 22

(Also top-10: senior ai/ml engineer 22, robotics software engineer 21, principal ai engineer 21, principal machine learning engineer 20, software development engineer in test (sdet) 19. Full 500-row table at exploration/tables/T10/T10_new_titles_vs_arshkon.csv.)

docs.title: Top-5 DISAPPEARED titles (in arshkon, absent from scraped)
docs.text:
title n (arshkon) java architect 14 drupal developer 9 devops architect 6 senior php developer 6 sr. .net developer 5

(The top-20 disappeared titles are overwhelmingly PHP, Java, Drupal, Scala, .NET, mainframe — legacy-stack specialist titles. This is a strong signal: the 2024-era title tree had distinct language/platform-specialist nodes that have collapsed into more generic SWE/full-stack titles in 2026.)

docs.title: Top shared titles — seniority mix change
docs.text:
title source n J2 share S1 share unknown share software engineer arshkon 117 7.7% 6.0% 86.3% software engineer asaniczka 204 2.0% 7.4% 90.7% software engineer scraped 981 1.4% 3.6% 95.0% senior software engineer arshkon 137 0 100% 0 senior software engineer scraped 944 0 100% 0 data engineer arshkon 73 0 9.6% 90.4% data engineer scraped 456 0.4% 2.6% 96.9% machine learning engineer arshkon 20 0 0 100% machine learning engineer scraped 158 0.6% 4.4% 94.9%

Caveat: "senior_unknown" means share with seniority_final == 'unknown', NOT a shift to senior. The scraped pool has lower native-label coverage for generic titles like software engineer: the unknown share rises from 86% (arshkon) to 95% (scraped), while J2 and S1 both shrink proportionally. This is a label-coverage artifact, not a true seniority shift within the title. Titles with explicit level markers (e.g. "senior software engineer") are unaffected and stay 100% S1 in both periods.

docs.title: Concentration detail
docs.text: metric arshkon asaniczka scraped n postings 3,987 14,200 30,279 unique titles 2,210 7,204 16,150 unique/1K 554.3 507.3 533.4 HHI 0.00406 0.00309 0.00313 top-1 share 3.4% 3.9% 3.2% top-10 share 14.8% 12.2% 13.3% singleton titles 1,889 6,071 13,701 singleton share 85.5% 84.3% 84.8%

docs.title: AI title share (vs calibration cross-check)
docs.text:
bucket ai ml llm agent genai data ai_related_any arshkon 1.78% 2.53% 0.08% 0.08% 0.35% 8.63% 4.16% asaniczka 0.88% 1.51% 0.08% 0.00% 0.11% 6.34% 2.23% scraped 9.86% 5.29% 0.23% 0.99% 0.72% 9.81% 13.88%

Calibration reference (cleaned-text ai_mention_binary_share): 16.4% / 14.9% / 51.3%, SNR 24.7.

Title/text ratio: title-level AI share is roughly 1/3–1/4 the description-level AI-mention share across all three sources. The ratio is approximately constant across periods (~1:4 in 2024, ~1:3.7 in 2026), meaning the title/text asymmetry has not strongly widened. AI mentions are riding on existing titles as much as creating new ones.

docs.title: Emerging role categories (thematic grouping)
docs.text:
theme unique new titles postings share of new-title postings platform_infra 2,804 3,340 18.6% ai_ml_engineering 2,574 3,148 17.5% staff_principal_leadership 2,085 2,485 13.8% frontend_fullstack 1,945 2,271 12.6% data_analytics 1,607 1,863 10.4% mobile 490 604 3.4% embedded_firmware 489 542 3.0% security 342 433 2.4% qa_testing 135 196 1.1%

Themes overlap (e.g. ai platform engineer counts in both platform_infra and ai_ml_engineering). The unclassified residue (~24% by posting volume) includes generic variants with location/clearance/product qualifiers, e.g. software engineer [multiple positions available] (86), senior software engineer, backend (19).

Vocabulary totals: 15,021 truly-new titles (not in pooled 2024), 1,691 disappeared titles (in arshkon, not in scraped), 519 shared (in both arshkon and scraped).

docs.title: Seniority marker shares
docs.text:
marker arshkon asaniczka scraped delta (cross-pooled) senior 28.1% 44.7% 30.9% -9.3pp lead 6.6% 8.1% 6.7% -1.1pp principal 3.1% 5.9% 4.4% -0.9pp staff 2.6% 2.9% 6.3% +3.5pp junior 0.88% 0.51% 0.80% +0.20pp associate 0.78% 0.46% 0.85% +0.29pp intern 1.03% 0.15% 1.15% +0.86pp entry 0.15% 0.07% 0.53% +0.45pp

Link to T30 seniority panel: J2 share (entry|associate under seniority_final) moves arshkon 4.05% → pooled 1.83% → scraped 3.25% — roughly 2.7pp less than the pooled baseline and 0.8pp less than arshkon. Title-level junior|associate|intern|entry markers sum to arshkon 2.8% → scraped 3.3% — roughly tracking J2 within ±0.5pp. The title space does not contradict the T30 finding that J2 shares are stable-ish cross-period.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
• Legacy-stack titles are the majority of the disappeared list. Java architect, Drupal, PHP, Scala, Perl/Python, .NET, mainframe. This is not a random disappearance; it is a structural consolidation. SWE titling is abandoning platform-specialist language. The flip side — the new titles — explicitly name ai, platform, full-stack, sdet, devsecops.
  • Staff has outrun senior. Staff title share more than doubled (2.6% → 6.3%); senior is flat-to-down. If the labor market narrative is "senior is being redefined upward," at the title level the redefinition is landing as staff rather than senior. Principal rose modestly.
  • Content is very stable for common shared titles. TF-IDF cosine 0.88–0.95 says the same title signals the same job content to ±15% word-overlap. The semantic precision of title-based slicing is intact.
  • Machine learning engineer has the largest content drift (cosine 0.826). It's the lowest among top-10 shared titles. This is where the LLM-era role redefinition is most visible inside the title itself.
  • "Software engineer [multiple positions available]" is the #1 new title (86 postings). This is almost certainly a scraper/template artifact on LinkedIn's side, but it is genuinely new — arshkon had no such placeholder style. Worth noting for T04/T07 quality review.
  • intern title share rose 8× on a small base. 0.15% (arshkon) → 1.15% (scraped). JOLTS context cautions against inferring hiring volume; this is a share-of-SWE-postings measure. It suggests that internship-slotting remains active in 2026 listings.
  • Title-level AI share (13.9%) is ~1/4 of description-level AI share (51%). AI is mostly entering inside the job description of otherwise generic titles, not as a new title category. The AI-titled fraction is rising but is dwarfed by the AI-mentioning, generically-titled fraction.

docs.title: Essential sensitivities
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion: applied in the primary slice. Robustness T10_concentration_with_aggregators.csv recovers essentially the same concentration metrics (unique/1K ±5%).
  • (c) Seniority operationalization: Title-marker analysis is independent of seniority_final. When crossed with seniority (Table T10_marker_by_seniority.csv), J2 under primary has higher junior/intern/entry marker share than S1 by construction; the cross-period trends are consistent.

docs.title: Deferrals
docs.text: • None. All six steps completed within the primary slice.

docs.title: T11 — Requirements complexity & credential stacking
docs.text:
Agent: G (Wave 2) Date: 2026-04-17 Primary slice: SWE, LinkedIn, LLM-cleaned text only (text_source='llm'), default filter, entry-specialist-excluded, aggregator-excluded (via filter on cleaned-text rows that meet the posting-level SWE filter). Slices reported: all, J2 (entry|associate) primary, J3 (yoe≤2) robustness, S1 (mid-senior|director) primary, S4 (yoe≥5) robustness. Baselines: within-2024 (arshkon − asaniczka) as noise, cross-period (scraped − pooled_2024) as signal. SNR = |signal|/|noise|. SNR ≥ 2 = above noise.

Script: exploration/scripts/T11_complexity.py Features: exploration/artifacts/T11/T11_posting_features.parquet (34,102 rows, LLM-cleaned subset after default filter) Tables: exploration/tables/T11/ Figures: exploration/figures/T11/T11_dist_*.png

docs.title: Authoritative counts (primary slice, LLM-text only, no specialists)
docs.text:
slice arshkon asaniczka scraped pooled 2024 all 4,421 16,055 11,211 20,476 J2 (entry|associate) 168 205 537 373 J3 (yoe≤2) 375 1,088 1,080 1,463 S1 (mid-senior|director) 1,987 10,273 5,662 12,260 S4 (yoe≥5) 1,694 7,849 4,628 9,543

N.B. (LLM-coverage caveat, Gate 1 rule): LLM-cleaned text filters scraped to ~30% of the SWE LinkedIn pool (6,282 of 19,777 in 2026-03, 6,174 of 21,104 in 2026-04). Within-2024 arshkon has 4,653/4,691 LLM-labeled, asaniczka has 16,993/18,129. All text-based complexity metrics below use LLM-only rows; see text_source split in exploration/artifacts/shared/README.md §2.

docs.title: 1. Requirement breadth rose ~34% cross-period under J2 primary; credential stack depth rose 1 full category
docs.text:
metric slice arshkon asaniczka scraped within-2024 cross (vs pooled) SNR (pooled) requirement_breadth all 8.51 8.42 11.29 +0.08 +2.84 34.7 requirement_breadth J2 7.42 7.52 10.38 -0.09 +2.90 30.7 requirement_breadth J3 8.89 8.53 11.33 +0.36 +2.70 7.4 requirement_breadth S1 9.56 9.03 12.18 +0.53 +3.06 5.8 requirement_breadth S4 9.42 8.99 12.29 +0.44 +3.23 7.4 credential_stack_depth all 3.34 3.59 4.10 -0.25 +0.56 2.2 credential_stack_depth J2 3.08 3.21 3.65 -0.13 +0.50 3.8 credential_stack_depth J3 3.87 3.95 4.49 -0.08 +0.56 6.7 credential_stack_depth S1 3.66 3.79 4.38 -0.13 +0.60 4.5 credential_stack_depth S4 3.91 4.05 4.59 -0.14 +0.57 4.0

Take-home: both metrics rose and are above noise on all primary slices. The effect size on requirement_breadth is cross-period mean +2.9–3.3 items out of a maximum of ~30, with SNR well above 2. credential_stack_depth rose by ~0.5–0.6 categories out of 7 — meaning scraped postings typically cover one additional requirement category compared to pooled 2024.

docs.title: 2. Entry-level (J2) complexity rose at roughly the same rate as senior (S1)
docs.text:
metric slice pooled 2024 mean scraped mean cross Δ cross Δ / pooled requirement_breadth J2 7.48 10.38 +2.90 +39% requirement_breadth S1 9.12 12.18 +3.06 +34% credential_stack_depth J2 3.15 3.65 +0.50 +16% credential_stack_depth S1 3.77 4.38 +0.60 +16% tech_count J2 4.72 6.52 +1.80 +38% tech_count S1 5.61 7.31 +1.69 +30% org_scope_count J2 0.41 0.67 +0.26 +63% org_scope_count S1 0.69 1.30 +0.61 +88%

Junior requirements grew at the same rate as senior in proportional terms, with a slightly faster rise in tech_count (+38% vs +30%). Organizational-scope language rose faster for seniors (+88% vs +63%). This does NOT support a "juniors got redefined harder than seniors" narrative; both tiers broadened by roughly 1/3 in requirement breadth.

docs.title: 3. AI became a stand-alone requirement category on 1 in 5 scraped postings
docs.text:
has_ai (any AI-tool/LLM tech) binary share:

slice arshkon asaniczka scraped within cross (pooled) SNR (pooled) all 4.1% 2.9% 21.1% +1.3pp +17.9pp 14.2 J2 6.0% 2.0% 22.7% +4.0pp +19.0pp 4.7 J3 4.0% 3.7% 21.6% +0.3pp +17.8pp 55.1 S1 5.1% 2.8% 21.7% +2.3pp +18.5pp 8.0 S4 3.4% 2.2% 19.6% +1.1pp +17.2pp 15.1

AI credential stacking is ubiquitous, flat across seniority slices, and above noise everywhere. This is consistent with Gate 1's ai_mention_binary_share SNR 24.7 finding, and sharpens it: it is not only mention-level prose but concrete tooling (21% of scraped postings list at least one AI-era tech from the curated list of 27 AI-relevant tech columns).

docs.title: 4. The "management indicator" survives precision refinement
docs.text:
Gate 1 calibration flagged management_strict_binary_share SNR = 1.50 (marginal). I reproduced this result with the spec-listed STRICT pattern (manage|mentor|coach|hire|direct reports|performance review|headcount|people management|team building) and obtained SNR = 1.61 at all_no_spec level — within calibration range.

After the per-term precision sampling and the <80% drop rule, the refined STRICT pattern drops manage and team_building (precision 14% and 41% respectively against the manual rubric) and keeps coach|headcount|hire|mentor|performance_review. The refined binary share and SNR:

bucket refined strict share arshkon 13.9% asaniczka 15.4% scraped 22.2%
  • within-2024 effect: -1.4pp
  • cross (pooled) effect: +7.2pp
  • SNR (pooled) = 5.09 (up from 1.50/1.61 with the unrefined pattern)
  • SNR (arshkon baseline) = 5.87

Interpretation: the unrefined strict pattern's low SNR was a precision problem, not a real effect absence. The noisy term was manage, which almost always appears in contexts like "manage data", "manage systems", "manage requirements" — not "manage people". Once the verb manage and the ambiguous team_building phrase are dropped, the pattern is dominated by mentor (13.9% in 2024 → 20.8% in 2026) and hire (1.2% → 1.7%), both precise people-management cues. The refined-strict share rose +7.2pp above a within-2024 noise of -1.4pp, an SNR of 5.09.

Mentor-heavy signal. mentor contributes 94% of the refined-strict hit volume. This makes the refined strict share better described as "mentorship is expected" than "people management is expected," but mentorship in a SWE JD IS a people-leadership responsibility and should be reported as such.

docs.title: 5. Management term prevalence (for transparency)
docs.text:
Per-term prevalence of strict-pattern terms on cleaned text (LLM-only, no specialists), 2024 pooled (21,646 rows) vs 2026 (12,456 rows):

term 2024 share 2026 share delta (pp) manage 28.2% 28.6% +0.4 mentor 13.9% 20.8% +6.9 hire 1.2% 1.7% +0.5 coach 0.34% 0.33% -0.01 team_building 0.08% 0.10% +0.02 headcount 0.01% 0.06% +0.05 performance_review 0.005% 0 -0.005 direct_reports 0 0 0 people_management 0 0 0

direct_reports, performance_review, people_management are effectively zero in LLM-cleaned text. Raw description has these (228, 119, 574 hits respectively across the 63,701 SWE LinkedIn default-filtered rows), but the description_core_llm extraction strips HR process language. This is a known limit of the text-source and applies equally to both periods — it does not bias the cross-period comparison.

docs.title: 6. Per-term precision (sampled, rule-based classifier)
docs.text:
From 50 stratified samples per term × tier (25/25 across 2024/2026):

term precision (managerial_any) precision (people_managerial) mentor 1.000 1.000 coach 1.000 1.000 headcount 1.000 1.000 performance_review 1.000 (n=1) 1.000 hire 0.940 0.940 team_building 0.414 0.379 manage 0.140 0.080 lead (BROAD) 0.320 0.180 team (BROAD) 0.080 0.020 stakeholder (BROAD) 0.080 0.060 coordinate (BROAD) 0.020 0.020

The BROAD pattern is >50% false-positive on lead, team, stakeholder, coordinate. These are the terms that inflate Gate 1's management_broad_binary_share (87.6% scraped share, SNR 0.48, below-noise). Confirms the prior-wave warning that broad lead/team/stakeholder patterns inflate the management indicator 3–5×.

Classifier methodology: rule-based snippet scoring with PEOPLE_CUES (direct reports, perform review, 1:1s, hire/recruit, career development, mentor, coach, team of N, reports to, head of), PROJECT_CUES (lead the project, project manage, lead developer, manage the system/codebase), and NOISE_CUES (lead[- ]free, leading edge, team member, stakeholder expectations, manage data, manage requirements, configuration management, etc.). Classes: MANAGERIAL_PEOPLE, MANAGERIAL_PROJECT, AMBIGUOUS_PROJECT, NON_MANAGERIAL_NOISE, OTHER_AMBIGUOUS. precision_any = share of samples in MANAGERIAL_PEOPLE ∪ MANAGERIAL_PROJECT. This rubric is auditable; per-sample classifications are in T11_management_precision_sample.csv for human override.

docs.title: 7. Domain-stratified scope inflation — DEFERRED
docs.text: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet was not present at run-time. T09 subsystem artifacts (bertopic_assignments.parquet, sample_embeddings.npy, etc.) are present but the final archetype-label join has not yet been written. Per spec, not proxying domain from title regex. Deferred to T28 Wave 3.

docs.title: 8. Top-1% requirement-breadth outliers
docs.text: Threshold: requirement_breadth ≥ 22 (99th percentile). 20/20 sampled outliers: 18 scraped 2026 (90%), 2 asaniczka (10%). Titles heavily concentrate on java full stack engineer / senior software engineer (java) / full stack ai/ml engineer / principal software engineer. None are apparently template-bloated from a quick snippet read; they are legitimately dense JDs from financial-services and staffing firms (BlackRock, Citi, JPMorgan-style long JDs) — the long-description, heavy-tech-stack, heavy-scope postings that exist at the tail. Sample at exploration/tables/T11/T11_top1pct_breadth_sample.csv.

J2 primary (entry|associate, no specialists, LLM text):

metric bucket n mean median p10 p90 requirement_breadth arshkon 168 7.42 6.5 2 14.3 requirement_breadth asaniczka 205 7.52 6.0 2 14.0 requirement_breadth scraped 537 10.38 9.0 4 18.0 credential_stack_depth arshkon 168 3.08 3 1 5 credential_stack_depth asaniczka 205 3.21 3 1 5 credential_stack_depth scraped 537 3.65 4 2 5 tech_count arshkon 168 4.71 4 0 11 tech_count scraped 537 6.52 6 1 13 org_scope_count arshkon 168 0.38 0 0 1 org_scope_count scraped 537 0.67 0 0 2 ai_count arshkon 168 0.11 0 0 0 ai_count scraped 537 0.66 0 0 3

S1 primary (mid-senior|director, no specialists, LLM text):

Full distributional table with p10/p50/p90 for 13 metrics × 5 slices × 3 sources at exploration/tables/T11/T11_complexity_distributions.csv.

docs.title: Arshkon-only senior cross-check (Gate-1 directive)
docs.text:
Per Gate-1 senior findings should lead with arshkon-only baseline. For S1 requirement_breadth: - arshkon mean 9.56 → scraped mean 12.18, Δ = +2.62 - within-2024 noise = 0.53 (arshkon − asaniczka) - SNR (arshkon baseline) = 4.96 above noise

For S1 credential_stack_depth: - arshkon 3.66 → scraped 4.38, Δ = +0.72 - within-2024 noise = -0.13 - SNR (arshkon baseline) = 5.37 above noise

For S1 refined-management-STRICT (mentorship-dominant) — rerun on just S1 slice: - From T11_binary_snr_by_slice.csv, S1 management_STRICT_binary (unrefined) SNR (pooled) = 26.97 at the S1 level. The S1-level SNR is much higher than the all-level SNR (1.61) because within-2024 variance at S1 is small (-0.4pp). - Senior-side management signal is unambiguously rising (+10.5pp at S1) regardless of pattern refinement.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
• Junior and senior complexity rose at roughly the same proportional rate (+34–39% in requirement_breadth). I expected the redefinition narrative to predict a steeper rise at entry-level. It is NOT supported by the measured complexity metrics.
  • Senior org-scope rose nearly 2× (+88% on org_scope_count at S1). Juniors only +63% (on a much smaller base). If any role is being "redefined upward" it is S1 — scope language is landing harder at senior than at entry.
  • Refined management STRICT is dominated by mentor (13.9% → 20.8%). This is mentorship expectation, which in SWE JDs has historically been a senior/staff marker. A 7pp rise in "mentor" prevalence (+50% relative) across all postings is meaningful — it is consistent with the hypothesis that expected scope/leadership at existing roles has migrated down.
  • The unrefined STRICT pattern's "marginal" Gate-1 verdict was a precision artifact, not a real no-effect. Dropping the noisy manage verb alone promotes SNR from 1.5 to 5.1. This demonstrates the value of mandated precision sampling before SNR judgements.
  • description_core_llm strips all HR-process language. direct reports, performance review, people management, headcount are all near-zero in cleaned text but present in raw text. This is symmetric across periods and does not bias the ratio, but it means my STRICT pattern cannot use these ideal-world HR terms — it must lean on mentor, hire, coach as proxies.
  • scope_density (scope mentions per 1K chars) rose +47% at S1 but only +26% at J2. Length-normalized, senior scope language is densifying faster than entry scope language. Consistent with senior-side scope inflation.
  • education_level modestly DROPPED cross-period (arshkon 0.71, asaniczka 1.00, scraped 0.82). Pooled → scraped delta -0.12 levels (BS/MS/PhD ordinal). Within-2024 noise -0.30 dominates. Not a credible trend — within-2024 variance is the arshkon-vs-asaniczka gap on degree-mention frequency. Education requirements are not measurably moving.
  • yoe_numeric is flat. Pooled 5.08, scraped 4.51. Within-2024 -0.64, cross -0.43. SNR 0.67 below-noise. YOE asks are NOT meaningfully shifting in the cross-period comparison. Seniority labels and complexity are moving; stated years of experience is not.
  • 90% of the requirement_breadth top-1% outliers are scraped 2026. Not evenly distributed. The long tail is lengthening with time.

docs.title: Essential sensitivities
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion: applied (via default filter, is_aggregator = false in the source parquet and specialist-list exclusion on top).
  • (b) Length normalization: tech_density and scope_density (per-1K-chars) are reported alongside raw counts. Tech density is flat cross-period because descriptions grew as much as tech mentions; scope density rose at S1 (+30–47%) because scope mentions grew faster than descriptions.
  • (c) Seniority operationalization: J2 primary + J3 robustness; S1 primary + S4 robustness. All reported.
  • (f) Text-source composition: restricted to text_source='llm'. Raw-text inclusion would over-count boilerplate (scraped has 70% raw, 30% LLM) and inflate length-based metrics — explicitly avoided per Gate 1 rule.

docs.title: T12 — Open-ended text evolution
docs.text: Agent: H (Wave 2) · Date: 2026-04-17 · Input: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet filtered to text_source='llm' (99% arshkon, 94% asaniczka, 31% scraped) · Method: Log-odds ratio with informative Dirichlet prior (Monroe et al. 2008)

docs.title: Cell sizes
docs.text:
Cell n (LLM-cleaned) arshkon 2024 (all SWE, primary A) 3,996 scraped 2026 (all SWE, primary B) 9,111 arshkon req+resp section 3,330 scraped req+resp section 8,796 entry 2024 (arshkon+asaniczka pooled) 344 entry 2026 (scraped) 452 mid-senior 2024 (pooled) 9,971 mid-senior 2026 (scraped) 4,705 arshkon mid-senior 2024 1,818 asaniczka mid-senior 2024 8,153

Entry 2024/2026 cells are small (344/452) but above the 100-row floor. Text-source filter restricts scraped to 9,118 rows (31% coverage); results should be read as "what the LLM-cleaned scraped subset shows". All cells have is_aggregator=False and specialist companies excluded.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
2024→2026 text change is a period effect, not a relabeling. 2026 entry postings are closer to 2024 entry postings than to 2024 mid-senior postings (cosine 0.953 vs 0.938). The shift is a uniform temporal drift applied to all seniority levels — not senior-content migrating downward.

Genuine content change concentrated on AI tooling. Top emerging terms are AI tools (rag, copilot, claude, cursor, langgraph, anthropic, gemini, ai-assisted) and AI domain vocabulary (multimodal, retrieval-augmented, embeddings, llm-based). Disappearing terms are legacy enterprise vocabulary (duties, j2ee, t-sql, angularjs, jquery, visio, redhat, sysml, tomcat, itil).

Section-filtered re-run confirms the genuine-vs-boilerplate split. Among the top-100 2026-characteristic terms from full-text, 60 also appear in the section-filtered top-100 (genuine content), 40 do not (boilerplate-driven — appearing outside requirements/responsibilities). Importantly, several AI tool names (copilot, langchain, openai, cursor) drop out of the section-filtered top-100 — they appear in role_summary/benefits/about_company more than requirements. AI domain language (rag, llm, llms, genai, agent, agentic, agents, ml, prompt) stays in both lists — it's genuinely being written into requirements sections.

Top 15 terms most characteristic of scraped 2026 (z-score, share 2024 → share 2026)

direction ai_tool ai_domain tech_stack org_scope sys_design mgmt method credential soft_skill boilerplate other arshkon 2024 0% 0% 19% 1% 0% 1% 2% 2% 3% 2% 70% scraped 2026 3% 9% 4% 5% 5% 1% 1% 0% 1% 0% 71%

Category shifts visible: 2024 tech_stack share (19%) vs 2026 ai_domain+ai_tool share (12%). 2026 sys_design share (5%) > 2024 (0%). org_scope share 5% vs 1%. See exploration/figures/T12/primary_category_share.png.

docs.title: 2. Section-filtered re-run — requirements + responsibilities only
docs.text:
Same log-odds comparison, but corpus restricted to text classified into requirements or responsibilities by the T13 classifier.

Of 100 terms characteristic of 2026 in full-text, 60 also appear in section-filtered top-100. 40 drop out.

docs.text:

cloud-native collaborate comfortable contribute deep demonstrated end-to-end engineering

llm llms long-term ll mentor ml observability operate optimize outcomes partner

docs.title: Full-text ONLY (likely boilerplate-driven: present in role_summary, benefits, about_company)
docs.text:
actions ambiguous capabilities copilot datasets deliver domains enabling event-driven
execution experimentation flows foundational frameworks generative gpu guardrails
hands high-impact infrastructure-as-code iteration langchain latency layers
maintainability measurable mission-critical openai patterns practices production-ready
regulated retrieval rust similar solutions thrives trade-offs translate ts

Notable: specific AI tool names (copilot, langchain, openai) drop out of the section-filtered top-100. AI domain vocabulary (rag, genai, llm, llms, ai, agent, agentic, agents) stays in both. Interpretation: employers write "Copilot" / "LangChain" / "OpenAI" in positioning/about-the-company text, but write rag, llm, agent into actual requirements/responsibilities. The genuine content of requirements has shifted to AI-domain vocabulary; specific tool-brand references are boilerplate.

Interestingly, hands appears only in full-text but hands-on appears in both — the section-filtered version sees "hands-on" as a single hyphenated token; the full-text sees "hands" as an alternate form. Both point to the same behaviour.

term 2024 share 2026 share n_companies_2026 category rag 0.10% 4.99% 366 ai_domain copilot 0.06% 4.08% 299 ai_tool claude 0.02% 3.83% 281 ai_tool ai-assisted 0.02% 3.82% 286 other (ai) cursor 0.01% 2.37% 192 ai_tool

Full 20: also hands (2.4% — from "hands-on"), retrieval-augmented (2.1%), langgraph (1.8%), cross (1.6%), anthropic (1.6%), embeddings (1.4%), ai-enabled (1.4%), llm-based (1.3%), llm-powered (1.1%), multimodal (1.1%), opentelemetry (1.0%), ai-native (1.0%), gemini (1.0%).

docs.title: 4. Accelerating terms (existed in 2024 but grew ≥3×, ≥0.5% in 2026, ≥20 companies)
5. Shrinking / disappearing terms (≥1% in 2024, ratio ≤0.5)
docs.text:
18 terms meet the strict "disappearing" bar (≥1% in 2024 and ≤0.5% in 2026). Top 5:

term 2024 share 2026 share ratio se (abbrev) 1.57% 0.48% 0.31 t-sql 1.47% 0.38% 0.26 itil 1.41% 0.45% 0.32 angularjs 1.38% 0.46% 0.33 technicians 1.33% 0.49% 0.37

Broader "shrunk by at least half" list (147 terms) includes: duties (13% → 6.4%), sr (6.6% → 2.9%), duration (6.5% → 2.5%), performs (5.1% → 2.0%), xml (4.1% → 1.8%), participates (3.3% → 1.5%), jquery (3.3% → 0.9%), analyzes (3.2% → 1.4%), js (2.7% → 1.3%), education (2.5% → 1.2%), j2ee (2.4% → 1.2%), powerpoint (2.0% → 0.7%), puppet (1.9% → 0.9%), bootstrap (1.8% → 0.6%), mbse (1.7% → 0.6%), visio (1.1% → 0.3%), redhat (1.0% → 0.4%).

Three themes in the disappearing list: 1. HR/formal language (duties, performs, participates, analyzes, assigned, assists, competent, shall, verbally, life-cycle) 2. Legacy .NET / Java / front-end stacks (j2ee, jquery, angularjs, bootstrap, spring spring, javascript [relatively], html, css, tomcat, php) 3. Enterprise admin/tools (itil, visio, redhat, puppet, powerpoint, subsystem, mbse, sysml, decomposition, schematics)

docs.title: 6. Bigrams — primary comparison
docs.text:
Top 2026-characteristic bigrams include ai platforms, ai workflows, ai engineer, design ai, build ai, leveraging ai, engineering workflows, mission-critical systems, features end-to-end, hands-on engineering, observability tools, real-time systems, real-time data, lieu degree, cd workflows, typescript react, python typescript, react next.js, translate business.

Top 2024-characteristic bigrams include azure cloud, azure devops, aws cloud, web services, web application, html css, verbal written, java j2ee, spring spring, full stack, rest api, bachelor degree, cloud services, net framework, net developer, business partners, good knowledge, day-to-day tasks.

lieu degree in 2026-heavy list deserves note — this reflects "Bachelor's degree or equivalent experience in lieu of a degree" becoming more common, a loosening of credentials. Combined with the visible drop in bachelor degree (still present but less dominant), this points to modest credential loosening.

docs.title: Entry 2024 vs entry 2026 (J2 pooled)
docs.text:
Entry_2024-heavy: junior, application, test, customer, system, developer, sql, engineer, business, specifications, java, position, written, possess, spring, knowledge, necessary, existing, web, software.

Entry_2026-heavy: unsupervised, containerization, maintainable, mission-critical, terraform, retrospectives, redis, cursor, typescript, well-documented, codebases, infrastructure-as-code, flows, regression, gcp, performance, stand-ups.

Z-scores in this comparison are much smaller (±2–7) because the cells are smaller (344 vs 452). The direction is nonetheless clear: 2026 entry postings use modern language (terraform, gcp, typescript, redis, retrospectives, stand-ups, mission-critical, containerization) and add AI-adjacent tooling (cursor), while 2024 entry postings use classical enterprise language (application, java, spring, specifications, possess).

docs.title: Mid-senior 2024 vs 2026
docs.text: Mid-senior_2026-heavy: next.js, ambiguity, excellence, low-latency, langchain, regulated, thrives, python, millions, modernization, retrieval, mlops, iteration, mission-critical, ai, llm, models. Classic senior evolution: AI infra (langchain, mlops, retrieval, ai, llm) plus system-scale vocab (low-latency, millions, modernization).

docs.title: Entry 2026 vs mid-senior 2024 — relabeling diagnostic
docs.text:
Terms characteristic of 2026 entry (vs 2024 mid-senior): scikit-learn, dashboards, cursor, co-op, receiving, authorization, well-documented, classification, chatbot, hands-on, field, resume, research, prompts, github, mcp, recommendation, participate.

Terms characteristic of 2024 mid-senior (vs 2026 entry): lead, system, senior, design, expertise, test, project, application, specifications, architect, mentor, engineer, principal, staff, systems, technology, documents, security, developer, proven.

Reading: The 2024 senior-marker vocabulary (lead, senior, architect, mentor, principal, staff, expertise) is NOT present in 2026 entry postings at elevated rates. Instead, 2026 entry postings add NEW vocabulary (cursor, mcp, chatbot, prompts, hands-on, scikit-learn, co-op). Conclusion: period-effect dominates; relabeling does not.

docs.title: Cosine similarity of term-share vectors
docs.text:
pair cosine entry24 vs entry26 0.953 entry26 vs midsr24 0.938 entry26 vs midsr26 0.958 entry24 vs midsr24 0.973 midsr24 vs midsr26 0.942 all24 vs all26 0.951

Entry_26 is closer to entry_24 (0.953) than to midsr_24 (0.938). If relabeling were dominant, the inequality would flip. Entry_26 is even closer to midsr_26 (0.958), consistent with a period-synchronized shift pulling entry and senior postings in the same direction.

docs.title: Within-2024 calibration (arshkon midsr vs asaniczka midsr)
docs.text: Top distinguishing terms within-2024: azure (z=21), cloud (z=21), ai (z=16), ml (z=14), backend (z=13), data (z=13), cd (z=13), aws (z=13), ci (z=13), platform (z=12), best, react, application, hybrid, java. The arshkon vs asaniczka instrument delta is non-trivial and concentrates on a similar topic-space (cloud/devops) to the cross-period delta — which is why per-term comparisons need SNR rather than raw deltas. Individual tool names like copilot/cursor/langgraph have minuscule within-2024 noise (all below 0.01%) so the 2026 shift in those terms is clearly above noise. Broader terms like ai have within-2024 z=16 and cross-period effects that need to beat that threshold to be credible.

docs.title: 8. Relabeling framing (interpretation)
docs.text:
The cosine and Fightin' Words results both say: the 2024→2026 shift represents a temporal drift uniformly applied to entry- and senior-level postings, not a relabeling of senior requirements into entry postings. Practical framing:

  • If 2026 entry = relabeled 2024 senior: we would expect 2026 entry to acquire lead, mentor, architect, principal, staff, expertise — it did not.
  • If 2026 entry = period-shifted 2024 entry: we would expect 2026 entry to acquire the same NEW terms 2026 senior acquires (ai, rag, llm, tools) — it did.

The implication for RQ1 "scope inflation": the "inflation" is NOT adding senior-level leadership duties to entry postings. It IS adding modern (AI-related) requirements across all seniority levels, with entry postings affected equally. What changes for entry specifically (vs senior) in 2026: more hands-on, co-op, chatbot, cursor, scikit-learn, prompts — entry-specific AI-literacy tooling.

docs.title: 9. Surprises
docs.text:
1. copilot, langchain, openai drop out of section-filtered top-100 — these specific tool names live more in role_summary/about-company than in actual requirements. This is a clean result of the T13 section filter.
  2. No terms meeting the strict "disappearing" bar (>1% 2024 → <0.1% 2026). Language evolution is softer than we might expect. 18 terms meet a looser bar (>1% → <0.5%).
  3. hands-on is the single most emerging-English phrase — the hyphenated form alone grew by 25× and appears in 193 companies. "Hands-on" has become a near-universal 2026 descriptor.
  4. f-1 and j-1 accelerate 100× — visa status references ("sponsorship available for F-1/J-1/OPT") are now routinely added to postings. This is HR-template expansion driven by the same post-ZIRP hiring-market conditions that drove the benefits-section doubling.
  5. lieu degree as a top bigram for 2026 — "Bachelor's degree or equivalent experience in lieu of a degree" is now a common 2026 clause. Credential loosening is real and measurable.
  6. The 2024→2026 shift is symmetric across seniority levels. I expected entry to shift more than senior. It doesn't — midsr24 vs midsr26 cosine (0.942) is actually lower than entry24 vs entry26 (0.953). Seniors changed more than juniors in absolute vocabulary terms.
