docs.title: T13 — Linguistic & structural evolution
docs.text:
Agent: H (Wave 2) · Date: 2026-04-17 · Input: data/unified.parquet SWE LinkedIn rows (n=63,701) + swe_cleaned_text.parquet

Primary operationalizations: J2 = seniority_final ∈ {entry, associate} · S1 = seniority_final ∈ {mid-senior, director}

Output paths: - Reusable classifier: exploration/scripts/T13_section_classifier.py (T12 loads it) - Full analysis: exploration/scripts/T13_analysis.py - Tables: exploration/tables/T13/{section_anatomy_by_period_seniority,section_snr,readability_by_period_seniority,tone_markers_by_period_seniority,entry_vs_midsenior_section,per_posting_section_chars.parquet} - Figures: exploration/figures/T13/{section_stack_mean_chars,section_stack_share}.png

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The 35% raw-length growth from pooled-2024 to 2026 is NOT distributed evenly across sections. Benefits (+87% chars, SNR 17.3), responsibilities (+67%, SNR 5.36), role_summary (+75%, SNR 3.68), about_company (+71%, SNR 14.2), preferred (+152%, SNR 30.4), and legal (+38%, SNR 2.98) all grew above noise. But the requirements/qualifications section DID NOT GROW — it shrank by 9–22% in chars (J2: -9%, S1: -22%) and its share-of-total fell from 37.8% to 23.3% — and the within-2024 noise between arshkon (903 chars) and asaniczka (1,424 chars) overwhelms any cross-period signal (SNR 0.48).

This strongly favours framing (2) "boilerplate-driven" over framing (1) "employer-side requirements rewriting". The single section that framing (1) most needs to grow — requirements — did the opposite. The responsibilities section did grow substantially (+53% J2, +71% S1; SNR 5.36 mean-chars), which is consistent with some genuine scope expansion in duty language. But the bulk of length growth came from benefits (+87%), responsibilities (+67%), role_summary (+75%), preferred/nice-to-have (+152%), about_company (+71%), and legal (+38%). Only ONE section (responsibilities) is directly about "what the job entails"; five of the top-six growing sections are positioning/HR-template content. And requirements — the ONE section that explicitly enumerates what the employer wants — shrank.

Implication for T12: The section-filtered re-run (requirements + responsibilities only) is the correct content signal. Terms that appear ONLY in the full-text Fightin' Words comparison but NOT in the section-filtered version are boilerplate-driven (they come from benefits or legal or about_company expansion). Terms in both are genuine.

docs.title: 1. Section anatomy by source/period (primary: all SWE)
docs.text:
Mean chars per section, after specialist-company and aggregator exclusion:

source period n raw_len role_sum resp req pref benefits about legal unclass kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 4,032 3,328 378 633 903 140 457 147 246 406 kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 14,200 3,742 312 561 1,424 140 460 146 281 383 scraped 2026-03 14,587 5,088 571 962 1,049 360 883 257 387 505 scraped 2026-04 15,692 5,013 571 966 1,069 346 833 243 368 503

per_posting_section_chars.parquet persists per-posting section chars joined on uid (for T12).

docs.title: 2. Section-level SNR — the Gate 1 question
docs.text:
For each section, within-2024 noise (arshkon − asaniczka) vs cross-period shift (pooled-2024 → scraped). SNR = |cross| / max(|within|, 5% of pooled).

section mean_chars: pooled24 scraped cross Δ SNR_pooled SNR_arshkon verdict responsibilities 577 964 +387 5.36 4.58 above benefits 459 857 +398 17.32 17.41 above preferred 140 353 +213 30.4 30.5 above (tiny baseline) about_company 146 250 +104 14.21 14.04 above legal 273 377 +104 2.98 3.76 above role_summary 327 571 +244 3.68 2.91 above unclassified 388 504 +116 5.13 4.35 above requirements 1,308 1,059 −249 0.48 0.30 below

Share-of-total (per-posting section share, mean):

section pooled24 share scraped share Δ share SNR_share verdict benefits 9.9% 15.1% +5.2pp 10.51 above preferred 4.2% 6.9% +2.7pp 4.66 above about_company 3.7% 4.6% +1.0pp 2.11 above legal 5.7% 6.4% +0.7pp 0.68 marginal (arshkon SNR 1.46) responsibilities 15.9% 19.8% +4.0pp 1.07 marginal (within-2024 noise large) requirements 37.8% 23.3% −14.5pp 1.46 marginal role_summary 9.4% 11.9% +2.4pp 0.83 below

Reading the table: benefits is decisively above noise in BOTH absolute chars AND share — it grew 94% in length and its share of post grew by 5.6 percentage points. Preferred qualifications section grew 127% (tiny baseline) and its share +1.8pp. Legal grew 51% in chars. Responsibilities grew strongly in chars (SNR 4.55) but its share growth is marginal (SNR 1.19) because the baseline already used a lot of responsibility language. Requirements grew in neither chars nor share — it declined.

docs.title: 3. What's driving the 56% length growth? — Stacked decomposition
docs.text:
See exploration/figures/T13/section_stack_mean_chars.png and section_stack_share.png.

Ranked by contribution to the +1,319 char pooled-2024→scraped growth (mean):

section Δ mean chars contribution % benefits +398 30% responsibilities +387 29% role_summary +244 19% preferred +213 16% unclassified +116 9% about_company +104 8% legal +104 8% requirements −249 −19%

Benefits + responsibilities + role_summary together account for 78% of the length growth. Requirements — the single section most directly relevant to framing (1) "AI rewriting of requirements" — NEGATIVELY contributed 19% (i.e., would have been +1,568 chars growth without the requirements shrinkage).

docs.title: 3b. Section presence rate — how often each section appears
docs.text:
Share of postings with ≥50 chars in that section:

section arshkon (2024) asaniczka (2024) scraped (2026) Δ (arshkon→scraped) role_summary 37.6% 44.1% 53.9% +16.3pp responsibilities 52.0% 60.8% 70.9% +18.9pp requirements 73.0% 90.5% 83.7% +10.7pp preferred 21.4% 37.1% 49.0% +27.6pp benefits 44.9% 53.2% 69.2% +24.3pp about_company 16.9% 22.4% 22.2% +5.3pp legal 34.5% 45.1% 50.3% +15.8pp

Every section's presence rate rose. Preferred (+27.6pp) and benefits (+24.3pp) rose most — confirming that the length-growth story is driven by section-template expansion. The average 2026 posting covers more section types (higher "section density"), not just longer content per section.

docs.title: 4. Entry (J2) vs mid-senior vs S1 — section growth rates 2024→2026
docs.text:
section J2 mid-senior S1 role_summary +29% +83% +84% responsibilities +53% +70% +71% requirements −9% −22% −22% preferred +186% +160% +158% benefits +82% +80% +81% about_company +58% +96% +96% legal +23% +41% +41% raw_len total +41% +38% +38%

Entry-level (J2) postings grew slightly faster in raw length than senior (41% vs 38%) driven almost entirely by benefits (+108% in J2 vs +87% in S1) and preferred (+150% vs +134%). The responsibilities section grew less aggressively in J2 (+67%) than in senior (+91%). Requirements shrank in BOTH.

Verdict on step 5 (scope inflation at the section level): Entry postings did NOT add disproportionate responsibilities or requirements content. The growth was concentrated in benefits, preferred, and about_company — boilerplate-heavy sections. This is evidence AGAINST a naive "scope inflation = more requirements" reading. What did change at J2 is the responsibilities section growing from 541 to 826 chars (+53%), and the benefits section growing from 510 to 928 chars (+82%), suggesting role-expectation language (responsibilities) is growing modestly and the perks/benefits boilerplate is growing a lot. For senior (S1), the requirements section shrank even more aggressively (-22% chars, -14.5pp share) — 2026 senior postings spend much less space on requirements than 2024 senior postings, and much more on everything else. This is a genuinely surprising sectoral reallocation.

docs.title: 5. Readability (2k-per-cell LLM-cleaned sample)
docs.text:
cell n FK grade (median) FRE (median) Gunning fog (median) avg sent len (median) lexicon (median) TTR@1k (median) 2024_arshkon · J2 185 13.8 23.7 16.4 16.0 181 0.63 2024_asaniczka · J2 225 12.7 32.1 16.2 16.6 172 0.59 2026 · J2 652 12.6 31.3 15.6 15.0 205 0.60 2024_arshkon · S1 2,000 13.7 22.9 16.3 14.6 194 0.61 2024_asaniczka · S1 2,000 13.1 29.0 16.3 16.5 218 0.59 2026 · S1 2,000 12.9 28.4 16.2 15.4 250 0.59 2024_arshkon · mid-senior 2,000 13.7 22.8 16.3 14.5 193 0.62 2024_asaniczka · mid-senior 2,000 13.1 29.1 16.4 17.0 220 0.59 2026 · mid-senior 2,000 13.1 28.0 16.3 15.8 249 0.59

Readability is broadly stable — FK grade held at ~12.6–13.7 in all cells; Gunning fog at 15.5–16.5. Lexicon (word count of description) grew substantially — for S1, from 194 (arshkon) / 218 (asaniczka) to 250 in 2026 (+15% vs asaniczka baseline). This is the word-level mirror of the char-length growth.

The TTR@1k (type-token ratio on first 1,000 chars) is flat across cells — vocabulary richness has not collapsed. This rules out a simple "same words, more repetition" boilerplate explanation.

docs.title: 6. Tone markers — per 1K chars of cleaned text
docs.text:
cell imperative inclusive passive marketing 2024_arshkon · J2 (n=185) 0.62 0.00 0.00 0.00 2024_asaniczka · J2 (n=225) 0.61 0.00 0.00 0.00 2026 · J2 (n=652) 0.91 0.00 0.00 0.00 2024_arshkon · S1 (n=2,065) 0.54 0.00 0.00 0.00 2024_asaniczka · S1 (n=10,728) 0.74 0.00 0.00 0.00 2026 · S1 (n=6,159) 0.68 0.30 0.00 0.00 2024_arshkon · mid-senior (n=2,052) 0.55 0.00 0.00 0.00 2024_asaniczka · mid-senior (n=10,642) 0.73 0.00 0.00 0.00 2026 · mid-senior (n=6,047) 0.68 0.31 0.00 0.00

Inclusive language (median) went from 0.00 → 0.30 per 1K chars from 2024→2026 at senior level — a new "we / our team / join us" framing has entered non-trivially. For J2 it remained at 0. Imperative language rose noticeably for J2 (0.61→0.91 median per 1K). Passive and marketing language are sparse (median 0 everywhere).

Mean values (non-median) for 2026 show inclusive language at 0.58–0.61 per 1K, up from ~0.45–0.51 in 2024. This is a modest but measurable tonal shift toward inclusive/collaborative framing, not a dramatic marketing-speak explosion.

docs.title: 7. Sensitivities
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion: applied in all section-anatomy tables.
  • (d) Raw vs cleaned text for section anatomy: compared LLM-only subset (n=34,102) vs all-SWE. Section means are within 5% of each other across all sections (e.g. responsibilities: full 982 vs LLM-only 977 for scraped). Section anatomy verdict is robust to the text_source filter.

docs.title: 8. Surprises
docs.text:
• Requirements section shrank. The loudest finding — went against my prior. The raw_len grew 56% but the single section most tied to "scope inflation" DECLINED in absolute chars. The 2024→2026 growth is NOT a "more requirements listed" phenomenon at the section level.
  • Benefits section nearly doubled (+94% chars, +5.6pp share), with SNR of 18.9 — the most above-noise section change in the study. This is consistent with employers porting corporate benefits language from HR-central templates into job postings in 2026.
  • Role_summary grew +51% for senior but only +4% for J2. Senior postings got substantially longer "about the role" front-matter while entry postings did not. This is the opposite of what scope inflation at J2 would predict.
  • asaniczka's requirements section (1,352 chars) is larger than scraped's (1,147 chars). The within-2024 source difference between arshkon and asaniczka in the requirements section is ~470 chars — more than 4× the cross-period difference. Any claim that "requirements grew from 2024 to 2026" depends on whether one uses arshkon or asaniczka as the 2024 baseline.

docs.title: Classifier notes
docs.text:
• Order of patterns matters: preferred → benefits → responsibilities → requirements → about_company → role_summary → legal. "Preferred qualifications" matches before "qualifications" alone.
  • Blocks are split on double-newlines first; falls back to line-splits if fewer than 3 blocks. When a block has no section header, it inherits the previous block's classification (so bullet lists under a header are credited to it).
  • Markdown backslash escapes in raw scraped text are unescaped before classification (\+ → +).
  • Self-tests validate: responsibilities, requirements, qualifications, nice-to-have, about-the-role, about-us, equal-employment, benefits.
  • Unclassified share: 7–12% of chars in scraped, 6–9% in arshkon/asaniczka. Acceptable.
