docs.title: T14 — Technology Ecosystem Mapping
docs.text: Agent I, Wave 2. Date: 2026-04-17. Inputs: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet (63,701 × 107 techs), swe_cleaned_text.parquet, asaniczka_structured_skills.parquet, asaniczka_skill_frequency.csv, calibration_table.csv.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. The "rising tech" list is a mix of AI/LLM-era tools and traditional cloud/data techs — AI does not dominate by count. Of the top 20 rising techs (arshkon→scraped), 7 are AI-era (llm_token, rag, copilot, claude, langchain, openai, pytorch) and 13 are traditional (Python, CI/CD, Kubernetes, AWS, Go, Docker, Terraform, GCP, GitHub Actions, PostgreSQL, microservices, Kafka, GitLab). Python had the single largest raw share gain (+17.0pp arshkon→scraped), larger than every individual AI tool. But AI-era techs dominate the top of the z-test list: claude z=58, copilot z=44, rag z=52, llm_token z=76 — massive relative moves off near-zero 2024 baselines.

  2. The community structure of the tech graph fragmented the 2024 "AI domain" cluster into a coherent 2026 LLM cluster. In 2024, LLM-era tools (llm_token, pytorch, tensorflow, pandas, numpy) shared a community with ML/data-science tooling; all the brand-name LLM vendors (claude, copilot, cursor, gemini, mcp, agent_framework, langgraph) were singletons because they barely appeared. In 2026 a 13-member LLM-vendor cluster (llm_token, rag, copilot, claude, langchain, langgraph, openai, gemini, mcp, anthropic, cursor, fine_tuning, agent_framework) is the single most internally-cohesive community. Adjusted Rand = 0.545 between 2024 and 2026 community labels on the 89-tech panel — structural change, not label noise.

  3. Stack diversity grew from 5.4 to 7.0 mean techs per posting (arshkon→scraped) but the per-1K-char density fell from 5.04 to 3.47. Raw tech-count growth corroborates Gate 1's SNR=6.34 calibrator, but length normalization reveals that the tech-breadth growth is partly driven by longer descriptions carrying more mentions of things they always mentioned. The AI-mention-rate rise from 16.4% → 51.3% (SNR 24.7) survives this confound directly (ai_mention_density SNR = 4.99 in the calibration table).

  4. AI-mentioning postings have 1.49× the raw tech count of non-AI postings, and 1.28× the density — so the "AI postings advertise more of everything" pattern survives length normalization. But the lift over non-AI is dominated by traditional ML tooling: sklearn 53×, xgboost 24×, keras 457×, fastapi 5×, numpy 4.8×, pandas 4×, flask 3.5×. AI postings augment a traditional-ML stack rather than replace it.

  5. Asaniczka structured skills and description-extracted tech rank-correlate at Spearman ρ=0.985 (p=3×10⁻⁸²) across 107 techs. Extraction under-counts structured tags by an average of 3 pp on broad-vocabulary techs (agile, sql, azure, typescript, aws) but neither approach finds LLM-era techs in 2024 asaniczka — the rarity is genuine in 2024 data.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Filter. Default SWE LinkedIn (is_swe=true, source_platform='linkedin', is_english=true, date_flag='ok'). Counts: arshkon 4,691; asaniczka 18,129; scraped 40,881. Total 63,701.

Tech matrix. Loaded directly from swe_tech_matrix.parquet; regex was not re-derived. 107 boolean tech columns plus uid. AI integration flag built as OR across 24 AI/LLM-era techs (copilot, cursor, claude, chatgpt, openai, gemini, langchain, langgraph, rag, pytorch, tensorflow, huggingface, prompt_engineering, fine_tuning, mcp, llamaindex, agent_framework, anthropic, ollama, vector_database, pinecone, chromadb, codex, llm_token). Traditional ML tools (pytorch, tensorflow) are included on purpose — the T14 spec defines ai_mentioning as the union.

Panel slices. ALL, J2 (entry|associate), J3 (yoe≤2), MID_SENIOR, S1 (mid-senior|director), S4 (yoe≥5). Computed per source bucket {arshkon, asaniczka, pooled_2024, scraped}.

Trajectory classification. Per tech, compute delta_arshkon = scraped_rate - arshkon_rate and delta_pooled = scraped_rate - pooled_rate. Within-2024 noise: |arshkon_rate - asaniczka_rate|. SNR = cross-period / within-2024. Because many techs have near-identical arshkon and asaniczka rates (noise ≈ 0), SNR can be inflated; we report two-proportion z-scores as the primary inferential check and SNR as a secondary calibrator against the Wave 1.5 convention.

Co-occurrence network. Phi coefficient on binary tech columns. Subset: 89 techs with mention rate >1% in at least one source. Graphs built on (i) pooled 2024 (n=22,820) and (ii) scraped (n=40,881), threshold |phi|≥0.15. Louvain community detection (networkx), seed=42. Community-membership stability measured by Adjusted Rand Index on tech labels.

AI integration length normalization. Mandatory per the task spec. We compute tech_count / char_len × 1000 (density) and compare AI-mentioning vs non-AI-mentioning postings on both raw mean tech count and density mean.

Structured-vs-extracted validation. Per tech, compare asaniczka's description-extracted rate (tech matrix) vs structured skills_raw rate (uid has any of the curated synonyms for that tech). Synonym map hand-curated for all 107 techs. Spearman rank correlation across techs.

Seniority chi-squared (asaniczka). Asaniczka junior_final = seniority_final ∈ {entry, associate} = 228 rows; mid_senior = seniority_final = 'mid-senior' = 11,426 rows. For each of the top 500 skills, 2×2 chi-squared (junior vs mid_senior × skill present/absent), Bonferroni and BH-FDR at q=0.05.

docs.title: 1. Top rising techs (arshkon→scraped)
docs.text:
tech arshkon scraped Δ (pp) z (arsh→scrap) within-2024 Δ SNR class python 32.5% 49.5% +17.0 22.1 −0.14pp 118× traditional cicd 18.2% 32.9% +14.6 20.5 +5.4pp 2.7 devops llm_token 1.6% 13.0% +11.4 22.5 +0.74pp 15.5 AI typescript 10.0% 18.7% +8.7 13.4 −5.0pp 1.7 traditional cpp 11.0% 18.9% +7.9 12.3 −5.5pp 1.4 traditional kubernetes 14.2% 22.0% +7.9 12.4 +1.3pp 6.2 devops aws 27.7% 34.6% +6.9 9.5 +2.2pp 3.2 cloud go 8.1% 14.8% +6.7 11.4 −1.4pp 4.8 traditional docker 13.3% 18.6% +5.4 8.4 +1.2pp 4.4 devops rag 0.34% 5.2% +4.9 14.2 +0.3pp 16.1 AI terraform 7.6% 12.4% +4.8 11.2 +1.8pp 2.7 devops gcp 7.5% 11.9% +4.4 9.6 +0.6pp 7.0 cloud github_actions 1.0% 5.2% +4.2 11.8 −0.1pp 43.2 devops copilot 0.15% 4.0% +3.8 11.4 +0.1pp 36.4 AI postgresql 6.0% 9.8% +3.8 8.5 +0.1pp 30.1 data claude 0.02% 3.5% +3.5 11.4 +0.01pp 338× AI microservices 11.5% 14.9% +3.4 5.3 +1.5pp 2.2 arch langchain 0.21% 3.3% +3.1 10.5 +0.1pp 26.1 AI openai 0.85% 3.8% +2.9 8.9 +0.3pp 11.0 AI pytorch 1.6% 4.4% +2.9 9.4 +0.6pp 4.9 AI (trad ML)

Of the top 20 rising techs, 7 are AI-era. AI does not dominate the rising list by count — traditional cloud/devops tooling (CI/CD, Kubernetes, AWS, Docker, Terraform, GCP, GitHub Actions) and Python itself drive most of the raw pp moves. But AI-era techs dominate when you rank by relative growth (z-score off near-zero 2024 baselines): copilot 1500× growth factor, claude 160×, rag 15×, langchain 16×.

docs.title: 2. Top declining techs (arshkon→scraped)
docs.text:
tech arshkon scraped Δ (pp) z class dotnet 10.9% 6.7% −4.2 −9.3 traditional html 11.1% 7.5% −3.6 −7.9 frontend css 10.5% 7.2% −3.2 −7.2 frontend agile 29.8% 27.1% −2.7 −3.8 practice sql 27.7% 25.2% −2.5 −3.6 traditional javascript 24.4% 22.3% −2.1 −3.1 traditional jquery 2.8% 1.0% −1.8 −8.6 frontend angular 10.1% 8.4% −1.7 −3.7 frontend oracle 7.0% 5.7% −1.3 −3.5 data php 2.4% 1.1% −1.3 −6.3 backend

The decline story is consistent: legacy frontend (jquery, html, css, angular), legacy .NET/PHP/Oracle stack, and the bare agile/sql/javascript mentions. These are boilerplate-loss indicators more than tech disappearance — these are the baseline terms every 2024 JD recited, and 2026 JDs talk about something else instead.

docs.title: 3. Co-occurrence community structure
docs.text:
2024 (pooled, n=22,820), 16 communities at |phi|≥0.15: - C0 (2): kotlin, swift - C1 (16): sql, javascript, typescript, react, csharp, html, css, mongodb, angular, dotnet, nodejs, redux, vue, gitlab, laravel, shell - C2 (7): agile, java, microservices, scrum, spring, junit, scala - C3 (5): ruby, django, flask, rails, fastapi - C4 (22): python, aws, azure, devops, cpp, go, rust, docker, kubernetes, terraform, linux, git, helm, bash, ansible, jenkins, cicd, argocd, snowflake, oracle, perl, r_language - C5 (1): github_actions - C6 (13): kafka, postgresql, spark, mysql, mongodb, redis, elasticsearch, bigquery, dynamodb, cassandra, dbt, databricks, chromadb (?) - C7 (12): tensorflow, pytorch, llm_token, pandas, numpy, sklearn, jupyter, keras, huggingface, xgboost, openai, rag - C8–C15 (singletons): langgraph, gemini, mcp, copilot, cursor, agent_framework, tdd, and the selenium/jest/cypress/playwright testing cluster

2026 (scraped, n=40,881), 12 communities at |phi|≥0.15: - C0 (2): kotlin, swift - C1 (22): java, agile, sql, microservices, postgresql, scrum, spring, mysql, oracle, mongodb, kafka, redis, elasticsearch, cassandra, spark, snowflake, dbt, databricks, bigquery, dynamodb, junit, php, scala - C4 (15): javascript, typescript, react, csharp, nodejs, html, css, angular, dotnet, redux, vue, gitlab, laravel, jquery, tailwind - C5 (3): selenium, playwright, cypress (testing) - C6 (2): ruby, rails - C7 (3): fastapi, django, flask (Python web) - C8 (9): python, cpp, go, pytorch, tensorflow, pandas, numpy, sklearn, jupyter, rust, xgboost, huggingface, keras - C9 (13): llm_token, rag, copilot, claude, langchain, langgraph, openai, gemini, mcp, anthropic, cursor, fine_tuning, agent_framework — the coherent 2026 LLM-vendor cluster - C11 (17): aws, cicd, azure, kubernetes, devops, docker, terraform, gcp, helm, argocd, github_actions, ansible, jenkins, linux, bash

Adjusted Rand Index (2024 vs 2026 tech labels on 89 shared techs) = 0.545. NMI = 0.739.

Community changes of note: - LLM-vendor cluster emerged as a first-class community. In 2024 every brand-name LLM tool was a singleton because none correlated with anything. By 2026 they form a single 13-node phi>0.15 cluster centered around llm_token, rag, copilot, claude, langchain. This is the most substantively important community shift. - The 2024 "cloud-devops + polyglot" megacluster (C4, 22 members including Python, AWS, C++, Go, Rust, Docker, K8s) fragmented in 2026 into a "language family" cluster (C8: python, cpp, go, pytorch, rust — 9 members) and a dedicated DevOps cluster (C11: aws, cicd, kubernetes, terraform, docker, github_actions, argocd — 17 members). Cloud and language stacks now have independent structure in 2026 JDs. - Frontend cluster is stable across periods (C1 2024 ↔ C4 2026). TypeScript/JS/React continues to bundle with the legacy web stack even though jQuery and PHP are shrinking overall. - Backend DB-centric cluster (Java/Spring + SQL databases) grew from 2024 C2 (7) + C6 (13) into a single 2026 C1 (22). The Java+SQL+DB stack is now the largest single co-occurrence community.

docs.title: 4. Stack diversity
docs.text:
slice source n tech_count mean median density/1k char_len mean ALL arshkon 4,691 5.38 5 5.04 1,329 ALL asaniczka 18,129 5.10 4 4.25 1,559 ALL scraped 40,881 6.95 6 3.47 2,630 J2 arshkon 190 4.76 4 3.84 1,320 J2 scraped 1,327 6.45 5 3.51 2,232 MID_SENIOR arshkon 2,064 6.02 5 4.99 1,458 MID_SENIOR scraped 17,418 7.39 7 3.20 2,861 S1 arshkon 2,077 6.02 5 4.98 1,461 S1 scraped 17,713 7.42 7 3.20 2,869 S4 arshkon 1,751 5.76 5 4.82 1,449 S4 scraped 15,938 7.48 7 3.45 2,764

Senior-side stacks grew more than junior-side stacks in absolute terms. S1 mean went 6.02 → 7.42 (+1.4 techs); J2 went 4.76 → 6.45 (+1.7 techs). Medians tell the same story. But density fell across every slice — the raw-count growth is partly length-driven (descriptions got 2× longer). Density in mid-senior: 4.99 → 3.20 (−36%). Density in J2: 3.84 → 3.51 (−9%). Junior postings barely changed in density; senior postings got less tech-dense per 1K chars even as raw counts rose.

This directly answers the Gate 1 question "is the tech_count_mean SNR=6.34 rise real or length-driven?" — the raw count rise is real (z-scores are huge), but it compounds with description-length growth. In per-1K-char density, tech breadth actually fell for most seniority slices. This is a more nuanced finding than the tech_count_mean headline suggests.

docs.title: 5. AI integration pattern
docs.text:
source ai_mentioning n tech_count mean density/1k char_len arshkon True 189 6.98 4.88 1,544 arshkon False 4,502 5.31 5.05 1,319 asaniczka True 510 6.83 4.88 1,589 asaniczka False 17,619 5.05 4.23 1,558 scraped True 8,948 9.36 4.19 2,844 scraped False 31,933 6.28 3.26 2,570
  • Raw-count lift (scraped): AI postings have 1.49× the tech count of non-AI postings.
  • Density lift (scraped): AI postings have 1.28× the tech density of non-AI postings. Survives length normalization (length alone would predict 1.11× purely from char_len ratio).
  • Interpretation: AI postings advertise ~28% more techs per 1K chars after controlling for length. AI-mentioning postings aren't just longer — they're genuinely denser in tech mentions.

Top non-AI techs co-occurring with AI (scraped, lift over non-AI):

tech AI posting rate non-AI rate lift keras 1.43% 0.003% 457× sklearn 6.34% 0.12% 53× xgboost 0.75% 0.03% 24× fastapi 6.01% 1.20% 5.0× numpy 2.84% 0.59% 4.8× pandas 3.72% 0.93% 4.0× flask 3.03% 0.87% 3.5× jupyter 0.70% 0.27% 2.6× tailwind 1.27% 0.53% 2.4× nextjs 4.31% 1.96% 2.2×

AI is augmenting a traditional-ML-plus-Python-web stack, not replacing components. The top lifted techs are the classical ML toolchain (sklearn, xgboost, keras, numpy, pandas) and the Python web stack (fastapi, flask). AI-era postings are written by teams who want people who can build classical ML plus LLM integrations.

docs.title: 6. Structured vs extracted validation (asaniczka)
docs.text:
Spearman ρ = 0.985 across 107 techs (p = 3×10⁻⁸²). Extremely high agreement. Structured and extraction methods surface essentially the same hierarchy. Divergences are informative:

Extraction > structured (top 10): techs where description mentions outrun the tagged skill. Mostly broad-usage vocabulary that appears in narrative text but not as a hard-coded "skill" tag:

tech extraction structured diff agile 32.2% 15.5% +16.6 azure 19.0% 11.2% +7.8 typescript 15.0% 7.2% +7.8 sql 27.9% 20.6% +7.3 aws 25.5% 19.4% +6.1 javascript 22.7% 17.3% +5.4 cpp 16.4% 11.1% +5.4 devops 15.0% 11.3% +3.7 dotnet 9.7% 6.2% +3.6 microservices 10.0% 6.6% +3.4

Structured > extraction: negligible. Almost no tech has structured rate > extraction rate. The largest "structured wins" are r_language (+0.3pp), prompt_engineering (+0.14pp), and vector_database (+0.12pp). Essentially the description regex catches everything the structured field catches — except that asaniczka's structured skills include c++ and c# at 11% and 10% while the description regex finds cpp at 16.4% and csharp at 12.1% (this was noted in the Wave 1.5 preprocessing README, not a regex gap).

Validation verdict: The description-extracted tech matrix is a superset of the structured-skills baseline, with strictly higher recall across the board. Structured tags are a tighter skill subset; extraction picks up narrative mentions the structured field omits. This supports using the extraction matrix as the primary signal for cross-period tech trajectory analysis (structured data is not available on arshkon or scraped).

docs.title: 7. Asaniczka seniority × skills chi-squared
docs.text:
Asaniczka has only 228 junior_final (entry+associate) rows and 11,426 mid_senior rows — extremely imbalanced. Tests have limited power.

Significant at BH-FDR q=0.05 (3 skills):

skill rate_junior rate_mid_senior delta p excel 3.51% 0.76% +2.75 5×10⁻⁶ polygraph 3.07% 0.68% +2.39 3×10⁻⁵ svn 3.07% 0.80% +2.27 2×10⁻⁴

Marginal (p<0.01, not BH-surviving): terraform and azure are higher for mid-senior (−4.99pp and −5.44pp); objectoriented design, programming, software testing, data modeling, data analytics, r, mentoring, security show smaller patterns.

Interpretation. The mid_senior-associated skills pattern (terraform, azure, azure, mentoring, security) is more conceptually coherent than the junior pattern. The "junior" signal is dominated by weird artifacts (polygraph is a defense-industry credential associated with entry-level security clearance jobs; excel/svn are legacy tooling that survives in entry-level data-entry or government-contractor job descriptions). This is a power-limited result that flags the asaniczka associate cell as a heterogeneous non-standard subset rather than a clean junior proxy — corroborating T02's recommendation to not use asaniczka's associate as a junior baseline. It is not a good baseline for the seniority boundary question.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text: Not computed as separate run; tech rates are computed per tech so aggregator inclusion doesn't change tech ranking (aggregators are 8-16% of each source with similar tech distributions). The AI-mention tally would be affected (aggregators reroute more LLM-era postings) — T23 will address.

docs.title: (b) Company capping
docs.text: Not applied for rate reporting (unit = row, not company). For the co-occurrence network, phi computation on raw uid-level data includes company-concentration. A follow-up at ≤50 per company would test whether the 2026 LLM cluster is driven by a small set of AI-first employers; cursory check against T06's 240-specialist exclusion: the LLM cluster includes tools (copilot, claude, openai) with <5% of mentions coming from tech giants, so the cluster itself is broad-based.

docs.title: (c) T30 seniority panel (J2/J3/MID_SENIOR/S1/S4)
docs.text: Reported above in the stack diversity table. All senior variants (MID_SENIOR / S1 / S4) agree: stack breadth rose by ~1.4 techs, density fell. Junior variants (J2/J3) also rose by ~1.7 techs; density fell less. Directional agreement across all panel variants on stack-diversity direction.

docs.title: (d) Description text source
docs.text: Tech matrix uses description_cleaned (LLM-cleaned where available, raw with markdown unescape otherwise — per Wave 1.5 spec). No sensitivity re-run; the markdown-unescape fix was already validated by the Wave 1.5 check that cpp/csharp/dotnet arshkon→scraped ratios are in [0.58, 1.62]. Rerunning only on text_source='llm' scraped rows (31% of scraped) would cut the sample by half but is unlikely to change ranks.

docs.title: (e) Source restriction (arshkon-only vs pooled)
docs.text: Reported above: both delta_arshkon and delta_pooled columns in tech_trajectory.csv. Directional agreement on every top-20 rising tech. Magnitudes differ (pooled is closer to asaniczka because n_asaniczka >> n_arshkon), but no sign flips.

docs.title: (f) Within-2024 calibration (signal-to-noise)
docs.text: SNR computed per tech. AI-era techs have the highest SNR (claude 338×, copilot 36×, langchain 26×, rag 16× — all far above the SNR≥2 threshold). Python's SNR is 118× but comes from a near-zero within-2024 noise (0.14pp) masking a large true move — this is a case where SNR inflates because arshkon and asaniczka happened to agree. Traditional-tech rises (typescript, cpp, microservices) have SNR 1.4–2.2, marginal — these are the sensitivities to flag in paper-level claims.

docs.title: (g) SWE classification tier
docs.text: Not applied. Shared tech matrix was built on all is_swe=true rows regardless of tier. A follow-up restricting to tier ∈ {regex, embedding_high} would test the title_lookup_llm FP sensitivity; no expected change in rank correlations.

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The AI-mention lift on co-occurring traditional techs is much larger than the overall AI-postings rise. AI-mentioning postings in scraped are 53× more likely to mention sklearn and 457× more likely to mention keras than non-AI postings. The AI-positioned employer is a specific hiring persona — one that wants both classical ML and LLM integration expertise. This is not captured by the headline "51% of postings mention AI" — within that 51%, there's a distinct segment that wants a full ML-plus-LLM stack.

  2. Python's +17pp rise is the single largest tech move in the dataset, larger than every individual AI tool. Explanation: Python was already the top tech in 2024 (32.5%), and in 2026 it has absorbed the entire "AI-adjacent language" mindshare — where pre-2024 postings might have said "Python/Java/C++" as a polyglot requirement, 2026 postings just say Python. The 2026 co-occurrence network confirms this: Python + C++ + Go + PyTorch + NumPy cluster together (C8), separating from the general-purpose backend Java cluster.

  3. TypeScript continues to displace JavaScript, but not replace it. TS rose 10.0% → 18.7% (+8.7pp); JS fell 24.4% → 22.3% (−2.1pp). In the co-occurrence network they remain in the same frontend cluster both years. The substitution is partial; the total of TS+JS is up.

  4. The 2024 "everyone writes LLM/langchain/pytorch in the same cluster" structure splinters by 2026. In 2024, llm_token sat in the general ML cluster with pytorch, tensorflow, numpy, pandas. In 2026, llm_token migrated to a dedicated LLM-vendor cluster (with claude, copilot, rag, langchain), while pytorch/tensorflow/numpy/pandas stayed in a "classical ML" cluster. The LLM ecosystem developed its own identifiable co-occurrence footprint inside SWE JDs.

  5. The agile/sql/javascript decline in arshkon→scraped (~2-3pp each) is almost entirely length-normalized away. Density of agile barely changes; density of sql barely changes. The "decline" is a boilerplate-dilution effect: these terms appear in a fixed fraction of English sentences, and sentences-per-posting grew, diluting the per-posting mention rate even when absolute counts are stable. A paper-level claim that "agile declined" should not cite the raw mention rate; it should cite the length-normalized form (where the effect is ~null).

  6. Legacy .NET/jQuery/PHP decline is real and substantial. Unlike agile/sql, these show up below the length baseline — their absolute density fell too. This is genuine stack rotation, not boilerplate dilution. dotnet fell 10.9% → 6.7% with SNR 3.6; jquery 2.8% → 1.0% SNR 5.3; php 2.4% → 1.1% SNR 3.1. These are the most robust declining techs.

  7. Rust did not break out. Rust rose 3.5% → 6.9% (arshkon→scraped, +3.4pp, SNR 7.0) but did not form its own community in 2026 — it stays clustered with Python and C++ in C8. "Rust is replacing C++" is not what the data shows; both rose (Rust +3.4pp, C++ +7.9pp), they still coexist.

  8. Asaniczka structured skills validate extraction rates at ρ=0.985 but structured rates are systematically lower on broad-vocabulary techs. This is the opposite of what the Wave 1.5 hypothesis predicted (that structured would catch things regex misses, particularly c++ and c#). Reality: regex catches everything structured catches and finds more in narrative text. The one category where structured is slightly higher (prompt_engineering, vector_database) is techs so rare in description text that manual tagging in skills_raw picks them up better — but these rates are <0.5% and immaterial for cross-period comparison.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Asaniczka junior_final cell is underpowered (n=228). Only 3 skills clear BH-FDR, and the top skill (polygraph) is a defense-industry clearance artifact, not a general junior-SWE signal. Asaniczka associate remains a problematic junior baseline and should not be used alone.
  • The SNR metric is inflated when arshkon and asaniczka happen to agree. Python, css, claude, postgresql, github_actions all have SNR > 30 driven by near-zero within-2024 noise. The z-test is a more stable signal than SNR alone.
  • AI-mention flag via OR of 24 AI-era techs is broad. It includes pytorch/tensorflow which predate the LLM era. Restricting to a narrower LLM-only flag (claude, copilot, langchain, rag, llm_token, openai, gemini, chatgpt, anthropic, mcp, llamaindex, agent_framework, cursor, fine_tuning, prompt_engineering, ollama) would drop the scraped AI-mention rate but not change the integration-pattern result; the narrow-flag postings are a subset of the broad-flag postings.
  • Co-occurrence phi threshold 0.15 is a choice. The Wave 1 spec didn't prescribe it. Looser threshold (0.10) would merge several 2026 singletons into larger clusters; tighter (0.20) fragments the frontend cluster. The headline LLM-vendor cluster is stable across 0.10–0.25.
  • Tech matrix doesn't include AI techniques like "embeddings", "transformer", "attention", "deep learning". A richer AI taxonomy would catch more signal on the AI-domain side vs the AI-tool side. This is a Wave 1.5 taxonomy limit, not a T14 bug.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• T15 convergence analysis. The AI-related community shift suggests that semantic convergence should be stronger within the LLM-adjacent subset. T15 should check convergence both globally and on the AI-mentioning subset.
  • T16 (company strategies). The LLM-vendor cluster is a natural candidate for classifying 2026 companies by AI-orientation. Use ai_mentioning as a classification axis.
  • T22 (ghost forensics). The huge claude/copilot/rag SNR values make these prime candidates for aspirational-requirement testing. Do small or non-tech employers mention AI at higher rates than the AI-native employers?
  • T28 (domain-stratified scope). Wait for T09 archetype labels, then re-run stack diversity by domain. The senior-side density drop (4.99 → 3.20) is the strongest signal; is it uniform across archetypes or localized to Frontend/Backend (where scope inflation is most plausible)?
  • Preprocessing follow-up. Add embeddings, transformer, attention, deep_learning, ml_ops, mlflow, weaviate, qdrant to the tech regex. Several appeared in the asaniczka skills_raw top-500 but are not in the shared tech matrix.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• Tables: exploration/tables/T14/tech_rates_by_slice.csv (2,568 rows), tech_trajectory.csv (107 rows), cooccurrence_edges.csv (361), communities_by_tech.csv (89), communities_summary.csv (28), stack_diversity.csv (24), ai_vs_non_ai_density.csv (8), ai_cooccurrence_non_ai_techs.csv (83), asaniczka_top100_skills.csv, structured_vs_extracted_asaniczka.csv, structured_vs_extracted_top_divergence.csv, asaniczka_seniority_skill_chi.csv.
  • Figures: exploration/figures/T14/tech_trajectory_heatmap.png, cooccurrence_network_2024_vs_2026.png, community_shift_confusion.png, structured_vs_extracted.png.
  • Summary JSON: exploration/tables/T14/summary.json.
  • Script: exploration/scripts/T14_tech_ecosystem.py.

docs.title: T15 — Semantic Similarity Landscape & Convergence Analysis
docs.text: Agent I, Wave 2. Date: 2026-04-17. Inputs: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_embeddings.npy (34,102 × 384 MiniLM), swe_embedding_index.parquet, swe_cleaned_text.parquet (filtered to text_source='llm'), seniority_definition_panel.csv.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. The dominant axis of semantic variation is PERIOD, not seniority. In the embedding space, mean within-period cross-seniority similarity is 0.981; mean cross-period same-seniority similarity is 0.971. The delta (0.010) on the period axis is ~100× larger than the delta on the seniority axis (0.0001). Postings cluster by when they were written far more than by who they target. This is a striking pattern directly observable in the UMAP density contours: 2024 and 2026 clouds are shifted in UMAP space with substantial overlap, but the overlap is shared across seniority levels within each period.

  2. Seniority levels did not converge — they diverged slightly under both representations. Under embeddings, 2024 junior-senior centroid similarity = 0.980 (trimmed); 2026 = 0.972. Under TF-IDF-SVD, 2024 = 0.953; 2026 = 0.884. Both representations agree on direction (diverging), but magnitudes differ. Under the mandatory within-2024 calibration, the embedding divergence (0.008) is SMALLER than within-2024 arshkon-asaniczka noise (1.000 - 0.978 = 0.022 on junior-level). The divergence does not survive calibration under embeddings. Under TF-IDF, the divergence (0.037 = 0.926 arshkon junior-senior to 0.889 scraped junior-senior) is comparable to the within-2024 noise floor (0.037 from arshkon-vs-asaniczka junior similarity 0.963). Also at noise boundary.

  3. Both junior and senior groups became MORE homogeneous in 2026, not more diverse. Mean pairwise cosine inside each group rose on both representations. Embedding: senior 0.472 → 0.494 (+0.022), junior 0.466 → 0.494 (+0.028). TF-IDF: senior 0.278 → 0.295 (+0.017), junior 0.277 → 0.283 (+0.006). This is the "templating / LLM-drafting" hypothesis — 2026 postings are more internally uniform than 2024, even as the overall centroid moved. Robust across both representations, though magnitudes differ.

  4. 2026 junior postings are measurably more "junior-like" than 2024 base rates predict. For 5-NN retrieval on each 2026 J2 posting against the 2024 ref pool: embedding nearest-neighbor-junior rate = 11.6% (base 8.5% → +3.1pp excess); TF-IDF nearest-neighbor-junior rate = 16.3% (+7.9pp excess). Under TF-IDF, 2026 senior neighbors drop below base rate (−6.3pp). Modest but consistent signal that junior-coded 2026 postings have real textual similarity to 2024 junior postings — not just random noise.

  5. Outliers in 2026 entry-labeled postings are dominated by AI/ML research-engineer roles. 11 of the top 15 2026 junior-group outliers (entry-labeled postings with cosine distance >0.45 from group centroid) are neuromorphic / AI / LLM / generative-recommendation / on-chip-AI / algorithm-research roles. These are entry-labeled postings asking for graduate-level specialist expertise — a pattern we might call "senior entry." This corroborates the Gate 1 finding about 2026 tech-giant intern pipelines (Google, Walmart, Microsoft J3 20-74%) and raises the question of whether "entry" in 2026 means something different than in 2024.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Data. 34,102 SWE LinkedIn rows with text_source='llm' (embeddings exist only for LLM-cleaned text). Joined with metadata from swe_cleaned_text.parquet. Grouped by period_bucket × seniority_3level (8 cells) and additionally by source-aware groups {2024ar, 2024as, 2026} × {junior, mid, senior, unknown} (12 cells) for within-2024 calibration.

Sample. Stratified, up to 2,000 per period × seniority_3level cell (some cells smaller due to population):

group n 2024_junior 375 2024_mid 39 2024_senior 2,000 2024_unknown 2,000 2026_junior 601 2026_mid 51 2026_senior 2,000 2026_unknown 2,000 Total 9,066

Thin cells: 2024_mid (n=39) and 2026_mid (n=51) are below the 100 target — report but flag.

Representations. - Embedding: MiniLM all-MiniLM-L6-v2 384-dim from shared artifact; L2-normalized for cosine-as-dot. - TF-IDF-SVD: TfidfVectorizer(max_features=20000, min_df=5, ngram_range=(1,2), stop_words='english', sublinear_tf=True) on the sample's description_cleaned, then TruncatedSVD(n_components=100) (explained variance 14.7%). L2-normalized.

Trimmed centroids. For each group, compute raw centroid, cosine-similarity each member to centroid, drop 10% most distant members, recompute centroid and renormalize. Reduces outlier sensitivity per the T15 spec.

Convergence metric. Centroid-to-centroid cosine similarity between junior and senior groups within each period. Convergence = 2026 value rises above 2024.

Within-2024 calibration. Compute arshkon_junior vs asaniczka_junior centroid similarity (same seniority, different 2024 instruments). If that similarity is smaller than the arshkon → scraped centroid similarity on the same seniority, convergence is above noise.

Dispersion. Per group: (i) mean centroid similarity (each row-to-centroid cosine), (ii) mean pairwise cosine within the group (sampled to 500 if larger). Both metrics measure compactness — higher = more homogeneous.

kNN. 5-nearest-neighbor query: for each 2026 J2 posting, find 5 nearest 2024 postings, tally their seniority_3level label distribution. Compare to 2024 base rate (ref pool distribution) to compute excess.

Sensitivity representations. For each finding: report under both embedding and TF-IDF-SVD. "Robust" = same direction in both.

docs.title: 2. Convergence verdict
docs.text:
Trimmed junior-senior similarity within each period:

rep 2024 jr-sr 2026 jr-sr Δ direction embedding 0.980 0.972 −0.008 diverging (small) tfidf_svd 0.953 0.884 −0.068 diverging

Within-2024 calibration (arshkon vs asaniczka centroid similarity, same seniority, trimmed):

seniority embedding tfidf_svd junior 0.978 0.964 mid NaN (arshkon n=15) NaN senior 0.986 0.966 unknown 0.988 0.979

Calibration verdict:

  • Embedding, junior: within-2024 gap 0.022 (= 1 − 0.978). Cross-period divergence 0.008. Divergence is 36% of within-2024 noise → does NOT survive calibration.
  • TF-IDF, junior: within-2024 gap 0.036 (= 1 − 0.964). Cross-period divergence 0.068. Divergence is 1.9× within-2024 noise → just above SNR threshold of 2, marginal.

A stricter within-2024 comparison using the same methodology on the source-aware junior-vs-senior metric:

source/period jr-sr sim (tfidf) jr-sr sim (emb) 2024 arshkon 0.926 0.973 2024 asaniczka 0.952 0.979 2026 scraped 0.889 0.973

TF-IDF source-aware analysis: The 2024 arshkon vs 2024 asaniczka within-period delta is 0.026; 2026 vs 2024 delta on the same statistic is 0.037 (arshkon) or 0.063 (asaniczka). Under arshkon-only-vs-scraped, the signal barely clears noise. Under asaniczka-only-vs-scraped, it is 2.4× noise — but asaniczka's junior population (n=201 in 2024) is known to be unreliable as a junior proxy (T02).

Embedding source-aware analysis: 2026 junior-senior similarity (0.973) is identical to 2024 arshkon junior-senior similarity (0.973). Convergence is zero under the arshkon-primary baseline and 0.006 under the asaniczka baseline. Under the embedding representation, there is no convergence or divergence — the seniority signal is stable.

Bottom line: There is no robust evidence for junior-senior convergence or divergence at the embedding level. Under TF-IDF, there is weak evidence of divergence (driven by changing vocabulary) that survives to marginal significance but not to robustness across representations.

docs.title: 3. Within-group dispersion
docs.text:
group n emb mean_pairwise tfidf mean_pairwise 2024_junior 375 0.466 0.277 2024_senior 2,000 0.472 0.278 2026_junior 601 0.494 0.283 2026_senior 2,000 0.494 0.295

2026 groups are more internally homogeneous than 2024 groups on both representations. Effect: +0.022–0.028 on embedding; +0.006–0.017 on TF-IDF. This is a robust finding — both representations agree on direction and approximate magnitude (within 50%).

Mechanism candidates: - LLM-assisted drafting producing more templated text - Aggregator consolidation (more aggregators means more similar boilerplate) - Platform taxonomy convergence (LinkedIn standardizing posting structure) - AI-era terminology creating a denser narrow vocabulary that drags similarity up

Note: The within-group homogenization is larger than the between-group divergence, so the dominant change is "postings got more templated" rather than "seniority levels changed relative to each other."

docs.title: 4. Nearest-neighbor analysis
docs.text:
2026 J2 (entry+associate) → 5 nearest 2024 neighbors, label distribution:

label base rate 2024 emb NN rate emb excess tfidf NN rate tfidf excess junior 8.50% 11.63% +3.13pp 16.35% +7.85pp mid 0.88% 1.41% +0.53pp 0.95% +0.07pp senior 45.31% 45.74% +0.43pp 39.05% −6.26pp unknown 45.31% 41.23% −4.08pp 43.65% −1.66pp

Both representations show 2026 J2 postings have excess 2024-junior neighbors over base rate. TF-IDF signal is ~2.5× stronger than embedding (7.9pp vs 3.1pp excess). Under TF-IDF, senior neighbors are UNDER base rate (−6.3pp) — 2026 junior text vocabulary is distinguishable from 2024 senior text.

2026 S1 → 2024 neighbors (embedding):

label base rate 2024 emb NN rate excess junior 8.50% 4.76% −3.74pp mid 0.88% 1.13% +0.25pp senior 45.31% 59.50% +14.19pp unknown 45.31% 34.61% −10.70pp

Senior 2026 postings look strongly like 2024 senior postings (+14pp excess senior neighbors). The signal is stronger on the senior side than the junior side.

Interpretation. Seniority-aware text structure is preserved across periods. A 2026 junior posting looks more like a 2024 junior posting than chance. A 2026 senior posting looks more like a 2024 senior posting than chance. This is consistent with the dominant-axis finding: period shifts all postings uniformly, but preserves the relative seniority structure.

docs.title: 5. Robustness table
docs.text:
Finding Emb 2024 Emb 2026 Emb dir TF-IDF 2024 TF-IDF 2026 TF-IDF dir agrees Junior-senior centroid sim (trimmed) 0.980 0.972 diverging 0.953 0.884 diverging YES Senior group dispersion 0.472 0.494 more homogeneous 0.278 0.295 more homogeneous YES Junior group dispersion 0.466 0.494 more homogeneous 0.277 0.283 more homogeneous YES Within-2024 noise (jr rep sim) 0.978 (emb) — — 0.964 (tfidf) — — — Cross-period > within-2024 noise? 0.008 < 0.022 FAIL — 0.068 > 0.036 marginal — disagrees

All three directional findings are robust across representations. The calibration verdict differs: embedding says cross-period divergence is below noise; TF-IDF says it is ~2× noise. This is a known tension — TF-IDF picks up vocabulary shifts more sharply than MiniLM embeddings, which are trained on general semantic similarity. Neither representation supports "convergence" — only the direction of divergence is marginal vs robust.

docs.title: 6. Outlier identification — top 50 per group × 8 groups = 400 outliers saved
docs.text:
Representative 2026 junior outliers (highest cosine distance from group centroid):

distance title/description preview 0.60 "neuromorphic & ai... opportunity to gpu-heavy deep... brainchip's akida..." 0.58 "motivated software... state-of-the-art... on-chip... in parasitic..." 0.58 "paypal's routing platform team... l7 routing... 9 api with zero-downtime availability..." 0.55 "cloud engineer i... research (uarc)... institutions... high-performance azure..." 0.55 "e-commerce and team... reducing costs... (ml), research..." 0.52 "3d aren't just part of our product, they are the product..." 0.52 "develop llm-based ccr/voc... llm-based design and implement..." 0.51 "robinhood's platform... event-driven... defining, validating, services that scale predictably..." 0.51 "netflix's vision... research partners (early-stage phd...)..." 0.50 "cutting-edge research... generative recommendation, weakly-supervised..."

11 of 15 top 2026 junior outliers are AI/ML research-engineer postings with senior-sounding requirements (PhD partnerships, neuromorphic, generative recommendation, cutting-edge research, LLM architecture). These are entry-labeled postings at Netflix/PayPal/Robinhood/BrainChip/tech research groups asking for specialist expertise. They pull the 2026_junior centroid off-center and explain why 2026_junior has higher internal dispersion on the variance metric (std 0.081 vs 2024_junior 0.077).

This is a specific mechanism for the "2026 entry means something different" hypothesis: 2026 tech-giant intern/entry pipelines include a visible tail of quasi-senior research-role-for-new-grads postings that don't exist in the 2024 sample.

docs.title: Dominant axis of variation
docs.text:
The T15 spec asks: what is the dominant axis — seniority, period, or tech domain?

Period dominates. Mean within-period cross-seniority sim = 0.9811; mean cross-period same-seniority sim = 0.9709. Delta 0.0181. The seniority-within-period delta averages 0.0001. Period is ~180× more important than seniority for embedding-centroid distance in this sample.

The UMAP plot shows this visually: 2024 and 2026 point clouds overlap heavily but have distinct density peaks. Within each period, seniority levels overlap substantially.

Comparison to T09's NMI result (not yet available). T09 archetype labels had not landed at T15 runtime (2026-04-17). Once available, we expect NMI(archetype, seniority) > NMI(archetype, period) — archetypes are domain-defined (Frontend/Embedded/ML) and should cut across both axes. Testing this is deferred to a post-T09 re-run.

If the T09 result is that archetypes explain more variance than period, the interpretation is: tech-domain structure is preserved across 2024→2026, and "period" in T15 is actually capturing the aggregate AI-era shift superimposed on all archetypes. If T09 shows period > archetype, then the "2024 vs 2026 is a broader shift" story holds.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text: Not applied. Aggregators are 8-16% of each cell; their embeddings cluster into a small region of the UMAP but don't dominate the centroid. A follow-up excluding them would be worth running if convergence is revisited in Wave 3.

docs.title: (c) T30 seniority panel
docs.text: J2 and J3 cells were reported where defined. MID_SENIOR and S1 are essentially coincident (director is 400 rows out of 31K S1). S4 (yoe≥5) cell was not computed separately because yoe_extracted coverage on embedded rows drops the sample substantially; a re-run using yoe-based seniority is recommended if Wave 3 wants to cross-check the junior-vs-senior stability result.

docs.title: (d) Description text source
docs.text: Embedding and TF-IDF both run on description_cleaned with text_source='llm' filter (34K rows). Running on the raw-fallback subset (15K scraped rows) would test whether the length/boilerplate difference inflates 2026 internal homogeneity. Not done here — the T15 spec explicitly restricts to text_source='llm' for fair embedding comparison.

docs.title: (e) Source restriction
docs.text: Source-aware analysis reported above: the convergence verdict flips depending on whether you use arshkon-only or asaniczka-only as the 2024 baseline. The TF-IDF junior-senior divergence is 0.037 under arshkon-only (noise floor 0.037) and 0.063 under asaniczka-only (noise floor 0.036). Under embeddings, divergence is 0.000 vs 0.006. The junior-senior divergence story under TF-IDF is asaniczka-driven.

docs.title: (f) Within-2024 calibration
docs.text: Reported above. Embedding: no evidence of cross-period signal beyond within-2024 noise. TF-IDF: marginal.

docs.title: (g) SWE classification tier
docs.text: Not applied; tech matrix and embeddings used all is_swe=true rows.

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Seniority levels barely differ in embedding space — within-period junior-senior cosine is 0.980. In embedding space, the distance between a junior posting and a senior posting is about the same as the distance between two senior postings. The seniority axis is almost invisible to MiniLM. This is consistent with T02's finding that asaniczka associate is a weak junior proxy; it extends that to say that seniority structure as-written in SWE JDs is remarkably subtle at the semantic level. TF-IDF picks up more of it (0.889 2026 junior-senior similarity vs 0.973 on embedding) because TF-IDF is vocabulary-sensitive and junior JDs use vocabulary like "entry" / "new grad" / "junior" that embeddings normalize over.

  2. The "2026 postings are more homogeneous" finding runs opposite to the naive intuition. We might expect 2026 to be more diverse because (a) the scraper collects a broader employer base and (b) AI-era postings bring new specialized niches. Neither prediction holds: 2026 is more homogeneous on both representations. Mechanism is probably templating (LLM-drafting, platform-standardization) rather than vocabulary shrinking.

  3. The 2026 J2 cell has a visible "senior entry" outlier tail. Top 15 outlying entry-labeled 2026 postings are research-engineer roles at tech giants. This is an emergent pattern in 2026 that doesn't exist in 2024 samples. It probably explains part of the Gate 1 finding that "J3 rise is 95% between-company compositional (Google, Walmart, Microsoft intern pipelines)" — those pipelines' JDs are senior-styled but entry-labeled.

  4. Embedding and TF-IDF disagree on convergence sharpness but agree on direction. TF-IDF shows 4× larger divergence than embedding. This is a representation artifact: TF-IDF is vocabulary-sensitive (2026 AI vocabulary pulls text apart), embedding is semantics-preserving (AI terms get mapped into the general-tech neighborhood). The "right" representation depends on the paper's question: is convergence about meaning or vocabulary? If meaning → embedding → no convergence. If vocabulary → TF-IDF → marginal divergence.

  5. Period dominates by ~180× is a genuinely large ratio. If period is the dominant axis, then paper-level cross-period comparisons that don't control for the period shift will confound every other signal. The correct analytical move is to always report 2024-vs-2026 comparisons within seniority strata, not pooled.

  6. The 2024 mid cell (n=39 arshkon, n=24 asaniczka) is too small for convergence testing. We cannot compute 2024_mid centroid robustly. The T15 spec asks for period × seniority_3level centroids; the mid cell is a known power gap.

  7. The kNN analysis strongly contradicts "convergence." If entry and senior converged, 2026 J2 postings would find more senior neighbors in 2024 than base rate. Instead they find more junior neighbors (+3pp emb, +8pp TF-IDF). 2026 junior postings are more "junior-like" in retrieval space than a period-indifferent baseline would predict.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• n=39 and n=51 for 2024_mid and 2026_mid. Mid centroids are noisy. Don't over-interpret the 2024_mid row in the similarity matrix.
  • MiniLM embeddings truncate at ~500 tokens. Scraped 2026 descriptions mean-length 2,630 chars ≈ 650 tokens. Some 2026 postings are embedded only partially. 2024 descriptions mean-length 1,500 chars ≈ 370 tokens, fully covered. This could inflate 2024-within-2024 similarity slightly (more of the text is in the embedding) relative to 2026. Not expected to affect direction of findings.
  • TF-IDF SVD explained variance is only 14.7% — most of the term-frequency variance is in higher components. The 100-dim SVD captures the top axes but not the tail. Raw TF-IDF would be more discriminating but infeasible for the centroid-similarity computation at scale.
  • The text_source='llm' filter reduces the scraped sample by ~70%. Only 12,456 of 40,881 scraped rows are embedded. Findings are conditional on the LLM-cleaned subset and may not generalize to the raw-fallback subset (which has different length distribution and more boilerplate).
  • T09 archetype labels not available at runtime. Per-archetype convergence analysis is deferred.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• T09 archetype post-hoc analysis. When T09 labels land, re-run: (i) compute archetype-level NMI against period vs seniority, (ii) compute junior-senior centroid similarity within each archetype. If ML/AI archetype shows strong junior-senior convergence and Frontend shows none, the Gate 1 "AI-mention rose +33pp within-company" finding gains a mechanism.
  • T16 (company clustering). The outlier subset (300 postings most distant from their group centroid) is a candidate sample for "companies posting atypical text" — useful for clustering into strategy archetypes.
  • T22 (ghost forensics). The 2026 junior outlier pattern (entry-labeled but senior-styled at tech giants) is a direct test case for aspirational/ghost patterns. Do those postings fill at high rates? The data doesn't tell, but the pattern is worth flagging.
  • Paper-level framing. Any cross-period comparison must control for period shift via within-seniority stratification. Pooled comparisons mix a ~180× period effect with a ~1× seniority effect.
  • Preprocessing follow-up. Consider embedding the raw-fallback subset separately and comparing. If they cluster very differently from LLM-cleaned rows, there is a Stage 9 coverage confound worth quantifying.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• Tables: exploration/tables/T15/centroid_sim_embedding.csv, centroid_sim_tfidf.csv, centroid_sim_embedding_srcaware.csv, centroid_sim_tfidf_srcaware.csv, convergence_trimmed.csv, within2024_calibration.csv, dispersion.csv, knn_2026j2_to_2024.csv, knn_2026s1_to_2024.csv, robustness_table.csv, outliers.csv (300 rows), sample_with_2d.parquet, summary.json.
  • Figures: exploration/figures/T15/umap_pca_landscape.png (UMAP+PCA, 2x2 with centroid movement arrows), umap_density_contours.png (publication-quality density contours), tsne_landscape.png, centroid_similarity_heatmap.png.
  • Script: exploration/scripts/T15_semantic_landscape.py.

docs.title: T16 — Company Hiring Strategy Typology
docs.text: Agent J, Wave 3. Date: 2026-04-17. Inputs: data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (LLM-cleaned), swe_tech_matrix.parquet, swe_archetype_labels.parquet, entry_specialist_employers.csv. Default filters: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true AND company_name_canonical <> ''.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. AI-mention rise is 102% within-company (STRICT primary). On the T16 overlap panel (240 companies, arshkon≥3 & scraped≥3), the Oaxaca decomposition of binary AI-mention (STRICT pattern) gives aggregate +12.1pp, within-company +12.4pp, between-company −0.3pp. The within component EXCEEDS the aggregate because composition shift (weight reallocation across these 240 cos) is slightly AI-dilutive. Robust 4-of-5 across sensitivities (all-cos, no-specialist, no-aggregator, cap-50, pooled-2024). Under the BROAD pattern (strict + \bai\b|\bml\b|machine learning|llm|...), within = 27.1pp of aggregate +39.1pp = 69% within — still majority within-company but with real composition contribution. This RAISES T06's earlier "91% within-company" finding on AI-mention: on the T16 overlap panel with the V1-refined strict vocabulary, within-company is the near-entirety of the signal.

  2. Length-residualized requirement_breadth rises +1.4 within-company on the primary panel (aggregate +1.40, within +1.43, between −0.03). The within-company share is 102% — i.e., the same companies are writing broader requirements in 2026 as in 2024, even after removing the length-proxy component (residualizing on cleaned description length). 76% of panel companies have positive residualized breadth Δ; 62% have Δ > 1.0 (large positive). This is the cleanest within-company test of scope inflation that survives V1's length-residualization critique.

  3. Five clusters emerge from k-means on the change vector, silhouette best at k=5 (0.188). The content-based names (derived from mean Δ on each dimension) are: (0) "Entry-contracting retreat" (n=15, −32pp entry-J2, modest +11pp AI, desc shrinks); (1) "AI-forward tech-stacking" (n=25, +41pp AI-strict, +3.4 tech, +3.9 breadth_resid); (2) "Mass-hiring tech-stackers" (n=84, modest +7pp AI, +1.6 tech, +1.6 breadth_resid, huge n_sc — includes JPMorgan, Amazon, Capital One); (3) "Broad-reqs tech-stacking" (n=54, +12pp AI, +4.8 tech, +4.8 breadth_resid — includes Booz Allen, TCS, Adobe); (4) "Consolidating giants" (n=62, +10pp AI, slight entry ramp, −0.5 tech, −1.2 breadth_resid — Google, Microsoft, AWS, Wells Fargo). Clusters are distinguishable on ALL dimensions simultaneously, not collapsed to a single axis.

  4. New entrants look MORE like generalists than returning specialists, not less. 5,916 new-entrant cos in scraped (74.5% of scraped company set; consistent with T06). New-vs-returning profile: AI-strict 16.1% vs 13.0% (new HIGHER), mean breadth_resid 0.44 vs 0.58 (new LOWER), mean tech_count 6.98 vs 6.94 (≈ same), J2 3.4% vs 3.1% (≈ same), aggregator rate 20.0% vs 13.9% (new more aggregator-heavy), specialist rate 6.0% vs 12.6% (new LESS specialist-dense). Finding: new entrants are AI-ambient and aggregator-leaning, but they are NOT driving the scope-inflation signal — that's a within-company phenomenon among the 240-co overlap panel.

  5. Aggregators do not have meaningfully different trajectories from direct employers. Mean change vectors for aggregator-heavy (agg_rate_sc ≥ 0.5, n=36) vs direct employer (n=204): d_ai_strict +0.141 vs +0.124 (nearly equal), d_entry_j2 +0.011 vs −0.016 (aggregators ramped entry modestly; directs dropped it modestly), d_mean_breadth_resid +1.78 vs +1.79 (essentially identical), d_mean_tech_count +1.77 vs +1.88 (close). Aggregators did NOT drive scope inflation differentially.

  6. Labeled-subset sensitivity: within-AI finding holds; within-J2 finding FLIPS (as V1 predicted). On the 196-company labeled-only overlap panel: ai_strict aggregate +11.4pp, within +12.0pp (cleanly within-company again). But entry_share_j2 aggregate flips from −0.5pp (full panel) to +2.0pp (labeled-only), with within +0.6pp. V1's "LLM-frame J2 flip" is corroborated at the company-level. Any J2-headline within-company claim must be reported for both the full panel AND the labeled-only subset.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Panels. 240 cos with ≥3 arshkon LinkedIn SWE + ≥3 scraped LinkedIn SWE (primary); 589 cos with ≥3 pooled-2024 LinkedIn SWE + ≥3 scraped LinkedIn SWE (secondary pooled); 196-co labeled-only subset (sensitivity on LLM-frame).

Metrics per company-period. (a) Entry shares: J1 (seniority_final='entry'), J2 (IN ('entry','associate')), J3 (yoe≤2). (b) AI-mention binary under STRICT pattern (V1-refined: copilot|cursor|claude|chatgpt|openai api|gpt-?\d+|gemini|codex|llamaindex|langchain|prompt engineering|fine-tuning|rag|vector database|pinecone|huggingface; agent_bare and mcp dropped per V1) and BROAD pattern (STRICT ∪ \bai\b|\bml\b|machine learning|llm|generative ai|genai|anthropic). (c) Mean raw description length, mean LLM-cleaned description length. (d) Mean tech_count (from shared tech matrix). (e) Org-scope count per 1k cleaned chars. (f) Composite breadth_raw = tech_count + org_scope_count + cred_count + soft_count (cleaned text). (g) breadth_resid = breadth_raw − (6.498 + 0.00182 × cleaned_length) (OLS length-residualization, global fit).

Decomposition. Symmetric Oaxaca: aggregate Δ = Σw_sc·y_sc/Σw_sc − Σw_ar·y_ar/Σw_ar; within = Σ s_avg·(y_sc − y_ar); between = Σ (s_sc − s_ar)·y_avg; where s = w/Σw and s_avg = (s_ar + s_sc)/2, y_avg = (y_ar + y_sc)/2. This splits the aggregate into (same-company change weighted by avg share) + (weight-reallocation term).

Clustering. Standardized 6-dim change vector: (Δ-J2, Δ-ai_strict, Δ-cleaned_len, Δ-tech, Δ-org_scope, Δ-breadth_resid). k-means k∈{3,4,5,6}, n_init=20/50, random_state=42. Silhouette scores: k=3 0.155, k=4 0.169, k=5 0.188, k=6 0.184. Selected k=5.

Sensitivities. (a) full 240-co panel; (b) no-specialist (221 cos; drop 19 of the 240-co specialists); (c) no-aggregator (213 cos; drop row-level aggregators then re-aggregate); (d) pooled-2024 baseline (589 cos); (e) cap-per-company at 50 postings (240 cos but sampled); (f) labeled-only subset (196 cos).

docs.title: 1. Overlap panel composition
docs.text:
Panel n cos n_ar total n_sc total arshkon-side median per co scraped-side median per co Primary (arshkon≥3 & scraped≥3) 240 ~1,940 ~13,440 7 29 Pooled (pooled-2024≥3 & scraped≥3) 589 ~11,640 ~23,500 8 17 Labeled-only (subset of primary) 196 ~1,425 ~10,600 6 26

Panel is overwhelmingly scraped-heavy — a characteristic of the corpus (arshkon: 9,300 total SWE rows; scraped SWE: 40,881 total, post-Mar-2026). Cap-50 sensitivity addresses this: the decomposition is stable under cap.

docs.title: 2. Within-vs-between decomposition on primary panel (240 cos)
docs.text:
Metric Aggregate Δ Within Δ Between Δ % within ar agg sc agg entry_share_j1 −0.44pp −0.95pp +0.50pp 214% (flip) 3.46% 3.02% entry_share_j2 −0.53pp −1.08pp +0.55pp 203% (flip) 3.74% 3.21% entry_share_j3 (yoe≤2) +5.86pp +0.21pp +5.65pp 4% 9.43% 15.29% ai_strict +12.09pp +12.36pp −0.27pp 102% 0.89% 12.98% ai_broad +39.05pp +27.07pp +11.98pp 69% 14.62% 53.67% mean_desc_length_raw +1,847 +935 +913 51% 3,473 5,320 mean_desc_length_cleaned −760 −848 +89 112% 1,265 506 mean_tech_count +1.40 +1.76 −0.36 125% 5.52 6.93 mean_org_scope_per_1k −0.097 −0.126 +0.029 129% 0.209 0.112 mean_breadth_raw +0.015 −0.115 +0.131 — 8.09 8.10 mean_breadth_resid +1.40 +1.43 −0.03 102% −0.72 +0.68

Interpretation. AI-strict, breadth_resid, tech_count, cleaned_length shrink, and org_scope per 1k are all >100% within-company — meaning the primary signal is the SAME companies rewriting their postings, with composition shift either neutral or slightly muting the aggregate. J3 (+5.86pp) is overwhelmingly between-company (96%) — consistent with T06's finding that J3 rise is a composition / tech-giant-intern-pipeline story. J2 direction is tiny and within-company is −1.08pp (direction −, magnitude small) in the full panel.

docs.title: 3. Decomposition sensitivities (key metrics)
docs.text:
ai_strict (STRICT, V1-refined):

Spec n Aggregate Within Between % within arshkon_vs_scraped_all 240 +12.09pp +12.36pp −0.27pp 102% no_specialist 221 +12.71pp +13.00pp −0.29pp 102% no_aggregator 213 +12.15pp +12.54pp −0.40pp 103% pooled2024_vs_scraped 589 +11.49pp +10.38pp +1.10pp 90% cap50 240 +12.41pp +12.73pp −0.32pp 103% labeled_only 196 +11.45pp +12.02pp −0.57pp 105%

Within-company share: 90–105% across all six specs. The pooled baseline reduces the within-share modestly (90%) because pooled-2024 adds more small-n asaniczka-only cos where any ai-mention in 2026 is amplified.

mean_breadth_resid (length-residualized):

Spec n Aggregate Within Between % within arshkon_vs_scraped_all 240 +1.40 +1.43 −0.03 102% no_specialist 221 +1.88 +1.77 +0.12 94% no_aggregator 213 +1.20 +1.25 −0.05 104% pooled2024_vs_scraped 589 +1.05 +1.54 −0.49 147% cap50 240 +1.59 +1.76 −0.17 111% labeled_only 196 +1.62 +1.64 −0.01 101%

Within-share 94–147% across all specs; the pooled-baseline outlier (147%) reflects negative-composition (new entrants pull breadth down), so the same-company signal is amplified when that composition effect is netted out. Scope inflation is a within-company phenomenon, robust across all sensitivities.

entry_share_j2 (full vs labeled — V1 flip):

Spec n Aggregate Within Between arshkon_vs_scraped_all 240 −0.53pp −1.08pp +0.55pp labeled_only 196 +1.99pp +0.60pp +1.39pp

Exactly the V1-predicted flip. Under the full panel J2 direction is negative (within-company slightly down); under labeled-only it is positive (within-company slightly up). Both are small in magnitude but opposite in sign. We do not use J2 direction as a within-company headline.

docs.title: 4. Company clusters (k=5; silhouette 0.188)
docs.text:
Cluster n cos mean d_J2 mean d_ai_strict mean d_cleaned_len mean d_tech mean d_org_scope mean d_breadth_resid Content-based name Top cos (by n_sc) 0 15 −32pp +11pp −1,033 +0.04 −0.014 −1.08 Entry-contracting retreat Boeing, Dexian, Docusign, Intel, INSPYR 1 25 −1pp +41pp −536 +3.44 +0.049 +3.87 AI-forward tech-stacking Deloitte, ICE, Pinterest, ConsultNet, VDart 2 84 0pp +7pp −514 +1.57 +0.004 +1.58 Mass-hiring mainstream (modest AI) JPMorgan, Amazon, Actalent, Motion Recruit., Capital One 3 54 0pp +12pp −944 +4.84 −0.173 +4.84 Broad-reqs tech-stacking Booz Allen, TCS, Adobe, Randstad, BLUE ORIGIN 4 62 +4pp +10pp −1,240 −0.52 −0.241 −0.74 Consolidating giants (entry-ramping) Google, Microsoft, AWS, Wells Fargo, Anduril

The naming is evidence-based: Cluster 0 has the ONLY substantial entry retreat (−32pp J2, concentrated at Boeing, Intel, Docusign — defense-industrial + legacy tech contracting entry hiring). Cluster 1's +41pp AI-strict is by far the biggest cluster-level AI jump (top exemplars are consulting firms with AI practice lines). Cluster 2 is the mainstream — 84 cos, tech/finance/staffing, modest but real +7pp AI and +1.6 tech (JPMorgan, Amazon, Capital One are the highest-volume mainstream posters). Cluster 3 is tech-stacking with large breadth growth (TCS, BLUE ORIGIN, Booz Allen — "broad-reqs" consultancies + gov-contractors). Cluster 4 is the consolidating giants: Google, Microsoft, AWS are HERE not in "AI-forward" — they shrank their tech lists and breadth, and they are the ones ramping entry share by +4pp. Google is NOT an "AI-forward" company by its posting-change vector; it is a "consolidating giant" that's running larger new-grad pipelines. Membership CSV: exploration/tables/T16/04_company_cluster_membership.csv (240 rows).

docs.title: 5. Within-company scope inflation distribution
docs.text:
Per-company Δ breadth_resid (length-residualized): n=240, mean +1.79, median +1.88, share positive 76.3%, share large-positive (>1.0) 61.7%.

The distribution is right-skewed, centered safely above 0, with a long positive tail. Figure 03_breadth_change_distribution.png shows the histogram. 76% of same companies broadened requirements 2024→2026 (after netting out length inflation) — and 62% broadened by a lot (>1 composite unit).

docs.title: 6. New-entrants profile
docs.text:
5,916 new-entrant cos (74.5% of scraped SWE companies; 18,317 scraped rows). Returning cos: 2,024 (22,564 rows).

Metric New entrants Returning Δ (new − ret) ai_strict 16.1% 13.0% +3.1pp ai_broad 51.1% 51.1% 0.0pp mean tech_count 6.98 6.94 +0.04 mean breadth_resid 0.44 0.58 −0.14 mean desc_length_raw 4,629 5,142 −514 j1 3.30% 2.97% +0.34pp j2 3.37% 3.15% +0.22pp j3 (yoe≤2) 9.27% 13.49% −4.22pp mean yoe 5.16 4.96 +0.19 aggregator rate 20.0% 13.9% +6.1pp specialist rate 5.96% 12.63% −6.7pp

Top industries for new entrants: Software Development (6,104), IT Services (1,783), Tech Info & Internet (1,262), Financial Services (1,005), Defense & Space (361). New entrants are NOT disproportionately entry-ramping (J3 is LOWER for new than returning — 9.3% vs 13.5%). New-entrants lead returning on AI-strict (+3.1pp) but both on ai_broad are the same. New entrants are not the "AI-forward disruptor" cohort that the headline decomposition might suggest.

docs.title: 7. Aggregator vs direct employer trajectories (overlap panel)
docs.text:
Group n cos mean n_ar mean n_sc d_J2 d_ai_strict d_cleaned_len d_tech d_org_scope d_breadth_resid Direct employers (agg_rate_sc<0.5) 204 6.7 47.0 −0.016 +0.124 −858 +1.88 −0.103 +1.79 Aggregator-heavy (agg_rate_sc≥0.5) 36 11.7 53.3 +0.011 +0.141 −693 +1.77 −0.053 +1.78

Aggregators and direct employers show essentially IDENTICAL trajectories on AI, tech, breadth_resid, and org_scope. They differ only modestly on J2 direction and on cleaned length contraction (direct employers contracted more). Aggregator-exclusion is NOT a confound for the within-company scope-inflation or AI finding (consistent with decomposition sensitivity showing the finding survives dropping aggregators).

docs.title: 8. Within-company × within-archetype AI-strict Δ (two-way decomposition)
docs.text:
Using T09 archetype labels joined to the overlap panel: 19 archetypes are represented. Per-archetype mean Δ ai_strict:

Archetype n company-archetype pairs mean d_ai_strict median d_ai_strict ai_ml_engineering 9 +46.8pp +50.0pp technology_leadership 3 +22.2pp 0.0pp unlabeled 214 +12.8pp +10.0pp cloud_devops 15 +8.9pp 0.0pp generic_software_engineer 33 +6.7pp 0.0pp java_spring_backend 15 +6.7pp 0.0pp data_engineering 19 +2.6pp 0.0pp frontend_react, mobile, systems, qa, network, etc. varies 0.0pp 0.0pp

AI-mention growth is concentrated in AI-adjacent archetypes (ai_ml_engineering +47pp at the company-archetype level; technology_leadership +22pp; generic +7pp). This is consistent with the domain-dominant NMI result from T09: the AI-mention rise is (a) within-company (102% of aggregate) AND (b) domain-selective (ML/AI archetypes grow fastest, while frontend/mobile/QA archetypes barely move). Within a given domain archetype, the same company adds AI vocabulary; across domains, the AI-specific archetypes grow fastest. These are independent effects.

docs.title: 9. V1 Gate 2 directives — compliance
docs.text:
• Primary J = J2; senior S = S1. Done. Decomposition run on J1/J2/J3; J2 flagged as flipping under labeled-only.
  • Senior findings lead arshkon-only. T16 is structural (junior-side stratification less central); both arshkon-only and pooled-2024 reported for all decompositions.
  • Strict AI-mention primary. Done. SNR 38.7 pattern. ai_broad reported as sensitivity.
  • breadth length-residualized when cited. Done. Coefs a=6.498, b=0.00182 (global OLS); mean_breadth_resid is the primary within-company breadth metric.
  • Specialist exclusion sensitivity. Done. 240→221 cos in no-specialist; all decomposition runs preserved.
  • JOLTS low context. Finding framed as share-of-SWE; absolute-volume comparisons avoided.
  • 31 GB RAM. DuckDB via pyarrow-chunked reads; no full-parquet pandas loads.

docs.title: Implications for RQ1a / RQ1b
docs.text:
RQ1a (junior share / employer restructuring of who gets hired). The within-company entry-share Δ is tiny in magnitude and direction-dependent on J definition (J1/J2 down ~0.5pp full panel; J2 flips to +2pp under labeled-only; J3 +5.9pp overwhelmingly between-company). The "same companies changed who they hired" hypothesis is weak on the T16 panel: 96% of the J3 rise is driven by weight reallocation across companies (new-entrant pipelines, tech-giant intern ramps), not by established posters changing their intake mix. The one cluster that DID contract entry (Cluster 0, n=15: Boeing, Intel, Docusign, Dexian, INSPYR) is a small concentrated segment, not a pervasive pattern. This corroborates the Wave-2 narrative: aggregate entry change is mostly composition; the period-rewriting story is what's real, not a hiring-mix story.

RQ1b (scope inflation within the same jobs). Length-residualized requirement_breadth is 102% within-company on the primary panel (+1.43 of +1.40 aggregate), and the within-share is 94–147% across all five sensitivities. 76% of same-company pairs have positive Δ; 62% have Δ > 1.0. This is the cleanest within-company test of scope inflation we've generated, and the result is affirmative: the same companies ARE writing broader requirements in 2026 (as measured by tech + soft + cred + org-scope counts) even after controlling for length. The mean Δ of 1.43 composite units is roughly a 20% rise relative to arshkon baseline (8.09 → 9.52 raw; length-residualized adds clarity). Mean tech_count also rises +1.76 within-company (from 5.52 to 7.28 raw tech items). Org_scope per 1k FALLS within-company (−0.126), which is interesting: companies are adding MORE diverse techs + credentials + soft skills, but the cross-functional/stakeholder/ownership language is actually getting DENSER-packed (total count up, but per-1k-char rate down because overall cleaned length went down). V1's interpretation ("breadth is 71% content / 29% length") holds at the within-company level as well.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Cleaned description length SHRANK −848 chars within-company on the primary panel, even as raw length grew +935 chars within-company. This is the boilerplate-growth story: raw length includes EEO / benefits / recruiter-boilerplate which grew a lot, while LLM-cleaned (core posting) SHRANK. Within-company cleaned length is −67% of aggregate: companies are writing leaner core requirements and wrapping them in more boilerplate. Consistent with T13's finding that requirements SHRANK while benefits + role_summary + about_company grew. This contradicts the naive "longer postings = more scope" reading; scope inflation is happening in the core, not in the wrapping.

  2. Google is a "consolidating giant" (cluster 4), not an "AI-forward" company by change-vector. Google's d_ai_strict is only +9.4pp — below average for the panel. Google's d_tech is +1.37, d_breadth_resid is −3.2 (!), d_cleaned_len is −850. Google is writing LEANER, NARROWER postings in 2026 while ramping its new-grad funnel (TikTok-clone d_J2 +0.9pp; modest). The "tech giants are the AI vanguard" intuition is wrong on this data: consulting firms (Deloitte, ICE, ConsultNet, VDart, Pinterest) are cluster-1 "AI-forward" and are the ones adding the most AI vocabulary per posting.

  3. Aggregator-heavy cos changed their ai_strict rate by +14.1pp vs direct employers +12.4pp — nearly identical. I expected aggregators to have structurally different trajectories (less context, more boilerplate, more recruiter-posted). They don't. Both subsets converged on similar AI-mention and similar breadth growth. This means the "aggregator = different market" framing is less central to T16 than we hypothesized pre-dispatch.

  4. V1's flipped J2 reproduces cleanly at the company level. I confirmed that J2 direction is SIGN-DEPENDENT on whether you use full panel (−0.53pp aggregate) or labeled-only (+1.99pp). Not just row-level — it flips at the company-panel level too. This is more disturbing than V1 suggested: within-company J2 is not a stable headline under any reasonable presentation.

  5. New entrants' J3 share is LOWER than returning, not higher. Returning cos have J3 = 13.5%; new entrants have J3 = 9.3%. The "new entrants are ramping juniors" hypothesis that would reconcile T06's between-company J3 story is WRONG at the cohort level: new-entrant cohorts post OLDER (mean yoe 5.16) jobs than returning cohorts. The J3 rise is coming from returning-but-reweighted cos (Google/Microsoft/Walmart-type intern pipelines becoming more visible), not from a surge of young companies hiring juniors.

  6. Breadth_resid median per co is +1.88 — larger than the mean (+1.79). Distribution is actually LEFT-skewed (a handful of companies SHRANK breadth dramatically, e.g., Intel −11.2, General Motors −1.6, Viasat −4.2; these are cluster-0 "Entry-contracting retreat"). For the typical (median) company, the scope inflation is MORE pronounced than the average shows. Scope inflation is the modal company's story, not just a fat-tailed few.

  7. The ai_ml_engineering archetype's +47pp within-company AI-strict growth happens in only 9 company-archetype pairs, but it's massive. This is a "specialist fast-growers" signature: cos that already posted ML roles in 2024 dramatically intensified their AI vocabulary by 2026. If we're using AI-mention as a labor-market diagnostic, the ML-archetype contribution is heavily disproportionate.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Overlap panel is scraped-heavy (6× more scraped rows than arshkon). Cap-50 sensitivity (decomp_arsh_scraped_cap50.csv) confirms the findings hold under equal-weight sampling, so the heavy-weighting of the scraped side is not driving the results.
  • Pooled-2024 baseline introduces asaniczka-only cos. 589 vs 240 co difference; these are lower-precision-on-entry-labels (asaniczka missing native entry labels per T02). Reported as sensitivity only.
  • agg_heavy definition uses agg_rate_sc ≥ 0.5. Only 36 of 240 panel cos are agg-heavy. Small-n comparison.
  • Breadth_resid uses GLOBAL OLS fit. Company-by-company residualization would be more principled but destroys the aggregate interpretation. Global fit means some short-desc cos have negatively-biased breadth_resid absolute levels, but Δ-within-company is unbiased.
  • Labeled-only panel shrinks panel from 240 to 196 cos. 44 cos are entirely scraped-unlabeled or arshkon-unlabeled at the labeled-at-n≥2 threshold. Results are close enough (within-AI consistent) that this is a minor coverage issue.
  • Only 19 of 240 panel cos are flagged specialists. The no-specialist sensitivity drops 19 cos; the finding survives. This means T06's 240 specialist flag is mostly NOT in the stable overlap panel — it's concentrated in new entrants and small-n cos.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks (T18+, V2, T24 synthesis)
docs.text:
• RQ1a narrative for T24 synthesis: entry-share rise is a between-company (composition / new-entrant + intern-pipeline) story; NOT a within-company restructuring story. J2 direction is unstable (flips with text-frame).
  • RQ1b narrative for T24 synthesis: scope inflation IS a within-company story, robust across sensitivities. Length-residualized breadth rises +1.43 on average within the same 240 panel cos. The magnitude (about 20% of baseline) is substantively large.
  • V2 verification priorities: (a) re-derive ai_strict within/between decomposition with an independent regex + a semantic sample, (b) verify the breadth_resid residualization coefficients match V1's (a=6.498, b=0.00182), (c) sanity-check cluster naming against the top-10 exemplars per cluster, (d) verify the labeled-only J2 flip at the company-panel level.
  • For T17 (geographic): domain archetype composition varies geographically (e.g., SF Bay = 32% ML archetype in scraped); T17 should inspect metro-level AI surge in concert with domain concentration.
  • For T21 (senior role evolution): Cluster 4 "consolidating giants" have d_entry_j2 +4pp — these are the cos where entry share rises WITHIN the established posting base. Worth isolating senior-rewriting within these 62 cos.
  • For T23 (employer-usage divergence): cluster-1 "AI-forward tech-stacking" (Deloitte, ICE, Pinterest, etc.) is the cohort with +41pp AI-mention. Any usage-vs-posting mismatch benchmark should oversample these cos.

docs.text:

docs.title: T17 — Geographic Market Structure
docs.text: Agent J, Wave 3. Date: 2026-04-17. Inputs: data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet, swe_tech_matrix.parquet, swe_archetype_labels.parquet, entry_specialist_employers.csv. Default filters: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true. Geographic unit: metro_area (preprocessing metro-normalized field; is_multi_location rows excluded by construction — not in metro rollups).

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. 26 metros are well-powered under pooled-2024 baseline (≥50 SWE/period); 19 are well-powered under arshkon-only. Matches T07's feasibility forecast (23/26 metros well-powered for J1 under pooled-2024). Report leads with pooled-2024 for metro-level J1/J2 analyses; arshkon-only reported as sensitivity.

  2. AI-mention (STRICT) surged in EVERY one of the 26 well-powered metros — there is no metro in this panel with an AI decline. Mean Δ_ai_strict +12.8pp, std 3.7pp, CV 0.29 — moderate metro heterogeneity, but the phenomenon is NOT tech-hub-concentrated. Tech hubs (SF Bay, NYC, Seattle, Austin, LA, Boston) mean Δ_ai_strict +13.1pp; non-hubs +12.7pp — essentially identical. The AI surge is geographically uniform.

  3. Top 3 metros by AI-strict surge: Tampa Bay (+20.0pp), Atlanta (+18.6pp), Charlotte (+17.2pp). Surprising: NONE of the top 3 is a traditional tech hub; these are financial-services / healthcare / aerospace / defense metros where AI vocabulary is being adopted at higher marginal rates than in baseline-high SF/NYC/Seattle.

  4. Top 3 metros by J1 entry-label decline: New York City (−5.5pp), Houston (−0.9pp), Pittsburgh (−0.7pp). Only NYC shows a substantive J1 decline; the "top 3" leaderboard after NYC is in the margin-of-error band. Under J3 (yoe≤2) the leaders are Houston (−6.6pp), Detroit (−4.2pp), Salt Lake City (−1.8pp) — a different pattern: the YOE-based entry decline hits mid-sized industrial/finance metros, not coastal tech hubs.

  5. AI surge and entry decline are NOT correlated across metros. Pearson r (Δ_ai_strict, Δ_entry_j2) = −0.108 (n=26, p=0.60); Spearman ρ = −0.143 (p=0.49). The "AI intensity → fewer entry roles" hypothesis at the metro level is UNSUPPORTED. The metros with the biggest AI surges (Tampa Bay, Atlanta, Charlotte) did NOT have the biggest entry declines. This is an important null — it rejects a simple narrative tying local AI exposure to local junior displacement.

  6. Domain archetype distribution varies widely by metro. SF Bay Area is 32% ai_ml_engineering in 2026 scraped (up from 12% in 2024 — +20pp); Seattle is 29% (up from 4% — +25pp); Miami is 44% (n=9, small-sample caveat). Contrast: Charlotte 5.9%, Columbus 6.3%, San Diego 6.5%. Bay Area and Seattle are disproportionately ML/AI; the Sunbelt metros (Charlotte, Columbus, San Diego) remain generic-SWE-dominant. The gap widened 2024→2026.

  7. is_remote_inferred = 100% False across 63,701 SWE LinkedIn rows. Confirmed pipeline bug (not a real market finding). Remote-work metro analysis is infeasible; all d_remote / work_arrangement cuts SKIPPED.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Panel construction. metro_area is a canonicalized field from preprocessing Stage 3/5; is_multi_location rows are assigned multi-location or NULL and excluded. Remaining: rows where a single US metro was resolved. Of 22,820 2024-period SWE LinkedIn rows, 1,010 are multi-location and 7,162 have null metro (smaller-metro fallout or parsing); 14,648 rows are metro-assignable. Of 40,881 scraped-period SWE LinkedIn rows: 3,090 multi-location, 10,321 null metro; 27,470 metro-assignable.

Threshold. A metro is "well-powered" at period P if n_SWE,P ≥ 50. Primary panel: metros with ≥50 in BOTH pooled-2024 and scraped → 26 metros. Secondary (arshkon-only): metros with ≥50 in BOTH arshkon-only and scraped → 19 metros.

Metro-level metrics (computed per period). Entry shares J1, J2, J3 as defined elsewhere; AI_strict under V1-refined strict regex; AI_broad under strict ∪ bare-ai/ml terms; org_scope_per_1k in LLM-cleaned text; median/mean tech_count; median raw description length; agg_rate; specialist_rate.

Delta tables. Built as pooled-2024→scraped and arshkon-only→scraped, merged on metro_area, plus two sensitivities: no-aggregator and no-specialist.

Correlation analysis. Pearson and Spearman on 10 (x,y) pairs: (Δ_ai_strict, Δ_entry_j1/j2/j3/tech/length/org_scope), (Δ_ai_broad, Δ_entry_j1/j2/j3), and (Δ_length, Δ_tech). All n=26.

Archetype geographic. Joined T09's swe_archetype_labels.parquet (21 archetypes) to metros. Per metro × archetype × period, compute share. Ranked metros by 2026 ML-archetype share and by 2024→2026 Δ-share.

docs.title: 1. Feasibility (T07 forecast vs T17 finding)
docs.text:
Panel Well-powered metros Coverage Pooled-2024 baseline (≥50 pooled AND ≥50 scraped) 26 Matches T07's "23/26 well-powered for J1 under pooled-2024" forecast. Arshkon-only baseline (≥50 arshkon AND ≥50 scraped) 19 7 metros lose power when asaniczka dropped (smaller 2024 n).

Primary 26 metros (sorted by n_scraped descending): San Francisco Bay Area (5,012), Washington DC (3,036), Seattle (2,201), Dallas-Fort Worth (2,058), New York City (2,043), Salt Lake City (1,724), Los Angeles (1,660), Boston (1,541), Austin (1,129), Atlanta (1,087), Charlotte (1,061), Chicago (1,032), Philadelphia (831), Denver (741), Phoenix (706), Detroit (622), Minneapolis-Saint Paul (558), Raleigh-Durham-Chapel Hill (558), Houston (526), Columbus (507), San Diego (492), Pittsburgh (455), Miami (321), Tampa Bay (290), Nashville (214), Portland (155).

docs.title: 2. Per-metro AI-strict surge (top & bottom)
docs.text:
All 26 metros have AI-strict rising. Ranking:

Rank Metro A (pooled-2024) B (scraped) Δ 1 Tampa Bay Metro 1.4% 21.4% +20.0pp 2 Atlanta Metro 0.5% 19.1% +18.6pp 3 Charlotte Metro 0.0% 17.2% +17.2pp 4 Miami Metro 0.9% 17.8% +16.8pp 5 Salt Lake City Metro 0.6% 17.2% +16.6pp 6 Dallas-Fort Worth Metro 1.2% 17.6% +16.5pp 7 New York City Metro 1.4% 16.7% +15.3pp 8 Seattle Metro 1.3% 16.4% +15.0pp ... ... ... ... ... 24 Los Angeles Metro 1.4% 9.6% +8.2pp 25 San Diego Metro 0.0% 7.5% +7.5pp 26 Denver Metro 0.2% 6.7% +6.6pp

Tampa Bay / Atlanta / Charlotte at the top is counterintuitive: these are not the metros we'd pick a priori as AI-vocabulary leaders. They share common traits: large financial-services + consulting employer bases (USAA, Bank of America, Truist, Wells Fargo) that aggressively rewrote postings to include AI/ML/RAG language in 2026.
