SF Bay (26th-most-recent but 14th largest surge +13.5pp) is NOT a leader on PER-POSTING basis because its 2024 baseline was already 2.7% (elevated) and it's at 16.2% now — the absolute level is still the highest on the panel, but the Δ is not top-quartile.

Denver anomaly (smallest AI surge). Denver rose only +6.6pp and has mean tech Δ only +0.87 (third-smallest behind Portland). Denver's Δ_ai_strict is nearly half the panel mean.

docs.title: 3. Per-metro entry-share decline (J1, J2, J3)
docs.text:
J1 (label-based entry): 4 of 26 metros decline; mean Δ_j1 = +0.89pp (overall RISE, not decline). Top J1 decliners: NYC −5.5pp, Houston −0.9pp, Pittsburgh −0.7pp. NYC is the major outlier.

J2 (entry + associate): 4 of 26 metros decline. Top J2 decliners: NYC −5.4pp, Houston −1.3pp, Columbus −1.1pp. Same picture: NYC dominates the J2 decline panel.

J3 (yoe ≤ 2): 8 of 26 metros decline; the pattern is more diffuse. Top J3 decliners: Houston −6.6pp, Detroit −4.2pp, Salt Lake City −1.8pp, Dallas-FW −1.8pp, Tampa Bay −1.0pp, Austin −0.6pp, Pittsburgh −0.2pp. J3 declines hit mid-sized industrial/financial metros (Houston, Detroit, Dallas), NOT the top-5 tech hubs. SF Bay J3 actually ROSE +7.4pp (intern-pipeline-driven, consistent with T06). Seattle J3 +10.4pp. Tech hubs are INCREASING yoe≤2 share; Sunbelt / industrial metros are LOSING it.

Key ranking table (J2 and J3 decline, arshkon-only baseline sensitivity): reported in 03_metro_deltas_arshkon.csv. Directional agreement with pooled-2024 (NYC J2 decline is the only sign-flipper — under arshkon-only the NYC 2024 n=11, J2 rate 18.2% → scraped 1.7%, which is an even bigger drop but underpowered).

docs.title: 4. Tech-hub vs non-hub aggregate
docs.text:
Hubs = {SF Bay, NYC, Seattle, Austin, LA, Boston} (6 metros). Non-hubs = the other 20.

Metric Non-hub mean Δ (n=20) Hub mean Δ (n=6) d_ai_strict +12.73pp +13.13pp d_entry_j1 +1.28pp +0.61pp d_entry_j2 +1.18pp +0.64pp d_entry_j3 +2.44pp +4.83pp d_tech +1.78 +1.85 d_median_length +940 +1,046

AI surge is not hub-concentrated (Δ +0.40pp difference is within noise). J3 rise IS hub-concentrated (+4.83 vs +2.44pp) — tech hubs are expanding their yoe-low intern pipelines disproportionately. J1/J2 are not hub-driven.

docs.title: 5. Metro-level correlations (AI surge × other changes)
docs.text:
x y n Pearson r p Spearman ρ p Δ_ai_strict Δ_entry_j1 26 −0.157 0.445 −0.186 0.362 Δ_ai_strict Δ_entry_j2 26 −0.108 0.599 −0.143 0.485 Δ_ai_strict Δ_entry_j3 26 −0.217 0.286 −0.224 0.271 Δ_ai_strict Δ_mean_tech_count 26 +0.310 0.124 +0.387 0.051 Δ_ai_strict Δ_median_desc_length 26 −0.187 0.361 −0.268 0.186 Δ_ai_broad Δ_entry_j1 26 −0.261 0.198 −0.156 0.448 Δ_ai_broad Δ_entry_j2 26 −0.277 0.171 −0.177 0.386 Δ_ai_broad Δ_entry_j3 26 +0.040 0.847 +0.010 0.962 Δ_median_length Δ_tech 26 +0.023 0.910 +0.079 0.701 Δ_ai_strict Δ_org_scope_per_1k 26 −0.053 0.796 −0.074 0.719

The only correlation approaching significance is (Δ_ai_strict, Δ_mean_tech_count) with Spearman ρ = +0.387, p = 0.051. Metros with larger AI surges tend to have larger tech-count expansion — consistent with the within-company finding that AI-adopting cos also stacked tech. All other correlations are weak and insignificant.

Implication for RQ1a: the cross-metro distribution does NOT support "AI exposure → entry displacement" as a spatially-detectable pattern. If such a mechanism is operating, it is not operating uniformly enough to show up at the metro aggregate.

docs.title: 6. Domain archetype geographic distribution
docs.text:
2026 ML-archetype share (scraped, per metro): SF Bay 32%, Seattle 29%, NYC 20%, Salt Lake City 19%, Atlanta 18%, Pittsburgh 17%, Dallas-FW 16%, Austin 15%, Nashville 14%, Detroit 14%, Boston 14%, Phoenix 12%, Washington DC 12%, Houston 12%, Chicago 11%, LA 10%, Denver 10%, Philadelphia 9%, Minneapolis-StP 8%, San Diego 6%, Columbus 6%, Charlotte 6%.

Miami Metro is 44% ML but with n=9 total archetype-labeled scraped rows; treated as small-sample outlier.

Biggest ML-archetype SURGES (2024→2026, d_share): Seattle +24.8pp (4.3→29.1%), SF Bay +20.2pp (11.6→31.8%), Raleigh-Durham +19.4pp (1.5→20.9%), Atlanta +17.1pp (0.7→17.7%), Salt Lake City +16.9pp (1.6→18.5%), Dallas-FW +14.4pp (1.3→15.7%), Boston +13.1pp (0.6→13.7%). Seattle and SF Bay are the clear geographic winners on domain-composition ML growth — they're pulling ahead of the pack. Sunbelt follow-ons (Atlanta, Raleigh-Durham, Salt Lake City, Dallas-FW) show large relative surges from tiny 2024 bases.

Dominant archetype by metro (2026 scraped): SF Bay = ai_ml_engineering (32%); Seattle = ai_ml_engineering (29%); NYC = ai_ml_engineering (20%); most others = generic_software_engineer (Denver 36%, San Diego 61%, Columbus, Washington DC, LA all generic-dominant).

Archetype losses: the biggest losers per metro are mostly generic_software_engineer share losses (Salt Lake City −24.6pp, Tampa Bay −16.7pp, Raleigh-Durham −13.7pp) — which reflects the mechanical replacement of unspecified-generic postings with more specific archetype postings (ML, data_eng, cloud_devops). Some non-ML shifts: NYC network_security_linux −21.9pp (fell from 24% to 1.6%); Tampa Bay data_engineering −14.4pp; Washington DC systems_engineering −13.9pp. These are reassignments, not net decreases.

docs.title: 7. Sensitivities (no-aggregator, no-specialist)
docs.text:
No-aggregator (Δ_ai_strict panel): 26 metros still well-powered; direction preserved for all; mean Δ_ai_strict shifts slightly (aggregator-excluded cos have slightly higher Δ). 15_metro_deltas_no_aggregator.csv.

No-specialist: 240 specialist cos excluded at row level. 26 metros remain well-powered. Direction preserved; magnitudes shift ≤1pp on each metric. 16_metro_deltas_no_specialist.csv.

Neither sensitivity overturns the AI-surge uniformity or the top-3 ranking.

docs.title: 8. Remote-work (INFEASIBLE)
docs.text: is_remote_inferred = 0 (False) for 100% of SWE LinkedIn rows. Checked directly: SELECT SUM(...) AS n_rem_true FROM u WHERE is_swe=true AND source_platform='linkedin' returned 0 true / 63,701 total. This is a preprocessing pipeline bug; it is not a real-world finding that "0% of SWE jobs are remote." Flagged for preprocessing / Agent Prep follow-up. All analyses keyed on remote status are SKIPPED.

docs.title: Implications for RQ1a / RQ1b / RQ2
docs.text:
RQ1a (entry share restructuring). Geographic heterogeneity is REAL but does not track AI surge. NYC has an outsized J1 decline (−5.5pp) that is NOT explained by its AI-surge magnitude (15.3pp, midpack). Houston's J3 decline (−6.6pp) is the largest on the panel but Houston's AI surge is 10.1pp (also midpack). At the metro level, entry-share changes are driven by idiosyncratic metro-industry mixes, not by a diffusible AI-mechanism. This corroborates T06/T16: entry change is a between-company (composition) story that surfaces via metro-industry mix, not a within-company mechanism.

RQ1b (scope inflation). Every metro in the panel shows AI surge + tech-count expansion + length expansion. The within-metro / within-company combination (not computable at this grain) would clarify; T16 already shows it's 102% within-company on the matched panel. Metros with biggest tech_count gains ARE metros with biggest AI surges (Spearman 0.39, near-sig at p=0.05) — scope inflation and AI-vocabulary rise are coupled spatially.

RQ2 (requirement migration). T17 doesn't directly test this but does constrain it: the ML-archetype share grew dramatically in SF Bay (+20pp), Seattle (+25pp), Atlanta (+17pp), Raleigh-Durham (+19pp) — mostly at the expense of "generic_software_engineer" share. This is a migration FROM generic labeling INTO specific AI/ML roles at the archetype level. Whether this reflects (a) genuine market shift toward ML work, (b) LinkedIn taxonomy drift (industry label = "Technology, Information and Internet" took over), or (c) posting-description rewriting to hit ML-flavored keywords (a within-company story) is testable with T16's company-level data. Candidate joint hypothesis: same cos in SF Bay + Seattle that rewrote JDs also reclassified into ML archetypes as a result.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Tampa Bay, Atlanta, Charlotte are the AI-surge leaders, not SF Bay or Seattle. The Sunbelt financial-services / healthcare / aerospace metros have the FASTEST AI-vocabulary adoption. Base rate in 2024 was ~0-1.4% (essentially zero mentions in 2024), jumped to 17-21% by 2026. Hub metros had higher 2024 baselines (SF Bay 2.7%, Seattle 1.3%), so their absolute Δ is smaller even though absolute levels in 2026 are highest. "Where is AI vocabulary being adopted fastest?" ≠ "where is absolute AI vocabulary highest?"

  2. AI surge is SIGNIFICANTLY UNCORRELATED with entry decline across metros (r=−0.11). I expected this correlation to be at least weakly positive, based on the widespread "AI is displacing juniors" framing. The data says: the metros where AI vocabulary grew fastest are not the metros where entry share fell most. This is a robust null — the narrative connecting AI-exposure to local junior displacement does NOT surface in metro-aggregate data.

  3. NYC's J1 decline (−5.5pp) is entirely an arshkon-only-baseline phenomenon. In the pooled-2024 baseline, NYC 2024 J1 rate = 7.1% (from a small asaniczka+arshkon pool of 70 rows). In 2026 scraped NYC has n=2,043 and J1 = 1.7%. The "decline" is partly artifact of a tiny 2024 NYC sample. Reported as observed but the N-asymmetry (70 vs 2,043) makes the estimated decline fragile. Under no-aggregator baseline, NYC 2024 n=64 and the decline holds (−6.0pp) — so the signal survives aggregator exclusion, but the N-asymmetry remains.

  4. Houston J3 decline (−6.6pp) is the largest in the panel and goes unnoticed by any AI story. Houston's AI-strict surge is 10.1pp (midpack). Houston's industry mix (energy, healthcare, defense) and 2024 high J3 share (12.9%) dropping to 6.3% suggests the decline is composition — maybe fewer energy-intern postings in 2026? Worth an industry-stratified drill-down in T22/T23.

  5. Seattle ML archetype share went 4%→29%. This is a massive 25pp shift in archetype composition in 3 years. Either (a) Seattle's employer base genuinely reallocated toward ML work, (b) the archetype classifier is more sensitive to AI-vocabulary in 2026 than in 2024 (a possibility — T09 archetype labels are bag-of-tokens-based), or (c) both. If (b) dominates, some of the metro ML-share "growth" is classifier drift. Needs V2 cross-check.

  6. San Diego's J3 SURGE (+18.4pp) is the largest J3 gain on the panel. San Diego went 10.8% → 29.3% yoe≤2 share. Simultaneously, San Diego's generic_software_engineer archetype share surged 29→61% — largest archetype shift up. My hypothesis: a specific employer (Northrop Grumman? Qualcomm? defense contractors? BAE?) ramped a new-grad pipeline. Would confirm with a within-SD employer decomposition (out of scope for T17).

  7. Denver and Portland are the two "quiet" metros. Both have the smallest AI surges (6.6pp and 13.5pp), smallest tech Δ, small length growth, small org_scope contraction. These metros have structurally different employer mixes (gov contractors, energy, infrastructure). They serve as a control group for "how much of the AI narrative is market-wide vs particular-industry-mix."

  8. Org-scope per 1k DECLINED in every one of the 26 metros (Δ range: −0.03 to −0.16). This is a UNIFORMLY NEGATIVE pattern that contrasts with the positive AI and tech Δs. Partly mechanical (the denominator — cleaned text length — went up in most metros, so per-1k rates fall even if raw counts rise); partly real (the "cross-functional / stakeholder / ownership" vocabulary really is thinning in 2026). Consistent with T13's finding that requirements-section contracted while other sections expanded.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• 22.4% of 2024-period and 25.2% of scraped-period SWE LinkedIn rows have null metro. These are dropped from metro analyses; results are representative of the 77% of rows with canonicalized metros.
  • Multi-location rows (1,010 in 2024; 3,090 in scraped) are excluded by construction. Multi-location cos (e.g., "Multiple US locations" postings) tend to be larger employers with distributed teams; the reported metro-scale AI surge numbers slightly UNDERESTIMATE the employer-level shift if multi-location postings were disproportionately AI-forward.
  • Tiny-n metros are excluded: Anchorage (n=2), Bakersfield (n=1), Cape Coral (n=3), etc. These are not represented. The primary panel's 26 metros are the urbanized bulk of US SWE.
  • is_remote_inferred pipeline bug (100% False) precludes remote-work analysis. Reported but not explored.
  • NYC 2024 pooled panel n=70 is small. Any NYC-specific finding must be flagged as underpowered-on-the-baseline-side. The NYC J1 decline direction appears to be real but its magnitude is noisy.
  • Archetype labeling sample sizes per metro can be small: e.g., Nashville total archetype-labeled scraped rows = 14, Miami = 9. Metros with fewer than ~30 archetype-labeled rows should not be ranked on per-archetype share.
  • is_multi_location and metro_area canonicalization differences between sources. arshkon's raw LinkedIn location strings are more variable than scraped's pre-normalized location field; slight canonicalization drift could affect 2024 metros more than 2026 metros.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• For V2 Gate 3 verification: (a) re-derive Δ_ai_strict × Δ_entry_j2 correlation with an independent regex and verify p=0.60 null; (b) spot-check top 3 AI-surge metros with semantic review (Tampa Bay, Atlanta, Charlotte — are their AI mentions actually ML, or are they chatbot-operator / policy-engineering roles?); (c) test Seattle ML archetype 25pp growth with a classifier-version-control check on the archetype labels parquet.
  • For T24 synthesis: geographic uniformity of AI surge + non-correlation with entry change is a key RQ1a / RQ2 story. NYC anomaly on J1 worth isolating.
  • For T21 (senior role evolution): metro-level seniors breakdown not done here; recommend pass on the same 26 metros.
  • Preprocessing follow-up: is_remote_inferred needs fixing before remote-work analysis is feasible.
  • Industry-stratified metro drill-down (candidate T23 extension): Houston's energy-heavy entry decline and San Diego's defense-heavy entry surge suggest industry×metro interactions explain more than metro alone.

docs.title: Appendix — output file index
docs.text:
Under exploration/tables/T17/: - 00_multi_location_exclusions.csv — multi-location row counts by period - 01_metro_profiles_all_periods.csv — per-metro profile for pooled_2024, arshkon, scraped_2026 (84 metro×period rows) - 02_metro_deltas_pooled_primary.csv — pooled-2024 → scraped delta (26 rows, primary) - 03_metro_deltas_arshkon.csv — arshkon-only → scraped delta (19 rows, sensitivity) - 04_metro_rank_ai_strict.csv — 26 metros ranked by d_ai_strict - 05_metro_rank_entry_j2.csv — 26 metros ranked by d_entry_j2 - 06_metro_rank_entry_j3.csv — 26 metros ranked by d_entry_j3 - 07_metro_rank_length.csv — 26 metros ranked by d_median_desc_length - 08_metro_rank_tech.csv — 26 metros ranked by d_mean_tech_count - 09_metro_rank_orgscope.csv — 26 metros ranked by d_org_scope_per_1k - 10_hub_vs_nonhub_aggregate.csv — 6-metro tech-hub vs 20-metro non-hub aggregate comparison - 11_metro_correlations.csv — 10 cross-metric Pearson/Spearman correlations - 12_archetype_by_metro_period.csv — per-metro × archetype × period shares (769 rows) - 13_ml_archetype_share_by_metro_2026.csv — 26 metros ranked by 2026 ML share - 14_archetype_share_change_by_metro.csv — per-metro × archetype d_share (472 rows) - 15_metro_deltas_no_aggregator.csv — no-aggregator sensitivity - 16_metro_deltas_no_specialist.csv — no-specialist sensitivity - 17_summary.json — per-task summary (26 metros, top-3 lists, all correlations)

Under exploration/figures/T17/: - 01_metro_heatmap.png — 26 metros × 8 metrics heatmap, sorted by d_ai_strict - 02_ai_vs_entry_scatter.png — Δai_strict × Δentry_j2 scatter with metro labels; fits the r=−0.11 null - 03_archetype_metro_2026.png — 26 metros × 12 archetypes 2026 share heatmap, sorted by ML share

Under exploration/scripts/: - T17_geographic_structure.py — main T17 pipeline

docs.title: T18 — Cross-occupation boundary analysis
docs.text: Agent K, Wave 3. 2026-04-17. Input: data/unified.parquet.

docs.title: Headline (<200 words)
docs.text: The SWE-specific restructuring framing is defensible for the AI, requirements, and tech-stack metrics but collapses to a macro template shift for description length and soft skills. DiD (SWE change minus control change) is ~99% of SWE-only change for AI-mention (strict), AI-mention (any), has_ai_tech, tech_count, and ai_tech_count; ~70% for org_scope_count and requirement_breadth. desc_len_chars DiD is only 37% of SWE-only (mostly macro); soft_skill_count DiD is ~0 (fully macro). The requirements-section shrink is NOT universal: SWE -10.7 pp, adjacent -10.9 pp, control +0.9 pp. Control expanded requirements-section share while SWE compressed it — the two groups move in opposite directions. The AI gradient is widening: SWE(strict) went 0.010 → 0.153 (+0.142); adjacent 0.007 → 0.118 (+0.111); control 0.002 → 0.002 (≈0). Control is not catching up. Among adjacent titles, ML engineer (+0.54) and data scientist (+0.15) overtake SWE (+0.13) on AI-mention — SWE is no longer the frontier; it is being surrounded by AI-native roles. Sensitivities (aggregators, tier) confirm direction. Boundary TF-IDF cosine SWE↔adj drops 0.80 → 0.75 (slight divergence, not blur).

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Three-group comparison (SWE / SWE-adjacent / Control) under the locked directives:

  • Filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND (is_swe OR is_swe_adjacent OR is_control).
  • AI-mention = V1-refined STRICT pattern; BROAD pattern as secondary.
  • Raw description used for cross-group comparability (SWE LLM coverage 54%, adjacent 99%, control 24% — filtering on cleaned text would bias by group).
  • Periods: 2024-01 (asaniczka), 2024-04 (arshkon), 2026-03 (scraped), 2026-04 (scraped).
  • Sample sizes (used): SWE 63,701 / adjacent 23,408 / control 155,745.
  • Scripts: exploration/scripts/T18_01_parallel_trends.py through T18_10_seniority_panel.py.
  • Tables: exploration/tables/T18/.
  • Figures: exploration/figures/T18/.

docs.title: Step 1 — Parallel trends
docs.text:
Per-period means for each metric by group (file T18_parallel_trends.csv):

Selected rows (SWE / adjacent / control, ai_strict):

period SWE adjacent control 2024-01 0.010 0.007 0.002 2024-04 0.014 0.018 0.002 2026-03 0.134 0.102 0.003 2026-04 0.153 0.118 0.002

ai_broad / tech_count / requirement_breadth show the same pattern: SWE moves most; adjacent moves nearly as much; control barely moves.

Within-2024 calibration (arshkon vs asaniczka, same group): arshkon SWE ai_strict 0.014 vs asaniczka 0.010 (+0.004), arshkon adj 0.018 vs asaniczka 0.007 (+0.011), arshkon ctrl 0.002 vs asaniczka 0.002 (≈0). Calibration gap is tiny relative to the 2024→2026 move (×30+).

docs.title: Step 2 — DiD table (baseline)
docs.text:
(T18_did_table.csv, exclude_aggregator=false, all bootstrap 400 reps)

metric SWE Δ ctrl Δ DiD DiD/SWE-only Flag ai_strict_binary 0.133 0.001 0.132 0.99 swe_specific ai_broad_binary 0.362 0.067 0.295 0.82 swe_specific has_ai_tech 0.191 0.002 0.189 0.99 swe_specific ai_tech_count 0.559 0.003 0.555 0.99 swe_specific tech_count 1.58 0.08 1.50 0.95 swe_specific org_scope_count 1.03 0.30 0.73 0.71 swe_specific requirement_breadth 3.41 0.96 2.45 0.72 swe_specific mgmt_strict_count 0.19 0.09 0.10 0.54 swe_specific desc_len_chars 1149 725 423 0.37 mostly_macro soft_skill_count 0.48 0.48 -0.01 -0.01 mostly_macro edu_level -0.06 0.12 -0.18 2.84 (sign flip) atypical

Bootstrap 95% CIs (DiD) are all well clear of zero for the swe_specific rows — e.g. ai_strict_binary DiD ≈ [0.1305, 0.1341]; requirement_breadth DiD ≈ [2.39, 2.52]. soft_skill_count DiD CI straddles zero.

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• AI content is SWE-specific. Nearly all of the SWE AI-mention growth is over and above what control white-collar did. Control moves 0.000–0.003 in absolute prevalence.
  • Breadth and scope are SWE-specific. Control's requirement_breadth rose only 0.96 compared to SWE's 3.41; 72% is SWE-specific.
  • Length and soft-skill growth are macro. Control postings got 725 chars longer and gained ~0.48 soft-skill tokens — that is a universal "postings got more verbose" shift. Only the ~423-char SWE excess is plausibly SWE-specific.
  • Education-level change is small and not consistent. SWE dropped slightly while control rose; the DiD flag is driven by a tiny denominator.

docs.title: Step 3 — Adjacent title trajectories (T18_adjacent_title_trajectories.csv)
docs.text:
Top adjacent buckets, ai_strict pre→post (2024-pooled → 2026-pooled):

bucket n_pre n_post pre ai_strict post ai_strict Δ ml_engineer 2 50 0.000 0.540 +0.540 data_scientist 815 2517 0.044 0.190 +0.145 SWE_baseline 22820 40881 0.011 0.144 +0.133 data_engineer 68 64 0.000 0.047 +0.047 security_engineer 615 1668 0.008 0.055 +0.046 platform_engineer 46 29 0.000 0.034 +0.034 quality_engineer 1299 1104 0.000 0.021 +0.021 data_analyst 552 710 0.004 0.017 +0.013 systems_admin 1335 81 0.004 0.012 +0.008 network_engineer 1429 1299 0.001 0.006 +0.005

ML Engineer and Data Scientist pull ahead of SWE on AI-mention by 2026 (post gap 2024-01 SWE vs bucket flips sign from –0.03 to –0.05 for data scientist). The rest of the adjacent roster is 1–3× behind SWE. "Surrounding the SWE boundary with AI-native roles" is a real cross-occupation pattern and is driven by data-science and ML-engineer growth rather than by quality / network / systems-admin roles.

docs.title: Step 4 — Boundary TF-IDF cosine
docs.text:
(T18_tfidf_cosine_swe_vs_adj.csv, n=200 per cell, TF-IDF 1+2-grams, English stop-list, shared-vocab stoplist stripped)

period cosine SWE vs adjacent 2024-01 0.800 2024-04 0.767 2026-03 0.751 2026-04 0.757

Cosine decreases from 2024 to 2026 (0.80 → 0.75). The SWE-adjacent group is not becoming more similar to SWE at the TF-IDF-content level. Interpreted together with step 3: adjacent roles are adopting AI-mention in parallel, but the surrounding language content (their specific tech stacks, duties) continues to differentiate. Net: no "content blur" at bag-of-words level; the convergence is in AI-specific vocabulary, not in the overall corpus.

Cross-period drift (anchor 2024-01 SWE): 2026-03 SWE cosine ≈ 0.737, 2026-03 adjacent ≈ 0.752 (adjacent is closer to 2024-01 SWE than 2026 SWE is). This is because 2026 SWE moved further from its 2024 self than 2026 adjacent did.

docs.title: Step 5 — AI gradient (T18_ai_gradient_table.csv, figs T18_ai_gradient.png, T18_parallel_trends_ai_strict.png)
docs.text:
Gap SWE − control widens:

period gap_ai_strict gap_ai_broad gap_tech gap_requirement_breadth 2024-01 0.008 0.135 4.41 5.96 2024-04 0.012 0.153 4.75 6.16 2026-03 0.131 0.429 5.96 8.38 2026-04 0.150 0.437 5.99 8.49

SWE is pulling ahead of control. Control is NOT catching up on AI-mention.

docs.title: Step 6 — Section anatomy (T18_section_anatomy_by_group_period.csv, fig T18_section_anatomy.png)
docs.text:
Requirements-section share (3,000 postings per cell, T13 classifier):

group pre (2024 pooled) post (2026 pooled) Δ SWE 0.336 0.229 -0.107 adjacent 0.342 0.234 -0.109 control 0.222 0.231 +0.009

Responsibilities-section share:

group pre post Δ SWE 0.166 0.202 +0.036 adjacent 0.194 0.228 +0.034 control 0.174 0.190 +0.016

The requirements-section shrink is SWE+adjacent-specific, NOT universal. Control's requirements share actually rose. SWE and adjacent both compressed requirements by ~11 pp while expanding responsibilities by ~3.5 pp. This is a template restructuring inside technical/engineering postings, not a generic 2024→2026 posting-style macro shift.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
(a) Aggregator exclusion (T18_sensitivity_did.csv): DiD direction and magnitude preserved. AI-strict DiD 0.133 without aggregators vs 0.132 with. Requirement_breadth DiD rises from 2.45 → 2.86 when aggregators removed, so including aggregators slightly attenuates the SWE-specific signal (aggregator content is more generic).

(g) SWE classification tier (keep only regex + embedding_high tiers, drop title_lookup_llm / embedding_adjacent / unresolved SWE rows): DiD direction and magnitude preserved. AI-strict DiD 0.134; requirement_breadth DiD 2.35. Findings do not depend on softly-classified SWE rows.

Not shown here but the T13 section classifier is SWE-agnostic (regex on description text); the section anatomy result therefore does not depend on SWE classification.

docs.title: T30 seniority panel per group (T18_seniority_panel_by_group.csv)
docs.text:
group period J1 J2 S1 SWE 2024-01 0.011 0.013 0.635 SWE 2024-04 0.037 0.041 0.443 SWE 2026-03 0.036 0.038 0.435 SWE 2026-04 0.026 0.027 0.432 adjacent 2026-04 0.041 0.046 0.483 control 2024-04 0.153 0.161 0.231 control 2026-04 0.031 0.031 0.306

LLM-frame J2 flip (V1-confirmed) applies for arshkon-labeled seniority here — J1/J2 arshkon spike for control (0.153/0.161) is a known data artifact of the arshkon labeling pattern; the scraped control J2 is 0.031, in line with non-arshkon-era expectations. Flagged.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Control postings do not adopt AI-mention at all. Raw prevalence 0.002 → 0.002. I expected some white-collar drift from HR/marketing/sales writing "AI-driven" in descriptions; it hasn't materialized in LinkedIn non-SWE, non-adjacent postings at the strict pattern level. Under broad pattern control goes 0.011 → 0.078 (+6.7 pp), so there is a real but small macro shift — but strict pattern is clean.
  2. Requirements-section behaves in OPPOSITE directions for SWE vs control. SWE contracts (-10.7 pp) while control expands (+0.9 pp). This is much stronger evidence for a SWE-specific template restructuring than simply "everyone compressed requirements."
  3. ML Engineer and Data Scientist overtake SWE on AI-mention. ML engineer post-period ai_strict is 0.54 (SWE: 0.14); data scientist is 0.19. Post ai_strict gap SWE minus ML-eng becomes -0.40 — the AI-native adjacent roles are now AHEAD of general SWE. The "AI frontier" inside LinkedIn postings has moved from SWE to ML-eng/DS.
  4. TF-IDF cosine decreases between SWE and adjacent (0.80 → 0.75). Content-level divergence coexists with AI-mention convergence. Adjacent roles are adopting similar AI vocabulary but their tech stacks and duties remain distinct — "vocabulary overlap, content divergence."
  5. Education level drops in SWE but rises in control (SWE -0.06 vs control +0.12). SWE postings in 2026 mention degrees less often; control postings mention them more. Possible signal: credentialing compression on SWE side (AI skills > formal education), ad-hoc degree gating on the control side.
  6. soft_skill_count moves identically across groups. SWE +0.48, control +0.48. Soft-skill inflation is the purest macro / template pattern in the data. Useful null control for the AI/tech findings.
  7. Aggregator exclusion INCREASES the SWE-specific signal on breadth/length (DiD 2.45 → 2.86). Aggregator postings are more generic and their inclusion pulls SWE means toward the middle. The headline finding is robust — aggregators weakly attenuate rather than create the result.

docs.title: Data-quality caveats
docs.text:
• arshkon window (2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20) contains within-window heterogeneity in AI-mention (range 0.00–0.10 across days, CV 1.44). Treat arshkon as a 2-week sample, not a point. Reported in T19 Step 2.
  • Tech-count on RAW description may over-count for long control/adjacent postings containing "python" mentions in unrelated contexts. Sensitivity (a) no_aggregators still preserves direction, so not a fatal confound.
  • Boundary TF-IDF uses raw text and company-stoplist stripping; N=200 per cell. For formal work, scale to 1,000+ per cell and use description_core_llm on LLM-labeled subset only with coverage caveat.
  • Section classifier precision is not exhaustively validated on adjacent/control. If adjacent/control postings use different section-header conventions, the absolute shares may shift; the change per group is less vulnerable because it's a within-group comparison.

docs.title: Action items (downstream)
docs.text:
• Wave 4 synthesis: Use the DiD table as the single empirical pillar for the "SWE-specific restructuring" paper framing.
  • AI-mention, ai_tech, tech_count, scope, breadth: SWE-specific, cite DiD.
  • Desc length, soft skills: demote to "macro template shift" in paper.
  • Adjacent role insight: a separate subsection on the AI-native adjacent roles (ML-eng, data-science) overtaking SWE on AI-mention. This is a publishable finding distinct from the SWE headline.
  • Requirements-section shrink figure (T18_section_anatomy.png) is paper-ready.
  • T19 / T30 feed: reuse the seniority panel by group for cross-group junior-share claims; flag LLM-frame J2 arshkon spike.
  • Verification (V2): spot-check 20 random SWE + 20 adjacent + 20 control postings on AI-mention classification and requirements-section attribution.

docs.title: T19 — Temporal patterns
docs.text: Agent K, Wave 3. 2026-04-17. Input: data/unified.parquet.

docs.title: Headline (<200 words)
docs.text: Cross-period changes are NOT a linear continuation of within-2024 trends. AI-mention (strict) cross-period annualized rate (≈0.063/yr) is ~3.8× the within-2024 annualized rate (0.017/yr); AI-mention (broad) is ~2.7×. Description length, by contrast, DROPS within-2024 (arshkon is shorter than asaniczka) but RISES cross-period — sign flip, not acceleration. Tech-count median and org-scope median are stable within-2024 (no signal) but rise cross-period. Junior J2 share has the opposite pattern: within-2024 it appears to rise (asaniczka 0.013 → arshkon 0.041) at an implausible 0.11/yr annualized, then falls cross-period (arshkon 0.041 → scraped 0.027), giving a negative acceleration ratio. This reflects arshkon's LLM-frame seniority labeling artifact (V1-confirmed) rather than real junior flows. Within the scraped window, AI-mention is stable (CV 0.14), tech-count and breadth are stable (CV 0.04–0.06), and Sunday postings have elevated broad AI-mention (0.58 vs ~0.49 weekday). Scraper first day is a 1.6× backlog spike (2,448 vs 1,537 mean). posting_age_days is degenerate (all 1) — age distribution is not informative. Three snapshots bracket key model releases: Asaniczka 2024-01 pre-Claude-3, arshkon 2024-04 post-Claude-3 pre-GPT-4o, scraped 2026-03+04 post-Claude-4 and Gemini-2.5-Pro.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
SWE-only temporal analysis (primary), with cross-group seniority views carried from T18.

  • SWE sample: 63,701 rows.
  • Periods: 2024-01 (asaniczka, N=18,129), 2024-04 (arshkon, N=4,691), 2026-03 (scraped, N=19,777), 2026-04 (scraped, N=21,104).
  • Scripts: exploration/scripts/T19_01_rate_of_change.py through T19_04_timeline_plot.py.
  • Tables: exploration/tables/T19/.
  • Figures: exploration/figures/T19/.

docs.title: Step 1 — Rate-of-change table (T19_rate_of_change.csv)
docs.text:
Midpoints: asaniczka 2024-01-14, arshkon 2024-04-12, scraped 2026-03-24 + 2026-04-06.

Within-2024 annualized = (arshkon − asaniczka) / years between midpoints (0.243 yr). Cross-period annualized = (scraped mean − 2024 pooled mean) / years between midpoints (≈2.16 yr). Acceleration ratio = cross / within.

metric asaniczka arshkon scraped_03 scraped_04 within-2024 ann cross ann accel ratio J2 share 0.013 0.041 0.038 0.027 +0.115 +0.003 0.03 S1 share 0.635 0.443 0.435 0.432 -0.790 -0.051 0.06 J1 share 0.011 0.037 0.036 0.026 +0.107 +0.003 0.03 ai_strict 0.010 0.014 0.134 0.153 +0.017 +0.063 3.81 ai_broad 0.146 0.161 0.507 0.515 +0.062 +0.171 2.75 desc_len_raw (median) 3733 2974 4920 4803 -3115 chars/yr +723 sign flip desc_len_cleaned (median) 2130 1823 2461 2452 -1260 +230 sign flip tech_count (median) 4 4 5 5 0 +0.48 NA org_scope (median) 1 1 2 2 0 +0.48 NA ai_tech_count (median) 0 0 0 0 0 0 NA

docs.title: Top 3 cross-period acceleration ratios
docs.text:
1. ai_strict: 3.81× — AI-mention growth is ~4× faster cross-period than within-2024 would predict.
  2. ai_broad: 2.75× — broad AI framing accelerates but less dramatically.
  3. No other metric has a positive acceleration ratio > 1; the others are either sign-flipping (length) or flat within-2024.

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text: The AI-specific language expansion is discontinuous relative to the within-2024 baseline — it is fundamentally a post-2024 phenomenon, not an extrapolation of a smooth 2024 trajectory. Description length has the opposite pattern: within-2024 it contracts (asaniczka → arshkon) and cross-period it expands; this tells us the within-2024 snapshots are not representative of a "posting verbosity" trajectory, just differences between two sources. Junior J2 share is contaminated by arshkon's labeling regime.

docs.title: Step 2 — Within-arshkon stability (T19_arshkon_daily.csv, T19_arshkon_stability_summary.csv)
docs.text:
Arshkon SWE spans 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20 (~16 days). Daily stability:

metric mean std cv range ai_strict_binary 0.019 0.027 1.44 0.00 – 0.10 ai_broad_binary 0.180 0.079 0.44 0.11 – 0.40 desc_len_chars 3404 735 0.22 2595 – 5148 tech_count 4.90 0.68 0.14 4.13 – 6.90 org_scope_count 1.29 0.22 0.17 0.98 – 1.87 requirement_breadth 8.70 0.68 0.08 7.83 – 10.40

AI-strict is highly unstable within-arshkon (CV 1.44). Two days have 0 AI-mention (04-15, 04-16, 04-20) and one has 0.10 (04-07 with n=10). requirement_breadth is the most stable (CV 0.08). The arshkon "point" on the temporal curve has substantial within-window heterogeneity and should be treated as a window, not a point, in formal analysis.

docs.title: Step 3 — Scraper yield (T19_scraper_yield_daily.csv)
docs.text:
• First scrape day (2026-03-20): 2,448 SWE postings.
  • Mean of subsequent days: 1,537.
  • Ratio: 1.59×.

First day is elevated but not pathologically so. Consistent with a backlog flush from initial LinkedIn scrape indexing — real effect, moderate magnitude. Down-weight first day when computing trends within-scraped.

Also: lowest daily SWE counts fall on Mondays (2,179 weekly total, only 474 and 310 on 2026-04-06 and 2026-04-13 respectively) — pattern of Mondays being low-yield scrape days that needs investigation (possibly LinkedIn posting volume, possibly scraper timing).

docs.title: Step 4 — Posting age (T19_posting_age_distribution.csv)
docs.text:
posting_age_days coverage: - Non-null n = 329 (out of 40,881 scraped SWE) - All values = 1 day - Distribution: degenerate (std = 0)

posting_age_days is not a usable lifecycle signal at this coverage and cardinality. Action item: either the scraper is only retaining fresh postings (so age is always 1) or the field is not being populated for most rows. Downstream tasks should not use posting_age_days for ghost / lifecycle analysis without source validation.

docs.title: Step 5 — Within-scraped stability (T19_within_scraped_stability.csv, excluding first day)
docs.text:
metric mean std cv range ai_strict 0.143 0.020 0.14 0.098 – 0.185 ai_broad 0.521 0.039 0.07 0.468 – 0.617 J2_share 0.037 0.020 0.54 0.012 – 0.111 S1_share 0.452 0.057 0.13 0.349 – 0.580 desc_len 5011 385 0.08 4400 – 6065 tech_count 6.04 0.36 0.06 5.18 – 6.46 requirement_breadth 11.84 0.43 0.04 10.68 – 12.48

Within the scraped window, content metrics are stable. AI-strict CV 0.14 (roughly ±20% of mean); tech-count and breadth CVs < 0.10. J2 share is the least stable (CV 0.54), reflecting small counts per day.

docs.title: Day-of-week (T19_day_of_week.csv)
docs.text:
dow n ai_strict ai_broad J2_share S1_share desc_len Monday 2179 0.128 0.523 0.073 0.514 5500 Tuesday 7493 0.135 0.483 0.031 0.400 4557 Wednesday 6850 0.149 0.505 0.026 0.419 4799 Thursday 5882 0.161 0.523 0.027 0.438 4928 Friday 8844 0.141 0.506 0.029 0.425 4900 Saturday 6565 0.142 0.512 0.035 0.417 4974 Sunday 3068 0.143 0.577 0.036 0.536 5488

Sunday broad AI-mention is elevated (0.577 vs weekday 0.48–0.52) and Sunday + Monday have higher S1 share (0.51–0.54 vs weekday 0.40–0.44) and longer descriptions. Low-volume days appear biased toward senior, longer, more AI-mentioning postings. Possible explanations: employer backfill of high-priority roles on weekends, scraper coverage asymmetries.

docs.title: Step 6 — Timeline contextualization (T19_timeline.png, T19_timeline_daily_ai.csv)
docs.text:
Snapshots relative to model-release chronology:

  • Asaniczka 2024-01 — pre-Claude-3 (March 2024), ~10 months post-GPT-4.
  • Arshkon 2024-04 — post-Claude-3 (March 2024), pre-GPT-4o (May 2024), pre-Claude-3.5-Sonnet (June 2024). Coding-agent wave not yet mainstream in postings.
  • Scraped 2026-03 + 2026-04 — post-o1 (Sep 2024), post-Claude-4 (Sep 2025), post-Gemini-2.5-Pro (Mar 2026). Cursor, Copilot, Claude-Code, Codex tools mature.

The acceleration ratio >> 1 for AI-mention aligns temporally with the post-Claude-3.5-Sonnet / post-Cursor-adoption period. Not formal causal identification — just temporal coincidence worth flagging for the paper narrative.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
(e) Date-window effects: within-arshkon CV for ai_strict is 1.44 — high. Reporting arshkon as "a 2-week sample mid-range 0.014" rather than a point is advised. Within-asaniczka (Jan 12–17) is not separated here but the 5-day window is short enough that heterogeneity is likely smaller.

(f) Seasonality / day-of-week: 7-day scraped window averaging absorbs day-of-week; within-2024 sources are single-week windows, so day-of-week is a nuisance variable. Monday+Sunday bias toward senior postings is flagged as a day-of-week sampling effect.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Acceleration ratio 3.8× for AI-strict. The AI-tool-vocabulary growth from 2024 → 2026 is NOT a linear continuation of within-2024 trajectory; it is a discrete post-2024 phenomenon. This is arguably the strongest piece of direct evidence in the project that something "stepped" after the within-2024 window ended.
  2. Description length sign flip: within-2024 length DROPS (asaniczka 3733 → arshkon 2974), cross-period length RISES. Any paper claim that "posting length grew 2024→2026" is actually measuring a cross-source artifact at the within-2024 level. Be careful when computing trend slopes.
  3. J2 share goes up then down: asaniczka 0.013 → arshkon 0.041 → scraped 0.027. Arshkon is the outlier. Consistent with V1's LLM-frame J2 flip finding — arshkon's labeled seniority is systematically different from other sources.
  4. Tech-count median is flat within-2024 (4→4) and then rises (5). The median is less sensitive than the mean; discrete unit changes tell you that the "typical" SWE posting now has one more tech token. This is consistent with the mean-level DiD in T18.
  5. Sunday broad AI-mention is 0.58 vs weekday 0.48–0.52. Weekend postings are AI-heavier. Possibly because AI-forward startups post on weekends more than traditional employers do, or because Friday/Saturday scheduling pushes high-priority postings into weekend scrape runs.
  6. posting_age_days is degenerate. 329/40,881 non-null, all value 1. A scheduled task should populate this field properly if it is to drive any lifecycle analysis.
  7. Within-scraped stability is solid for content metrics but poor for J2. CV 0.04–0.14 for AI-mention/tech/breadth means we can treat "scraped" as a coherent window; CV 0.54 for J2 means seniority cells are still underpowered.
  8. Low scraper yield on Mondays (474, 310 on sample Mondays). Needs infra investigation; is this LinkedIn volume reality, or scraper cadence?

docs.title: Data-quality caveats
docs.text:
• posting_age_days unusable — flag for pipeline.
  • arshkon within-window heterogeneity — treat as 2-week sample.
  • Within-2024 calibration uses asaniczka Jan and arshkon April, ~3-month gap. All within-2024 annualizations assume linearity over a very short window — they are not reliable trend extrapolations, they are calibration checks.
  • Cross-period annualization uses a single 2-year midpoint diff; with only two periods we cannot distinguish a step at a specific time from a smooth 2-year trend. The acceleration ratio is a comparison of slopes, not a test of step timing.

docs.title: Action items (downstream)
docs.text:
• Wave 4 synthesis: quote the 3.8× / 2.75× acceleration ratios as the cleanest temporal evidence that the AI-mention shift is post-2024.
  • T30 / seniority work: continue to flag arshkon J1/J2 as LLM-frame-affected; use scraped J2 (0.03) as the more reliable 2026 value.
  • Scraper owner: investigate posting_age_days (all-1 degeneracy) and Monday-volume asymmetry; re-populate age if needed.
  • Paper narrative: timeline figure shows the three snapshots against model releases — usable for the introduction/setup figure.

docs.title: T20 — Seniority boundary clarity 2024 → 2026
docs.text: Agent: L (Wave 3) Date: 2026-04-17 Primary slice: SWE, LinkedIn, is_english=true, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true, text_source='llm', known-seniority. Feature set (7): yoe_numeric (median-imputed at 3), tech_count, ai_mention (V1-refined strict), org_scope_density (per 1K chars), management_density (V1-refined strict, per 1K chars), description_length_cleaned, education_level (ordinal 0-3). All standardized (z-score) per boundary fit. Model: L2 logistic regression, 5-fold stratified CV, random_state=42. Cells: 2024 n=13,237 (entry 375 / associate 39 / mid-senior 12,724 / director 99). 2026 n=6,816 (entry 601 / associate 51 / mid-senior 6,052 / director 112). Scripts: exploration/scripts/T20_build_features.py, T20_boundary_analysis.py, T20_domain_stratified.py, T20_domain_junior_senior.py. Tables: exploration/tables/T20/ (7 CSVs). Figures: exploration/figures/T20/ (5 PNG).

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Every adjacent boundary SHARPENED 2024→2026. associate_vs_entry AUC rose 0.559 → 0.613 (+0.054). mid-senior_vs_associate rose 0.736 → 0.820 (+0.084). director_vs_mid-senior held 0.678 → 0.681 (+0.003). The global director boundary is flat; entry/associate and mid-senior differentiation sharpened. No boundary blurred.
  2. The J3 vs S4 yoe panel sharpened dramatically: 0.548 → 0.688 (+0.14 AUC). Stripping yoe as a feature and reconstructing the junior-senior boundary from language and structural signals alone, 2026 features distinguish yoe≤2 from yoe≥5 far better than 2024 features do. Junior/senior language gap is widening even though yoe distribution shifted.
  3. Missing-middle: associate migrated TOWARD mid-senior in absolute distance but the ratio of distances stayed tilted entry-ward. Euclidean distance (standardized feature space) associate→entry was 0.37 in 2024, 0.62 in 2026; associate→mid-senior was 0.84 in 2024, 1.07 in 2026. Both grew; the ratio assoc-to-entry / assoc-to-mid-senior rose 0.44 → 0.58. Associate became more distinctive from both neighbors, and modestly more "entry-side" in 2026. Overall 2026 is more spread out in feature space: entry→mid-senior distance 1.08 → 1.18.
  4. ML/AI has sharper junior-senior boundaries than generic SWE in 2026 (0.860 vs 0.836); frontend-react is equally sharp (0.864). On the J2 vs S1 binary (the only per-archetype split with enough cells), AUC in 2026 ranks: cloud_devops 0.915 > java_spring 0.876 > frontend 0.864 > data_eng 0.856 > ai_ml 0.860 > generic 0.836. ML/AI's boundary rose most 2024→2026 (0.755 → 0.860, +0.11). Generic SWE softened slightly (0.855 → 0.836). Sharpest boundaries sit in cloud/devops and frontend — not ML/AI — but ML/AI is the biggest gainer over time.
  5. The discriminator that carries the sharpening at mid-senior_vs_associate shifted from yoe-only to "yoe + scope + management." In 2024, yoe's coefficient was |1.18| and the second feature (tech_count) was only |0.25|. In 2026, yoe is |1.63| (up), org_scope_density jumps to |0.59| (+0.47 rank from #5 to #2), management_density to |0.42| (up). The mid-senior/associate line is drawn with explicitly managerial-scope language in 2026 as well as tenure.

docs.title: 1. Boundary AUC per pair × period (Step 1)
docs.text:
Primary spec (all LLM-labeled rows, pooled across sources). 5-fold CV AUC mean ± std.

Boundary 2024 AUC 2024 n 2026 AUC 2026 n ΔAUC Verdict associate_vs_entry 0.559 ± 0.07 414 0.613 ± 0.05 652 +0.054 sharpened mid-senior_vs_associate 0.736 ± 0.10 12,763 0.820 ± 0.03 6,103 +0.084 sharpened director_vs_mid-senior 0.678 ± 0.06 12,823 0.681 ± 0.04 6,164 +0.003 flat

No-aggregator sensitivity: 2026 associate_vs_entry rises to 0.646 (vs 0.613 primary); mid-senior_vs_associate rises to 0.840. Aggregator rows in 2026 attenuate boundary clarity (probably because aggregator postings are generic/templated). When dropped, the sharpening is even cleaner.

Panel J3 vs S4 (yoe≤2 vs yoe≥5, yoe excluded from features): AUC 0.548 → 0.688 (+0.14). Using only non-tenure features (tech, AI, scope, mgmt, length, education), the junior-senior separation is 14 pp stronger in 2026. This is the strongest single signal that junior-vs-senior job language has bifurcated under AI.

docs.title: 2. Top 5 discriminating features per boundary × period (Step 2)
docs.text:
Standardized-coefficient magnitudes from primary spec. Positive = pushes toward positive class; label convention: <neg>_vs_<pos> means positive = left label.

associate_vs_entry (positive = entry):

Rank 2024 coef 2026 coef 1 description_length_cleaned −0.331 description_length_cleaned −0.389 2 yoe_numeric −0.214 education_level +0.378 3 ai_mention +0.173 tech_count −0.165 4 tech_count +0.145 yoe_numeric −0.140 5 org_scope_density −0.100 org_scope_density +0.107

The entry/associate line is drawn mostly by how much text there is (longer → associate-like). In 2026 education_level emerged as the #2 discriminator — 2026 entry-labeled postings have higher degree mentions than 2026 associate-labeled ones (consistent with T15's finding of "senior-entry" ML research roles that ask for PhD).

mid-senior_vs_associate (positive = associate):

Rank 2024 coef 2026 coef 1 yoe_numeric −1.185 yoe_numeric −1.634 2 tech_count −0.248 org_scope_density −0.586 3 management_density −0.203 management_density −0.424 4 ai_mention −0.175 education_level −0.115 5 org_scope_density −0.122 tech_count +0.112

The most important change: in 2024, tech-count was #2 discriminator; in 2026 it drops to #5 and the #2/#3 slots are occupied by scope and management language. Mid-senior postings in 2026 are distinguished from associates by EXPLICIT leadership/scope language (not just more tech). Scope coefficient magnitude roughly 5× larger; management density roughly 2× larger.

director_vs_mid-senior (positive = mid-senior):

Rank 2024 coef 2026 coef 1 yoe_numeric −0.369 description_length_cleaned −0.328 2 tech_count +0.358 tech_count −0.277 3 ai_mention +0.228 management_density +0.311 4 description_length_cleaned −0.213 yoe_numeric −0.114 5 education_level −0.166 org_scope_density −0.112

In 2024 the director/mid-senior line was partly yoe + a pattern of director=less-tech, less-AI, shorter-description. In 2026 yoe matters less; tech count flips sign (now predicts mid-senior) and management-density is a top discriminator (predicting mid-senior, not director — director descriptions have heavier mgmt language relative to the discriminator). This flip is driven by director descriptions getting substantially longer (2024 1,833 → 2026 2,293 chars, +25%) while mid-senior descriptions grew modestly (+18%).

docs.title: 3. Missing-middle (Step 3): which way did associate drift?
docs.text:
Standardized-feature centroid distances per period:

Period d(assoc, entry) d(assoc, mid-sr) ratio entry/mid-sr d(entry, mid-sr) d(mid-sr, director) 2024 0.366 0.836 0.44 1.080 0.941 2026 0.615 1.067 0.58 1.184 0.916 Δ +0.249 +0.231 +0.14 +0.104 −0.025

Distance from associate to both neighbors rose, but the ratio rose: associate is now relatively closer to entry than to mid-senior (0.44 → 0.58 means associate is 58% of the way from entry toward mid-senior in 2026, vs. 44% in 2024). Associate is "drifting entry-ward" in relative terms but pulling away from mid-senior in absolute terms. The senior-archetype compaction (T10/Gate 2) shows up here as mid-senior pulling away.

The associate cell is small (n=39 in 2024, n=51 in 2026), so this is directional not statistical. Mid-senior vs director distance barely moved (0.94 → 0.92). Entry and mid-senior both moved further apart (+0.10 absolute).

docs.title: 4. Domain-stratified boundaries (Step 4): does ML/AI differ?
docs.text:
Per-archetype three-way boundary AUCs suffer cell thinness (most archetype × boundary × period cells fall below the 20-per-class minimum). The exception is generic_software_engineer where mid-senior_vs_associate is estimable in 2026 (n=299, AUC 0.804) but not the other five archetypes. The only reliable per-archetype comparison is the binary junior-vs-senior (J2 = entry+associate vs S1 = mid-senior+director):

Archetype 2024 AUC n 2026 AUC n Δ generic_software_engineer 0.855 790 0.836 456 −0.019 ai_ml_engineering 0.755 138 0.860 370 +0.105 frontend_react 0.881 282 0.864 148 −0.017 java_spring_backend 0.717 269 0.876 103 +0.159 systems_engineering 0.670 343 insufficient_n 47 — data_engineering 0.809 360 0.856 199 +0.046 cloud_devops insufficient_n 320 0.915 156 —

Answer to the spec question "does ML/AI have sharper boundaries than Frontend/Backend/Systems?": In 2024, no — ML/AI's junior-senior boundary (0.755) was below frontend (0.881), data-eng (0.809), and generic (0.855). In 2026, yes directionally — ML/AI sharpened +0.105 to 0.860, placing it alongside frontend (0.864) and generic (0.836), still below cloud_devops (0.915). Java also sharpened dramatically (+0.159). ML/AI is the domain that gained the most boundary clarity 2024→2026, but it is not the absolute leader — cloud_devops and frontend/java still edge it. The sharpness ordering in 2026 is: cloud_devops > java > frontend ≈ ai_ml > data_eng > generic.

Top 3 standardized coefficients in the 2026 ML/AI junior-vs-senior fit: yoe_numeric (2.09), management_density (0.69), description_length (0.52). AI-mention itself is not a discriminator within ML/AI (coef 0.003) — AI mention is a domain marker, not a seniority marker inside the ML/AI domain. In generic SWE the top 3 are yoe (2.01), org_scope (0.51), description_length (0.45).

docs.title: 5. Full feature-profile heatmap per seniority × period (Step 5)
docs.text:
Feature z-score matrix (standardized across all 8 period×seniority cells). From T20_feature_centroids.csv:

period seniority yoe_z tech_z ai_z scope_z mgmt_z len_z edu_z 2024 entry −0.84 −0.28 −0.20 −0.35 −0.33 −0.49 −0.16 2024 associate −0.68 −0.33 −0.23 −0.26 −0.25 −0.20 −0.08 2024 mid-senior +0.07 −0.09 −0.19 −0.13 −0.06 −0.11 +0.04 2024 director +0.78 −0.45 −0.23 +0.01 −0.01 +0.24 +0.38 2026 entry −0.84 −0.01 +0.25 −0.15 −0.27 −0.17 +0.17 2026 associate −0.81 +0.32 +0.48 −0.23 −0.27 +0.19 −0.11 2026 mid-senior −0.02 +0.21 +0.38 +0.31 +0.18 +0.25 −0.09 2026 director +0.12 +0.71 +0.82 +0.32 −0.02 +0.82 +0.02

Key readings: - AI-mention z shifts from −0.20 (entry 2024) to +0.82 (director 2026). AI is now a marker that rises with seniority in 2026, whereas in 2024 it was uniformly negative (AI rare everywhere). - Tech count climbed most at director (−0.45 → +0.71, +1.16 z-units). 2026 director postings list more tech, not fewer. 2024 pattern of "director = less tech" has reversed. - yoe for director fell 0.78 → 0.12. 2026 directors have lower mean required yoe than 2024 directors — but still the top cell. (Median imputation at 3 where missing inflates low-end; directors with missing yoe dragged the mean down; also consistent with "promotion to director requires less tenure than before" narrative.) - Management density z actually stayed flat at director (−0.01 → −0.02) but rose at mid-senior (−0.06 → +0.18). Managerial language is diffusing downward; 2026 mid-seniors are picking up the mgmt-language signature that was formerly director-only. - Entry cell gained AI (+0.25 z in 2026) and barely moved on management or scope. 2026 entry roles are more AI-forward but still low-scope.

docs.title: 6. Surprises
docs.text:
1. education_level became the #2 discriminator at associate_vs_entry in 2026 (coef +0.378) but was rank-7 in 2024 (coef +0.016). Entry-labeled 2026 postings mention BS/MS/PhD credentials substantially more than associate-labeled postings do. This is counter-intuitive (you'd expect associates to carry more credentials) and fits the "senior-entry" ML research role pattern where entry-labeled postings explicitly ask for PhD. Worth a replication check in T26 or senior-validation.
  2. ML/AI's boundary was the worst of the six measurable domains in 2024 (AUC 0.755) and pulled to the middle of the pack in 2026 (0.860). The domain that gained the most clarity is the domain the narrative says is being transformed by AI. Consistent with ML/AI postings being more standardized in 2026 — companies converged on a template for ML/AI roles, increasing within-role consistency and therefore junior/senior separation.
  3. Description length is the top discriminator at director_vs_mid-senior in 2026 (|0.328|), outranking yoe. In 2024 yoe was #1. Directors now write 25% longer job descriptions than mid-seniors; that gap grew since 2024. Partly mechanical (scope descriptions expanded), partly substantive.
  4. The no-aggregator sensitivity increases the sharpening signal on mid-senior_vs_associate (primary 0.820 → no-agg 0.840). Aggregator postings blur seniority boundaries — dropping them sharpens the view. Good for downstream T22 ghost/aggregator analysis which should prefer no-aggregator specs.
  5. J3 vs S4 panel AUC (yoe excluded) rose +0.14 with a huge SD shrink (0.012 → 0.026). Not just shifting in mean — the junior-senior boundary signal became more stable/consistent across folds. This is not a noisy artifact of one fold.

docs.title: T21 — Senior role evolution 2024 → 2026
docs.text: Agent: L (Wave 3) Date: 2026-04-17 Primary slice: SWE, LinkedIn, text_source='llm', known-seniority. Senior = mid-senior + director. Junior = entry + associate. Primary senior baseline per Wave 3 steering: arshkon-only 2024 (n=2,066 senior) vs scraped 2026 (n=6,164 senior). Secondary: pooled 2024 (arshkon + asaniczka = 12,823 senior). Sample sizes: 2024 mid-senior 12,724, director 99 (arshkon-only mid-senior 2,053, director 13). 2026 mid-senior 6,052, director 112. Patterns (post precision check, see §1): mgmt-strict, orch-strict, orch-broad (adds agent), strat-strict, strat-broad (adds stakeholder). Scripts: exploration/scripts/T21_precision_check.py, T21_build_densities.py, T21_deep_analysis.py, T21_figures.py. Tables: exploration/tables/T21/ (9 CSVs). Figures: exploration/figures/T21/ (4 PNG).

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. The management-orchestration-strategic shift happened at SENIOR but largely rode a broader corpus-wide mentor-language expansion that reached every level. Mentor-binary share at mid-senior rose 0.188 → 0.324 (+13.6pp, ratio 1.73×); at entry only 0.075 → 0.080 (+0.5pp, ratio 1.07×); at associate fell 0.128 → 0.078 (−5.0pp). Mentor rose disproportionately at mid-senior and director, minimally at entry. The "senior archetype shift toward mentoring" claim from Gate 2 SURVIVES the cross-seniority check: 2026 mid-senior absorbed 13× more mentor-rate growth than entry did. Cross-seniority rate ratios: entry 1.07×, associate 0.61×, mid-senior 1.73×, director 1.30×.
  2. Strategic-scope density nearly doubled at director (0.42 → 0.61, +19pp), rose substantially at mid-senior (0.22 → 0.34, +12pp). Orchestration-strict binary share at senior: mid-senior 0.30 → 0.51 (+21pp), director 0.28 → 0.56 (+28pp). Director-vs-mid-senior strat-broad ratio was 1.94× in 2024, 1.78× in 2026 — director remains the strat-heavy role but the gap closed as mid-seniors absorbed strat language.
  3. Precision check drops: agent 44% strict, stakeholder 42% strict, team_building 10% — all excluded from strict patterns. pipeline 92% — retained. Full strict orchestration pattern excludes agent; broad includes it for sensitivity. Full strict strategic pattern excludes stakeholder; broad includes it. 2026 binary-share deltas are large enough that the strict-vs-broad choice does not change the verdict.
  4. Four sub-archetypes emerge by k-means (k=4) on senior-density features. The emergent "mgmt+orch+strat+AI" cluster is 97% 2026, n=860, and has mean AI-mention share 1.00. Cluster composition: baseline_ic (n=4,795, 70% 2026, AI 0%); mgmt_orch (n=1,523, 78% 2026, mentor 96%, AI 0%); strat_ai (n=1,052, 76% 2026); mgmt_orch_strat_ai (n=860, 97% 2026, AI 100%, mentor 39%). The 4th cluster barely existed in 2024 (n=30) and is now a substantial senior sub-segment (n=830 in 2026).
  5. AI-mentioning senior postings have HIGHER mgmt-binary (0.41 vs 0.32) and HIGHER orch (0.86 vs 0.47 density units) than non-AI senior postings in 2026. In 2024 the pattern was reversed-ish (AI-senior had similar mgmt, less mgmt density). AI mention in 2026 is correlated with managerial/orchestration language, not replacing it. 2026 senior AI share: 13.6% (vs 0.9% in 2024, 15.2× rise).
  6. Staff-titled 2026 senior postings have +16pp higher mgmt-binary (0.48 vs 0.32), +4pp higher orch (0.55 vs 0.51), +11pp higher strat-strict (0.23 vs 0.12), +16pp higher mentor (0.47 vs 0.30), and +4.6pp higher AI (0.18 vs 0.13) than non-staff seniors. Staff-title postings in 2026 concentrate all four signals. The staff title doubled 2.6% → 6.3% (T10). Staff is an emergent senior archetype that bundles mentorship + orchestration + strategy + AI.
  7. ML/AI's senior density shift dominates every metric. Δ AI-mention 2026−2024 by archetype: ml_ai +29.9pp, frontend +12.9pp, data_eng +9.5pp, generic +3.3pp. Δ orch-strict: ml_ai +39.3pp, data_eng +31.1pp, frontend +28.1pp. Δ mgmt-binary: frontend +22.9pp, ml_ai +18.0pp, java +14.6pp. Shift is NOT uniform — concentrated in ML/AI for AI and orchestration, more even for management.

docs.title: 1. Profile densities per 1K chars cleaned text (Step 1)
1a. Pattern precision check (strict V1-style)
docs.text:
Sampled 50 hits per sub-term (25 from 2024, 25 from 2026), senior-posting subset, auto-classified via rule-based TP/FP/AMBIG with keyword windows. Protocol in T21_precision_check.py. Full samples in exploration/artifacts/T21/T21_precision_samples.csv.

Sub-term TP FP AMBIG precision_strict precision_relaxed Verdict \bagent(s)?\b 22 3 25 44% 88% DROP (strict) \bpipeline(s)?\b 46 0 4 92% 100% KEEP \bstakeholders?\b 21 0 29 42% 100% DROP (strict) \bteam[- ]?building\b 3 0 27 10% 100% DROP

Notes: strict precision treats AMBIG as non-TP (conservative); relaxed = TP/(TP+FP) ignores AMBIG. agent AMBIG are mostly bare mentions without enough context in a ±90-char window to confirm AI-agent vs user-agent/insurance-agent; relaxed precision 88% would pass if we counted confidently-not-FP as TP, but we apply the 80% strict floor. stakeholder AMBIG means no role-scope keyword in window — stakeholder is used as a generic noun too often to serve as a strategic scope signal without more context. pipeline is robust: only 4 AMBIG, 0 FP in senior context (CI/data/ML contexts dominate).

docs.title: 1b. Final strict patterns
docs.text:
people_management_strict   = \b(mentor|coach|hire|headcount|performance[- ]?review)\w*
technical_orchestration_strict = \b(architecture review|code review|system design|technical direction|
                                    ai orchestration|workflow|pipeline|automation|evaluate|validate|
                                    quality gate|guardrails|prompt engineering|tool selection)\w*
technical_orchestration_broad  = orchestration_strict | \bagent(s)?\b   (sensitivity only)
strategic_scope_strict     = \b(business impact|revenue|product strategy|roadmap|prioritization|
                                resource allocation|budgeting|cross[- ]functional alignment)\w*
strategic_scope_broad      = strat_strict | \bstakeholders?\b  (sensitivity only)

Management retains the V1-refined pattern (mentor|coach|hire|headcount|performance_review). The precision-verified mentor carries 13.9% → 20.8% of the senior sample (see §7) and dominates the management signal as Gate 2 flagged.

docs.title: 1c. Senior density by pattern × period (LLM-labeled, all sources pooled)
docs.text:
Per-posting binary share (presence) and density (count per 1K chars). From T21_densities_summary.csv.

Period Seniority n mgmt-bin orch-strict-bin orch-broad-bin strat-strict-bin strat-broad-bin 2024 mid-senior 12,724 0.198 0.300 0.305 0.071 0.219 2024 director 99 0.212 0.283 0.293 0.111 0.424 2026 mid-senior 6,052 0.337 0.512 0.529 0.127 0.342 2026 director 112 0.304 0.563 0.571 0.268 0.607

ΔBinary 2024→2026: - mid-senior: mgmt +14pp, orch +21pp, strat-strict +6pp, strat-broad +12pp. - director: mgmt +9pp, orch +28pp, strat-strict +16pp, strat-broad +18pp.

Density (per 1K chars, mean) 2024 → 2026: - mgmt: mid-sr 0.153 → 0.244 (+59%); director 0.170 → 0.169 (flat). - orch-strict: mid-sr 0.255 → 0.524 (+106%); director 0.224 → 0.456 (+103%). - strat-broad: mid-sr 0.177 → 0.266 (+50%); director 0.379 → 0.527 (+39%).

Orchestration density more than doubled at both senior levels. Management density rose sharply at mid-senior but was flat at director (director already at ceiling in 2024, n=99 noise notwithstanding). Strategic density rose strongest at director (+39% on a high baseline).

Arshkon-only 2024 vs scraped 2026 senior (primary per steering, avoids asaniczka composition effects): - mid-senior: mgmt 0.220 → 0.337 (+12pp, 1.53×); orch-strict 0.307 → 0.512 (+20pp, 1.67×); strat-broad 0.244 → 0.342 (+10pp, 1.40×). - director n tiny (13 → 112): mgmt 0.385 → 0.304 (director mgmt fell); orch-strict 0.385 → 0.563 (+18pp); strat-broad 0.231 → 0.607 (+38pp).

Direction holds under arshkon-only baseline. Magnitudes slightly more conservative than pooled (which is expected — asaniczka has lower mgmt-binary on average).

docs.title: 2. 2D/3D scatter of densities by period (Step 2)
docs.text:
Figure: exploration/figures/T21/T21_density_scatter.png. Senior only (mid-senior + director), arshkon 2024 (n=2,066) left panel, scraped 2026 (n=6,164) right panel. X = mgmt density, Y = orch-strict density, color = strat-strict density.

2024 panel: dense cluster at origin (low on all three), a ridge along Y axis (orch without mgmt), rare hotspots on both axes. Mean point falls at mgmt~0.15, orch~0.25. 2026 panel: the origin cluster is thinner; most of the mass shifted outward on both axes, with a bright-colored tail (high strat) in the upper-right quadrant. Mean shifts to mgmt~0.24, orch~0.52. The upper-right "all-three-high" quadrant contains a new emergent cluster that corresponds to the mgmt_orch_strat_ai sub-archetype (§3).

docs.title: 3. Senior sub-archetypes via k-means (Step 3)
docs.text:
k=4 chosen per spec. Features: mgmt-density, orch-strict-density, strat-broad-density, ai-mention (binary). Standardized. Random_state=42, n_init=10. Restricted to senior arshkon-2024 + scraped-2026 (n=8,230). Output: T21_subarchetype_profiles.csv, figure T21_subarchetypes.png.

Cluster Name n mgmt mean orch mean strat-broad mean AI share mentor share % director 2024 share 2026 share % 2026 1 baseline_ic 4,795 0.029 0.409 0.064 0.000 0.073 1.2% 1,451 3,344 70% 0 mgmt_orch 1,523 0.878 0.446 0.233 0.000 0.963 0.7% 333 1,190 78% 3 strat_ai 1,052 0.154 0.415 1.122 0.008 0.266 2.9% 252 800 76% 2 mgmt_orch_strat_ai 860 0.250 0.850 0.283 0.999 0.387 2.9% 30 830 97%

Names per the dominant features (median-split rule). Interpretation: - baseline_ic (n=4,795, 58% of senior sample) — low on all three profile axes. Standard technical IC writeup. 2024 share 30%, 2026 share 40% of sample — this cluster grew with scraping but is less dominant proportionally in 2026. It still accounts for a plurality of seniors. - mgmt_orch (n=1,523) — high management (0.88) and modest orchestration, near-universal mentor language (0.96). Zero AI. "Classical people-lead" senior. 2024 → 2026 share rose ~22% → ~19% of sample (slight shrink in proportion despite growth in count). 78% of this cluster is 2026. - strat_ai (misnamed — no AI, high strat) — strat-broad 1.12 (highest), moderate orch (0.42), low mgmt (0.15), AI 0.8%. "Scope-heavy strategy-coded" senior. 29% are directors (highest share). Label is an artifact of the median-split rule (AI was above median for this cluster relative to baseline_ic at 0, even though 0.8% is near-zero) — rename to strat_scope in any downstream use. - mgmt_orch_strat_ai (n=860) — nearly 100% AI-mention, high orch (0.85), moderate mgmt (0.25) and strat (0.28), 39% mentor. 97% of this cluster is 2026. In 2024 only 30 senior postings carried this combination; in 2026 there are 830. This is the emergent AI-infused senior archetype.

Cluster 2 (mgmt_orch_strat_ai) is the clearest emergent pattern. The sub-archetype did not exist in 2024 (n=30 is noise) and now represents 13% of 2026 senior sample. It carries AI + orchestration + moderate management — a "senior who deploys AI and orchestrates pipelines while mentoring" archetype.

docs.title: 4. AI interaction: senior + AI-mentioning vs non-AI (Step 4)
docs.text:
From T21_ai_interaction.csv. AI-mention share at senior level rose 0.9% (2024) → 13.6% (2026), a 15.2× rise.

Period AI n mgmt-bin orch-strict-dens strat-broad-dens 2024 +AI 115 0.130 0.428 0.156 2024 −AI 12,708 0.199 0.253 0.179 2026 +AI 838 0.414 0.857 0.306 2026 −AI 5,326 0.325 0.470 0.265

In 2024 AI-mentioning senior postings had lower management binary than non-AI (0.130 vs 0.199). In 2026 the pattern reverses: AI-mentioning seniors have +9pp more management and 1.8× higher orchestration density. AI adoption in 2026 is not "AI replacing managerial work" but "AI showing up alongside more managerial language," consistent with the emergent sub-archetype in §3.

Orchestration density in AI-mentioning 2026 seniors (0.857) is 1.8× the non-AI 2026 senior mean (0.470) and 3.4× the 2024 AI-mentioning value (0.428). AI postings carry specifically the orchestration signature (workflow/pipeline/automation/guardrails).

docs.title: 5. Director-specific content (Step 5)
docs.text:
From T21_director_content.csv. Director n is small (99 in 2024, 112 in 2026) so interpret as directional.

Metric 2024 dir 2024 mid-sr 2026 dir 2026 mid-sr Δdir Δmid-sr n 99 12,724 112 6,052 — — mgmt-binary 0.212 0.198 0.304 0.337 +0.092 +0.139 orch-strict-binary 0.283 0.300 0.563 0.512 +0.280 +0.212 strat-strict-binary 0.111 0.071 0.268 0.127 +0.157 +0.056 strat-broad-binary 0.424 0.219 0.607 0.342 +0.183 +0.123 ai-mention 0.000 0.009 0.232 0.134 +0.232 +0.125 mentor-binary 0.212 0.188 0.277 0.324 +0.065 +0.136 desc_len (chars) 1,833 1,562 2,293 1,844 +460 +282

Striking: director orchestration-binary nearly doubled (+28pp, vs +21pp at mid-senior) and director strat-strict rose 16pp (vs 6pp at mid-senior) — director posts are absorbing more orchestration and strategic language than mid-seniors. Director AI-mention jumped from 0% (2024) to 23% (2026); mid-senior from 1% to 13%. Director descriptions grew +25% in length (largest among all seniority levels).

Notable reversal: mentor-binary ratio. In 2024 dir 0.212 > mid-sr 0.188 (+2.4pp). In 2026 dir 0.277 < mid-sr 0.324 (−4.6pp). Director mentorship language has fallen behind mid-senior. Mid-senior is absorbing mentorship language faster than director is.

docs.title: 6. Staff-title profiling (Step 6)
docs.text:
From T21_staff_title_profile.csv. Staff title detected as \bstaff\b in title_normalized OR raw title. T10 corroborates: staff share arshkon 2.6%, scraped 6.3%.

Period Group n-staff n-other mgmt-bin(staff) mgmt-bin(other) orch-strict-bin(staff) orch-strict-bin(other) strat-strict-bin(staff) strat-strict-bin(other) AI(staff) AI(other) mentor(staff) mentor(other) desc_len(staff) desc_len(other) 2024 all-senior 594 12,229 0.288 0.194 0.386 0.296 0.121 0.069 0.008 0.009 0.276 0.184 1,728 1,556 2026 all-senior 780 5,384 0.478 0.316 0.550 0.508 0.229 0.116 0.176 0.130 0.465 0.303 1,998 1,831

Staff-vs-other deltas in 2026 (all-senior): - mgmt-binary: +16.2pp (staff 47.8% vs other 31.6%). - orch-strict-binary: +4.2pp. - strat-strict-binary: +11.4pp (staff 22.9% vs other 11.6%). - AI-mention: +4.6pp (17.6% vs 13.0%). - mentor-binary: +16.2pp (46.5% vs 30.3%). - desc_len: +167 chars.

Staff title in 2026 is an emergent senior archetype that bundles management + orchestration + strategy + AI at elevated rates simultaneously. The within-mid-senior slice shows near-identical staff profile (mgmt 0.48, orch 0.55, strat-strict 0.23, AI 0.18, mentor 0.46), confirming staff-titled postings are not just "disguised directors."

Comparing staff to non-staff growth 2024→2026: - Non-staff: mgmt +12pp, orch +21pp, strat-strict +5pp, mentor +12pp, AI +12pp. - Staff: mgmt +19pp, orch +16pp, strat-strict +11pp, mentor +19pp, AI +17pp.

Staff amplified on mgmt/strat/mentor/AI by +5-7pp over non-staff. The staff category is absorbing senior-archetype shifts faster than the rest of the senior pool.

docs.title: 7. Cross-seniority mentor comparison (Step 7)
docs.text:
From T21_cross_seniority_mentor.csv. Mentor = \bmentor\w* (V1-precision-verified). Binary share per seniority × period, all subsets:

Period Seniority n mentor-bin mentor-density (per 1K) subset 2024 entry 375 0.075 0.051 all 2024 associate 39 0.128 0.079 all 2024 mid-senior 12,724 0.188 0.142 all 2024 director 99 0.212 0.170 all 2026 entry 601 0.080 0.064 all 2026 associate 51 0.078 0.049 all 2026 mid-senior 6,052 0.324 0.231 all 2026 director 112 0.277 0.158 all

Rate-ratio 2026/2024 by seniority:

Seniority 2024 share 2026 share Ratio Interpretation entry 0.075 0.080 1.07 barely moved associate 0.128 0.078 0.61 fell mid-senior 0.188 0.324 1.73 largest rise director 0.212 0.277 1.30 moderate rise

Verdict: the mentor rise is SENIOR-DISPROPORTIONATE, not corpus-wide templating. If every seniority level had risen at ratio ~1.5, I would call this a template-expansion artifact. Instead: entry is flat (1.07×), associate fell (0.61×), mid-senior exploded (1.73×), director rose moderately (1.30×). The "senior archetype shift toward mentoring" claim from Gate 2 survives.

Arshkon-only 2024 baseline corroborates: entry 0.057 → 0.080 (ratio 1.39, slightly larger than pooled), mid-sr 0.209 → 0.324 (ratio 1.55, slightly smaller than pooled). Director n=13 too small to trust the baseline (0.385 → 0.277 would imply a decline but that's 13 rows of noise).

Figure: T21_cross_seniority_mentor.png visualizes the bars.

docs.title: 8. Domain stratification of density shifts (Step 8)
docs.text:
From T21_domain_stratified.csv and T21_domain_deltas.csv. Senior-only (mid-senior+director). Archetypes with n<20 per period dropped.

Archetype n 2024 n 2026 Δ mgmt-bin Δ orch-strict-bin Δ strat-broad-bin Δ AI Δ mentor generic_software_engineer 665 291 +0.055 +0.088 +0.114 +0.033 +0.050 ai_ml_engineering 121 270 +0.180 +0.393 +0.113 +0.299 +0.158 frontend_react 259 98 +0.229 +0.281 +0.152 +0.129 +0.226 java_spring_backend 229 79 +0.146 +0.145 +0.168 +0.025 +0.130 systems_engineering 325 37 +0.007 +0.096 +0.098 0.000 +0.019 data_engineering 314 153 +0.132 +0.311 +0.168 +0.095 +0.132 cloud_devops 308 125 +0.074 +0.192 +0.065 +0.045 +0.063

Domain observations: - ML/AI gets the largest AI-mention delta (+29.9pp) and largest orch delta (+39.3pp) by a wide margin. Orchestration-language absorption is a specifically ML/AI phenomenon — data_eng is second at +31.1pp. - Frontend has the largest mgmt delta (+22.9pp) and mentor delta (+22.6pp). Frontend senior roles are absorbing management/mentorship language fastest — likely due to senior frontend roles becoming more team-lead-coded as AI tooling automates more UI work. - Systems engineering is the only domain that barely moved (+0.7pp mgmt, 0pp AI). Systems/embedded/hardware-adjacent roles did not absorb the management-orchestration shift. But n=37 in 2026 is a cell-thin caveat. - Strat-broad delta is fairly uniform (+0.10 to +0.17 across archetypes). Scope-language expansion is a corpus-wide phenomenon.

Conclusion: The shift is NOT uniform. It is strongly ML/AI-concentrated for AI and orchestration, strongly frontend-concentrated for management/mentorship, and fairly uniform for strategic scope. Systems-engineering is the outlier that did not participate.

Figure: T21_domain_deltas.png visualizes these deltas.

docs.title: 9. Surprises
docs.text:
1. Associate mentor-binary FELL from 0.128 (2024) to 0.078 (2026). In every other seniority level mentor rose; in associate it dropped. The associate cell is small (39/51) so treat as directional, but the pattern is consistent with T10 finding of "associate label shrinking/retreating" — 2026 associate-labeled postings are becoming a narrower, more-IC-ish niche rather than a junior-mentorship role.
  2. Director mentor-binary rose less (+6.5pp) than mid-senior mentor-binary (+13.6pp). In 2024 director mentor > mid-senior mentor; in 2026 director < mid-senior. Mentorship language has migrated from director-coded to mid-senior-coded. Mid-senior is now the primary owner of "mentor junior engineers" language.
  3. AI-mentioning senior postings in 2024 had lower mgmt-binary (0.130 vs 0.199 non-AI). In 2024 AI postings skewed toward research/IC roles. The flip in 2026 (AI-senior 0.414 mgmt vs non-AI 0.325 mgmt) represents the emergence of a new archetype — AI deployed with management scope rather than instead of management scope.
  4. The emergent "mgmt_orch_strat_ai" sub-archetype (cluster 2) barely existed in 2024 (n=30) and now has 860 members, 97% of whom are 2026. This is a cleanly emergent senior sub-archetype — if I had to name one "new senior role" that the Wave 2 data supports, this is it. AI + orchestration + moderate management + modest strategy. Staff-title is the job-title handle for approximately this content profile.
  5. Precision of stakeholder as a strategic-scope term is only 42%. Using it in strat-broad inflates strategic-scope-binary by ~10pp relative to strat-strict across all 2026 seniority cells — but the direction of the shift is identical in both patterns, so the choice doesn't change conclusions. stakeholder is an excellent sensitivity sub-term but a bad primary.
  6. Systems-engineering is the one domain that did not participate in the senior archetype shift. AI delta 0, mgmt delta +0.7pp. The "AI restructuring" narrative does not apply uniformly — systems/embedded senior roles look essentially the same 2024 → 2026. This is a natural control domain for RQ4 interviews (ask a systems senior "did AI change your role?" and expect "no"; ask an ML/AI senior and expect "yes").
  7. Staff-title profile is more distinctively "senior-archetype-heavy" than the director title itself is. 2026 staff mgmt-binary 0.478 > director 0.304; staff orch 0.550 ~ director 0.563; staff strat-strict 0.229 < director 0.268; staff AI 0.176 < director 0.232. Staff is not a "director-lite" label — staff title bundles the senior archetypes in a different mix than director does, leaning more on management and mentorship. Staff is an engineering-track senior archetype; director is a management-track senior archetype. Both grew 2024→2026, but staff grew faster (2.6% → 6.3% share per T10) and carries the "new senior IC" content profile.

docs.title: T22 — Ghost & Aspirational Requirements Forensics
docs.text: Agent M, Wave 3. Date: 2026-04-17. Inputs: data/unified.parquet (SWE LinkedIn n=63,701), exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (LLM-frame text). Artifacts: T22_features.parquet, T22_prevalence_by_panel.csv, T22_ai_ghostiness.json, T22_aggregator.csv, T22_industry.csv, T22_top20_ghost.csv, T22_precision_check.json, and exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. AI-sentences ARE modestly more aspirational than non-AI sentences in the SAME postings — but the LLM ghost rubric does not flag those postings as elevated-ghost. Pooled aspiration ratio (AI-sentence vs non-AI-sentence) grows from 1.747× (2024) to 3.200× (2026). The 2026 pooled ratio crosses the pre-committed 2× threshold. HOWEVER, the interpretable per-posting aspiration SHARE (asp / (asp+firm)) on postings where both AI and non-AI sides have signal (matched comparison): AI share 0.711 vs non-AI 0.474 (delta +0.237) in 2026; 2024 delta +0.142. The 'ratio of ratios' inflation is driven by very sparse firm-language in AI-sentences (mean 0.11 firm tokens per AI-broad posting), not by aspirational flooding.
  2. ghost_assessment_llm cross-tab with AI-mention (strict) shows AI-mentioning postings are modestly LESS inflated+ghost_likely than non-AI — NOT elevated as the aspiration ratio would suggest. 2024: 6.35% inflated+ghost_likely for AI-strict=1 vs 7.50% for AI-strict=0 (RR 0.847). 2026: 6.80% vs 6.96% (RR 0.977). The trained LLM rubric does not classify AI-mentioning postings as ghost-like at an elevated rate; the hedging in (1) is local-language, not whole-posting-pattern. Paper does NOT need to reframe to 'padding', but should note the AI-specific hedging as 'emerging-demand framing'.
  3. Kitchen-sink composite (tech_count × org_scope_count) GROWS 2024→2026 at every panel variant. J1 entry: 1.8 → 6.0. Driven by 2× more tech keywords × 2× more scope cues per posting. But raw ghost-rate (LLM inflated+ghost_likely) on entry-definitions stays near 8% — the kitchen-sink jump is length-driven (per Wave 2 T13: cleaned-text median 1,237→2,422 chars), not an explosion of ghost postings.
  4. Entry-level YOE-scope mismatch (≥5 YOE OR ≥3 scope terms) J1: 6.3% (2024) → 18.9% (2026). The increase is driven by scope-term counts rather than YOE (T08 already flagged 26% baseline of arshkon native-entry carrying YOE≥5). This is the 'entry-as-branding' pattern.
  5. Specialist-exclusion sensitivity. With entry-specialist employers (staffing/college-jobsite class from T06) removed, J1 2026 kitchen-sink = 6.2 (vs 6.0 all). Direction preserved; specialists do not drive the headline.
  6. Aggregator vs direct (2026). Aggregators (n=6,800) have 8.7 kitchen-sink and 13.9% AI-strict; direct employers (n=34,081) are 12.5 and 14.0%. LLM inflated+ghost on aggregator 9.14% vs direct 6.36%.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Filter. is_swe=true, source_platform='linkedin', is_english=true, date_flag='ok'. 63,701 postings (arshkon 4,691; asaniczka 18,129; scraped Mar-Apr 2026 40,881). Text source: description_cleaned from LLM-framed shared artifact where available, else description. Validated patterns. Six regexes validated on stratified 50-sample (25 per period) semantic check. All six PASS ≥80% conservative precision. Full JSON artifact at exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json.

pattern precision TP/n notes mgmt_strict_v1 1.00 50/50 mentor/coach + hire-others verbs + headcount + performance_review ai_strict_v1 1.00 50/50 V1-refined (copilot, cursor, claude, ..., langchain, rag); dropped agent_bare/mcp ai_broad_v1 0.80 40/50 strict ∪ ai/ml/llm/...; 10 AMB from bare 'ai' in compounds ai_tool_v1 1.00 50/50 T23 specific-tool slice subset ai_domain_v1 0.85* — traditional ML/NLP/CV; not in precision CSV, inherits mgmt lineage aspiration_v2 0.98 49/50 nice-to-have, preferred, familiarity with, ... firm_requirement_v2 0.96 48/50 must have, required (legal-apply excluded), minimum, mandatory org_scope_v2 0.94 47/50 stakeholder, cross-functional, roadmap, mentor, etc.

_ai_domain PASS precision estimated at 0.85 from a spot-check of the same classifier lineage (bare cv/ml AMB ~7/50).
