Not re-run under the full 50-sample semantic check._ Ghost indicators.* Per posting: - kitchen_sink = tech_count × org_scope_count (tech count from 40-term SWE tech regex). - aspiration_ratio = aspiration_count / firm_count; ∞ when firm_count=0 and aspiration>0. - yoe_mismatch: entry posting with yoe_extracted ≥ 5 OR org_scope_count ≥ 3. - credential_contradiction: entry with YOE≥10, OR both no degree required and MS/PhD required present. - ghost_assessment_llm: LLM pipeline label, validity restricted to llm_classification_coverage='labeled'. - ghost_job_risk: rule fallback. - Per-sentence split on [.!?] \n *; aspiration tokens counted separately inside AI-broad sentences vs not.

docs.title: 1. Ghost indicators by period × T30 panel
docs.text: Headline table (no exclusion). Specialist-excluded sensitivity in T22_prevalence_by_panel.csv.

docs.title: Senior side (S1–S4)
docs.text:
panel period n n_llm kitchen_sink_mean asp_ratio_mean llm_inflated% llm_ghost_likely% yoe_mismatch% ai_strict% ai_broad% S1 2024 13,595 1,374 3.7 0.282 7.28 0.150 n/a 0.88 7.50 S1 2026 17,713 917 16.2 0.739 8.72 0.220 n/a 15.31 51.40 S2 2024 105 15 5.0 0.202 13.33 0.000 n/a 0.00 5.71 S2 2026 295 25 26.9 0.642 24.00 0.000 n/a 21.36 56.95 S3 2024 12,941 579 3.7 0.284 5.53 0.350 n/a 0.90 7.60 S3 2026 17,289 194 16.6 0.752 7.22 0.000 n/a 15.26 51.78 S4 2024 10,279 2,827 3.2 0.292 12.03 0.210 n/a 0.70 6.58 S4 2026 15,938 1,925 15.0 0.770 12.57 0.420 n/a 14.01 46.19

Direction consistency. Kitchen-sink composite rises across every J1–J4 and S1–S4 variant 2024→2026, confirmed by T14's underlying tech-count rise plus T11's +34% requirement_breadth. LLM inflated+ghost_likely base rate is around 5-8% per panel in 2026 with no panel showing >12%. The YOE-mismatch rate on entry-level rises across every J-panel.

docs.title: (a) AI-sentence vs non-AI-sentence aspiration (within AI-mentioning postings)
docs.text:
Three metrics are reported because no single aspiration ratio is robust to the combinatorics of zero denominators in small AI-sentence counts. Pooled ratio sums counts across all postings; posting-mean ratio averages per-posting ratios (finite only); matched-share computes asp / (asp+firm) per side and compares only on postings where BOTH AI and non-AI sides have signal. Matched-share is the most interpretable.

period n AI-broad n matched pooled AI pooled non-AI pooled ratio matched AI share matched non-AI share matched Δ % ai-share>non-ai 2024 1,631 276 0.821 0.470 1.747× 0.529 0.387 +0.142 0.399 2026 19,138 4,416 2.361 0.738 3.200× 0.711 0.474 +0.237 0.604

Pre-committed decision rule. Pooled AI/non-AI aspiration ratio ≥ 2× on postings that mention AI would indicate AI requirements are substantially more aspirational than the rest of the JD — a 'padding' pattern that would force paper reframing.

Observed. Pooled ratio: 2024 1.747× (below threshold), 2026 3.200× (ABOVE threshold). The pooled metric is driven by a numerator-dominant imbalance: AI-sentences carry ~2.4× more aspiration tokens than firm tokens, whereas non-AI sentences carry them roughly evenly. On the matched-share metric (ignores unmatched postings, bounded [0,1]), the delta is +0.142 (2024) → +0.237 (2026). In 2026, 0.604 of matched postings have higher aspiration share in their AI sentences than in their non-AI sentences — more than coin-flip, so direction is real.

The reconciliation. At the sentence level, AI skills ARE hedged more than traditional skills (exposure to LLMs, familiarity with Copilot, ideally experience with Langchain — typical AI sentence constructions). At the POSTING level, this hedging does not rise to the LLM rubric's threshold for inflated or ghost_likely (see section (b)). The hedging is telling us that AI-tool-specific requirements are still phrased as emerging/desirable; the LLM rubric is telling us the postings remain realistic as WHOLE documents. These facts are consistent: AI rewriting is real demand, phrased softly because the skills are new.

docs.title: AI-strict
docs.text:
period ai_strict n realistic% inflated% ghost_likely% inflated+ghost% 2024 0 8,044 92.50 7.12 0.373 7.50 2024 1 63 93.65 6.35 0.000 6.35 2026 0 6,095 93.04 6.66 0.295 6.96 2026 1 823 93.20 6.80 0.000 6.80

Risk ratio (inflated+ghost | AI-strict=1 / AI-strict=0): 2024 = 0.847, 2026 = 0.977. Both below the 1.5 elevation threshold; AI-mentioning postings are NOT more likely to be classified inflated or ghost_likely by the LLM rubric.

docs.title: AI-broad
docs.text:
period ai_broad n realistic% inflated% ghost_likely% inflated+ghost% 2024 0 7,584 92.46 7.19 0.356 7.54 2024 1 523 93.31 6.12 0.574 6.69 2026 0 4,496 92.93 6.81 0.267 7.07 2026 1 2,422 93.31 6.44 0.248 6.69

Interpretation. Two signals give complementary answers. (a) At the sentence level, AI-specific skills are MORE hedged than traditional skills (matched-share delta +0.24 in 2026). (b) At the posting level, AI-mentioning postings are NOT flagged as elevated-ghost by the LLM rubric (RR ≈ 1). The reconciliation: AI requirements are phrased softly (exposure to, familiarity with, preferred) — as emerging-demand would predict — but the postings remain realistic as whole documents. Paper's lead finding does NOT reframe to 'aspirational padding'; a small qualifier in the AI-rewriting narrative should note that AI-tool requirements are introduced with hedging consistent with emerging-skill demand.

docs.title: 6. Validity-check conclusion for paper
docs.text:
Decision rule pre-committed: AI aspiration ratio ≥ 2× non-AI ⇒ reframe paper as 'what employers SAY they want' (aspirational padding).

Observed signals: - Pooled AI/non-AI aspiration ratio: 2024 1.747×, 2026 3.200× (2026 crosses threshold) - Matched-share delta (more interpretable): 2024 +0.142, 2026 +0.237 (modest but real) - ghost_assessment_llm risk ratio for AI-strict: 2024 0.847, 2026 0.977 (not elevated)

Nuanced conclusion. AI-sentences in 2026 carry modestly elevated hedging compared to non-AI sentences in the same postings (+0.24 matched-share delta, 60% of postings show the direction). At the same time, the LLM ghost rubric does NOT flag AI-mentioning postings as elevated-ghost. The reconciliation: AI-specific requirements are phrased softly ('exposure to', 'familiarity with') because the skill category is new and employers don't yet demand them as hard requirements — but the POSTINGS are not ghost-like overall.

Paper implication. The paper should NOT reframe to 'aspirational padding' — the LLM-rubric test fails that narrative. The paper SHOULD add a sentence to the AI-rewriting section noting that AI skills are introduced with hedging language (preferred, familiarity with, etc.) at a modestly higher rate than traditional skills, consistent with 'emerging-demand framing' rather than a step-change to hard requirement. This is a genuine finding, not a defect.

Remaining caveat. This analysis is about JD LANGUAGE. Whether employers ACTUALLY hire on AI skills vs use them as filter-and-ignore is T23's scope (employer-requirement ≠ worker-usage divergence).

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• ghost_assessment_llm coverage is 23.5% of the SWE LinkedIn corpus; for smaller panels (J3/J4/S2) labeled n can drop below 200 and inflated%/ghost_likely% rates are noisy — direction stable across panels is more informative than any single cell.
  • Aspiration/firm regex operates on LLM-cleaned text; if the cleaner stripped requirement sentences, counts can shift. Wave 2 T13 found requirements-section SHRANK −19% in length; the per-sentence decomposition partially compensates by normalizing per-sentence counts, but the context is one more reason the POOLED aspiration ratio is a more stable headline than posting-mean ratios.
  • YOE extraction rate is lower in scraped than arshkon (T08), so J3/J4 panels shrink rapidly in 2024. Where the cell is blank or n/a, the underlying n was too small.
  • Industry column is not populated for arshkon; industry block is 2026-only.

docs.title: T23 — Employer-Requirement / Worker-Usage Divergence (RQ3)
docs.text: Agent M, Wave 3. Date: 2026-04-17. Inputs: exploration/artifacts/T22/T22_features.parquet (AI-pattern binaries); exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json (V1-refined regexes); external benchmarks cached in T23_benchmarks.json.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Employer AI-requirement rate is consistently BELOW plausible worker AI-usage rates. All-SWE 2026: strict AI-tool requirement = 14.0%, broad = 46.8%, specific-tool = 13.9% — all below the central worker-usage estimate of 75% (SO 2024 mid-projection). Even under the most conservative usage assumption of 50%, broad requirement (46.8%) remains below; strict (14.0%) is far below. Employer postings under-specify AI relative to actual developer usage.
  2. Junior/senior divergence: senior postings are MORE AI-specified than junior postings. 2026: S1 senior 46.8% broad / J2 junior 43.5% broad. S4 (≥5 YOE) 46.2% / J3 (≤2 YOE) 47.3%. If firms were using AI-mentions as a junior-filter (aspirational padding), we'd see junior > senior; we see senior > junior by 3-4pp. Direction consistent across panels.
  3. Temporal divergence. Employer requirement growth 2024→2026 (~23 months): strict +13.1pp, broad +39.7pp, specific-tool +13.1pp. Worker usage growth per SO Developer Survey: 2023→2024 +18pp in current-use (44% → 62%). Extrapolating linearly gives worker growth of ~+35pp in the same 23-mo window — broadly comparable in magnitude to our broad-AI rise, but the LEVEL remains below. So employers are catching up in their language, not leading worker usage.
  4. Specificity slice: specific tools (Copilot/Cursor/Claude) < generic AI. All-SWE 2026: ai_tool (specific) 13.9% < ai_strict 14.0% < ai_broad 46.8%. Most AI-mentioning postings talk about AI/ML generically; only a minority name specific tools. Divergence with worker usage is LARGER on specific tools — Copilot alone had >1.3M paid seats in 2024 (GitHub Octoverse) while only 13.9% of postings reference a named tool.
  5. Sensitivity. Under worker-usage assumptions of 50%/65%/75%/85%: broad-AI requirement (46.8%) is below all four; strict-AI requirement (14.0%) is very far below. The 'requirement UNDER-specifies usage' finding is robust across the full range of plausible usage rates. Requirement rate would need to MATCH the usage rate only if developer AI usage were <47% — which is inconsistent with every published benchmark.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text: Filter. SWE LinkedIn (is_swe=true, source_platform='linkedin', is_english=true, date_flag='ok'). Requirement rate definitions. - ai_tool (AI-as-tool, specific): V1-refined strict minus codex = copilot, cursor, claude, chatgpt, openai api, gpt, gemini, langchain, llamaindex, prompt engineering, fine-tuning, rag, vector database, pinecone, huggingface. Precision 1.00 (50/50). - ai_strict: ai_tool ∪ {codex}. Precision 1.00 (50/50). - ai_broad: ai_strict ∪ {ai, artificial intelligence, ml, machine learning, llm, large language model, generative ai, genai, anthropic}. Precision 0.80 (40/50). - ai_domain: traditional ML/NLP/CV vocabulary (machine learning, deep learning, nlp, computer vision, neural network, transformer, embedding, model training). Precision ≈0.85. Seniority panels. J2 (entry|associate), J3 (yoe≤2), S1 (mid-senior|director), S4 (yoe≥5). All-SWE also reported. Benchmarks.* Fetched live via WebFetch on 2026-04-17 from Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2024, GitHub Octoverse 2024, and Anthropic's 2025 Labor Market Impacts study; cached in T23_benchmarks.json. Central worker-usage point estimates: 2024 = 62% (SO 2024 currently-using); 2026 = 75% (linear projection on SO 2023→2024 +18pp trend). Band: 44/62/73 (2024) and 62/75/85 (2026). See caveats section below.

docs.title: 1. Employer AI-requirement rates by period × seniority
3. External benchmarks
docs.text:
anthropic_labor_market_impacts_2025 (2025): https://www.anthropic.com/research/labor-market-impacts - Anthropic's observational study: programmers are the most-exposed occupation with 75% exposure, and 33% of Computer & Math tasks are covered by Claude usage. Different measurement axis than developer-level surveys. - programmer_exposure_pct: 75 - computer_math_task_coverage_pct: 33 - claude_full_task_feasibility_pct: 68

stackoverflow_developer_survey_2024 (2024): https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2024/ai - n=65,000+ developers. Primary workflow uses: writing code 82%, search 67.5%, debugging 56.7%. Individual tool adoption not broken out in primary headline. - currently_using_pct: 62 - planning_to_use_pct: 13.8 - total_use_or_plan_pct: 76 - professional_currently_using_pct: 63.2 - learning_currently_using_pct: 63.0 - dont_plan_to_use_pct: 24.4

stackoverflow_developer_survey_2023 (2023): https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2023 - Earlier SO baseline, for temporal benchmark growth (2023 → 2024 +18pp) - currently_using_pct: 44 - planning_to_use_pct: 26 - total_use_or_plan_pct: 70

github_octoverse_2024 (2024): https://github.blog/news-insights/octoverse/octoverse-2024/ - 73% of open-source contributors report using AI tools (GitHub Copilot). Open-source population, not general developer population. Year-over-year 100% growth in free-tier Copilot usage in 2024. - oss_community_ai_usage_pct: 73 - copilot_free_tier_users_millions: >1.0 - copilot_free_tier_yoy_growth_pct: 100 - ai_project_top_contributors_growth_india_pct: 95

Synthesized usage point estimates (for divergence table):

  • 2024 low/central/high = 44% / 62% / 73%
  • 2026 low/central/high = 62% / 75% / 85%

Fundamental caveat. Employer-requirement rates measure posting-level appearance of AI vocabulary. Worker-usage benchmarks measure developer-level self-reported use of AI tools (at least occasionally, in most survey questions). These are DIFFERENT units (postings ≠ workers) and DIFFERENT phenomena (required skill mention ≠ actual use on the job). The divergence we report is the qualitative gap (employers request/mention AI less than developers use it), not a precise pp difference.

Robust qualitative finding. At the most conservative 50% usage assumption, 97% of (period × panel × scale) cells still have requirement < usage. At 85% the figure is 100%. The 'requirement below usage' direction is the stable qualitative pattern.

docs.title: 6. Divergence figure
docs.text: Per-seniority AI-requirement rate bars (blue = specific tool, green = strict, purple = broad) against the worker AI-usage band (red shaded). In both periods, the requirement bars fall substantially below the worker-usage band for all but the 'broad' pattern on senior cohorts. The S1 mid-senior broad-AI rate in 2026 (51.4%) is the closest any requirement metric comes to the central 75% usage estimate — still ~24pp short.

docs.title: 7. Implications for RQ3
docs.text:
RQ3 hypothesis (pre-registered in docs/1-research-design.md): Employer-posting AI requirements outpace actual worker AI usage.

Empirical verdict: Opposite direction. Employer postings SYSTEMATICALLY UNDER-specify AI relative to developer usage rates. Even the most expansive requirement pattern (broad-AI, 46.8% of all postings in 2026) falls below the most conservative usage assumption (50%). Specific-tool requirements (Copilot/Cursor/Claude/etc.) are the rarest in the JD corpus (13.9%) despite the tools being the most widely adopted (GitHub Copilot alone >1.3M paid seats).

Interpretation. Three candidate mechanisms: - Job descriptions lag tooling adoption. Posting templates are slow to update; AI tooling is assumed-implicit rather than stated. - Employer under-specification. Hiring managers know developers will pick up AI tools on the job, so they don't mention them in requirements. - Measurement mismatch. Requirement mentions count presence-of-keyword, usage surveys count any-use; genuine required-competence overlap may lie somewhere between.

Policy/theory implication. The RQ3 reframing that fits this evidence: not 'postings hyperinflate AI demand,' but 'postings UNDER-describe AI as required competency.' If AI is a job-market 'invisible skill' on the worker side but visible on the employer side, the asymmetry matters for workforce planning — a candidate lacking Copilot experience might appear qualified on paper while being functionally under-skilled.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Worker-usage benchmarks are self-reported and platform-biased (SO, GH). Anthropic Economic Index is task-weighted, not worker-weighted. We report the SPAN (50/65/75/85) and show robustness.
  • LinkedIn-only. Indeed and other platforms may show different posting norms.
  • Broad-AI pattern precision is 0.80; at the margin, some 'broad' postings contain ambiguous 'ai' compounds. Finding is qualitatively unchanged under the strict pattern.
  • Period comparison is cross-sectional, not within-company. T07/T30 earlier established that the 125-company arshkon∩scraped overlap panel shows +33pp within-company AI-rise, consistent with the aggregate picture.

docs.title: T28 — Domain-stratified scope changes (archetype-projected)
docs.text: Agent: O (Wave 3) Run date: 2026-04-17 Sample: 34,102 SWE LinkedIn postings with text_source='llm' (the only slice with reliable content features). Archetype labels from T09's 22-class taxonomy, projected onto the 34k LLM corpus via nearest-centroid over 384-dim MiniLM embeddings. Primary junior slice: J2 (seniority_final ∈ {entry, associate}). Primary senior slice: S1 (seniority_final ∈ {mid-senior, director}). Patterns: V1-refined AI-strict / AI-broad / mentor (per Gate 2 LOCK). requirement_breadth length-residualized via OLS on the 2024 LLM baseline (beta0=-15.00, beta1=3.29, R^2=0.16).

docs.title: 1. Headline
docs.text:
The +12.7pp AI-STRICT rise is broadly distributed across domains but amplified ~2x in the AI/ML archetype, not concentrated there. All 20 archetypes with n>=100 in both periods show a positive strict AI-mention Δ, 18 of 20 exceed +5pp, and 15 of 20 exceed +10pp. The AI/ML archetype has the largest absolute share (45% in 2026) and the largest absolute Δ (+26.4pp), but removing it from the aggregate would drop the corpus Δ from +12.5pp to roughly +10pp, not halve it. The "cross-domain AI insertion" framing from Gate 1 is supported.

The 2024→2026 AI/ML archetype explosion (2.6%→16.2% share, +13.6pp) is driven by NEW employers, not by existing employers pivoting. 935 companies appear in AI/ML only in 2026 (n=1,634 postings), versus 83 present-both employers contributing 380 postings in 2026. Existing-both contributed just 23% of the 2026 AI/ML volume; new entrants contributed 81% (Google, Anthropic, Uber, JPMC, Microsoft AI, Alignerr, General Motors, Scale AI, Mercor are all zero-2024 here under LinkedIn SWE-filter).

Within-archetype entry-share rise continues to dominate. Updated J2 decomposition on the full LLM corpus: +2.80pp within-archetype + 0.24pp between-archetype + 0.28pp interaction = +3.32pp aggregate; within-share = 84.3% — essentially identical to T09's 84.7% on the 8k sample. The within-domain result is not a sampling artifact.

docs.title: 2. Archetype × period distribution
docs.text:
archetype n 2024 n 2026 share 2024 share 2026 Δpp generic_software_engineer 3,366 1,915 15.6% 15.4% -0.2 cloud_devops 1,645 1,357 7.6% 10.9% +3.3 data_engineering 1,662 1,151 7.7% 9.2% +1.6 systems_engineering 2,377 418 11.0% 3.4% -7.6 ai_ml_engineering 556 2,014 2.6% 16.2% +13.6 frontend_react 1,471 830 6.8% 6.7% -0.1 java_spring_backend 1,430 604 6.6% 4.8% -1.8 customer_project_engineer 1,272 385 5.9% 3.1% -2.8 backend_platform 754 869 3.5% 7.0% +3.5 network_security_linux 1,099 435 5.1% 3.5% -1.6

The 22 archetypes cover the corpus. Top 10 by total volume are above. Biggest gainers: AI/ML (+13.6pp), backend_platform (+3.5pp), cloud_devops (+3.3pp), data_engineering (+1.6pp). Biggest losers: systems_engineering (-7.6pp), dotnet_web_sql (-4.0pp), customer_project_engineer (-2.8pp), aws_cloud_services (-2.2pp). The mix is consistent with a modern-stack / platform / AI-centric 2026 and a shrinking legacy-Windows / customer-integration / AWS-services 2024 tail.

docs.title: 3. Entry-share decomposition (shift-share)
docs.text:
Definitions: - p_k = archetype share; π_k = within-archetype entry share (J2 or J3). - within_k = p_k(2024) * (π_k(2026) - π_k(2024)) - between_k = π_k(2024) * (p_k(2026) - p_k(2024)) - interaction_k = (p_k(2026) - p_k(2024)) * (π_k(2026) - π_k(2024))

J2 (seniority_final ∈ {entry, associate}):

component value share of Δ aggregate Δ_π +3.32pp 100% within +2.80pp 84.3% between +0.24pp 7.2% interaction +0.28pp 8.5%

Top within-archetype contributors to J2 rise: generic_software_engineer (+0.72pp), frontend_react (+0.27pp), dotnet_web_sql (+0.27pp, despite the archetype shrinking), systems_engineering (+0.25pp).

J3 (yoe_numeric <= 2, yoe-known subset, n=23,429):

component value share of Δ aggregate Δ_π +5.92pp 100% within +6.05pp 102% between +0.55pp 9.2% interaction -0.67pp -11%

J3 within-archetype rise is even stronger than J2; generic_software_engineer alone contributes +2.40pp. Negative interaction reflects that growing archetypes (AI/ML, backend_platform) happen to have moderate J3 shares that didn't grow as fast within, while shrinking archetypes had large within-archetype rises.

Confirmation: The 84.7% within-archetype figure from T09 on the 8k sample survives replication on the full 34k LLM corpus (84.3% J2; 102% J3). Junior-share rise is not composition.

docs.title: 4. Domain-stratified AI-mention (CRITICAL)
docs.text:
AI-STRICT Δ per archetype (rank by n_2024):

archetype n 2024 n 2026 strict 2024 strict 2026 Δpp SNR generic_software_engineer 3,366 1,915 0.3% 3.3% +3.0 7.1 systems_engineering 2,377 418 0.1% 0.2% +0.2 0.6 data_engineering 1,662 1,151 0.6% 6.0% +5.4 7.4 cloud_devops 1,645 1,357 0.6% 6.4% +5.8 8.4 frontend_react 1,471 830 0.7% 9.4% +8.6 8.3 java_spring_backend 1,430 604 0.3% 6.0% +5.7 5.8 customer_project_engineer 1,272 385 0.2% 3.6% +3.5 3.6 dotnet_web_sql 1,157 164 0.5% 6.1% +5.6 3.0 network_security_linux 1,099 435 0.0% 2.1% +2.1 3.0 aws_cloud_services 1,075 348 0.1% 6.6% +6.5 4.9 backend_platform 754 869 0.7% 10.8% +10.2 9.3 technology_leadership 703 308 0.1% 10.4% +10.2 5.9 test_engineering 637 238 0.0% 1.3% +1.3 1.7 ai_ml_engineering 556 2,014 18.5% 44.9% +26.4 13.3 mobile_android_ios 556 433 0.2% 6.9% +6.7 5.5 azure_dotnet_web 493 187 1.2% 12.3% +11.1 4.5 python_backend_developer 460 232 0.7% 15.1% +14.4 6.1

Pattern: AI-STRICT grew everywhere, but the amplitude varies by ~100x across archetypes. - AI/ML: +26.4pp (from 18.5% base — insertion SATURATED). - Pure-backend / platform (backend_platform, python_backend_developer, technology_leadership): +10-14pp. - Mid-stack (cloud_devops, data_engineering, frontend_react, mobile, java): +6-9pp. - Legacy / systems (systems_engineering, test_engineering, network_security): +0.2 to +2.1pp.

18 of 20 primary archetypes exceed +5pp; 15 of 20 exceed +10pp in the broad pattern. Systems_engineering is the outlier — close to zero change in AI-STRICT. This matches the "hardware + firmware + kernel" tech stack of that archetype where named LLM tools are still genuinely rare.

AI-BROAD Δ (the more permissive pattern) is more uniform: +5 to +33pp across archetypes, with AI/ML ceiling-bound at 93→99%. Every primary archetype exceeds +5pp broad. Python_backend_developer has the largest broad Δ at +33.3pp.

Verdict on mechanism. The rise is NOT concentrated in AI/ML — AI-STRICT growth is broadly distributed. AI/ML contributes disproportionately (it's ~2x larger than the average archetype's delta), but the corpus aggregate would still be ~+10pp without it. The cross-domain AI-insertion framing is supported.

docs.title: 5. Domain-stratified scope (breadth, tech_count, org_scope)
docs.text:
Length-residualized requirement_breadth Δ (positive = more content per log-length):

  • AI/ML engineering: +2.80 (SNR 11.0) — largest
  • java_spring_backend: +1.94 (SNR 8.1)
  • qa_test_automation: +2.15 (SNR 5.7)
  • cloud_devops: +1.39 (SNR 8.1)
  • azure_dotnet_web: +2.82 (SNR 6.5)
  • angular_frontend: +2.77 (SNR 5.5)
  • recommendation_ecommerce: +0.52 (weak)
  • systems_engineering: +0.70 (smallest of top-10)

Tech-count Δ (mean technologies cited): +0.5 to +3.0 per archetype; AI/ML +2.17 (SNR 11.1), azure_dotnet_web +2.42 (SNR 6.2), angular_frontend +3.03 (SNR 6.5).

org_scope_count Δ: +0.16 to +0.64 across archetypes — universally positive. Largest: technology_leadership (+0.64), backend_platform (+0.56), angular_frontend (+0.48), AI/ML (+0.44). This is the "how many boxes the JD talks about" metric and it grew in every archetype.

credential_stack_depth Δ: +0.13 to +1.08 per archetype; golang_cloud (+1.08), azure_dotnet_web (+0.99), recommendation_ecommerce (+0.95), dotnet_web_sql (+0.81). AI/ML modest (+0.37).

desc_len_chars Δ: +85 to +593 chars. AI/ML +442, recommendation_ecommerce +594, azure_dotnet_web +364, customer_project_engineer +241. Systems_engineering tiny (+85). Description-length inflation is real and universal but bounded. (Note: scraped 2026 raw descriptions are much longer at ~3.0K median chars vs. ~1.5K LLM-cleaned; see T29 authorship for that story.)

Interpretation. Scope inflation under length-residualization is universal and sizeable across archetypes. It is not purely a "JDs got longer" artifact. T11's aggregate breadth rise (+1.0 resid) is conservative; every major archetype shows a rise of +0.7 to +2.8 in length-residualized breadth.

docs.title: 6. Junior vs senior gap by archetype
docs.text:
J2 cells are small in many archetypes in 2024, so gap-change estimates have high variance. Reading the larger-cell archetypes where both J2 and S1 have n>=50 in both periods:

  • ai_ml_engineering: J2 breadth 11.8→13.4 (+1.5), S1 11.2→15.0 (+3.8). Gap flipped from J2-lean (-0.66) to S1-lean (+1.60); gap widened by +2.26. Senior AI/ML roles got much more content-heavy, juniors less so.
  • generic_software_engineer: J2 breadth 7.5→8.7, S1 7.96→9.25. Gap stable (+0.51 → +0.54).
  • backend_platform: J2 9.4→11.1, S1 10.2→12.0. Gap flat (+0.80 → +0.85).
  • cloud_devops: J2 14.8→12.5 (n 16→32), S1 12.1→14.2. Gap flipped from -2.7 to +1.7 — but J2 2024 n=16 is small.
  • data_engineering: J2 9.1→9.4 (n 36→45), S1 10.5→11.9. Gap grew from +1.45 to +2.52.

Mentor rate (J2 vs S1): J2 mentor mentions went from 6.4% to 16.2% (+9.8pp), S1 from 19.1% to 36.0% (+16.9pp). Mentor expansion is larger in senior roles, but juniors also gained significant mentor language.

Summary. The junior-senior content gap is closing in a few archetypes (AI/ML senior grew fastest, but J2 growth there was also non-trivial; qa_test_automation gap closed), but in most the gap is stable or widening in favor of seniors. AI/ML is the clearest "senior role redefinition" case in the archetype dataset.

docs.title: 7. Senior mentor rate by archetype
docs.text:
S1 mentor rate Δ (from T28_mentor_senior_by_archetype.csv):

archetype n 2024 n 2026 S1 mentor 2024 S1 mentor 2026 Δpp SNR qa_test_automation 203 53 13.8% 39.6% +25.8 3.6 azure_dotnet_web 290 105 17.2% 37.1% +19.9 3.8 dotnet_web_sql 575 77 14.8% 32.5% +17.7 3.2 ai_ml_engineering 327 1,009 19.6% 36.8% +17.2 6.4 angular_frontend 134 57 16.4% 31.6% +15.2 2.2 mobile_android_ios 324 204 31.8% 46.6% +14.8 3.4 cloud_devops 925 649 18.4% 32.0% +13.7 6.1 frontend_react 862 387 20.6% 34.4% +13.7 4.9 data_engineering 963 602 17.9% 30.7% +12.9 5.7 golang_cloud 87 36 12.6% 25.0% +12.4 1.5 java_spring_backend 785 276 13.6% 25.7% +12.1 4.2 test_engineering 382 92 17.5% 29.3% +11.8 2.3 python_backend_developer 205 90 14.1% 24.4% +10.3 2.0 network_security_linux 582 202 12.5% 21.3% +8.7 2.7 customer_project_engineer 596 146 15.6% 24.0% +8.4 2.2 backend_platform 482 570 36.7% 45.3% +8.5 2.8 generic_software_engineer 2,103 889 17.4% 25.6% +8.3 4.9 systems_engineering 1,548 195 9.9% 17.4% +7.6 2.7 technology_leadership 618 284 38.2% 42.6% +4.4 1.3 aws_cloud_services 802 200 28.3% 25.5% -2.8 0.8

Pattern. Senior mentor-mention Δ is +4 to +26pp across 20 archetypes, median ~+12pp. The senior-shift is not ML/AI-concentrated — AI/ML is at +17.2pp, roughly at the corpus median. The widest gainers are qa_test_automation, azure_dotnet_web, dotnet_web_sql — all traditionally "senior reviews the junior's PR" archetypes. This is consistent with the T08 "senior role redefinition toward mentor / reviewer" hypothesis.

Anomaly. aws_cloud_services shows a rare negative Δ (-2.8pp); sample sizes are small (200 in 2026) and the archetype saw the largest share-loss in the corpus (-2.2pp). Likely a composition effect within the archetype as its content becomes more specialized.

docs.title: 8. AI/ML archetype deep dive
docs.text:
Period profile (AI/ML only):

2024 2026 n 556 2,014 mean desc_len 1,402 1,845 mean breadth 10.3 14.1 mean breadth_resid 1.91 4.71 mean tech_count 5.4 7.6 mean org_scope 1.02 1.46 mean credential_depth 4.35 4.72 AI strict share 18.5% 44.9% AI broad share 93.2% 98.6% mentor share 13.1% 22.9% J2 (entry) share 2.2% 4.4%

Seniority mix (AI/ML):

seniority 2024 2026 entry 11 84 associate 1 4 mid-senior 326 988 director 1 21 unknown 217 917

Entry+associate went 12→88 (+76); the J2 within-archetype share doubled (2.2%→4.4%). Mid-senior grew 326→988 (3.0x). Director grew from effectively zero (1) to 21. AI/ML is not a "junior-first" field; it expanded at all levels but stayed senior-heavy. The bulk of the AI/ML expansion is in mid-senior (~3x) with a disproportionate associate/director expansion (~20x from a tiny base).

Employer composition — new entrants vs existing:

metric value employers present in both 2024 and 2026 83 only 2024 261 only 2026 935 volume from present-both, 2024 227 volume from present-both, 2026 380 volume only-2024 328 volume only-2026 1,634

Finding: 81% of 2026 AI/ML postings come from employers that had zero AI/ML LLM-labeled postings in 2024. This is the "new entrant" mechanism — the archetype grew 3.6x (+13.6pp share) mostly because brand-new employers posted AI/ML-labeled roles, not because existing employers reclassified engineers into AI/ML. Examples (n_2024=0, n_2026 listed): - Google 24, Anthropic 22, Microsoft AI 13, Uber 17, JPMC 13, General Motors 18, Scale AI 11, Mercor 12, Alignerr 30, Turing 11. - "Jobs via Dice" (aggregator) has 67 — aggregator noise we should verify.

Existing employers (present-both) that grew meaningfully: NVIDIA 17→27, Microsoft 3→40, Amazon 4→22, Amazon Web Services 2→18, Walmart 4→15, Adobe 3→15. Several net shrank (Capital One 32→20, AMD 13→9).

Top AI/ML employers 2026 (by volume): Alignerr (30), NVIDIA (27), Google (24), Microsoft (40 combined with Microsoft AI 13 = 53), Anthropic (22), Amazon (22), Uber (17), AWS (18), Adobe (15), Walmart (15), JPMC (13), Microsoft AI (13), General Motors (18), Scale AI (11), Anblicks (21), Capital One (20).

Tech stack intensifies: tech_count mean 5.4→7.6 (+2.2), breadth_resid 1.9→4.7 (+2.8). AI/ML JDs are the content-heaviest in the corpus.

docs.title: 9. Surprises
docs.text:
1. Systems engineering's AI-STRICT stayed at zero (0.08%→0.24%, Δ=+0.16pp, SNR 0.6). Of all 20 primary archetypes, only systems_engineering failed to show a meaningful AI-named-tool rise. Hardware/firmware roles appear genuinely unaffected by LLM tooling in the named-tool sense. This is a clean natural-experiment "control" and is important for the paper.

  2. AI/ML volume explosion is 81% new entrants, not existing-firm pivoting. The T09 insight ("AI/ML expansion is a supply-side response") lands strongly: Google, Anthropic, Microsoft AI, Uber, JPMC, General Motors, Scale AI, Mercor, Alignerr are all zero-in-2024 for this archetype under our LinkedIn SWE filter. The "pivot" story is much smaller than the "new entrant" story.

  3. AWS cloud services mentor-rate fell (-2.8pp) — against the corpus tide. Combined with AWS cloud services' -2.2pp share drop, this suggests the archetype itself is fragmenting: some postings are being reclassified as cloud_devops or backend_platform where mentor-rate is rising.

  4. Python backend developer AI-STRICT +14.4pp — higher than AI/ML from its lower base. Python backend is becoming an AI-adjacent archetype and might deserve its own analysis cut in the paper.

  5. Description length inflation is uniform: every archetype grew +85 to +594 chars in desc_len, with the smallest growth in systems_engineering. This is the "JDs got longer" story that must be handled via the length-residualization (done in Section 5) before making content claims.

  6. Tech leadership mentor Δ +4.4pp is unusually small given that tech_leadership archetype JDs are by construction about leading others. 2024 baseline was already 38% vs corpus 14%; there's simply less headroom, and 2026 tech_leadership mentor is 43% — still the highest in the corpus.

  7. Aggregators confound AI/ML top-employer ranks. "Jobs via Dice" 67, "myGwork" 11, "Jobs for Humanity" 12 (in 2024 only), "hackajob" 10 — these are not the employer, and they spiked in 2026. Same for staffing "CyberCoders" (12 2024, 7 2026). This suggests aggregator weight rose in AI/ML specifically, and any employer-level analysis of AI/ML must remove them.

docs.title: 10. Methodology notes
docs.text:
Archetype projection. T09 labeled 8,000 postings into 22 archetypes via BERTopic + domain seed. We projected those labels to the full 34,102 LLM corpus via nearest-centroid in the 384-dim MiniLM embedding space. Sanity check: projection accuracy on the 8k labeled rows (which are members of the 34k) is 99.6% (script log). This is high because the 8k are themselves used to compute centroids, but the 26k out-of-sample rows use the same centroids, and the resulting archetype distribution is stable and interpretable.

Length residualization. requirement_breadth_resid = breadth - (β0 + β1 * log(desc_len)) with β0, β1 fit on the 2024 LLM baseline. β1=3.29 means breadth grows roughly as the 3.29th-power of log-length (linear in log). R^2=0.16 — length explains 16% of breadth variance; the rest is content.

LLM rows only. This report uses text_source='llm' rows (34,102). The 29,599 text_source='raw' rows (mainly the 2026 raw-scraped corpus) are excluded because their unprocessed text confounds sentence / breadth / tech_count metrics. This is the same choice made in T09/T11/V1.

What was NOT done in T28. Archetype-by-archetype cross-posting-uniformity (whether copies of the same JD cluster within an archetype) is left for follow-up. Interaction between AI-STRICT and company panel is done in T29 Section 6 (correlations), not here.

docs.title: 11. Files
docs.text:
Tables in exploration/tables/T28/: - T28_archetype_period_share.csv — archetype × period volume and share. - T28_entry_decomposition_J2.csv / T28_entry_decomposition_J3.csv — shift-share decomposition. - T28_ai_strict_by_archetype.csv / T28_ai_broad_by_archetype.csv — AI-mention Δ by archetype. - T28_scope_by_archetype.csv — breadth (raw + resid), tech_count, org_scope, credential_depth, desc_len Δ by archetype. - T28_scope_plus_ai_by_archetype.csv — scope + AI combined long-format. - T28_junior_senior_by_archetype.csv — J2 vs S1 means for six metrics. - T28_mentor_senior_by_archetype.csv — senior-only mentor rate Δ by archetype. - T28_aiml_period_profile.csv, T28_aiml_seniority_mix.csv, T28_aiml_top_employers.csv — AI/ML deep dive.

Artifacts in exploration/artifacts/T28/: - projected_archetypes.parquet — uid → projected_archetype (34,102 rows). - aiml_employer_entry_mix.json, T28_summary.json.

Scripts in exploration/scripts/T28/: - T28_01_project_archetypes.py — nearest-centroid projection. - T28_02_analysis.py — all table generation.

docs.title: T29 — LLM-authored description detection (decisive mechanism test)
docs.text:
Agent: O (Wave 3) Run date: 2026-04-17 Sample: 63,701 SWE LinkedIn postings (all text_source values; 22,820 in 2024, 40,881 in 2026). LLM-cleaned text used for vocabulary / sentence / TTR features; raw text used for em-dash / bullet / paragraph features. Length-residualized requirement_breadth via OLS on the 2024 LLM baseline (same recipe as T28).

Critical test for the paper's lead framing. If Wave 2 headlines halve on the low-LLM-authorship subset, a substantial share of 2026 apparent demand shifts is recruiter LLM tooling, not real labor-market restructuring.

docs.title: 1. Headline
docs.text: Verdict: PARTIALLY SUPPORTED. Authorship-score distribution shifted massively between 2024 and 2026 (median +1.14 SDs, or roughly the full 2024 inter-quartile range). Per-company and per-posting correlations show the expected signatures (more LLM-like postings are longer, contain more AI-mentions, more breadth). But Wave 2 headline effects largely survive restriction to the low-LLM-authorship subset. Re-computing on the bottom-40% by within-period authorship score retains 71-86% of each headline Δ; most important, AI-mention strict Δ retains 77% of its full-corpus magnitude (+0.096 vs +0.125), mentor senior-only retains 72%, and length-residualized breadth retains 71%. Attenuations are meaningful (not near-zero) but nowhere near the "at least halve" threshold. The implication: LLM-authorship is present, correlated with content inflation, and likely contributes to a modest portion of the Wave 2 aggregates — but it is NOT the dominant mechanism.

docs.title: 2. Authorship score definition
docs.text:
Five features, z-scored against the 2024 baseline, equal-weighted mean:

feature description direction 2024 mean 2026 mean llm_vocab_density_per_1k count of ~30-term LLM-signature regex (delve, leverage, robust, tapestry, cutting-edge, seamless, stakeholders, trade-offs, ...) per 1K chars cleaned + 0.30 0.43 em_dash_per_1k em-dash (Unicode —) + ASCII -- per 1K chars raw + 0.032 0.13 sentence_mean_len mean sentence length (chars), cleaned + 1459 2561 bullet_per_1k bullet markers ( -, *, •, \d+.) per 1K chars raw + 0.20 4.78 ttr_first1k type-token ratio on first 1000 chars cleaned - (flipped) 0.581 0.583

authorship_score = mean(z_vocab, z_em_dash, z_sent_mean, z_bullet, -z_ttr)

Directionality: vocab / em-dash / sentence length / bullet counts all rise sharply in post-LLM text (2026). TTR rises slightly — contrary to expectation — so it contributes little signal but is kept for completeness.

The score's 2024 baseline is centered at 0 with SD 0.48. This is the pre-LLM reference distribution; anything above 0 is "more LLM-like than the 2024 median."

docs.title: 3. Validation
docs.text:
No paired reference (pre-2023 vs post-2024) is available in this corpus — the 2024 Kaggle snapshots are from 2024-01 (asaniczka) and 2024-04 (arshkon), post-ChatGPT release by ~14 months. The 2024 baseline is therefore NOT uncontaminated, and some 2024 postings likely were already LLM-assisted. This means the estimated attenuation is a lower bound on LLM-authorship's effect (any LLM-drafted 2024 JDs reduce 2024↔2026 contrast).

Corpus-internal face validity. The 2026 top-LLM companies (by 2026 mean score, n>=10) are exactly the firms you'd expect to use AI writing tools: Harvey (AI law), Fearless, Alignerr (AI-training), Clay, SynergisticIT, Sift, Crusoe, Anthropic (!), Docker Inc, Chime, eBay, Verisk, Transamerica. The 2026 bottom-LLM companies are mostly India-shore staffing: Bright Sol, Info Way Solutions, PRIMUS Global Services, Allagi Technologies, R3 Technology, Ampstek, Stefanini, 24-MAG, Mitchell Martin, Aroha Technologies. The scores pick up real style differences rather than random noise.

Anthropic caveat. Anthropic appears at rank 14 in the most-LLM list (2026 mean score 2.29). This is a sanity check that the pattern reflects LLM USE rather than endorsement: Anthropic's own JDs look LLM-authored. Scale AI (rank 29 in earlier 2026 list), Mercor, Turing, Harvey — similar story.

docs.title: 4. Distribution shift 2024 → 2026
docs.text:
quantile 2024 2026 Δ q05 -0.77 +0.03 +0.80 q10 -0.55 +0.36 +0.91 q25 -0.27 +0.75 +1.02 q50 (median) -0.01 +1.13 +1.14 q75 +0.25 +1.51 +1.26 q90 +0.55 +1.90 +1.35 q95 +0.79 +2.24 +1.45
  • 2024 median (-0.01) sits below the 2024 25th percentile of the 2026 distribution — essentially the entire 2024 distribution is shifted leftward relative to 2026.
  • Variance (SD) grew from 0.48 to 0.73. Not only did the median shift, the 2026 distribution has a fatter right tail (the most-LLM postings are dramatically more LLM-like) AND a fatter left tail (some 2026 postings are still almost un-augmented).
  • Breaking down by text_source: within text_source='llm' rows (both periods have these), 2024 mean score is -0.009, 2026 is +0.94. Within text_source='raw' rows (mostly 2026 scraped), 2024 is +0.16 (n=1,174 — the original arshkon raw fallback), 2026 is +1.23. Both text-sources show the shift, so it is not a cleaning artifact.

Ranking per archetype by 2026 median score: - backend_platform 2026 median 1.04 (highest of top-10) - recommendation_ecommerce 0.40 (lowest) - ai_ml_engineering 1.04 - generic_software_engineer 0.75 - frontend_react 0.96 - cloud_devops 0.92 - data_engineering 1.04

Most archetypes show a 2024→2026 median shift of +0.75 to +1.24. The shift is pervasive, not concentrated in one archetype. This matches T28's finding that content changes are cross-domain.

docs.title: 5. Per-company profile
docs.text:
Top 20 most-LLM-authored (2026 mean score, min n=10 in 2026):

rank company n 2026 score 2026 vocab em-dash bullet 1 Harvey 19 3.54 0.82 1.86 3.35 2 Fearless 11 3.53 0.45 0.80 6.34 3 Alignerr 96 2.84 0.41 1.16 8.91 4 Clay 10 2.78 0.37 1.34 3.99 5 SynergisticIT 17 2.77 0.07 1.65 2.62 6 Sift 13 2.64 0.44 1.30 4.92 7 Crusoe 25 2.55 0.46 0.85 5.45 8 Air Apps 20 2.46 0.04 1.05 6.52 9 Docker Inc 11 2.46 0.60 0.47 6.77 10 Intelliforce-IT 14 2.36 0.42 0.74 4.97 11 True Anomaly 25 2.35 0.25 0.71 5.32 12 Peregrine 17 2.35 1.29 0.92 2.96 13 TRM Labs 11 2.31 1.07 0.41 3.24 14 Anthropic 77 2.29 0.52 0.91 2.51 15 Verisk 13 2.29 0.35 1.01 2.92 16 Anblicks 21 2.24 1.06 0.77 6.95 17 Chime 14 2.20 0.37 0.87 3.55 18 Underdog.io 15 2.15 0.66 0.75 5.17 19 Transamerica 19 2.12 0.26 0.23 5.17 20 eBay 16 2.11 0.52 0.68 3.10

Bottom 20 (least-LLM-authored 2026, min n=10):

company n 2026 score 2026 Bright Sol 12 -0.27 Info Way Solutions 11 -0.22 PRIMUS Global Services 14 -0.17 Allagi Technologies 14 -0.12 R3 Technology 16 -0.05 Marici Solutions 11 +0.00 Saragossa 13 +0.00 24-MAG 13 +0.02 Entegee 10 +0.04 Stefanini 13 +0.08 Ampstek 34 +0.09 Bright Vision Technologies 29 +0.14 TechSpace Solutions 13 +0.15 AARATECH 12 +0.16 Mitchell Martin Inc 17 +0.16 Learn Beyond Consulting 10 +0.17 HAN IT Staffing 10 +0.19 The Avian Consulting 11 +0.19 Aroha Technologies 10 +0.23 Goliath Partners 37 +0.26

Pattern. Low-LLM employers are almost exclusively India-based staffing firms reposting client requirements verbatim. High-LLM employers are US-based tech (especially AI-native / fintech / hot-recruit) firms. The LLM-authorship axis is not random — it tracks firm type. This matters for the mechanism test: the attenuation in Section 7 is partly controlling for firm-type (the low-LLM subset is disproportionately non-tech staffing firms), not purely for authoring style.

Panel (343 companies with n>=5 in both periods) correlations:

Δ metric rho with Δ score Δ AI-broad +0.147 Δ desc-length +0.219 Δ AI-strict +0.033 Δ breadth_resid +0.043

Positive correlation of Δ score with Δ AI-broad (r=0.15) and Δ desc-length (r=0.22) — companies that became more LLM-like also expanded their AI-mentions and lengthened JDs. But correlations are modest: same-company changes in authorship score explain only 2-5% of the variance in headline metrics. This is consistent with a LIMITED mediation role.

docs.title: 6. Correlation with Wave 2 findings (posting-level)
docs.text:
Pearson correlation of authorship_score with posting-level metrics:

metric pooled 2024 only 2026 only desc_len_chars 0.44 0.57 0.31 ai_broad_bin 0.37 0.09 0.18 ai_strict_bin 0.21 0.04 0.10 requirement_breadth 0.31 0.25 0.20 mentor_bin 0.18 0.14 0.15 tech_count 0.17 0.09 0.11 breadth_resid 0.16 0.004 0.11 req_header_bin -0.03 -0.04 -0.03

Reading. - Length drives the biggest correlation (r=0.44 pooled). Longer JDs score as more LLM-authored. This partly reflects the sentence_mean and bullet_rate components of the score. - After length-residualization, breadth correlation with authorship drops from 0.31 to 0.16. Roughly half the raw correlation between "LLM-authored" and "breadth" is explained by length alone. The remaining r=0.16 is the non-length portion of breadth. - AI-mention correlations are weak within a single period (2024: 0.04, 2026: 0.10 for strict), much stronger pooled (0.21). The pooled correlation is primarily driven by period itself — both authorship score AND AI-mentions jump together from 2024 to 2026. Within-period correlations are tighter to the mechanism test. - Req-header presence shows essentially zero correlation, suggesting the structural JD anatomy (having a requirements section at all) is independent of authorship style. T13 sectioning therefore remains a robust comparator.

docs.title: 7. UNIFYING-MECHANISM TEST (decisive)
docs.text:
Subset definition (within-period, primary). For each period, keep postings with authorship score in the bottom 40th percentile. This preserves within-period identification while cutting to the lowest-LLM tail.

  • Low-subset n: 25,480 (9,128 in 2024; 16,352 in 2026).
  • 2024 mean score in low-subset ≈ -0.35; 2026 mean ≈ +0.46. Still some period drift, but shift reduced from +1.15 to +0.81.

Sensitivity definition (global). Threshold = global q40 = +0.486. Keep postings with score <= 0.486. - Low-global n: 25,481 (19,960 in 2024 — 87%; 5,521 in 2026 — 13%). - This is the more conservative cut: the global low-40% subset is disproportionately 2024 postings because the entire 2026 distribution shifted right. Attenuations under this cut are a lower bound because 2026 sample shrinks to the 13% most-human-sounding postings (likely staffing reposts).

Attenuation table (low subset Δ / full-corpus Δ):

metric full Δ low40 within-period Δ attenuation low40 global Δ attenuation (global) AI-strict +0.1247 +0.0959 0.77 +0.0891 0.71 AI-broad +0.3744 +0.3219 0.86 +0.2403 0.64 Mentor (senior-only) +0.1693 +0.1222 0.72 +0.0243 0.14 Mentor (all) +0.0943 +0.0755 0.80 -0.0066 -0.07 breadth (length-residualized) +2.034 +1.443 0.71 +0.985 0.48 breadth (raw) +2.710 +3.112 1.15 +0.635 0.23 desc_len_chars +254.8 +558.1 2.19 -143.2 -0.56 req_header_bin -0.003 -0.005 1.63 -0.004 1.28

Reading the primary (within-period) column. - AI-STRICT Δ retains 77% of its magnitude. Still significant at SNR 38.9 (full is 70.0). - AI-BROAD Δ retains 86%. - Mentor senior-only retains 72%. - Length-residualized breadth retains 71%.

None of these headline effects halves. All exceed the 50% attenuation threshold.

Reading the global column. - AI-STRICT retains 71%, AI-BROAD retains 64%, breadth_resid retains 48% (just under half). - Mentor effects collapse under the global cut (to 14% and -7%). Mentor rate Δ is therefore disproportionately concentrated in the more-LLM-authored postings. - Raw breadth and desc_len attenuations below zero or above 1 indicate the global cut strongly selects on length (longer postings score higher and are trimmed), mechanically suppressing those metrics.

Which result is the right interpretation? - The within-period cut is methodologically cleaner: it identifies postings as "low-LLM-authorship within their period" rather than conflating period and authorship. Under this cut, the answer is clear — Wave 2 headlines attenuate by 14-29% but do not halve. - The global cut is the stress test: under the most adversarial definition (only the most-human-sounding 2026 postings), AI-mention rises still retain 64-71% of their magnitude, but mentor-mentions largely disappear. This suggests the mentor-mention Δ is the finding most vulnerable to LLM-authorship mediation.

Requirements-section anatomy. We only have a crude proxy here (header-present binary) because the T13 classifier parquet has not been re-run on the full corpus. The proxy shows near-zero Δ in both full and low subsets, so the T13 section-inflation finding cannot be adjudicated in T29 directly — it requires a targeted rerun.

docs.title: 8. Verdict
docs.text:
Hypothesis (LLM-authorship mediates Wave 2 headlines): PARTIALLY SUPPORTED.

Supporting points: - Authorship score distribution shifted by ~1.1 SDs 2024→2026, with variance widening. This is a real measurable change. - The score correlates with each Wave 2 headline in the expected direction (more LLM-like = more AI-mention, more breadth, more mentor language). - Mentor-rate Δ (especially senior-only) is the most attenuated and most sensitive to the global cut — the mentor story has the largest LLM-authorship contribution. - The length correlation (r=0.44) confirms that desc-length inflation is partly style, and using length-residualized metrics is the right approach.

Against: - Under the primary within-period bottom-40% cut, all headline effects retain 71-86% of their magnitude. The dominant mechanism is NOT recruiter tooling. - AI-STRICT Δ retains 77% — the clearest "real demand" indicator still largely survives. - Length-residualized breadth retains 71% — scope inflation is not pure prose. - Even the global cut leaves +7-9pp AI-STRICT Δ — roughly 3/4 of the aggregate. If all 2026 aggregate were recruiter LLMs, the low subset should have a near-zero Δ. It doesn't.

Implication for the paper framing. The paper does NOT need to reframe toward methodological contribution. Recruiter-LLM mediation contributes to a modest portion of headline Δs (15-30% in the primary cut) but does not drive them. The right story for the paper is hybrid: a real cross-domain AI-insertion and senior-role-redefinition shift that is partly styled by LLM writing tools. Reporting both full-corpus and low-LLM subset estimates is the robustness check.

Uncertainty. (1) The 2024 baseline is already LLM-contaminated (post-ChatGPT); estimated attenuations are lower bounds. If a true pre-2023 reference were available, low-subset Δ values might be larger (i.e., less attenuation, stronger support for real-demand). (2) The score is a rank, not a ground-truth classifier — sampling 100 low-scoring 2026 postings and spot-labeling would confirm they are indeed human-written. (3) Within-company sampling: a single recruiter-style LLM reposting could drive per-company within-period score variance; the 343-company panel correlations are reassuring on this front but not definitive. (4) The req_header proxy is crude; T13's full classifier on the 63,701 corpus would resolve the section-anatomy part.

docs.title: 9. Surprises
docs.text:
1. The 2024 baseline is not clean. 2024 median authorship score of -0.01 with SD 0.48 means roughly 50% of 2024 postings already look moderately LLM-authored against the 2024 distribution itself. ChatGPT launched Nov 2022, and early 2024 JDs show clear adoption. The "pre-LLM" baseline we'd want is pre-2023; we don't have it. All attenuation estimates should be read as conservative.
  2. Anthropic at rank 14 on the "most LLM-authored" list. A clean face-validity check for the score — it isn't a vibes-based heuristic that just flags "slop," it tracks actual LLM-writing-tool adoption which Anthropic obviously uses.
  3. India-based staffing firms dominate the bottom of the LLM score. Bright Sol, Info Way Solutions, PRIMUS, Allagi, R3, Marici, 24-MAG, Stefanini, Ampstek all cluster at or below zero. These firms appear to repost raw client requirements without LLM augmentation, which is intuitive — they're not writing originals.
  4. Mentor Δ is the most LLM-sensitive of the headline findings. Under the global cut, senior-only mentor Δ collapses from +17pp to +2pp. The "senior roles redefined as mentor/reviewer" narrative is the finding most at risk of being a writing-style artifact. Worth a sensitivity check in the paper.
  5. Raw description length inflation is LARGER in the low-subset than in the full corpus (+558 vs +255 chars 2024→2026 under within-period cut). Mechanically: the low-subset discards the longest 2026 postings (which score highest), so what remains is still longer in 2026 than in 2024 — the scraped-format change is real. Under the global cut, desc_len actually shrinks (because 2026 sample drops to the shortest / most human-sounding postings). This indicates genuine format change in scraped data independent of authorship.
  6. Req-header presence is essentially period-independent (-0.3pp Δ, SNR 4.3). JDs still have "Requirements" sections at the same rate as before. The T13 "requirements-section character count" rise is about section sizing within that structure, not about whether the section exists.
  7. breadth_raw Δ attenuates to 0.23 under the global cut (from +2.71) while breadth_resid attenuates only to 0.48. After controlling for length, the breadth Δ is more robust — meaning the content (not prose length) is what survives the authorship cut. This strengthens the breadth-as-real-demand reading.

docs.title: 10. Files
docs.text:
Tables in exploration/tables/T29/: - authorship_scores.csv / authorship_scores.parquet — per-posting feature & score (63,701 rows, 18 cols). - authorship_feature_means.csv, authorship_score_by_period.csv — aggregate summaries. - T29_score_quantiles.csv, T29_score_distribution_period.csv — distribution details. - T29_most_llm_companies.csv, T29_least_llm_companies.csv — top / bottom 20 by 2026 mean score, n>=10. - T29_company_panel.csv, T29_company_panel_corr.csv — 343-company panel and Δ-correlations. - T29_correlations_posting.csv — per-posting correlations (all, 2024, 2026). - T29_low_llm_subset_headlines.csv — full / low40-within / low40-global deltas. - T29_low_llm_vs_full_detailed.csv — attenuation ratios per metric. - T29_score_by_archetype.csv — per-archetype 2024 / 2026 medians.

Artifacts in exploration/artifacts/T29/: - authorship_scores.parquet — same as CSV but parquet-typed. - T29_feature_summary.json, T29_analysis_summary.json.

Scripts in exploration/scripts/T29/: - T29_01_features.py — feature extraction on full 63,701 SWE LinkedIn corpus. - T29_02_analysis.py — distribution / per-company / correlations / low-LLM subset test.

docs.title: 11. Recommendations for the paper and next steps
docs.text:
1. Report both full and low-LLM-subset estimates for Wave 2 headlines. Primary cut = within-period bottom-40%. Secondary = global bottom-40%.
  2. Use length-residualized breadth as the primary scope metric; report raw only as a comparator. Length is the single biggest confound.
  3. Rerun T13 section classifier on the 63,701 corpus so the requirements-section Δ can be adjudicated under the same low-LLM subset. This is the one Wave 2 finding we could not test cleanly in T29.
  4. Treat mentor-rate Δ with more caution. It is the most LLM-sensitive of the headline findings and should carry explicit robustness language.
  5. Consider a direct human-vs-LLM classifier trained on a labeled subset (pre-2022 OSS JDs, for instance) for a sharper mediation estimate. Current authorship score is corpus-internal.

docs.title: Exploration Tracking Index
docs.text:
Last updated: 2026-04-17 (Gate 2 memo written; V1 verification dispatching)

Gate 2 takeaways (see exploration/memos/gate_2.md): - Requirements section SHRANK −19% absolute chars (T13); responsibilities + benefits + role_summary + about_company expanded. Scope-inflation hypothesis INVERTED. - Domain dominates NMI by ~7× (T09: domain=0.26, period=0.04, seniority=0.03). Period dominates embedding space by ~180× over seniority (T15). - AI-mention signal SURVIVES every sensitivity: specialist + aggregator exclusion; SNR 24.7 broad / 35.4 strict; density SNR 4.99. Effect size ~34pp junior ~35pp senior — cross-seniority, not differential. - Seniors changed MORE than juniors 2024→2026 in content (T12: cos 0.942 vs 0.953); seniors reallocated from requirements (−22%) to role_summary (+84%) and about_company (+96%). - T11 refined strict-mgmt: mentor|coach|hire|headcount|performance_review after manage sub-pattern failed semantic precision (14% in SWE JDs). SNR 5.09, dominated by mentor (13.9%→20.8%). - J2 junior share DIRECTION STILL BASELINE-DEPENDENT (arshkon-only down, pooled-2024 up) — Gate 1 finding confirmed. - Emerging lead narrative: "Period-dominated, cross-seniority JD RE-WRITING toward AI vocabulary + section reallocation" — NOT restructuring of who gets hired. - V1 MUST re-derive: (1) AI-mention semantic precision on 50-sample, (2) requirements-section shrink, (3) requirement_breadth component-length correlation, (4) NMI on full corpus, (5) within-LLM-frame J2 flip, (6) relabeling cosines.

Gate 1 takeaways (see exploration/memos/gate_1.md): - Within-2024 SNR < 1 on every junior metric; junior-share direction depends on baseline choice. - J3 YOE-based entry rise is 95% between-company compositional (not within-company scope change). - AI-mention prevalence rose +33.3pp within-company on the 125-company arshkon∩scraped overlap panel — the only aggregate that survives composition, calibration, and concentration checks cleanly. - 2026-03/04 is a JOLTS Info-sector hiring low (91K openings = 0.66× of 2023 avg). - Primary seniority slices for Wave 2: J2 (seniority_final IN ('entry','associate')) junior, S1 (seniority_final IN ('mid-senior','director')) senior. Robustness: J3 (yoe≤2), S4 (yoe≥5). - Senior-side findings MUST lead with arshkon-only baseline (asaniczka's missing native entry labels inflate pooled-2024 senior share).

docs.title: SYNTHESIS — 2024→2026 SWE Labor Market Exploration
docs.text:
Author: Agent N (Wave 4 synthesis) · Date: 2026-04-17 Upstream: Gate 0 memo, T01–T23, T28–T30, V1/V2 verification, Gate 1/2/3 memos. Downstream: analysis-phase pipeline + paper draft (consumed first by any agent who writes the paper).

This document is the single synthesis the analysis phase reads first. It contains the lead finding, the three supporting claims, the recommended analytical samples for each claim, the confounders and their mitigations, the ranked findings with evidence strength, the new hypotheses for follow-on analysis, and the method recommendations that every analysis-phase task must respect.

docs.title: Executive summary
docs.text:
Between 2024 and 2026, US LinkedIn software-engineering job postings underwent a SWE-specific, cross-seniority, within-company rewriting toward AI-tool vocabulary. Three facts establish this pattern. First, AI-tool mentions in SWE postings rose from 1.5% to 14.9% (strict pattern, SNR 35.4; broad pattern 7.2% → 46.8%, SNR 24.7); 99% of that strict-pattern rise is SWE-specific under a difference-in-differences comparison to occupation controls (T18), and 102% of the strict-pattern rise on the 240-company arshkon∩scraped overlap panel is a within-company shift, not a composition effect (T16). Second, the cross-domain surface-vocabulary expansion coexists with a senior- disproportionate role-content shift: mid-senior mentor-binary rate rose 1.46–1.73× (vs entry 1.07×; T21), orchestration and strategic-scope densities both approximately doubled at mid-senior (T21), and an emergent "management + orchestration + strategic + AI" sub-archetype (97% of its members are 2026, n=860) now occupies 13% of the senior sample (T21 cluster 2). Third, this employer-side rewriting under-specifies AI relative to observed developer usage: broad employer AI-requirement rate 46.8% vs Stack Overflow 2024 worker usage 62–76% vs GitHub Octoverse 73% vs Anthropic 2025 exposure 75% — the gap is robust across all four usage assumptions in the 50/65/75/85% band, and seniors are more AI-specified than juniors (51.4% S1 vs 43.5% J2), ruling out an "AI-as-junior-filter" interpretation (T23). The originally hypothesized direction (anticipatory employer over-specification) is inverted.

The exploration also reframes two pre-registered constructs. Junior "scope inflation" is not supported as a junior-specific phenomenon: requirement-breadth rose equally at junior and senior (+34% vs +30%; T11), and the formal requirements section actually contracted (−19% chars corpus-wide; SWE −10.7pp share vs control +0.9pp; T13, T18). Seniority convergence is rejected: all three adjacent boundaries sharpened in 2026, with a +0.134 AUC gain on a yoe-excluded junior-vs-senior panel (T20). The 2024→2026 junior-share direction is baseline-contingent (J1/J2 down under arshkon-only, up under pooled-2024) and decomposes into: between-company composition (T06's 95% between-company J3 rise; 81% of AI/ML archetype growth comes from employers only-in-2026 per T28) plus LLM-label-routing selection (T29 +1.14 SDs authorship-score shift; V1-confirmed 2-3× labeled-vs-not-labeled J2-share gap). The paper should not cite "junior share rose" as an unqualified headline.

The recommended paper positioning is hybrid: a dataset + measurement contribution anchoring a substantive labor finding. The substantive lead is "SWE-specific AI- vocabulary rewriting," not "junior scope inflation." Framing (1) — SWE-specific AI rewriting — is the lead; framing (3) — tech-giant intern pipeline expansion — is a methodological subplot; framing (2) — pure platform/boilerplate evolution — is partially supported (requirements-section contraction, 2026-more-homogeneous corpus, recruiter-LLM mediation ~15–30% of the rise per T29) but does not dominate.

docs.title: Paper's lead sentence (draft)
docs.text: "Between 2024 and 2026, US LinkedIn software-engineering job postings added AI-tool vocabulary at a rate 99% specific to SWE versus control occupations (DiD, T18) and 102% attributable to the same 240 companies rewriting their own postings (T16), while employers simultaneously under-specified AI by 15–30 percentage points relative to the most conservative developer-usage benchmark (T23) — a cross- seniority, cross-archetype, geographically uniform rewrite that coexists with a senior-disproportionate role-content shift toward mentoring and orchestration (T21) and sharpening rather than blurring of seniority boundaries (T20)."

docs.title: Three supporting claims (one sentence each)
docs.text:
1. Within-company scope broadening is real (T16). On the 240-company arshkon∩scraped overlap panel, length-residualized requirement-breadth rose +1.43 composite units within-company (102% of aggregate; stable 94–147% within across five sensitivities), 76% of companies broadened, 62% broadened by >1.0 unit — the "same companies are asking for more kinds of things" pattern holds even after length residualization and equally at junior and senior seniority levels.
  2. Senior-role content shift is senior-specific, not corpus-wide templating (T21). Mid-senior mentor-binary rate ratio is 1.46–1.73× 2024→2026, entry is 1.07×, associate fell to 0.61× — the mentor rise is concentrated at mid-senior; an emergent "management + orchestration + strategic + AI" sub-archetype (n=860, 97% of its members are 2026) and a doubled staff-title share (2.6% → 6.3%) indicate a new senior role type rather than proportional template expansion.
  3. Employer posting-level AI requirements systematically under-specify worker AI usage (T23). 2026 SWE broad-AI requirement rate is 46.8%, below every plausible developer-usage assumption (50, 65, 75, 85%; the closest match is an unusually conservative 50% at which S1 broad-AI 51.4% just crosses); under central 75% usage the gap is −28 pp for broad and −61 pp for specific tools, inverting the pre-registered direction (anticipatory over-specification).

docs.title: Research question evolution (original → post-exploration)
docs.text:
Original RQ Post-exploration successor Verdict Lead evidence RQ1 (junior scope inflation + senior redefinition) RQ1a — SWE-specific AI-vocabulary rewriting (LEAD) Confirmed, reframed T18 DiD 99% SWE-specific ai_strict; T16 102% within-company RQ1b — Within-company scope broadening (SUPPORTING) Confirmed T16 breadth-resid +1.43, 76% of 240 cos RQ1c — Senior archetype shift (SUPPORTING) Confirmed, senior-disproportionate T21 mentor 1.46–1.73× mid-senior vs 1.07× entry; cluster 2 97%-2026 RQ1d — Seniority boundary sharpening (NEW) Confirmed T20 all three adjacent boundaries +0.054/+0.084/+0.003 AUC; J3/S4 yoe panel +0.134 RQ2 (task migration across seniority) RQ2 — Task/requirement migration (RETAINED, narrowed) Diffuse cross-seniority, not migration T11 breadth +34% vs +30% junior vs senior; T28 20/22 archetypes positive RQ3 (employer over-specification) RQ3 — Employer under-specification of AI Direction INVERTED T23 46.8% < 50/65/75/85% band; robust across benchmarks RQ4 (mechanisms — interviews) RQ4 — Mechanisms, with quantitative priors Strengthened T29 15–30% LLM-authorship mediation; T17 geographic null; T28 81% new-entrant

Key conceptual changes: - "Junior scope inflation" → rejected as junior-specific; breadth rose equally at every seniority level (T11). - "Junior-senior convergence" → rejected; boundaries SHARPENED (T20). - "Anticipatory employer restructuring" → rejected; employers TRAIL workers (T23). - "Tech-giant intern pipeline expansion" → supporting methodological subplot (T06, T16, T28); 81% of AI/ML-archetype growth is new-entrant-driven. - "Senior shift to management" → refined; senior shift is to mentoring + orchestration + AI co-deployment, not traditional people-management (T11 manage had 14% semantic precision; refined to mentor|coach|hire|headcount|performance_review).

docs.title: Data quality verdict per RQ
docs.text: RQ Analyses SAFE Analyses NEEDING CAVEATS Analyses UNSAFE RQ1a (AI rewriting) DiD SWE vs control on ai_strict / tech_count / ai_tech_count (T18 bootstrap CI clear of zero); within-company 102% on 240-co panel (T16); 4-of-4 J1-J4 panel unanimous AI rise (V1) ai_broad pattern (0.80 precision per V1 — report strict as primary, broad as sensitivity); any rate cited must specify exact regex + subset + denominator (V1 §4) Broadly conflating ai_broad and ai_strict (V2 corrected T28 ≥+10pp attribution) RQ1b (scope broadening) Length-residualized breadth on 240-co overlap (T16); composite-score correlation documented (V1.3 reports soft/scope/mgmt r>0.3 with length) Raw breadth numbers (report residualized as primary; V1 §3 rule); specialist and aggregator sensitivity mandatory (both survive, T16 §3) Raw requirement_breadth without length-residualization (71% content / 29% length per V1) RQ1c (senior archetype) Mentor rate cross-seniority (T21 §7); strict management pattern = mentor|coach|hire|headcount|performance_review (V1-refined); arshkon-only baseline on senior Mentor-rate magnitude method-dependent (V2.7 1.46× vs T21 1.73× mid-senior); precision of mentor senior-context is 100% on 20-sample (V2) Broad management pattern including bare manage (14% precision T11) or stakeholder (42% precision T21) RQ1d (boundary sharpening) Per-boundary AUC with T20 7-feature spec (5-fold CV, L2 logistic); J3/S4 yoe-excluded panel (AUC +0.134, V2 reproduced +0.134 exact) Associate cell n=39/51 → directional only; class-balance sensitivity on associate_vs_entry Under-cell-threshold per-archetype boundary fits (most < n=50 per boundary × period) RQ2 (migration narrowed) Archetype-stratified AI/breadth/mentor Δ (T28 22-archetype projection); within-company × within-archetype decomposition (T16 §8) Junior-senior gap closing claims require report of both arshkon-only and pooled baselines (senior-side asaniczka artifact) Any "juniors now look like 2024 seniors" claim — T12 relabeling rejected, T20 boundaries sharpened RQ3 (under-specification) Benchmark comparison across 50/65/75/85% band (T23); alternative framings (V2.2: 63.2% "currently using" gives gap −49.3pp at ai_tool vs broader +28pp at ai_broad) Worker-benchmarks are self-reported and platform-biased; unit mismatch (postings ≠ workers); the paper must report the qualitative gap direction, not a precise pp Single-benchmark citation without the 4-assumption sensitivity RQ4 (mechanisms) AI-strict attenuation 75–77% under low-LLM authorship cut (T29 + V2 agree); new-entrant decomposition AI/ML 81% (T28, V2 exact); geographic null r=−0.11 (T17 + V2 alternative control) Mentor-rate attenuation 72% (T29) vs 105% (V2 3-feature) — method-sensitive; cite as "15–30% mediated with uncertainty"; T29's 2024 baseline is already post-ChatGPT (lower bound on attenuation) Sharp citation of "71%" / "72%" T29 retention on mentor/breadth (V2 flagged method-sensitive)

docs.title: Recommended analytical samples for the analysis phase
docs.text:
Default SWE filter. source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true. n = 63,701 (arshkon 4,691; asaniczka 18,129; scraped 2026-03 19,777; scraped 2026-04 21,104).

Text-dependent analyses. Additional filter text_source='llm' via swe_cleaned_text.parquet (34,102 rows); always report per-cell coverage.
