docs.title: Seniority validation summary
docs.text:
seniority_final is defensible (Cohen's κ vs the Stage-5 title rules: arshkon 0.40, scraped 0.49; T03). No dual-flag violations; YOE ordering is correct. BUT:

  • LLM-frame selection artifact (V1.5 confirmed): scraped 2026 llm_extraction_coverage='labeled' rows have J2 share 4.3–6.2% while not-selected rows have 2.1–2.6%. LLM-labeling preferentially selects junior postings; analyses restricted to the labeled frame systematically overstate junior direction by +1–2pp. Every junior claim must report BOTH labeled and full-corpus directions.

  • arshkon native entry has 26.4% ≥5 YOE (T08). Native entry labels are YOE- incoherent on arshkon; any "entry postings want 5 YOE" finding likely predates AI and is a long-standing posting convention, not an AI-era shift.

  • "Graduate"/"New College Graduate" under-detected (T03): ~1,000 scraped SWE rows have clear junior titles but seniority_final='unknown'. Preprocessing fix recommended; in current data J1 is under-counted by ~2.4pp on scraped.

  • title_normalized strips level indicators (T30): J5/S3 title-keyword matches must operate on raw title, not title_normalized.

  • Seniors changed more than juniors 2024→2026 in content (T12 + V1.6 verified): cos(entry26, entry24) = 0.9602 > cos(entry26, midsr24) = 0.9294 → period-effect dominates; relabeling rejected; seniors' cosine shift (0.9421) is LARGER than entry's (0.9602).

docs.title: Known confounders with severity assessment
docs.text: Confounder Severity Mitigation Residual risk Description length growth (+35% median cleaned, +56% raw) High T13 section decomposition shows requirements SHRANK while narrative grew; V1.3 length-residualization on requirement_breadth (71% content / 29% length); T14 density reports (AI-mention 1.28× density over non-AI, not just longer) Some composite scores still length-correlated; cite residualized primary Asaniczka 0 native entry labels High Arshkon-only baseline primary on senior-side; pooled as sensitivity; YOE-based proxies (J3/J4) label-independent Senior-share direction depends entirely on baseline choice; paper MUST name the baseline Aggregator share doubled (9.2% → 16.6%) Medium Aggregator-exclusion is pre-committed sensitivity (Gate 0); T16 shows aggregator and direct trajectories are nearly identical on AI+breadth (+14.1 vs +12.4pp); T18 aggregator exclusion sharpens DiD (+10% on ai_broad) Aggregator-excluded should always be reported as sensitivity Company composition shift (74.5% new entrants in scraped) High T06 decomposition: 95% between-company on J3; T16 within-company 102% on AI-strict; T28 81% of AI/ML growth is new-entrant-driven Any longitudinal corpus-level claim needs returning-cos-only sensitivity Field-wide vs SWE-specific uncertainty Medium T18 DiD: ai_strict 99% SWE-specific; tech_count 95%; desc_len 37% (macro); soft_skills 0% (macro) Paper can safely cite SWE-specific for ai_strict/tech/breadth; CANNOT for length or soft skills LLM-frame selection artifact (V1.5) High (for junior claims) Every junior claim reports full-corpus AND labeled subset Junior-direction is fundamentally baseline-contingent AND frame-contingent Recruiter-LLM mediation (T29) Medium Report low-LLM subset as sensitivity; AI-strict retains 75–77% across score variants; mentor retention 72% (T29) vs 105% (V2); cite as 15–30% mediated with uncertainty Mentor-rate is the most LLM-sensitive finding; cite with explicit caveat 2026 = JOLTS hiring low (Info-sector openings 91K = 0.66× 2023 avg) Medium All claims framed as share-of-SWE, not volume; T07 JOLTS context in every metro / temporal analysis Volume-shift language prohibited in paper Platform taxonomy drift ("IT Services and IT Consulting" → "Software Development" +17pp relabel; bare "developer" lost 61pp native entry) High T05 shared-title stability check (14/20 common titles lost native entry); T10 legacy-stack title consolidation; Indeed cross-validation matches LinkedIn on J1/J3 levels LinkedIn native-label semantics drifted; any cross-period native-label comparison must flag is_remote_inferred 100% False Pipeline bug Confirmed T17; no remote/hybrid analysis is feasible until preprocessing fix Blocks any remote-work claim Description length as confound in authorship score (r=0.44 with score) Medium Length-residualized breadth r drops to 0.16 from 0.31; structural req_header presence has zero correlation Authorship score cannot separate prose from length AI-broad pattern 0.80 precision Medium V1 refinement dropped agent (66%) and mcp (57%); residual broad pattern includes bare ai/ml which pull AMB in narrow contexts Strict pattern (SNR 38.7) is primary; broad is sensitivity

docs.title: Confirmed hypotheses (with confidence)
docs.text:
1. AI-vocabulary rewriting is SWE-specific (T18, V2 verified). 99% of the ai_strict rise is SWE-specific under DiD; tech_count 95%; requirement_breadth 72%. Control postings do not adopt named AI tools (0.002 → 0.002). Bootstrap 95% CIs clear of zero. Confidence: high.
  2. Within-company scope broadening (T16 + V2 exact reproduction). Length-residualized +1.43 breadth; 102% within-company on AI-strict; 102% within on breadth_resid; 76% of 240 cos positive. Confidence: high.
  3. Senior archetype shift is senior-specific (T21 + V2 direction verified). Mid- senior mentor 1.46-1.73× vs entry 1.07×; mgmt+orch+strat+AI sub-archetype 97%-2026 n=860; 8-domain stratification (T28) shows senior mentor Δ +4 to +26pp across 20 archetypes. Confidence: high (direction); moderate (magnitude — report as range).
  4. Seniority boundaries sharpened (T20 + V2 within 0.01-0.02 AUC). All three adjacent boundaries +0.054/+0.084/+0.003; J3/S4 yoe-excluded panel +0.134 AUC (V2: +0.134 exact). ML/AI gained most clarity (+0.105). Confidence: high.
  5. AI rise is cross-archetype (T28 + V2 corrected). AI-BROAD positive in 22/22, ≥+10pp in 18/22; AI-STRICT positive in 21/22, ≥+5pp in 16/22, ≥+10pp in 6/22; systems_engineering is the clean zero-AI-strict control (+0.16pp, V2 exact). Confidence: high.
  6. AI rise is geographically uniform (T17 + V2 spot-check). 26/26 metros positive; CV 0.29; r(ΔAI, ΔJ2) = −0.11 null; Sunbelt leaders (Tampa Bay/Atlanta/Charlotte) not tech hubs. Confidence: high.
  7. New-entrant-driven AI/ML growth (T28 + V2 exact). 81% of 2026 AI/ML volume from employers only-in-2026 (935 cos); 23% from both-period (83 cos). Confidence: high.
  8. Requirements-section contracted SWE-specifically (T13 + T18 + V2 non-SWE validation). SWE requirements-section share −10.7pp; adjacent −10.9pp; control +0.9pp (opposite direction). V2 verified on 500 non-SWE postings that the classifier works on control (has_req_rate 73-86%). Confidence: high.

docs.title: Contradicted hypotheses (with evidence)
docs.text:
1. Junior scope inflation (original RQ1 junior-specific). Breadth rose equally at junior and senior (J2 +39% vs S1 +34%; T11); scope inflation is NOT junior-specific.
  2. Junior-senior convergence (original RQ1 embedded claim). All boundaries SHARPENED, not blurred (T20); the 2026 junior-vs-senior language gap is larger than 2024 by ~14 AUC points on the yoe-excluded panel.
  3. Anticipatory employer over-specification of AI (original RQ3). Direction INVERTED: employers under-specify by 15–30 pp vs worker-usage benchmarks (T23).
  4. Junior share rose unambiguously (default pre-exploration reading). BASELINE- CONTINGENT: arshkon-only J1/J2 DOWN −0.6 pp, J3/J4 UP; pooled-2024 all UP (T30); SNR < 1 on every junior metric (T05); LLM-frame flip confirmed (V1.5).
  5. Management language rose in senior postings (under broad pattern). Broad manage has 14% SWE semantic precision (T11); signal dominated by non-managerial uses. Refined to mentor|coach|hire|headcount|performance_review → mentor is the single driving term and is senior-disproportionate.
  6. AI postings are more aspirational than non-AI (junior-filter hypothesis). Modest at sentence-level (matched-share +0.24), NOT elevated at posting-level (LLM ghost risk-ratio 0.98 for ai_strict, 0.84 for ai_broad vs non-AI); reframe as "emerging-demand framing" not "aspirational padding" (T22).
  7. Period ~180× seniority in embedding space (Gate 2 overstatement). V1 re-derived as ~1.2× centroid-pairwise; NMI period/seniority ratio is 1.9× (V1 §4). Use NMI 8.6× domain/period as the defensible ratio.

docs.title: New discoveries (with novelty assessment)
docs.text:
1. T23 RQ3 inversion (NOVEL, high). The 2026 employer ai_broad rate (46.8%) is below the 50% floor of the plausible worker-usage range. Pre-registered direction was opposite. This is a publishable finding on its own.
  2. T21 "mgmt+orch+strat+AI" sub-archetype (NOVEL, medium-high). 97% of cluster 2 members are 2026 (n=860; vs n=30 in 2024 = noise). Staff-title profile concentrates the same bundle. Names a new senior role type for the management-literature.
  3. T20 seniority boundary SHARPENING (novel, medium). All three adjacent boundaries gained AUC; ML/AI gained the most (+0.105). Management-density replaced tech-count as the #2 discriminator at the mid-senior boundary in 2026.
  4. T28 AI-rise cross-archetype + systems_engineering zero-AI control (novel, medium). AI-STRICT rose in 21/22 archetypes; systems_engineering is +0.16pp — a clean natural-experiment control for interviews (RQ4).
  5. T17 geographic null (novel, medium). r(ΔAI, ΔJ2) = −0.11 rejects the spatial "AI exposure → junior displacement" narrative. Tampa Bay/Atlanta/Charlotte lead AI surge, not SF Bay/Seattle.
  6. T29 partial-mediation by recruiter-LLM (novel, medium-high). Authorship-score shift 1.14 SDs; AI-strict Δ retains 75–77% under primary cut; mentor/breadth method-sensitive. Establishes that 15–30% of the rewrite is recruiter tooling — a substantive methodological contribution.

docs.title: Unresolved tensions
docs.text:
1. T11 breadth +39% vs T13 requirements-section −19%. Compatible but must be explained explicitly in the paper. Breadth counts TYPES across the whole cleaned description; requirements-section counts CHARS in one section. The mechanism is "types migrating from requirements into responsibilities + role_summary + about_company."
  2. T06 95% between-company J3 vs T09 84% within-archetype J2. Different operational variables (YOE-based vs label-based). The aggregate "junior rose" decomposes into (a) new-entrant firms (tech-giant intern pipelines) and (b) within-archetype label drift — compatible.
  3. T12 seniors-changed-more vs T20 ML/AI-sharpened-most. Seniors changed more in absolute cosine; ML/AI gained the most boundary clarity because its 2024 junior- senior overlap was worst. Both true; separate claims.
  4. T29 mentor retention 72% vs V2 3-feature 105%. Method-sensitive. The defensible claim is "method-dependent attenuation 0–30% on mentor/breadth; AI-strict is robust across specifications at 75–77%."

docs.title: Posting archetype summary (T09 + T28)
docs.text:
22 archetypes discovered via BERTopic (k_min_topic=30, seed=20260417) on 8,000 LLM- cleaned stratified sample; projected to full 34,102 LLM corpus via nearest-centroid (T28). Primary NMI axis: domain (0.275) >> period (0.032) >> seniority (0.016). Domain/period ratio 8.6× (V1 full-corpus verified).

Top 10 archetypes by 2024→2026 volume and share change:

Archetype 2024 share 2026 share Δpp AI-strict Δ Key mechanism ai_ml_engineering 2.6% 16.2% +13.6 +26.4pp 81% new-entrant driven (T28 §8) backend_platform 3.5% 7.0% +3.5 +10.2pp Content-dense growth cloud_devops 7.6% 10.9% +3.3 +5.8pp AI tooling in CI/CD contexts data_engineering 7.7% 9.2% +1.6 +5.4pp Modest share growth generic_software_engineer 15.6% 15.4% −0.2 +3.0pp Largest single driver of J2 within-archetype rise frontend_react 6.8% 6.7% −0.1 +8.6pp Stable java_spring_backend 6.6% 4.8% −1.8 +5.7pp Shrinking slightly network_security_linux 5.1% 3.5% −1.6 +2.1pp Declining customer_project_engineer 5.9% 3.1% −2.8 +3.5pp Declining systems_engineering 11.0% 3.4% −7.6 +0.16pp Zero-AI natural control

docs.title: Technology evolution summary (T14)
docs.text:
• Rising (top 5 by raw pp): python +17.0, ci/cd +14.6, llm_token +11.4, typescript +8.7, kubernetes +7.9. 7 of top 20 rising are AI-era; 13 are traditional cloud/DevOps/Python.
  • Declining (top 5): dotnet −4.2, html −3.6, css −3.2, agile −2.7, sql −2.5. Length-normalized, agile/sql/javascript are null; dotnet/jquery/php are genuine stack-rotation.
  • New coalescence (2026 LLM-vendor cluster, n=13): llm_token, rag, copilot, claude, langchain, langgraph, openai, gemini, mcp, anthropic, cursor, fine_tuning, agent_framework at phi > 0.15. In 2024 these were singletons; by 2026 they form the single most internally-cohesive community. Adjusted Rand Index 0.545 between 2024 and 2026 on 89-tech shared panel.
  • Structured-vs-extracted validation (asaniczka skills_raw vs description regex): Spearman ρ = 0.985 across 107 techs. Description extraction is a recall superset.
  • Stack-diversity: tech_count mean 5.4 → 7.0 (+1.6) but density/1k fell from 5.04 → 3.47. The raw-count rise is partly length-driven; AI-mention density 1.28× (length-normalized) is real.

docs.title: Geographic heterogeneity summary (T17)
docs.text:
• 26 metros well-powered (≥50 SWE/period in both pooled-2024 and scraped).
  • AI surge uniform: 26/26 metros positive; mean Δ_ai_strict +12.8pp; CV 0.29. Tech-hub mean +13.1pp; non-hub mean +12.7pp (essentially equal).
  • Top 3 AI surges: Tampa Bay +20.0pp, Atlanta +18.6pp, Charlotte +17.2pp (financial-services / healthcare / aerospace Sunbelt metros — NOT tech hubs).
  • Null correlation: r(Δ_ai_strict, Δ_entry_j2) = −0.108 (p = 0.60); Spearman −0.143. Spatial "AI exposure → junior displacement" story is rejected.
  • Domain concentration: SF Bay 2026 ML-archetype share 32% (up from 12%); Seattle 29% (up from 4%). Other metros grew ML from tiny bases (Atlanta 17%, Raleigh-Durham 21%). The ML-archetype concentration widened in tech hubs.
  • is_remote_inferred = 100% False pipeline bug precludes remote analysis.

docs.title: Senior archetype characterization (T21)
docs.text:
• Patterns (V1 precision-verified):
  • people_management_strict = mentor|coach|hire|headcount|performance_review
  • technical_orchestration_strict = architecture review|code review|system design| technical direction|ai orchestration|workflow|pipeline|automation|evaluate| validate|quality gate|guardrails|prompt engineering|tool selection
  • strategic_scope_strict = business impact|revenue|product strategy|roadmap| prioritization|resource allocation|budgeting|cross-functional alignment

  • stakeholder (42% precision) and agent (44% precision) dropped from strict; team_building (10% precision) dropped entirely.

  • S1 (mid-senior + director) binary-share Δ 2024→2026:

  • mgmt +14pp (mid-senior 19.8% → 33.7%) / +9pp at director
  • orch-strict +21pp at mid-senior / +28pp at director
  • strat-strict +6pp mid-senior / +16pp director
  • strat-broad +12pp mid-senior / +18pp director

  • AI-strict +14pp mid-senior / +23pp director

  • Cross-seniority mentor ratio (the central senior-shift evidence): entry 1.07×, associate 0.61×, mid-senior 1.46–1.73× (T21 / V2), director 1.30×. Senior-disproportionate — rejects corpus-wide templating.

  • Emergent sub-archetypes (k-means k=4, senior-only, standardized features):

  • baseline_ic (n=4,795, 70% 2026)
  • mgmt_orch (n=1,523, 78% 2026, mentor 96%, AI 0%)
  • strat_scope (n=1,052, 76% 2026)

  • mgmt_orch_strat_ai (n=860, 97% 2026, AI 100%, orch 0.85, mgmt 0.25, mentor 0.39)

  • Staff-title profile: 2026 staff-titled seniors have +16pp mgmt, +11pp strat, +16pp mentor, +5pp AI vs non-staff seniors. Staff-title share doubled 2.6% → 6.3%.

  • Domain stratification: shift is ML/AI-concentrated for AI and orchestration, frontend-concentrated for management/mentorship, systems-engineering near-zero.

docs.title: Ghost / aspirational prevalence (T22)
docs.text:
• Validated patterns (all ≥80% precision): validated_mgmt_patterns.json.
  • AI-sentence-level aspiration: pooled ratio 1.75× (2024) → 3.20× (2026); matched-share delta +0.24 in 2026.
  • Posting-level LLM ghost rubric: AI-mentioning postings NOT elevated. RR(ai_strict) = 0.98; RR(ai_broad) = 0.89. No reframe to "aspirational padding."
  • Conclusion: AI-specific requirements are introduced with hedging (exposure to, familiarity with) — emerging-skill demand framing, not a step-change hard requirement. Postings remain realistic as whole documents.

docs.title: New hypotheses (from T24) — ranked for analysis-phase priority
docs.text:
# Hypothesis Test with existing data Priority 1 H_A — Cross-occupation employer under-specification of AI Stratify is_swe_adjacent + is_control by ONET; benchmark vs occupation-specific usage 1 2 H_B — Requirement-section contraction as hidden hiring-bar signal Regress requirements-share on period × seniority × archetype; company-level correlation with J3 rise 2 3 H_C — "AI-enabled tech lead" as emergent senior role type Within T21 cluster 2: title distribution + within-company trajectory 3 4 H_D — Senior-specific mentor rise is team-multiplier IC, not manager-ladder Classify title as IC vs manager; mentor rate stratification 4 5 H_E — Same-company J1 drop + J3 rise = labeling-regime shift Joint seniority_native × yoe distribution on 240-co panel 5 6 H_H — 2026 corpus samples different firm population Restrict 2026 to returning-cos-only; recompute every headline 6 7 H_I — AI as coordination signal, not skill demand Cross-reference with firm-level AI patents / earnings-call AI mentions 7 8 H_F — Sunbelt/tech-hub convergence on AI baseline Per-metro 2026 level convergence check 8 9 H_G — Staff-title doubling as senior-tier internal redistribution Within-co: 2024 mid-senior count vs 2026 staff count 9 10 H_J — Recruiter-LLM adoption senior-biased by HR tooling defaults T29 score × seniority stratification 10

See exploration/reports/T24.md for full statements and motivating evidence.

docs.title: Method recommendations for the analysis phase (MANDATORY)
docs.text:
1. T30 seniority panel. Every seniority-stratified headline must report the 4-row ablation (J1/J2/J3/J4 for junior claims, S1/S2/S3/S4 for senior). Load exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv; do not re-derive.
  2. V1-refined AI patterns. Strict pattern primary (SNR 38.7): \b(copilot|cursor|claude|chatgpt|openai api|gpt-?\d+|gemini|codex|llamaindex| langchain|prompt engineering|fine[- ]tuning|rag|vector database|pinecone| huggingface)\b. Broad as sensitivity (dropped agent_bare, mcp). Always cite by name.
  3. Length-residualization on any composite with length-correlating components. V1.3 found soft_skill_count r=0.363, org_scope_count r=0.399, management_STRICT r=0.351 with desc_cleaned_length. Primary metric for breadth is residualized (a=6.498, b=0.00182 global OLS fit). Cite raw as sensitivity only.
  4. Semantic precision sampling for every new pattern. Sample 50 matches per sub-term stratified 25/25 by period; apply 80% precision floor; drop failing sub-terms and re-run compound. manage (14%), stakeholder (42%), agent (44%), mcp (57%), team_building (10%) are all known fails — do not use unqualified.
  5. DiD vs control for every SWE-specific claim. Any claim "SWE is experiencing X" must test against is_swe_adjacent AND is_control. Use T18's framework. Bootstrap CI per metric.
  6. Section-decomposed text analyses. T13 classifier is the primary tool; V2 verified it works on non-SWE at 73-86% has_req_rate. Any prose-level finding must be cross-checked at the section-level.
  7. Report BOTH full-corpus AND LLM-labeled subsets. The J2 flip (V1.5) and the 30% scraped LLM-frame coverage make every junior/text claim require dual reporting.
  8. Full-corpus vs 240-co overlap sensitivity. For any longitudinal claim, report the within-company decomposition on the 240-co panel AND the full-corpus aggregate. If they disagree by >30%, the aggregate is composition-dominated.
  9. Arshkon-only baseline for senior-side findings. Asaniczka's missing native entry labels inflate pooled-2024 senior share by 19pp. Arshkon-only is the fairer comparison for native-label content; pooled-2024 as sensitivity.
  10. T29 authorship-subset sensitivity for any AI-content claim. Report headline Δ on full corpus AND on low-40% within-period subset. Mentor/breadth findings carry an explicit "method-sensitive 0-30% mediation" caveat.

docs.title: Sensitivity requirements per finding
docs.text: Finding Required sensitivities Status AI-strict rewriting cross-period Specialist-excl, aggregator-excl, LLM-frame, arshkon-only AND pooled, cap-50, broad-pattern-as-alt All reported (T16 / T18 / T28 / V1 / V2) Within-company breadth +1.43 No-specialist (221 cos), no-aggregator (213), pooled (589), cap-50, labeled-only (196) 94-147% within across all five (T16 §3) Senior mentor 1.46-1.73× Cross-seniority panel; arshkon-only vs pooled; pattern precision 100% on 20-sample Done (T21 / V2 §1.7) Seniority boundary sharpening Primary + no-aggregator sensitivity (increases sharpening); per-archetype (where n≥50) Done (T20 + V2 replicate) Employer-usage divergence 4-band usage 50/65/75/85%; strict AND broad; J2/J3/S1/S4 panels; alternative-usage framing ("currently using 63%") All done (T23 + V2.2) LLM-authorship mediation Within-period vs global cut; 5-feature vs 3-feature score (V2); per-metric attenuation Done (T29 + V2.5) Cross-occupation DiD Aggregator-excl, tier-strict; binary vs count metric Done (T18 + V2.1) Junior-share baseline-contingency 4-row panel J1/J2/J3/J4 × 2 baselines (arshkon-only, pooled); LLM-frame vs full Done (T30 + V1.5) AI/ML 81% new-entrant Exact employer-count decomposition; aggregator-contamination check Done (T28 + V2.4) Geographic AI uniformity No-aggregator, no-specialist sensitivities; arshkon-vs-scraped additional panel Done (T17 §7)

docs.title: Interview priorities (T25 handoff)
docs.text:
RQ4 interviews should adjudicate five concrete questions, each with a dedicated artifact in exploration/artifacts/T25_interview/:

  1. Scope realism: Would a 2024 or 2026 entry-level candidate realistically meet the stated scope in inflated_junior_jds.md? Four ghost-flagged entry-labeled JDs are attached; probe hiring-manager intent vs recruiter boilerplate.
  2. Content change vs stylistic change: What in the 2026 version of each paired JD (in paired_jds_over_time.md) reflects real hiring-requirement change vs LLM-augmented template? Four same-company 2024/2026 pairs on the 240-co overlap panel.
  3. Employer-usage gap mechanism: Why do SWE employers mention AI tools in 46.8% of JDs when developers use them at 62-75% rates (employer_usage_divergence.png)? Four candidate mechanisms: JD template lag, implicit assumption, hiring-manager belief that AI is "picked up on the job," or AI as an internal filtering criterion not stated in public postings.
  4. Senior role redefinition: Is the mgmt+orch+strat+AI senior profile (senior_archetype_chart.png) a new role or a relabeling of existing seniors? Probe with systems-engineering as the natural-experiment control (AI-strict +0.16pp; senior mentor Δ +7.6pp).
  5. LLM-authorship: When drafting a JD today vs 3 years ago, what is your pipeline? What percentage of JD language came from an LLM draft vs a copy-edit of an existing template? This probes T29's 15-30% mediation estimate qualitatively.

Sample sizes for interviews: 10-15 hiring managers + 5 recruiters + 3 HR-tooling operators, stratified by archetype (AI/ML, generic SWE, systems-engineering control) and by firm size (T06 entry-specialist vs. direct employer).

docs.title: Recommended paper positioning
docs.text:
Primary framing: hybrid dataset + substantive empirical labor paper with "SWE-specific AI rewriting" as the substantive lead.

Three contributions stacked:

  1. A longitudinal SWE JD dataset + preprocessing pipeline harmonizing three sources (arshkon, asaniczka, scraped) with 63,701 LinkedIn SWE rows and 155,745 control-occupation rows.
  2. A measurement framework (T30 panel, sensitivity dimensions, semantic precision protocol, composite-score correlation check, LLM-authorship mediation test) usable by future researchers working on posting data in the LLM era.
  3. Three substantive findings in order of evidence strength:
  4. SWE-specific AI-vocabulary rewriting (99% DiD) is cross-seniority, cross- archetype (20/22 positive strict), within-company (102%), geographically uniform (26/26 metros);
  5. RQ3 direction inversion: employers under-specify AI by 15-30 pp vs workers;
  6. Senior-disproportionate role shift toward mentoring + orchestration + AI-deployment, with an emergent mgmt+orch+strat+AI sub-archetype.

Venue implications. - ICWSM / WWW / CSCW: the methodological + hybrid positioning lands. - Labor economics (ILR Review, JOLE, BE Journal): the RQ3 inversion alone is publishable; the extended findings support the substantive contribution. - CHI / Management Science: the emergent senior role type (H_C / T21 cluster 2) could anchor a specific paper on tech-lead work. - Nature Human Behaviour / PNAS: the cross-occupation extension (H_A) could elevate the paper if implemented.

Recommended lead venue for initial submission: ICWSM dataset & methods track with a separate labor-economics follow-up. The RQ3 inversion is novel enough to anchor a standalone short paper or a dedicated section.

Lead sentence draft (from earlier in document):

"Between 2024 and 2026, US LinkedIn software-engineering job postings added AI-tool vocabulary at a rate 99% specific to SWE versus control occupations (DiD), 102% attributable to the same 240 companies rewriting their own postings, while employers simultaneously under-specified AI by 15–30 percentage points relative to the most conservative developer-usage benchmark — a cross-seniority, cross- archetype, geographically uniform rewrite that coexists with a senior- disproportionate role-content shift toward mentoring and orchestration and sharpening rather than blurring of seniority boundaries."

docs.title: T24 — Hypothesis Generation Beyond RQ1–RQ4
docs.text: Agent N · Wave 4 · 2026-04-17 Upstream: Gate 3 memo, V1/V2 verification, T01–T23, T28, T29, T30. Downstream: SYNTHESIS.md (T26) analysis-phase priority list, T25 interview prompts.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The pre-registered research questions (junior scope inflation, senior archetype shift, employer/worker divergence, mechanisms) have been either confirmed, inverted, or decomposed by the exploration. What the exploration surfaced is a harder problem: the 2024→2026 rewriting has specific structural features (SWE-specificity, senior- disproportion, within-company persistence, requirement-section shrinkage while breadth rises) that cry out for explanation beyond the original RQs. This report generates 10 new hypotheses that use the exploration artifacts as jumping-off points and can be tested in the analysis phase with the data already available.

Hypotheses are ordered by priority = novelty × evidence strength × testability (high / medium / low on each axis; top-line ranking at the end).

docs.title: Confirmation inventory
docs.text:
For completeness before the new hypotheses, here is what the RQ1–RQ4 framings now look like:

Original RQ claim Verdict Evidence RQ1a: Junior scope inflation (narrower junior rung, more senior-flavored requirements in entry postings) REJECTED as stated; DECOMPOSED T05 (SNR <1 junior-share); T16 (within-company breadth +1.43 residualized but equal at entry vs senior); T20 (boundaries SHARPENED not blurred) RQ1b: Senior archetype shift to mentor/orchestration/review CONFIRMED, senior-disproportionate T21 (mid-senior mentor ratio 1.46–1.73× vs entry 1.07×); T28 (senior mentor Δ +4 to +26pp across 20 archetypes, median ~+12pp); mgmt+orch+strat+AI sub-archetype 97%-2026 RQ2: Task/requirement migration (downward, upward, or cross-stratified) DIFFUSE CROSS-SENIORITY, not migration T08 (AI +34pp junior / +35pp senior ≈ equal); T16 (within-company AI-strict 102%); T28 (20/22 archetypes positive, 6/22 ≥+10pp strict) RQ3: Employer AI-requirement outpaces worker usage (anticipatory restructuring) DIRECTION INVERTED T23 (broad 46.8% < 50/65/75/85% band); V2 confirmed; seniors MORE specified than juniors (rules out junior-filter interpretation) RQ4: Mechanisms (interview-grade) STRENGTHENED, with quantitative priors T29 (authorship score +1.14 SDs; AI retention 77%, mentor 72%, breadth 71% under primary cut); T17 (geographic null r=−0.11); T28 (AI/ML 81% new-entrant-driven)

docs.title: Surprise inventory (findings that contradicted pre-exploration priors)
docs.text:
1. Requirements section SHRANK −19% chars pooled-2024 → 2026 (T13), with senior falling −22%. Pre-exploration framing assumed scope inflation in requirements. Instead, responsibilities (+67%), benefits (+87%), about_company (+71%), preferred (+152%), role_summary (+75%) expanded while requirements shrank. Pre-exploration intuition failed.
  2. Seniors changed MORE than juniors 2024→2026 (T12, T21). Cosine shift senior 0.942 vs entry 0.953. Mentor rate ratio mid-senior 1.73× vs entry 1.07×. Original RQ1 was framed around the junior rung as the dynamic cell; the data says seniors moved first.
  3. Seniority boundaries SHARPENED, not blurred (T20). All three adjacent boundaries (+0.054, +0.084, +0.003) gained AUC; J3-vs-S4 yoe panel gained +0.134 AUC. "Junior/senior convergence under AI" hypothesis cleanly rejected.
  4. Employers UNDER-specify AI vs developer usage (T23). Every published worker benchmark (SO 2024 62%, Octoverse 73%, Anthropic 75%) exceeds employer ai_broad (46.8%). Direction opposite to pre-exploration.
  5. Domain dominates seniority by 8.6× in NMI (T09 + V1). Pre-exploration organizing axis was seniority; actual organizing axis is tech domain archetype.
  6. AI/ML archetype growth is 81% new-entrant-driven (T28). Existing firms pivoting into AI/ML contribute only ~19%; 935 companies appear in the AI/ML archetype only in 2026. "Firms pivoting to AI" narrative is the minor story; "new firms post AI roles" is the major one.
  7. AI surge is geographically uniform: 26/26 metros positive, r(ΔAI, ΔJ2)=−0.11 null (T17). Top 3 AI-surge metros are Tampa Bay / Atlanta / Charlotte — not tech hubs. Spatial "AI exposure → junior displacement" story is rejected.
  8. manage has 14% semantic precision in SWE JDs (T11). Previous "management language rose" claims were regex-dominated by non-managerial uses ("manage data," "manage state"). Refined pattern landed on mentor as the single driving term.
  9. Staff title doubled 2.6% → 6.3% while senior share flat (T10 + T21). The senior tier is redistributing internally, not growing.
  10. AI-mentioning senior postings have MORE management and orchestration language in 2026, not less (T21). In 2024 AI was in IC/research roles; by 2026 AI is alongside managerial/orchestration content. The emergent mgmt+orch+strat+AI sub-archetype (97%-2026, n=860) is a new senior role type.

docs.title: Method suitability per RQ (post-exploration)
docs.text: RQ Best methods for analysis phase Cautions RQ1a SWE-specific AI rewriting T18 DiD (primary), T16 within-company Oaxaca (robustness), T28 cross-archetype coverage V1-refined strict pattern primary; length-residualize any composite RQ1b Within-company scope broadening T16 240-co overlap panel, length-residualized breadth; sensitivity across 5 ablations Must cite 71% content / 29% length split; cap-50 sensitivity RQ1c Seniority boundary sharpening T20 per-boundary AUC, J3/S4 yoe-excluded panel Associate cell small (39/51) — report as directional; per-boundary 5-fold CV RQ2 Task migration narrowed T28 archetype stratification, T21 senior-vs-junior mentor ratios Equal magnitude junior vs senior = diffuse, not migration RQ3 Inverted: employer under-specification T23 benchmark comparison across 50/65/75/85% bands; V2 alternative-framing check Posting ≠ worker units; report qualitative gap not precise pp RQ4 Mechanisms T29 authorship score; T17 geographic null; T28 new-entrant decomposition Mentor effect most LLM-sensitive; AI-strict most robust to score cut

docs.title: Key tensions to resolve in analysis phase
docs.text:
Tension 1 — T11 breadth rises +39% vs T13 requirements shrinks −19%. Both are real. - requirement_breadth = count of distinct requirement types mentioned anywhere in cleaned description (tech + scope + soft + cred + mgmt + AI + YOE + edu). - requirements section chars = character length of text classified into the requirements section. - The 2026 posting mentions more kinds of things across responsibilities, role_summary, and about_company while the formal requirements section contracted. The mechanism is section reallocation + breadth diversification. - The paper must explain this explicitly — it is not a contradiction but is guaranteed to be raised by a reviewer.

Tension 2 — T06 says J3 rise is 95% between-company vs T09/T28 say J2 rise is 84% within-archetype. Compatible: J3 uses a YOE-based proxy that is dominated by new-entrant tech-giant intern pipelines (compositional); J2 uses label-based entry+associate which within-archetype rose via LLM-labeling shift plus content labeling drift. Different operational variables.

Tension 3 — Seniors changed more than juniors (T12) but boundary sharpened most in ML/AI (T20). Seniors changed more in absolute cosine distance; ML/AI gained the most boundary clarity because its junior-vs-senior gap widened. Both true; the paper should separate "who changed how much" from "who became easiest to tell apart."

Tension 4 — T16 breadth residualized +1.43 within 240 companies vs T13 requirements-section-chars shrank. Breadth measures types across the whole posting; requirements-section measures chars in one section. Both can rise or fall independently.

Tension 5 — T29 AI-strict retention 77% (low40 within-period) vs T29 mentor retention 72% (within-period) vs V2 3-feature score gives mentor retention 105%. The AI-strict estimate is robust across score variants; the mentor estimate is method-sensitive. Analysis phase should cite AI as "15-30% mediated" and mentor as "mediation uncertain, possibly 0-30%."

docs.title: Data gaps for the analysis phase
docs.text:
1. is_remote_inferred is 100% False (T17). Preprocessing pipeline bug. Blocks any remote-work/hybrid analysis. Needs a preprocessing fix before the paper can contain any hybrid claim.
  2. No T13 classifier re-run on full 63,701 corpus. T29 could not adjudicate the section-anatomy finding under the low-LLM subset; a full-corpus classifier run would close this.
  3. No ground-truth "LLM-authored" labels. T29's authorship score is corpus-internal; a labeled calibration set (e.g., 100 postings with manually-verified "recruiter-LLM-drafted" vs "human-written") would sharpen mediation estimates.
  4. No pre-2023 baseline. 2024-01 asaniczka is already post-ChatGPT (Nov-2022). Every attenuation estimate in T29 is a lower bound. An archived pre-2022 SWE JD set (e.g., OSS job-postings corpus, internet archive snapshots) would let us compute a clean baseline.
  5. Indeed cross-validation only at end-of-pipeline levels (T05). Cannot diagnose LinkedIn native-label semantic drift over time without a period-matched Indeed labeled panel.
  6. Worker-usage benchmarks are self-reported. Direct observational measures (e.g., GitHub copilot telemetry, IDE plugin counts) would be more authoritative than SO Developer Survey but are not public at the population-weighted scale.

docs.title: New hypotheses (H_A through H_J)
docs.text: Each hypothesis is stated precisely, then: (a) exploration evidence that motivates it, (b) specific analysis-phase test with data already in hand, (c) novelty / publishability assessment. Hypotheses flagged by the orchestrator (H_A–H_H) retained; new H_I and H_J added.

docs.title: H_A — Cross-occupation employer under-specification of AI
docs.text:
Statement. The employer/worker AI-requirement gap (−28pp broad at the central benchmark) observed in SWE (T23) is a general feature of AI-exposed occupations, not a SWE-specific phenomenon. In occupations with high AI-task exposure (per Anthropic 2025 Economic Index), the employer posting-level AI-mention rate trails worker-survey usage rates by a similar or larger margin.

Motivating evidence. - T23 SWE AI-broad 46.8% vs SO 2024 workers 62% (−15pp at the most permissive benchmark; −28pp at central). - T18 DiD shows SWE-adjacent (ML eng, data sci, data eng, security eng) ALSO show substantial AI-strict rise (ml_eng +54pp, data_sci +15pp) and rise 95% of SWE-only change on tech_count. - Anthropic 2025 Labor Market Impacts study: 75% of Computer & Math TASKS are covered by Claude — task-weighted coverage is large across occupations.

Testable in analysis phase. - Use the existing unified.parquet AI-mention features stratified by is_swe / is_swe_adjacent / is_control × ONET-coded occupation (derivable from title regex). - For each occupation bucket with n≥500 in both 2024 and 2026, compute ai_strict / ai_broad rates and compare against published AI-usage benchmarks from the same occupation (e.g., accountants ~22% AI use per 2024 surveys; nurses ~8%; marketers ~30–40%). - Expected result: employer/worker gap persists across all high-exposure occupations with a similar direction as SWE. - Alternative result: SWE is uniquely under-specified because of its tech-savvy hiring audience, in which case the gap should close in occupations with less-expert hiring audiences.

Novelty / publishability. High. RQ3's inversion is already the paper's most novel single finding. Generalizing to cross-occupation extends the contribution from "SWE labor dynamics" to "employer-side AI signaling in general." Appropriate for labor-economics venues (ILR Review, BE Journal of Economic Analysis & Policy) or science/management venues where cross-occupation framing is welcomed.

Priority: 1 (novelty high, evidence direct, test feasible with existing data).

docs.title: H_B — Requirement-section contraction as hidden hiring-bar signal
docs.text:
Statement. The SWE-specific shrinkage of the formal requirements section (T13 −19% chars, T18 −10.7pp share vs control +0.9pp) reflects an implicit hiring-bar lowering that is masked by the simultaneous expansion of narrative sections (responsibilities +67%, role_summary +75%, about_company +71%, preferred +152%). Employers are removing hard requirements while expanding the "who we are / what you'll do" frame, which signals hiring-bar softening without an explicit announcement.

Motivating evidence. - T13 SWE requirements section fell −19% absolute chars, while responsibilities +67%, role_summary +75%, preferred +152%. - T18: SWE requirements-share fell −10.7pp; adjacent −10.9pp; control +0.9pp (opposite direction). - V2.9b verified on 500 non-SWE postings: classifier works on control (has_req_rate 73-86%); the divergence is real. - Meanwhile lieu degree emerged as a 2026 bigram (T12) — credential loosening is in the text. - T28 cross-archetype: universally positive desc_len_chars Δ (+85 to +594) but universally negative requirements-section pattern implied in the diffuse rise of benefits/preferred.

Testable in analysis phase. - Regression on the 34,102 LLM-labeled corpus: dependent = requirements-share (T13 classifier), independent = period × seniority × archetype × is_aggregator × posting_length. - Sub-analysis: do postings with shrunken requirements-section have higher yoe_extracted distributions or lower? If lower, hiring bar is literally lowering. - Qualitative: within the 240-co overlap panel, do companies with the largest requirements-section contraction also have the largest J3 (yoe≤2) rise?

Novelty / publishability. Medium-high. Connects a substantive finding (section restructuring) to a policy-relevant interpretation (hidden bar softening). Candidates for CSCW or CHI: JD-as-signaling literature is active.

Priority: 2.

docs.title: H_C — "AI-enabled tech lead" as emergent senior role type
docs.text:
Statement. The mgmt+orch+strat+AI sub-archetype observed in T21 (97% 2026, n=860) is not an artifact of clustering but a genuinely new senior role: an individual contributor or team lead whose mandate is to deploy AI tooling across a team's workflow. This role bundles: mentorship (39% mentor-binary in cluster), orchestration (85% density), AI deployment (100% AI-mention), and moderate management (25%). It is distinct from both the traditional "manager-track director" and the "IC-track staff engineer."

Motivating evidence. - T21 k-means cluster 2: n=860, 97% are 2026, AI 100%, orch 0.85, mgmt 0.25, mentor 0.39. - Staff title doubled 2.6% → 6.3% (T10); 2026 staff postings have +16pp mgmt, +11pp strat, +16pp mentor, +5pp AI vs non-staff seniors (T21). - T20: management_density jumped to |0.42| coefficient at mid-senior-vs-associate boundary in 2026 (from |0.20| in 2024) — managerial language is diffusing downward. - T16 cluster 4 "consolidating giants" (Google, Microsoft, AWS) shrank breadth but ramped entry-share — consistent with seniors-do-more, juniors-get-hired-in-batches.

Testable in analysis phase. - Within the 860 cluster-2 postings, stratify by title — what fraction are "staff engineer," "tech lead," "principal," "ML lead," "AI engineer"? Is the emergent role labelled? - Within-company trajectory: do the 62 "consolidating giants" (T16 cluster 4) disproportionately post cluster-2 profile postings in 2026? - Cross-tab with T28 archetypes: does cluster 2 concentrate in specific domain archetypes (AI/ML, cloud_devops, backend_platform)?

Novelty / publishability. High. "AI-enabled tech lead" is a qualitative management-literature concept; quantifying its emergence via clustering is novel. Useful for HBR/MIT Sloan style outlets and for RQ4 interviews.

Priority: 3.

docs.title: H_D — Senior-specific mentor rise reflects team-multiplier repositioning, not management-ladder promotion
docs.text:
Statement. The 1.46–1.73× rise in mid-senior mentor-binary rate (T21) reflects a shift from "individual contributor senior" to "team-multiplier senior" driven by AI automating IC tasks, NOT a conventional management-ladder promotion shift. This predicts: (a) IC titles with "senior/staff" grew faster than manager titles, (b) mentor language diffused more strongly in archetypes most affected by AI tooling (per T28 mentor Δ), and (c) within- company senior headcount didn't shift toward manager titles.

Motivating evidence. - T21 mentor senior-disproportionate: mid-senior 1.73×, entry 1.07× — NOT a corpus-wide shift. - Staff title doubled but director share essentially flat (T10, T30 S2=0.3% → 0.7%). - T20: management-density diffused from director-only to mid-senior (|mgmt_z| 2024 director 0.18 → 2026 mid-senior 0.18) — downward diffusion, not upward promotion. - T28 senior mentor Δ highest in qa_test_automation (+26pp), azure_dotnet_web (+20pp), dotnet_web_sql (+18pp), ai_ml (+17pp) — diverse archetype pattern consistent with "senior is now a reviewer role across the stack."

Testable in analysis phase. - On the 17,713 scraped senior (S1) postings, classify each title as IC / manager via regex (IC = "engineer," "developer," "architect," "staff," "principal" without "manager"; manager = contains "manager," "director," "lead"). Compute mentor-rate separately. - Expected: IC senior postings carry MORE mentor than 2024; manager postings carry mentor at a SIMILAR rate to 2024. The shift is in IC senior content, not in manager headcount. - T16 240-co overlap: do companies with falling IC headcount (cluster 0 entry-contracting retreat) also carry more mentor at IC senior level in 2026?

Novelty / publishability. Medium. The IC-senior-as-team-multiplier narrative is a corporate-management staple but has not been quantitatively demonstrated at scale on JD data. Could anchor a Management Science submission.

Priority: 4.

docs.title: H_E — Same-company J1 label drop + same-company J3 YOE rise = hiring juniors by YOE but not labeling them entry
docs.text:
Statement. Companies like Google, Walmart, Microsoft are running large new-grad pipelines (high J3 yoe≤2 share) but are NOT labeling those postings as seniority_native='entry' on LinkedIn. This is a platform-taxonomy/labeling convention — not a real hiring change — that makes J1 label-based entry share appear to shrink while YOE-based J3 rises. The paper must treat this as a measurement-regime shift: "employers label 'entry' differently on 2026 LinkedIn than on 2024 Kaggle snapshots."

Motivating evidence. - T06: Google J3=52% / J1=0.9%; Walmart J3=74% / J1=0%; Microsoft J3=39%. 48% of entry-specialist postings come from 11 tech giants with essentially zero seniority_native='entry' labels. - T05: bare "developer" lost 61pp native-entry share arshkon vs scraped. - Gate 1 T30: arshkon-only J1/J2 DOWN −0.6pp; J3/J4 UP +2.3pp/+3.4pp. Split direction = measurement-regime difference. - T16 Cluster 4 "consolidating giants" has d_J2 +4pp (label entry rising within cluster) but the corpus J1/J2 under arshkon-only goes DOWN — compatible only if the 2026 entry-labeled cohort in cluster-4 cos is a smaller fraction of their overall entry-hire intake (T06's intern pipelines).

Testable in analysis phase. - Within the 240-co panel, for each cos stratify postings by seniority_native (NULL / entry / associate / ...) × yoe_extracted bucket (0-1 / 2 / 3-4 / 5+). Compute 2024→2026 shift in the joint distribution. - Expected: 2024 postings have stronger alignment (entry label → yoe≤2); 2026 postings have weaker alignment (many yoe≤2 postings labelled unknown or mid-senior). - Cross-check with Indeed labels (T05): does Indeed still align labels tightly with YOE in 2026? If yes, it's a LinkedIn-specific drift.

Novelty / publishability. Medium. Methodological contribution about cross-period longitudinal JD data. Important caveat for the paper; worth a dedicated methods section.

Priority: 5.

docs.title: H_F — Sunbelt AI surge is late-adopter catch-up; tech-hub AI surge is baseline-approach
docs.text:
Statement. The top AI-surge metros (Tampa Bay +20pp, Atlanta +18.6pp, Charlotte +17.2pp) are late-adopter financial-services / healthcare / aerospace clusters whose 2024 baseline was ~0-1% and whose 2026 level is 17-21%. The top "baseline" metros (SF Bay, Seattle) had 2024 baselines of 1.3-2.7% and 2026 levels of 16.2-17.7% — essentially the same 2026 level but different 2024 starts. Both groups are converging on ~17% ai_strict — the Sunbelt closed the gap from 0, tech hubs closed the last mile from elevated-but-not-saturated.

Motivating evidence. - T17: 26/26 metros positive ai_strict Δ; mean +12.8pp, CV 0.29. Tech-hub Δ +13.1pp; non-hub Δ +12.7pp — essentially equal. - Top AI-surge: Tampa Bay (2024 1.4% → 2026 21.4%), Atlanta (0.5% → 19.1%), Charlotte (0.0% → 17.2%). - Top baseline 2026: SF Bay 16.2%, Seattle 16.4%, NYC 16.7%. All three sit at the national-mean level.

Testable in analysis phase. - Per-metro, compute 2026 ai_strict level (not Δ). Plot against 2024 baseline. If convergence hypothesis holds, the 2026 level should be nearly constant across metros (~17%) while 2024 varies. - Within Sunbelt metros, stratify by industry (financial, healthcare, defense) — is the AI surge concentrated in financial-services cos? - Within tech hubs, stratify by archetype — is ML/AI archetype share carrying the residual baseline-approach? (T17 reports SF Bay 32%, Seattle 29% — consistent with ML-archetype saturation explaining the national-mean 2026 level.)

Novelty / publishability. Medium. Geographic convergence interpretation for a technology-diffusion phenomenon. Interesting for economic geography; less central to the lead paper.

Priority: 8.

docs.title: H_G — Staff-title doubling reflects senior-tier internal redistribution, not growth
docs.text:
Statement. The staff-title share doubling (2.6% → 6.3%, T10 + T21) alongside a flat overall senior share (S1 43.3% → 43.3% in arshkon-only-vs-scraped) reflects the senior tier redistributing internally: mid-senior postings are being renamed "staff" at a higher rate. This is a title-inflation signal within the senior tier that does not correspond to real expansion of the senior workforce.

Motivating evidence. - T10 title concentration stable: 554 → 507 → 533 unique titles per 1K. - T30 S1 share: arshkon 44.3% / pooled 59.6% / scraped 43.3% (near-flat under arshkon-only). - T21 staff-title 2026 profile: mgmt-binary 0.48 (+16pp over non-staff), mentor 0.47 (+16pp), AI 0.18 (+5pp). Staff-title concentrates the senior-archetype shift. - T10 disappearing titles: java architect, drupal developer, devops architect, senior php developer, sr. .net developer — legacy-stack consolidation.

Testable in analysis phase. - Within the 240-co overlap panel, for each company: count of mid-senior postings in 2024 vs staff-labelled postings in 2026. If hypothesis holds, the per-company mid-senior count should be stable while staff count rose. - Compare the content (T21 density features) of "senior X" postings in 2024 vs "staff X" postings at the same company in 2026. If the staff profile just takes the 2024 senior content + more orch/AI, it's title inflation.

Novelty / publishability. Medium. Connects to broader "job title inflation" literature (Eisfeldt-Falato-Xiaolan 2023); useful color but not a paper-lead.

Priority: 9.

docs.title: H_H — 2026 LinkedIn SWE corpus reflects a different firm population than 2024
docs.text:
Statement. The 81% new-entrant share of AI/ML archetype growth (T28) means the 2026 LinkedIn SWE corpus is sampling a substantively different employer population than 2024 — more startups, more AI-native firms, more tech-giant coverage, more aggregators. The paper's longitudinal claims are partly a sampling-frame artifact: the within-company decomposition (T16 102% on AI) corrects for this, but any corpus-level metric has an implicit "which companies are posting" confound.

Motivating evidence. - T28: 935 AI/ML cos only-in-2026 vs 83 both-period; new-entrant volume 1,634 / 2,014 = 81%. - T06: 74.5% of scraped cos are new entrants; aggregator share 9.2% → 16.6%. - T17: Seattle ML-archetype share 4.3% → 29.1% (+25pp); SF Bay 11.6% → 31.8% (+20pp) — compositional shifts are large. - T16 within-vs-between: AI-strict 102% within (same cos changing); J3 only 4% within (new cos entering).

Testable in analysis phase. - Restrict 2026 analysis to the 2,024 returning companies (co in both periods). Recompute every headline. - If the restricted-panel Δ matches the full-corpus Δ within 20%, the sampling-frame artifact is minor. If it differs by >50%, the longitudinal claim needs a sampling caveat. - Expected (from T16): AI-mention rise survives restriction (102% within); J3-based junior rise attenuates (96% between); AI/ML archetype share rise may halve under restriction.

Novelty / publishability. High. Methodological contribution about longitudinal posting data. Important for the paper to address explicitly; a clean sample-frame sensitivity table makes the paper bulletproof to reviewer objections.

Priority: 6.

docs.title: H_I — AI rewriting is a "coordination signal," not a "skill demand"
docs.text:
Statement. The SWE-specific AI-vocabulary rewriting (T18 DiD 99% SWE-specific) is not primarily communicating "we need workers with AI skills" (since T23 shows employers under-specify vs worker usage); it is communicating "we are an AI-using firm" — a coordination signal to other stakeholders (investors, candidates considering offers, competitors). This predicts that AI-mention rates correlate with firm-level AI-adoption signals (funding rounds tagged "AI," public AI strategy announcements) more than with actual AI-use-on-the-job indicators.

Motivating evidence. - T23 RQ3 inversion: employer ai_broad 46.8% < SO 2024 worker usage 62-76%. If postings were communicating skill demand, they would track usage. - T22 AI-ghostiness: AI-sentences are modestly more aspirational (matched-share +0.24) but posting-level ghost rate is NOT elevated — postings are realistic as documents but individual AI requirements are hedged. - T17 correlation: Δ_ai_strict × Δ_entry_j2 null (r=−0.11). If AI-mention were tracking actual hiring-demand shifts, we'd expect non-zero correlation. - T16 "Consolidating giants" (Google, Microsoft, AWS): d_ai_strict +9.4pp — below panel mean. The firms most likely to use AI tools internally post it LEAST. Consulting firms with AI practice lines (cluster 1 "AI-forward tech-stacking" +41pp) post it most.

Testable in analysis phase. - Cross-reference the 240-co overlap panel with public AI-adoption signals: Does AI-mention rate correlate with (a) count of AI patents filed 2023-2025, (b) count of "AI strategy" announcements in earnings calls, (c) press-release AI mentions? If r is high, the coordination-signaling hypothesis is supported. - Compare intra-company posting consistency: do companies that mention AI in some postings mention it in ALL postings (template-wide signal) or only in AI-flavored roles (demand-specific)? If template-wide, coordination-signaling wins.

Novelty / publishability. High. Bridges labor-economics (hiring signals) with information-economics (firm-level coordination games). Publishable in a management/info-systems venue. The paper could add a sentence in the discussion noting this as a mechanism hypothesis.

Priority: 7.

docs.title: H_J — Recruiter-LLM adoption is senior-biased because HR tooling defaults to mid-senior templates
docs.text:
Statement. The observation that seniors changed more than juniors (T12: senior cosine shift 0.942 vs entry 0.953) combined with T29's finding that mentor-rate Δ is the most LLM-sensitive of the headline findings (attenuates most under the global cut, 14% retention) suggests: recruiter-LLM tooling preferentially drafts mid-senior JDs because that's the default template in HR software (ATS systems default to mid-senior roles for re-use). Junior postings are more often direct recruiter text (intern pipelines, new-grad programs) while senior postings are LLM-augmented templates.

Motivating evidence. - T12: senior 2026-vs-2024 cosine 0.942 < entry 0.953 (seniors changed more). - T29: mentor-rate Δ retention under global cut = 14% (most attenuated); AI-strict 71% (robust); senior-only mentor attenuation matches the "seniors more LLM-drafted" prediction. - T29 top-LLM companies: Harvey (AI law startup, ~60-70% senior), Alignerr (ML startup, 80% senior), Anthropic (tech giant, 85% senior by T30 headline). These are companies with mostly senior postings. - T29 bottom-LLM companies: India-based staffing firms (Bright Sol, PRIMUS, Allagi). Staffing firms have more generic (often yoe-leaning-low) postings with less LLM augmentation.

Testable in analysis phase. - Within the 63,701 corpus, compute T29 authorship score × seniority_final. If hypothesis holds, senior mean score should be higher than junior mean within each period. - Intra-company analysis: for each co with ≥5 senior + ≥5 junior postings, compute score-gap (senior − junior). If hypothesis holds, the senior > junior gap should widen 2024 → 2026. - Behavioral test: interview 3-5 recruiters; ask "when you draft a new JD from scratch vs edit an existing template, which is it for senior vs junior roles?"

Novelty / publishability. Medium-high. Connects the LLM-authorship mechanism (T29) to a specific behavioral/tooling driver. Directly actionable for RQ4 interviews.

Priority: 10.

docs.title: Ranked priority list (for analysis-phase effort allocation)
docs.text:
Rank Hypothesis Why priority 1 H_A — Cross-occupation employer under-specification Extends lead novel finding (RQ3 inversion) to a broader class. Direct test with existing control-occupation data. 2 H_B — Requirement-section contraction as hidden hiring-bar signal Explains the T13 shrinkage substantively; connects to policy. Testable with existing T13 classifier outputs. 3 H_C — "AI-enabled tech lead" as emergent senior role Names the 97%-2026 cluster 2; directly informs RQ4 interview guide. 4 H_D — Senior IC-as-team-multiplier vs promotion shift Resolves "seniors changed more" finding into an IC/manager title split. 5 H_E — Same-company J1 drop + J3 rise = labeling regime shift Critical methods caveat; paper cannot cite J1 headlines without addressing. 6 H_H — 2026 corpus samples different firm population Sampling-frame robustness check; required for longitudinal claim defensibility. 7 H_I — AI as coordination signal, not skill demand Explains RQ3 inversion mechanism; publishable on its own. 8 H_F — Sunbelt/tech-hub convergence on AI baseline Geographic color; less central to lead. 9 H_G — Staff-title doubling as senior-tier redistribution Title inflation; useful but secondary. 10 H_J — Recruiter-LLM adoption is senior-biased by tooling defaults Mechanism for T12 "seniors changed more"; actionable for interviews.

Top three (H_A, H_B, H_C) are candidates for the paper's "additional findings" section. H_E and H_H should be treated as methodological robustness checks and folded into the "limitations / robustness" section. H_D, H_I, H_J are good interview seeds for RQ4.

docs.title: Cross-references
docs.text:
• Gate 3 memo (exploration/memos/gate_3.md) ranked findings table.
  • V1 verification (exploration/reports/V1_verification.md) for pattern precision and length-residualization rules.
  • V2 verification (exploration/reports/V2_verification.md) for DiD CI, T29 method-sensitivity, T28 strict/broad correction.
  • T21 (senior role evolution) + T28 (archetype stratification) for H_C, H_D mechanism tests.
  • T16 company clusters for H_E, H_H within-company decomposition.
  • T17 geographic null + T23 benchmarks for H_F, H_I.
  • T29 authorship score + company rankings for H_J.

End T24.
